topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Introduction to Field Robotics

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> *Field Robotics* is the sub-discipline of robotics concerned with the design, development, deployment, and control of **autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems** that operate in **unstructured, unstructured, and dynamic real-world environments** outside the controlled confines of a factory floor.

Unlike **industrial robotics** (which thrives in highly structured, predictable, caged environments with repetitive tasks), field robotics tackles the *opposite* problem: the environment is unknown, the terrain is uneven, the weather changes, lighting varies, and obstacles are non-standard.

**Standard KTU Terminology at a Glance:**

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Unstructured Environment** | A workspace without fixed landmarks, defined paths, or pre-mapped geometry. | A forest, a disaster site, open farmland. |
| **Autonomy Level** | The degree to which a robot can perceive, decide, and act without human intervention. | SAE Level 0 (manual) → Level 5 (fully autonomous). |
| **All-Terrain Platform** | A mobile base designed to traverse rough, uneven, or hazardous surfaces. | Mars Rover, agricultural tractor, tracked rescue vehicle. |
| **Teleoperation** | Remote human control of a robot via a communication link. | Subsea ROV, bomb-disposal robot. |
| **SLAM** | Simultaneous Localization and Mapping — building a map while tracking the robot's pose within it. | LIDAR-based mapping for a search robot. |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** | The number of independent parameters that define a robot's configuration. | 6-DoF robotic arm, 4-DoF mobile base. |
| **Payload** | The maximum mass a robot can carry/operate with while maintaining performance. | 5 kg for a small UAV, 1500 kg for a mining truck. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Factory Worker vs. The Safari Guide Analogy**
> Imagine a **factory robot** as a worker in a kitchen where every spice jar is in the exact same spot every day, the recipe never changes, and the lights are always on. Easy.
> Now imagine a **field robot** as a safari guide dropped into a jungle at night, in the rain, with a broken compass. The path is muddy, animals are moving, the river has changed course, and no map exists. The guide must still *get to the destination, avoid the crocodiles, and build a mental map of the terrain — all in real time*.
> **That** is the field robotics problem.

The key physical constants and metrics a KTU student must internalize:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** for a fully spatial rigid body in 3D: **6** (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- **Standard field autonomy levels** are graded on a scale from **0 (teleoperated)** to **5 (fully autonomous)** per the SAE J3016-inspired robotics framework.
- **Operational range** of field robots spans from **sub-meter (micro-UAVs)** to **planetary-scale (Mars rovers, ~225 million km)**.

## 1.3 Why Field Robotics Matters — KTU 2024 Motivation

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 specifically motivates field robotics through four pillars:
> 1. **Safety** — replacing humans in hazardous jobs (bomb disposal, mine clearance, disaster response).
> 2. **Productivity** — 24/7 operation in agriculture, mining, surveillance.
> 3. **Exploration** — deep sea, polar caps, planetary surfaces where humans cannot easily go.
> 4. **Sustainability** — precision agriculture reduces water/fertilizer use by **20–40%** (FAO estimate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Structured Logic: The Four Pillars of a Field Robot

Every field robot, regardless of application, is built from **four tightly-coupled subsystems**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to explain how these interact.

### Pillar 1 — **Perception (Sensors)**
- **Function:** Convert physical phenomena (light, sound, distance, chemical concentration) into digital signals.
- **Field-specific challenge:** Sensors must survive **dust, water, vibration, temperature swings, and EMI**.
- **Common sensor stack:**
  - **LIDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging) — 2D/3D laser scanning.
  - **Stereo / RGB-D Cameras** — visual odometry, object detection.
  - **IMU** (Inertial Measurement Unit) — 6-DoF pose estimation via accelerometers + gyroscopes.
  - **GNSS** (Global Navigation Satellite System) — GPS/GLONASS/Galileo for outdoor absolute positioning.
  - **Ultrasonic / Infrared** — short-range obstacle detection.
  - **Force/Torque sensors** — for manipulators in contact with terrain.

### Pillar 2 — **Cognition (Computation)**
- **Function:** Sensor fusion, path planning, decision-making, SLAM.
- **Field-specific challenge:** Limited onboard power and CPU; algorithms must be **real-time and energy-efficient**.
- **Core algorithms in the KTU syllabus:**
  - Kalman Filter / Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for state estimation.
  - A*, D*, RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) for path planning.
  - Occupancy Grid Mapping.
  - Particle Filters for Monte-Carlo Localization.

### Pillar 3 — **Action (Actuators & Locomotion)**
- **Function:** Convert commands into physical motion.
- **Locomotion taxonomy (must memorize for KTU):**
  - **Wheels** — efficient on flat, hard ground.
  - **Tracks (Tracked)** — superior traction on mud/sand.
  - **Legs (Multi-legged)** — negotiate stairs, rubble; biologically inspired.
  - **Hybrid (Wheel-Leg)** — combines speed + adaptability.
  - **Aerial (Rotary/Flapping wing)** — UAVs, drones.
  - **Marine (Propeller/Thruster)** — AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles), USVs (Unmanned Surface Vehicles).
  - **Subterranean (Screw/Burrowing)** — mining and tunneling.

### Pillar 4 — **Communication (Networking)**
- **Function:** Teleoperation, multi-robot coordination, data offload.
- **Field-specific challenge:** Limited bandwidth beyond the urban wireless envelope.
- **Common protocols:** Wi-Fi (short range), 4G/5G (cellular), RF radios (long range, low data), acoustic modems (underwater), satellite links (interplanetary).

## 2.2 The Why and How Behind Each Pillar

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is field robotics harder than industrial robotics?**
> The **V-DIM-V principle** — **Variability, Dynamics, Incompleteness, Maplessness, Vulnerability**.
> - *Variability* — Lighting, weather, terrain texture change unpredictably.
> - *Dynamics* — Obstacles may be *moving* (humans, animals, vehicles).
> - *Incompleteness* — Sensors have finite range and noise; partial observability is the norm.
> - *Maplessness* — Often, no prior map exists.
> - *Vulnerability* — A single sensor failure or wheel slip can cascade into mission failure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield and frequently tested.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (spatial body)** | $DoF = 6$ | — | 3 translations + 3 rotations. Fixed for any free rigid body in 3D. |
| 2 | **Wheel odometry (forward kinematics)** | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k)\,\Delta t$ | $x, y$ in $\mathrm{m}$, $v$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$, $\theta$ in $\mathrm{rad}$ | $\Delta t$ = sampling interval (s). Assumes no slip. |
| 3 | **Wheel odometry (lateral)** | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k)\,\Delta t$ | Same as above | — |
| 4 | **Wheel odometry (heading)** | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$ | $\omega$ in $\mathrm{rad/s}$ | — |
| 5 | **Ackermann turning radius** | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta)}$ | $L$ = wheelbase (m), $\delta$ = steering angle (rad), $R$ in $\mathrm{m}$ | Valid when $\delta \neq 0$. For $\delta = 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight line). |
| 6 | **Differential drive linear velocity** | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(v_R + v_L)$ | $r$ = wheel radius (m), $v_R, v_L$ in $\mathrm{rad/s}$ or $\mathrm{m/s}$ | Average of two wheels. |
| 7 | **Differential drive angular velocity** | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{b}(v_R - v_L)$ | $b$ = wheel separation (m) | — |
| 8 | **LIDAR range equation (power received)** | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t\,G_t\,G_r\,\lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | $P$ in $\mathrm{W}$, $G$ = antenna gain, $\lambda$ in $\mathrm{m}$, $\sigma$ = cross-section in $\mathrm{m^2}$, $R$ in $\mathrm{m}$ | Classical radar/range equation. |
| 9 | **GPS Dilution of Precision (DOP)** | $\sigma_{pos} = \sigma_{UERE} \times DOP$ | $\sigma$ in $\mathrm{m}$, dimensionless DOP | DOP $\geq 1$; lower is better. |
| 10 | **Power budget for mobile robot** | $P_{total} = P_{motion} + P_{compute} + P_{sensors} + P_{comm}$ | $P$ in $\mathrm{W}$ | All four terms are mission-critical. |
| 11 | **Endurance (battery-limited)** | $T = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{total}}$ | $E$ in $\mathrm{J}$ or $\mathrm{Wh}$, $T$ in $\mathrm{s}$ or $\mathrm{h}$ | $1\ \mathrm{Wh} = 3600\ \mathrm{J}$. |
| 12 | **Steering angle saturation (Ackermann)** | $\delta_{max} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R_{min}}\right)$ | $R_{min}$ = minimum turning radius | Defines physical limit. |
| 13 | **Field of View (camera)** | $FoV = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | $d$ = sensor size (m), $f$ = focal length (m) | For a pinhole camera. |
| 14 | **Update rate requirement (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f$ in $\mathrm{Hz}$ | Sampling theorem for sensor bandwidth. |

**Real-world utility:** These formulas directly govern design decisions — from choosing a battery (eq. 11) for an agricultural UAV to sizing a LIDAR's range (eq. 8) for an autonomous mining truck.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Kinematic Model

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU 2024 high-frequency exam topic** (Module 1, Part B type questions).

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A two-wheeled differential-drive robot has:
- Wheel radius $r$ (in $\mathrm{m}$)
- Wheel separation (axle length) $b$ (in $\mathrm{m}$)
- Right and left wheel angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$ (in $\mathrm{rad/s}$)
- World-frame pose $q = (x, y, \theta)$

**Goal:** Derive the robot's instantaneous linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in terms of $r$, $b$, $\omega_R$, $\omega_L$.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the contact-point velocity of each wheel.**

When a wheel rolls without slipping, the linear speed at the contact point is the product of its radius and its angular velocity.

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R \qquad v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

**Step 2 — Define the linear velocity of the robot as the average of the two wheel speeds.**

This is the standard rigid-body assumption: the chassis is rigid, so its instantaneous center of rotation lies on the line connecting the two wheels. The forward velocity of the chassis midpoint equals the average of both wheel contact velocities.

$$
v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$
v = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega_R + r \cdot \omega_L}{2} = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(\omega_R + \omega_L\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the angular velocity from the differential wheel speeds.**

The two wheels trace circles of different radii. The difference $(v_R - v_L)$ over the separation $b$ gives the rate at which the heading rotates.

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 4.**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega_R - r \cdot \omega_L}{b} = \dfrac{r}{b}\left(\omega_R - \omega_L\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Combine into the standard kinematic state equation.**

Using the unicycle model convention:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Discretize for digital implementation (sampling time $\Delta t$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k)\,\Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k)\,\Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Given:** $r = 0.1\ \mathrm{m}$, $b = 0.5\ \mathrm{m}$, $\omega_R = 10\ \mathrm{rad/s}$, $\omega_L = 6\ \mathrm{rad/s}$.
> **Find:** $v$, $\omega$, and the pose after $\Delta t = 0.5\ \mathrm{s}$ starting from $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{0.1}{2}\,(10 + 6) = \dfrac{0.1}{2}\times 16 = 0.8\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{0.1}{0.5}\,(10 - 6) = 0.2 \times 4 = 0.8\ \mathrm{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Update pose with $\theta_0 = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.8 \times \cos(0) \times 0.5 = 0.4\ \mathrm{m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.8 \times \sin(0) \times 0.5 = 0\ \mathrm{m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.4\ \mathrm{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final pose:** $q_1 = (0.4\ \mathrm{m},\ 0\ \mathrm{m},\ 0.4\ \mathrm{rad})$. The robot moves $0.4\ \mathrm{m}$ along the x-axis while rotating $0.4\ \mathrm{rad}\ (\approx 22.9^\circ)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.** A car-like robot with wheelbase $L$ makes a turn of radius $R$ when the inner and outer front wheels turn at angles $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Geometric constraint (no tire slip).** All wheel axles must intersect at the instantaneous center of rotation (ICR).

$$
\tan(\delta_i) = \dfrac{L}{R - \dfrac{b_w}{2}} \qquad \tan(\delta_o) = \dfrac{L}{R + \dfrac{b_w}{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Ackermann approximation (bicycle model).** To simplify, treat the front axle as a single virtual wheel at the centerline:

$$
\tan(\delta) = \dfrac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for turning radius:**

$$
R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for required steering angle to achieve a target radius $R$:**

$$
\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Boundary condition.** As $\delta \to 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight motion). As $\delta \to \delta_{max}$, $R \to R_{min} = L / \tan(\delta_{max})$ — the tightest possible turn.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to compare robot platforms in exam answers.**

| Property | Wheeled | Tracked | Legged | Aerial (UAV) | Marine (AUV) |
|----------|---------|---------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Energy efficiency on flat ground** | High | Medium | Low | N/A | Medium |
| **Rough-terrain capability** | Low | Medium-High | Very High | High | N/A |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Payload capacity** | High | Very High | Low-Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Speed range** | 0–30 m/s | 0–5 m/s | 0–2 m/s | 0–30 m/s | 0–5 m/s |
| **KTU example platform** | Ackermann car | Mars Rover | BigDog / Spot | Quadcopter | REMUS AUV |
| **Localization dependency** | GNSS / IMU | Vision / LIDAR | Vision / Force | GNSS / Vision | Acoustic / DVL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Four-Pillar Architecture of a Field Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    env1["UNSTRUCTURED ENVIRONMENT"]:::env
    perc1["PERCEPTION LAYER"]:::perception
    cogn1["COGNITION LAYER"]:::cognition
    actn1["ACTION LAYER"]:::action
    comm1["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]:::comm
    hum1["HUMAN OPERATOR"]:::human
    task1["MISSION GOAL"]:::mission

    task1 --> cogn1
    env1 --> perc1
    perc1 -->|"Sensor data stream"| cogn1
    cogn1 -->|"Control commands"| actn1
    actn1 -->|"Physical effect"| env1
    comm1 -->|"Telemetry uplink"| hum1
    hum1 -->|"Teleop commands"| comm1
    comm1 --> cogn1

    classDef env fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef perception fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cognition fill:#D8B4FF,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#B4FFB4,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#FFD8B4,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef human fill:#FFB4D8,stroke:#800040,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mission fill:#FFFFB4,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** The mission goal flows *down* to the cognition layer, which fuses sensor data from the perception layer, issues commands to the action layer, and interacts bidirectionally with the human operator via the communication layer. The action layer's physical effect modifies the environment, completing the closed loop.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — The Sense-Plan-Act Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STEP 1: SENSE"]:::step
    s2["STEP 2: PERCEIVE"]:::step
    s3["STEP 3: LOCALIZE"]:::step
    s4["STEP 4: PLAN"]:::step
    s5["STEP 5: ACT"]:::step
    s6["STEP 6: FEEDBACK"]:::step

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s1

    classDef step fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Taxonomy — Locomotion Modes in Field Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    loco["LOMOTION MODES FOR FIELD ROBOTS"]:::root
    grnd["GROUND"]:::grp
    aer["AERIAL"]:::grp
    mar["MARINE"]:::grp
    sub["SUBTERRANEAN"]:::grp

    whl["WHEELED ACKERMANN"]:::leaf
    dif["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE"]:::leaf
    trk["TRACKED"]:::leaf
    leg["LEGGED"]:::leaf
    hyb["WHEEL LEG HYBRID"]:::leaf

    rot["ROTARY WING UAV"]:::leaf
    fxd["FIXED WING UAV"]:::leaf

    auv["AUV"]:::leaf
    usv["USV"]:::leaf

    scr["SCREW TYPE"]:::leaf
    snk["SNAKE TYPE"]:::leaf

    loco --> grnd
    loco --> aer
    loco --> mar
    loco --> sub

    grnd --> whl
    grnd --> dif
    grnd --> trk
    grnd --> leg
    grnd --> hyb

    aer --> rot
    aer --> fxd

    mar --> auv
    mar --> usv

    sub --> scr
    sub --> snk

    classDef root fill:#FFFFB4,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef grp fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Multi-Robot Field Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gcs["GROUND CONTROL STATION"]:::gcs
    uav1["UAV SCOUT"]:::uav
    ugv1["UGV CARGO"]:::ugv
    asv1["ASV RESCUE"]:::asv
    cloud["CLOUD MISSION PLANNER"]:::cloud

    gcs -->|"Tasking and telemetry"| uav1
    gcs -->|"Tasking and telemetry"| ugv1
    gcs -->|"Tasking and telemetry"| asv1
    gcs <-->|"Bidirectional data sync"| cloud
    uav1 <-->|"Aerial relay mesh"| ugv1
    ugv1 <-->|"Surface acoustic link"| asv1

    classDef gcs fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#800000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef uav fill:#B4FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ugv fill:#B4FFB4,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef asv fill:#B4B4FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style, Module 1]**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Field Robotics. List any four major application domains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Field Robotics is the branch of robotics that deals with autonomous or semi-autonomous robots operating in unstructured, outdoor, and dynamic environments outside factory settings.
**Four application domains (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Agriculture** — autonomous tractors, drones for crop monitoring, precision spraying.
2. **Search and Rescue** — rubble-penetrating robots, snake robots for collapsed buildings.
3. **Underwater Exploration** — AUVs for coral mapping, pipeline inspection.
4. **Planetary Exploration** — Mars rovers (e.g., NASA's Perseverance, Curiosity).
*(Acceptable alternates: mining, military, forestry, surveillance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between Industrial Robotics and Field Robotics in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per aspect):**

| Aspect | Industrial Robotics | Field Robotics |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Environment** | Highly structured, caged, predictable. | Unstructured, dynamic, unpredictable. |
| **Task Type** | Repetitive, pre-programmed (e.g., welding). | Mission-oriented, adaptive (e.g., exploration). |
| **Sensing** | Minimal — often position encoders only. | Rich sensor suite — LIDAR, vision, IMU, GNSS. |
| **Mobility** | Usually fixed-base manipulator. | Mobile platform (wheeled/tracked/legged/aerial/marine). |
| **Safety standards** | ISO 10218, caged. | Human–robot collaboration, failsafe remote kill. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]*

**Q.A.** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four functional subsystems of a field robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematics equations of a differential-drive mobile robot. A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.08\ \mathrm{m}$ and wheelbase $b = 0.4\ \mathrm{m}$ has wheel angular velocities $\omega_R = 12\ \mathrm{rad/s}$ and $\omega_L = 8\ \mathrm{rad/s}$. Compute its linear and angular velocities and update its pose after $0.5\ \mathrm{s}$ starting from origin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four subsystems (7 marks — 1.5 each + 1 for diagram):**
1. **Perception Layer:** LIDAR, cameras, IMU, GNSS, ultrasonic — converts physical signals to digital data.
2. **Cognition Layer:** Onboard computer runs SLAM, sensor fusion (EKF), path planning (A*, RRT).
3. **Action Layer:** Motors, wheels/tracks/legs, manipulators — executes the commands.
4. **Communication Layer:** Wireless links (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, RF, acoustic modems) for teleoperation and data relay.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the neat block diagram.)*

**(b) Derivation + numerical (7 marks — 4 derivation + 3 numerical):**

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$
v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \qquad \omega = \dfrac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
v = \dfrac{0.08}{2}(12 + 8) = 0.04 \times 20 = 0.8\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{0.08}{0.4}(12 - 8) = 0.2 \times 4 = 0.8\ \mathrm{rad/s}
$$

**Pose update** (from origin $(0, 0, 0)$ with $\Delta t = 0.5\ \mathrm{s}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0.5) &= 0 + 0.8 \cos(0) \times 0.5 = 0.4\ \mathrm{m} \\
y(0.5) &= 0 + 0.8 \sin(0) \times 0.5 = 0\ \mathrm{m} \\
\theta(0.5) &= 0 + 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.4\ \mathrm{rad}\ (\approx 22.92^\circ)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Linear velocity $v = 0.8\ \mathrm{m/s}$, angular velocity $\omega = 0.8\ \mathrm{rad/s}$, updated pose $(0.4\ \mathrm{m},\ 0\ \mathrm{m},\ 0.4\ \mathrm{rad})$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]*

**Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the major challenges of field robotics using the **V-DIM-V principle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car-like robot has wheelbase $L = 2.5\ \mathrm{m}$. It must navigate a curved path with a target turning radius $R = 10\ \mathrm{m}$. Compute the required steering angle $\delta$ using the Ackermann model. What is the minimum turning radius if the maximum steering angle is $35^\circ$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) V-DIM-V challenges (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**
1. **Variability** — Lighting, weather, terrain texture change unpredictably.
2. **Dynamics** — Moving obstacles (people, animals, vehicles) require real-time re-planning.
3. **Incompleteness** — Sensors have finite range, noise, and occlusion; partial observability is the rule.
4. **Maplessness** — Often no prior map of the environment exists; SLAM is required.
5. **Vulnerability** — A single sensor or actuator failure can compromise the mission; fault tolerance is critical.

**(b) Ackermann calculation (7 marks — 3 for first part, 4 for second part):**

**Step 1 — Required steering angle for $R = 10\ \mathrm{m}$:**

$$
\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2.5}{10}\right) = \arctan(0.25)
$$

$$
\delta \approx 14.04^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum turning radius for $\delta_{max} = 35^\circ$:**

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta_{max})} = \dfrac{2.5}{\tan(35^\circ)} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.7002}
$$

$$
R_{min} \approx 3.57\ \mathrm{m}
$$

**Results:** Required $\delta \approx 14.04^\circ$; minimum turning radius $R_{min} \approx 3.57\ \mathrm{m}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write $\mathrm{m/s}$, $\mathrm{rad/s}$, $\mathrm{rad}$, $\mathrm{m}$. Losing 0.5–1 mark per omission is common.
> 2. **Mixing $^\circ$ and rad** in trigonometric functions. $\sin(0.4\ \mathrm{rad}) \neq \sin(0.4^\circ)$. Most calculators default to *degree* mode — check it.
> 3. **Skipping the intermediate steps** in derivations. KTU awards *step marks* — a 7-mark derivation broken into 4 well-labeled steps scores higher than a single-line answer.
> 4. **Confusing the differential-drive formula signs** — $\omega_R > \omega_L$ means the robot turns *left* (counter-clockwise); students often write the formula with the wrong sign convention.
> 5. **Ignoring boundary conditions** in formulas like Ackermann — for $\delta = 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight motion), and division-by-zero must be flagged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Carry to Exam Hall)**

- **Definition:** Field robotics = mobile robots in **unstructured, real-world** environments.
- **Four pillars:** Perception, Cognition, Action, Communication — must appear in any 7-mark architecture question.
- **V-DIM-V** — Variability, Dynamics, Incompleteness, Maplessness, Vulnerability.
- **Locomotion taxonomy:** Wheels / Tracks / Legs / Hybrid / Aerial / Marine / Subterranean.
- **Differential-drive kinematics:**
  - $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
  - $\omega = \dfrac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- **Ackermann:** $\tan(\delta) = L / R$, valid for $\delta \neq 0$.
- **Degrees of Freedom** of a free rigid body in 3D = **6**.
- **Autonomy ladder:** 0 (teleoperated) → 5 (fully autonomous).
- **Core sensors:** LIDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS, ultrasonic, force/torque.
- **Core algorithms:** EKF, SLAM, A*, RRT, occupancy grid, particle filter.
- **Power budget:** $P_{total} = P_{motion} + P_{compute} + P_{sensors} + P_{comm}$.
- **Endurance:** $T = E_{batt} / P_{total}$.
- **Conversion:** $1\ \mathrm{Wh} = 3600\ \mathrm{J}$ ; $1\ \mathrm{rad} = 180/\pi\ \mathrm{deg}$.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — sensor update rate must be at least twice the fastest dynamic in the scene.
- **Domain examples:** Agriculture (precision farming), mining (autonomous haul trucks), search and rescue (rubble robots), marine (AUVs), space (Mars rovers), military (UGVs/UAVs).
- **KTU weightage tip:** Module 1 questions often anchor around the *block diagram* (7 marks) and *differential-drive derivation* (7 marks) — practice both thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Issues for Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Key Issues for Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Field Robotics**, **locomotion** refers to the mechanism by which a mobile robotic system generates motion to traverse and operate within unstructured, real-world environments (terrestrial, aquatic, or aerial). The **Key Issues for Locomotion** constitute a foundational taxonomy of design constraints, engineering trade-offs, and mechanical principles that govern the selection, configuration, and control of locomotion systems in field robots. These issues address the intersection of **mechanical engineering**, **terrain mechanics**, **energy systems**, and **control theory**.

The principal key issues include:
- **Locomotion mechanism selection** (wheeled, tracked, legged, aerial, aquatic, hybrid)
- **Stability** (static and dynamic, in 2D and 3D)
- **Contact mechanics and traction**
- **Number and configuration of actuators (Degrees of Freedom – DOF)**
- **Energy efficiency and power-to-weight ratio**
- **Terrain adaptability and obstacle negotiation**
- **Speed, maneuverability, and control complexity**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The KTU board examiner expects a clear distinction between *locomotion* (the act of moving) and *mobility* (the capacity to move). Locomotion is the *mechanism*; mobility is the *capability* that mechanism enables.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a **hiker traversing a mountain trail**. The human body solves locomotion challenges in a remarkable way:
- The **feet** are the "wheel/leg" choice (selected for grip on uneven surfaces, not for raw speed).
- The **spine and inner ear (vestibular system)** continuously maintain **balance** — a stability problem.
- The **legs** provide **compliance** — they absorb shock and adapt to terrain.
- The **energy budget** is finite (calories), so efficiency is paramount.

A **wheeled field robot** is like a car on a paved road — fast and efficient but unable to handle the mountain trail. A **legged field robot** is like the hiker — slower but adaptive. A **tracked robot** is like a tank — a compromise between speed and adaptability. These trade-offs define the **key issues** of locomotion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** When answering KTU questions, always tie the locomotion choice to the *operational environment*. There is no universally "best" locomotion type — only *contextually optimal* ones.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Priority Terms)

The following bolded metrics appear routinely in KTU examination questions:
- **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** — measured in **meters (m)**
- **Center of Gravity / Center of Mass (CoG / CoM)**
- **Support Polygon** — geometric region defined by ground contact points
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — dimensionless count
- **Specific Resistance (SR)** — dimensionless coefficient of locomotion efficiency
- **Froude Number (Fr)** — dimensionless number comparing inertial to gravitational forces
- **Gravity acceleration constant: g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Issue 1 — Selection of Locomotion Mechanism

The first key issue is the *categorical choice* of locomotion. The KTU syllabus expects classification along three axes:

**Axis 1: Physical Contact Type**
- **Static Contact** (e.g., legs touching ground) — discrete contact points
- **Rolling Contact** (e.g., wheels) — continuous contact patches
- **Sliding Contact** (e.g., tracks, skids) — extended contact regions

**Axis 2: Number of Contact Points**
- 1-point: Biped (single-leg phases)
- 2-point: Biped, bicycle
- 3-point: Tripod, tricycle
- 4-point: Quadruped, 4-wheeled vehicle
- 6-point: Hexapod, 6-wheeled (Rocker-Bogie)

**Axis 3: Environment**
- **Terrestrial** → wheels, tracks, legs
- **Aerial** → rotors, fixed-wing, flapping wings
- **Aquatic** → thrusters, undulating fins
- **Hybrid** → amphibious, all-terrain

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A higher number of contact points generally yields *higher static stability* but *lower maneuverability* and *higher control complexity*. KTU exam answers should explicitly state this trade-off.

### 2.2 Issue 2 — Stability

**Stability** is the second key issue. Two regimes exist:

**(a) Static Stability:**
A robot is *statically stable* if it can remain upright with **all actuators frozen**. The condition is:

$$C \in \mathcal{S}$$

where $C$ is the vertical projection of the **Center of Mass (CoM)** onto the ground plane, and $\mathcal{S}$ is the **support polygon** (the convex hull of all ground contact points).

**(b) Dynamic Stability:**
A robot is *dynamically stable* if it does not tip over during motion, even if it would fall when stationary. Walking and running are *inherently dynamically stable* systems — they constantly "fall forward" and catch themselves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Callout:** The **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** is defined as the *shortest distance* from the CoM projection to any edge of the support polygon. A larger SSM means a more stable robot.

### 2.3 Issue 3 — Contact Mechanics and Traction

Traction determines whether a robot can move *forward* or simply *spin its wheels in place*. The maximum tractive force is governed by the **Coulomb friction model**:

$$F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot N$$

where:
- $F_{traction,max}$ = maximum tractive (propulsive) force, in **Newtons (N)**
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless), dependent on terrain material pair
- $N$ = normal force (weight on contact patch), in **Newtons (N)**

For a robot of mass $m$ on flat ground: $N = mg$, hence:

$$F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

### 2.4 Issue 4 — Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and Configuration

DOF defines *how many independent actuators* the robot uses. More DOF → more adaptability, but more complex control. KTU problems often involve counting DOF for a given leg/wheel configuration.

**Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion (Planar Mechanism):**

$$\text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

For spatial mechanisms:

$$\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

where $n$ = number of links, $j_i$ = number of joints with $i$ DOF.

### 2.5 Issue 5 — Energy Efficiency

Field robots operate with finite onboard power. The standard efficiency metric is the **Specific Resistance (SR)** by Gabrielli & von Krenner (1950):

$$SR = \frac{P}{m g v}$$

where:
- $P$ = power consumed (Watts, W)
- $m$ = mass (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $v$ = forward velocity (m/s)

A *lower* SR indicates a more efficient locomotion system. Biological systems like human walking achieve $SR \approx 0.2$.

### 2.6 Issue 6 — Froude Number and Dynamic Similarity

For legged locomotion, the **Froude Number** is a dimensionless gait-classification parameter:

$$Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot L}$$

where $v$ = forward speed, $g$ = gravity, $L$ = leg length. Different gaits (walk, trot, gallop) naturally transition at characteristic Froude numbers.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Tractive Force | $F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | $\mu$: friction coeff.; $m$: mass; $g$: 9.81 | N | 0 to $\mu m g$ |
| Static Stability Condition | $C \in \mathcal{S}$ | $C$: CoM projection; $\mathcal{S}$: support polygon | m (geometry) | Boolean |
| Static Stability Margin | $SSM = \min_i d(C, e_i)$ | $d$: perpendicular distance to edge $e_i$ | m | 0 to $\infty$ |
| Specific Resistance | $SR = \frac{P}{m g v}$ | $P$: power; $v$: velocity | dimensionless | 0.1 to 10 |
| Froude Number | $Fr = \frac{v^2}{g L}$ | $L$: leg length | dimensionless | 0.01 to 5 |
| Coulomb Friction | $F \leq \mu N$ | Incipient slip condition | N | Scalar |
| Grübler DOF (planar) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$: links; $j_i$: $i$-DOF joints | count | Integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU LaTeX Rule:** When writing absolute value (e.g., $SSM = \min \vert d \vert$) in inline math, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the vertical pipe `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Static Stability Margin for a Three-Legged Robot

**Problem Setup:** Consider a field robot standing on three legs. The feet are located at the vertices of a triangle on the ground plane. We wish to derive the static stability margin when the robot is in this configuration.

Let the three ground contact points be:
- $P_1 = (0, 0)$
- $P_2 = (L, 0)$
- $P_3 = (L/2, h)$

where $L$ is the base width and $h$ is the height of the apex.

**Step 1: Define the Support Polygon**
The support polygon $\mathcal{S}$ is the triangle with vertices $P_1, P_2, P_3$.

**Step 2: Locate the Center of Mass Projection**
Let the CoM project to point $C = (C_x, C_y)$ in the ground plane. The static stability condition requires $C \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Step 3: Compute Distances to Each Edge**
The three edges of the support triangle are:
- Edge $e_{12}$: from $P_1$ to $P_2$ — the line $y = 0$
- Edge $e_{23}$: from $P_2$ to $P_3$
- Edge $e_{31}$: from $P_3$ to $P_1$

Distance from $C$ to edge $e_{12}$ (the line $y=0$):

$$d_{12} = \frac{\vert 0 \cdot C_x + 1 \cdot C_y - 0 \vert}{\sqrt{0^2 + 1^2}} = \vert C_y \vert$$

Distance from $C$ to edge $e_{23}$: The line through $P_2 = (L, 0)$ and $P_3 = (L/2, h)$ has the equation:

$$h(x - L) - (-L/2)(y - 0) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow hx + \frac{L}{2} y - hL = 0$$

The perpendicular distance is:

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert h \cdot C_x + (L/2) C_y - hL \vert}{\sqrt{h^2 + (L/2)^2}}$$

Distance from $C$ to edge $e_{31}$: The line through $P_3 = (L/2, h)$ and $P_1 = (0, 0)$ has the equation:

$$h \cdot x - (L/2) \cdot y = 0$$

The perpendicular distance is:

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert h \cdot C_x - (L/2) C_y \vert}{\sqrt{h^2 + (L/2)^2}}$$

**Step 4: Static Stability Margin**

$$\text{SSM} = \min(d_{12}, d_{23}, d_{31})$$

The robot is statically stable **if and only if** SSM > 0, meaning $C$ lies strictly *inside* the triangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** SSM = 0 means the CoM projection is on the support polygon edge — the robot is on the verge of tipping. SSM < 0 means it has already tipped.

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Efficiency Comparison for Wheeled vs. Legged Locomotion

**Problem Setup:** Compare the specific resistance of a 4-wheeled robot of mass $m = 50$ kg moving at $v = 1$ m/s, versus a quadruped of the same mass and speed.

**Step 1: Define Power for Wheeled Locomotion**
For a wheeled robot on hard ground, the dominant power loss is rolling resistance. The power required is:

$$P_{wheel} = F_{roll} \cdot v = \mu_r \cdot m g \cdot v$$

where $\mu_r$ is the rolling friction coefficient (typically 0.01 to 0.05 on hard ground).

**Step 2: Compute Specific Resistance for Wheeled**

$$SR_{wheel} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{m g v} = \frac{\mu_r \cdot m g v}{m g v} = \mu_r$$

For $\mu_r = 0.02$: $SR_{wheel} = 0.02$.

**Step 3: Define Power for Legged Locomotion**
For a legged robot, energy is expended to lift and accelerate the CoM during each step. Per step, work done against gravity:

$$W_{step} = m g \cdot \Delta h$$

where $\Delta h$ is the vertical CoM excursion. With step length $\lambda$ and leg cycle frequency $f = v / \lambda$:

$$P_{leg} = W_{step} \cdot f = m g \Delta h \cdot \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4: Compute Specific Resistance for Legged**

$$SR_{leg} = \frac{P_{leg}}{m g v} = \frac{m g \Delta h \cdot v / \lambda}{m g v} = \frac{\Delta h}{\lambda}$$

For typical walking: $\Delta h / \lambda \approx 0.05$, so $SR_{leg} \approx 0.05$.

**Step 5: Comparison and Insight**

$$SR_{wheel} = 0.02 < SR_{leg} = 0.05$$

This quantitatively confirms that **wheeled robots are ~2.5× more energy-efficient** than legged robots on hard, flat ground — which is why Mars rovers (e.g., NASA's Curiosity) use wheels, not legs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The trade-off reverses on *rough terrain*. On broken ground, legged systems maintain $SR \approx 0.2$ because each footfall is selected, while wheeled systems suffer increased rolling resistance (often $\mu_r$ doubles or triples), pushing $SR$ to 0.04–0.06 or more. This is why search-and-rescue robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics' Spot) use legs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Locomotion Mechanism Trade-offs

The KTU examiner often asks for a comparison table. Below is a complete analytical matrix:

| Locomotion Type | Static Stability | Speed | Energy Efficiency (SR) | Terrain Adaptability | Mechanical Complexity | Control Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheeled (4-wheel) | High (polygon) | High | Low ($\approx$ 0.02) | Low | Low | Low |
| Tracked | High (long polygon) | Medium | Medium ($\approx$ 0.1) | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Biped (2-leg) | Low (point) | Medium | Medium-High | Very High | Very High | Very High |
| Quadruped (4-leg) | Medium (multi-polygon) | Medium | Medium | High | High | High |
| Hexapod (6-leg) | Very High | Low | High | Very High | High | Medium |
| Aerial (Quadrotor) | N/A (3D stabilized) | High | High ($\approx$ 0.5) | N/A (flies) | Medium | High |
| Aquatic (Thruster) | N/A (buoyancy) | Medium | Low-Medium | N/A (water) | Low | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The *hexapod* is a sweet spot — 6 legs allow a stable *tripod gait* (always 3 feet on the ground), which is statically stable, with control complexity manageable via simple alternating patterns. This is why insects and many field robots use 6 legs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Locomotion Decision Architecture

The following diagram maps the **decision flow** a field robotics engineer follows when selecting a locomotion system. It captures all the key issues discussed above as a single, navigable architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Field Mission Requirement] --> B{Operating Environment?}
    B -- Terrestrial Structured --> C[Wheeled Robot]
    B -- Terrestrial Semi-Structured --> D[Tracked Robot]
    B -- Terrestrial Unstructured --> E{Leg Count?}
    B -- Aerial --> F[Aerial Robot Multirotor]
    B -- Aquatic --> G[Aquatic Thruster]
    E -- 2 Legs --> H[Biped Dynamic Stability]
    E -- 4 Legs --> I[Quadruped Crawl Trot Bound]
    E -- 6 Legs --> J[Hexapod Tripod Gait Static]
    C --> K[Stability Check: SSM over Polygon]
    D --> K
    H --> L[Stability Check: ZMP Zero Moment Point]
    I --> L
    J --> K
    K --> M{SSM greater than zero?}
    L --> N{ZMP inside Polygon?}
    M -- Yes --> O[Statically Stable Deploy]
    M -- No --> P[Reconfigure Reduce CoG Height]
    N -- Yes --> O
    N -- No --> P
    O --> Q[Energy Audit Specific Resistance SR]
    P --> K
    Q --> R{SR below Threshold?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Field Deployment]
    R -- No --> T[Redesign Geometry or Actuator]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Locomotion System

This block diagram shows the **functional subsystems** that must integrate to achieve effective locomotion, irrespective of mechanism choice.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mission Planner] --> B[Locomotion Controller]
    B --> C[Actuator Drive Module]
    C --> D[Mechanical Locomotion Element]
    D -- Force Velocity Feedback --> E[Sensor Suite IMU Encoders Force]
    E --> B
    B -- Stability Estimator --> F[Stability Module CoG Polygon SSM]
    F -- Stability State --> B
    D -- Ground Contact --> G[Terrain Interaction Friction Compliance]
    G -- Traction Model --> B
    B --> H[Energy Management Power Budget]
    H -- SR Metric --> B
    B --> I[Actuator Commands]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Support Polygon Visualisation (Triangular Tripod)

For the three-legged static stability case derived in Section 3, the following describes the geometry (rendered as a Mermaid graph for clarity):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[P1 0 comma 0] --> V2[P2 L comma 0]
    V2 --> V3[P3 L over 2 comma h]
    V3 --> V1
    V4[CoM Projection C] -.->|SSM shortest distance| V1
    V4 -.->|perpendicular to edge| V2
    V4 -.->|perpendicular to edge| V3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The triangle $P_1 P_2 P_3$ is the *support polygon*. The point $C$ inside is the *CoM projection*. The dashed lines represent the three perpendicular distances whose minimum is the **Static Stability Margin (SSM)**. If $C$ exits the triangle, the robot tips over.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "locomotion" in the context of field robotics. List any three key issues that must be addressed when designing a locomotion system for a field robot.*

**Model Answer:**

**Locomotion** in field robotics refers to the mechanism by which a mobile robot generates physical motion to traverse and operate within unstructured real-world environments.

The three key issues in designing a locomotion system are:
1. **Selection of locomotion mechanism** (wheeled, tracked, legged, aerial, aquatic) — dictated by terrain and mission profile.
2. **Stability** — ensuring the robot does not tip over (static via CoM within support polygon, or dynamic via ZMP / gait control).
3. **Energy efficiency** — quantified by the Specific Resistance (SR), since field robots have limited onboard power.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three issues.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "support polygon" of a mobile robot? How is it used to determine static stability?*

**Model Answer:**

The **support polygon** $\mathcal{S}$ of a mobile robot is the convex hull of all points where the robot is in contact with the ground, projected onto the horizontal plane. For example, a 4-wheeled vehicle has a quadrilateral support polygon formed by the four wheel contact points.

It is used to determine static stability by checking whether the **vertical projection of the Center of Mass (CoM)**, denoted $C$, lies *inside* the support polygon. The condition is:

$$C \in \mathcal{S}$$

If this holds, the robot is *statically stable* — it will not tip over even with all actuators frozen. The **Static Stability Margin (SSM)**, defined as the minimum perpendicular distance from $C$ to any edge of $\mathcal{S}$, quantifies *how far* the robot is from tipping.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the condition, 1 for SSM.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** *Explain the various locomotion mechanisms used in field robotics. Discuss the advantages and limitations of wheeled, tracked, and legged systems with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the static stability condition for a four-wheeled mobile robot. Compute the Static Stability Margin (SSM) for a square-wheeled robot of side $L = 0.8$ m with its CoM projection at the geometric center, when the robot is on a 20° transverse slope.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Locomotion Mechanisms in Field Robotics:**

1. **Wheeled Locomotion:**
   - **Principle:** Rolling contact between wheel and ground. Wheels rotate about a fixed axis to produce forward motion.
   - **Types:** Standard wheels, castor wheels, Swedish wheels (omnidirectional), spherical wheels.
   - **Advantages:** High energy efficiency (low SR), simple control, high speed on smooth terrain, mature technology.
   - **Limitations:** Poor performance on rough/soft terrain; small contact patch limits traction on deformable surfaces.
   - **Examples:** NASA's Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance), autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs), Roomba vacuum cleaners.

2. **Tracked Locomotion:**
   - **Principle:** Continuous track (like a tank tread) provides extended ground contact area, distributing weight over a large surface.
   - **Advantages:** Excellent traction on soft/loose terrain (sand, mud, snow), high obstacle-climbing ability, good stability due to elongated support polygon.
   - **Limitations:** High energy loss due to internal friction of track links, slow speed, track wear and tear.
   - **Examples:** Demining robots, forestry/logging machines, military unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs), rescue robots.

3. **Legged Locomotion:**
   - **Principle:** Discrete foot contacts; the robot lifts and places each leg to generate motion. May use static gaits (always stable) or dynamic gaits (running, jumping).
   - **Advantages:** Superior terrain adaptability — can step over obstacles, climb stairs, traverse gaps; works on broken ground.
   - **Limitations:** High mechanical complexity, high energy cost, sophisticated control required.
   - **Examples:** Boston Dynamics' Spot (quadruped), Atlas (biped), ASIMO (Honda, biped), RHex (hexapod).

**Comparison Summary:**

| Feature | Wheeled | Tracked | Legged |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy efficiency | High | Low | Medium |
| Terrain adaptability | Low | Medium | High |
| Speed | High | Low | Medium |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | Medium | High |

*(7 marks: 2 for wheeled, 2 for tracked, 2 for legged, 1 for comparison table.)*

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation of Static Stability for a 4-Wheeled Robot:**

**Step 1: Define Geometry on Flat Ground**
Let the four wheel contact points on flat ground be:
- Front-left: $A = (0, 0)$
- Front-right: $B = (L, 0)$
- Rear-right: $C = (L, W)$
- Rear-left: $D = (0, W)$

where $L$ = wheelbase (front-to-rear) and $W$ = track width (left-to-right).

**Step 2: Support Polygon**
$\mathcal{S} = \text{ConvexHull}(A, B, C, D)$ — a rectangle.

**Step 3: Static Stability Condition on Flat Ground**
The CoM projection $C_0 = (L/2, W/2)$ lies at the center of the rectangle, so:

$$C_0 \in \mathcal{S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Statically stable.}$$

**Step 4: Tipping Analysis on a 20° Transverse Slope**
A *transverse* slope tilts the robot about its longitudinal axis. The CoM, originally at height $h$ above ground and laterally at $W/2$ from the centerline, shifts laterally when the robot sits on a slope of angle $\theta$.

When the robot is on a slope, the **effective gravity** (in the robot's frame) acts at angle $\theta$ to the vertical. The CoM projection $C'$ in the ground frame is offset from the geometric center by:

$$\Delta x = h \sin\theta$$

**Step 5: Compute SSM on 20° Slope**
For a square robot ($L = W = 0.8$ m), with CoM height $h$ (assume $h = 0.4$ m as a typical mid-height value for a small field robot), the lateral offset of the CoM projection from the robot's centerline is:

$$\Delta x = 0.4 \times \sin(20°) = 0.4 \times 0.342 = 0.1368 \text{ m}$$

The CoM projection is now at $(L/2, W/2 - \Delta x) = (0.4, 0.4 - 0.1368) = (0.4, 0.2632)$ m.

**Step 6: Distance to Nearest Edge (the lower edge)**
The lower edge of the support polygon (the downhill side) is at $y = 0$. The distance is:

$$d = 0.2632 \text{ m}$$

This is the **SSM** on the 20° transverse slope.

**Step 7: Critical Slope for Tipping**
The robot tips when $\Delta x = W/2$, i.e., when $h \sin\theta_{crit} = W/2$:

$$\sin\theta_{crit} = \frac{W}{2h} = \frac{0.8}{2 \times 0.4} = 1.0$$

This gives $\theta_{crit} = 90°$, which is physically impossible — the robot **cannot tip** on a transverse slope with these proportions. The lower CoG height makes it highly stable laterally.

**Step 8: Conclusion**
The robot has $SSM = 0.2632$ m on a 20° transverse slope, and is essentially immune to transverse tipping for any realistic slope. However, the *longitudinal* slope tipping (about the lateral axis) would use a similar analysis with $L$ and the longitudinal offset.

*(7 marks: 2 for derivation on flat ground, 2 for slope geometry, 2 for numerical computation, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of Specific Resistance (SR) as a metric for locomotion energy efficiency. Why is this metric important for field robots specifically? Compare SR for typical wheeled and legged systems.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A quadruped robot has a mass of 30 kg and moves with a forward speed of 0.5 m/s. Each leg lifts the CoM by 0.03 m during a step, with a step length of 0.2 m. Calculate the Specific Resistance (SR) for this robot and comment on its energy efficiency compared to a wheeled robot on hard ground ($\mu_r = 0.02$).* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Specific Resistance — Conceptual Explanation:**

**Specific Resistance (SR)** is a *dimensionless* metric that measures the energy cost of locomotion, originally introduced by **Gabrielli and von Krenner** for biological locomotion studies and adopted for robotic systems. It is defined as:

$$SR = \frac{P}{m g v}$$

where:
- $P$ = power consumed for locomotion (W)
- $m$ = mass of the robot (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $v$ = forward velocity (m/s)

**Physical Meaning:** SR represents the *fraction of gravitational power* ($mgv$, the rate at which the robot "gains potential" by moving forward at speed $v$) that must be supplied as input power. A lower SR means a more efficient locomotion system.

**Importance for Field Robots:**
- Field robots operate with **limited onboard power** (batteries, fuel cells, solar) — energy efficiency directly determines mission duration.
- Refueling or recharging in remote field environments is **difficult or impossible**.
- SR allows a *fair comparison* between different locomotion types (wheels, legs, tracks) and different robot sizes, since it normalizes out mass and speed.

**Comparison — Wheeled vs. Legged SR:**

| System | Typical SR | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Wheeled on hard ground | 0.01 – 0.05 | Very efficient, dominant loss is rolling friction |
| Tracked on hard ground | 0.05 – 0.15 | Track internal friction increases loss |
| Legged walking (4 or 6 legs) | 0.05 – 0.2 | Energy spent lifting/accelerating CoM |
| Legged running (dynamic) | 0.2 – 0.5 | High inertial losses |
| Human walking (biological) | ~0.2 | Evolutionary benchmark |

**Key Insight:** On *smooth, hard* ground, wheeled systems are typically 2–5× more energy-efficient than legged systems. On *rough* ground, the difference narrows or reverses because wheeled systems suffer increased rolling resistance, while legged systems can select individual footfalls.

*(7 marks: 2 for SR definition and meaning, 2 for field-robot importance, 3 for comparison and insight.)*

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 30$ kg
- $v = 0.5$ m/s
- $\Delta h = 0.03$ m (CoM vertical excursion per step)
- $\lambda = 0.2$ m (step length)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Compute Step Frequency**
The leg cycle frequency is:

$$f = \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Compute Work per Step**
The work done per step against gravity is:

$$W_{step} = m g \Delta h = 30 \times 9.81 \times 0.03$$

$$W_{step} = 8.829 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Compute Power Consumed**
Power is work times frequency:

$$P_{leg} = W_{step} \times f = 8.829 \times 2.5$$

$$P_{leg} = 22.07 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Compute Specific Resistance**

$$SR_{leg} = \frac{P_{leg}}{m g v} = \frac{22.07}{30 \times 9.81 \times 0.5}$$

$$SR_{leg} = \frac{22.07}{147.15} = 0.150$$

**Step 5: Compare to Wheeled Robot**
For the wheeled robot on hard ground:

$$SR_{wheel} = \mu_r = 0.02$$

**Step 6: Comparison**

$$\frac{SR_{leg}}{SR_{wheel}} = \frac{0.150}{0.02} = 7.5$$

**Step 7: Interpretation and Comments**
The quadruped is **7.5 times less energy-efficient** than a wheeled robot on hard ground under the given conditions. This large ratio is driven by the relatively high CoM excursion ($\Delta h = 0.03$ m) compared to the step length ($\lambda = 0.2$ m), giving a ratio $\Delta h / \lambda = 0.15$ — a moderately aggressive gait. The robot would benefit from:
- A flatter walking gait (reducing $\Delta h$),
- Smoother CoM trajectories (e.g., using compliant leg designs),
- A longer step length or higher speed to amortize the cost per step over more distance.

Despite the higher SR, the legged robot is *justified* if the mission requires traversing stairs, rubble, or unstructured terrain where a wheeled robot would fail.

*(7 marks: 1 for step frequency, 1 for work per step, 1 for power, 2 for SR calculation, 2 for comparison and interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "locomotion" with "mobility"** — Locomotion is the *act*; mobility is the *capability*. Marks deducted if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always state units (N, m, J, W, dimensionless) for quantities like $F_{traction}$, $SSM$, $P$, and $SR$. Marks deducted for unitless final answers.
> 3. **Static vs. Dynamic stability confusion** — A running biped is *dynamically* stable, not statically unstable. Do not write "running robots are unstable" — write "they are statically unstable but dynamically stable via ZMP/flight-phase control."
> 4. **Confusing CoM with CoG** — In a uniform gravitational field, $CoM \equiv CoG$. In non-uniform fields, they differ. KTU expects the simpler CoG terminology.
> 5. **Forgetting $g$ in SR calculation** — The denominator of SR is $mgv$, not $mv$. This is a frequent error worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Using the wrong Grübler formula** — Planar ($3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$) and spatial ($6(n-1) - \ldots$) formulas are different. Pick the right one based on whether the mechanism is 2D or 3D.
> 7. **Writing "SSM = distance"** — Always specify *shortest* distance to *an edge of the support polygon*. Generic "distance" is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Locomotion** = the mechanism of motion; **Mobility** = the capability to move in a given environment.
- **Key issues** span mechanism choice, stability, contact mechanics, DOF, energy efficiency, and terrain adaptability.
- **Support polygon** $\mathcal{S}$ = convex hull of ground contact points. **Static stability** requires the CoM projection $C$ to lie inside $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** = minimum perpendicular distance from $C$ to any edge of $\mathcal{S}$. SSM = 0 means on the verge of tipping; SSM < 0 means already tipped.
- **Coulomb friction** governs traction: $F_{traction,max} = \mu m g$ on flat ground. Without sufficient friction, wheels spin in place.
- **Grübler's formula** counts DOF in mechanisms: planar $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$; spatial $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - \ldots$
- **Specific Resistance (SR) = P / (mgv)** is the *dimensionless* energy efficiency metric. Lower is better. Wheeled systems typically have $SR \approx 0.02$; legged systems $SR \approx 0.1 - 0.2$.
- **Froude Number $Fr = v^2 / (gL)$** classifies legged gaits: walk (Fr < 0.5), trot (0.5 < Fr < 2), gallop (Fr > 2). Dimensional analysis tool.
- **Trade-off axis:** More contact points → higher stability, lower speed, higher complexity. The **hexapod tripod gait** is a popular sweet spot.
- **Mission-driven design:** There is no "best" locomotion — only *contextually optimal* for the terrain, mission, and power budget.
- **Aerial and aquatic** systems bypass ground stability entirely (relying on thrust/buoyancy) and are governed by 3D dynamic stability criteria (e.g., attitude control for quadrotors).
- **For 14-mark questions**, always include: (i) definition, (ii) derivation/formula, (iii) numerical computation if data is given, (iv) physical interpretation, and (v) at least one comparison or trade-off comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legged Mobile Robots (LMRs)** are a class of field robotic platforms that use a discrete number of articulated limbs (legs) for locomotion over unstructured, uneven, and discontinuous terrains. Unlike wheeled or tracked robots, legged systems achieve mobility by applying a sequence of intermittent ground contact forces through coordinated leg actuation, allowing them to traverse obstacles comparable to or larger than their leg length.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the analysis of LMRs requires understanding three foundational pillars:
1. **Morphological Configuration** (kinematic arrangement of legs)
2. **Locomotion Stability** (the ability to maintain equilibrium during motion)
3. **Gait Generation** (the temporal-spatial coordination pattern of footfalls)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a *spider* walking on a rocky wall. The spider has **8 legs**, but at any given moment, only a few legs are touching the ground. The remaining legs are swinging in the air to take the next "step." A robot mimicking this behavior is called an **octopod** (eight-legged robot). Now consider a *human walking* on a balance beam — only one foot is on the beam at a time, and we must constantly shift our body weight to keep from falling. The robot's brain must perform similar continuous balance calculations, but mathematically and instantaneously.

> **Key Insight:** More legs = greater inherent stability but heavier and more complex. Fewer legs = higher mobility and efficiency but require sophisticated dynamic balancing.

## 1.3 Leg Count Classification (Industry-Standard Taxonomy)

| # Legs | Common Name | Examples in Nature | KTU Engineering Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 (Monopod)** | Hopping robot | Kangaroo rat | Boston Dynamics RHex precursors, Raibert hoppers |
| **2 (Biped)** | Humanoid / Bipedal | Human, bird | Honda ASIMO, Atlas, Cassie, Digit |
| **3 (Triped)** | Tripedal | (Rare in nature) | Academic prototypes, STriDER |
| **4 (Quadruped)** | Quadruped | Dog, horse, cat | Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYbotics ANYmal, MIT Mini-Cheetah |
| **6 (Hexapod)** | Hexapod | Insects (cockroach) | Weaver Ant robot, Messor, RHex variants |
| **8 (Octopod)** | Octopod | Spider, scorpion | Lauron series, COMET series |
| **>8 (Myriapod)** | Centipede-like | Centipede, millipede | Research prototypes only |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A **hexapod (6-legged) robot** is the most mechanically and computationally efficient legged platform, because it can achieve **static stability** in nearly all gait configurations — at least 3 legs are always on the ground, forming a stable support triangle.

## 1.4 Critical Stability Terminology (Master These)

- **Center of Gravity (COG) / Center of Mass (COM):** The point where the robot's entire weight is assumed to be concentrated.
- **Support Polygon (SP):** The convex hull formed by the contact points of all stance legs (legs touching the ground).
- **Static Stability:** Robot remains balanced even when motion is paused at any instant.
- **Dynamic Stability:** Robot is balanced only because of motion-induced inertial effects.
- **Stability Margin (SM):** The shortest distance from the COG projection onto the ground plane to the nearest edge of the Support Polygon.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Leg Configurations (Mammalian vs. Reptilian)

The two canonical leg arrangements studied in KTU curriculum:

### 2.1.1 Mammalian Configuration
- **Knee joint bends forward** (anterior flexion).
- Legs are positioned **directly under the body**.
- **Center of mass is low** and between the legs.
- Provides **superior stability** at rest.
- **Example:** Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYmal, four-legged mammals.

### 2.1.2 Reptilian Configuration
- **Knee joint bends outward/downward** (lateral or sprawled flexion).
- Legs are positioned **laterally to the body**.
- **Center of mass is higher** above the ground.
- Lower stability but allows **lower body height** to the ground.
- **Example:** Cockroaches, salamanders, RHex, lizards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The mammalian configuration is preferred in field robotics for static walking, whereas reptilian configurations are preferred for **high-speed dynamic locomotion** in confined spaces.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) per Leg

The number of independently actuated joints per leg determines its **dexterity** and **reachable workspace**.

| Leg DOF | Configuration | Capability | Field Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2 DOF** | Planar leg (2-link planar) | 2D plane walking only | Educational, simulations |
| **3 DOF** | 3-link manipulator | 3D positioning of foot | Common for hexapods |
| **4 DOF** | 3-link + 1 passive | Approximate isotropic foot | Quadrupeds |
| **5 DOF** | Full 3D leg + wrist | Substantial dexterity | Humanoid-like legs |
| **6 DOF** | Full spatial leg | Workspace identical to 6DOF arm | Anthropomorphic bipeds (e.g., Atlas) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a quadruped:** If each leg has 3 DOF, total leg DOF = 4 × 3 = 12. The body may contribute 6 DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation), giving **18 DOF total**.

## 2.3 Static vs. Dynamic Stability — Conceptual Breakdown

### Static Stability
A robot is **statically stable** if, for *every instant* in time, the vertical projection of its center of gravity onto the ground plane lies **within** the support polygon formed by the supporting legs.

**Mathematical condition:**

$$\vec{r}_{COG}^{proj} \in \text{Support Polygon}$$

### Dynamic Stability
A robot is **dynamically stable** if it does not tip over when the COG projection leaves the support polygon, provided the inertial forces and friction can recover balance. Dynamic stability allows for:
- Faster locomotion
- Tighter maneuvers
- Reduced ground contact time (running, trotting, galloping)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical**. Memorize the geometry and the boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Static Stability Margin (SSM) | $S_{sm} = d_{min}$ | $d_{min}$ = shortest distance from COG projection to SP edge | m |
| 2 | Normalized SSM | $S_{nsm} = \dfrac{d_{min}}{L_{max}}$ | $L_{max}$ = distance to farthest support point | dimensionless |
| 3 | Longitudinal SSM (LSM) | $S_{lsm} = d_{min}^{long}$ | shortest distance along longitudinal axis | m |
| 4 | Support Polygon (for n feet) | $SP = \text{ConvHull}\left(P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\right)$ | $P_i$ = foot contact point in 2D | m² |
| 5 | Effective COG accounting for gravity | $\vec{r}_{COG} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i}$ | $m_i$ = mass of i-th link, $\vec{r}_i$ = its COG position | m |
| 6 | Force Stability Margin (FSM) | $F_{sm} = F_{min}^{tip}$ | smallest tip-over force magnitude | N |
| 7 | Energy Stability Margin (ESM) | $E_{sm} = m g d_{min}$ | potential energy needed to tip over | J |
| 8 | Zero Moment Point (ZMP) | $\tau_{ZMP}^{x} = 0$ | resultant moment about ZMP along x-axis is zero | condition |
| 9 | Leg duty factor | $\beta = \dfrac{t_{stance}}{T}$ | $t_{stance}$ = ground contact time, $T$ = gait period | dimensionless |
| 10 | Number of legs, n, for static walking | $n \geq 4$ (or $n = 3$ for tripod) | minimum to maintain static support | count |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in:
> - **Boston Dynamics Spot** gait planner → uses ZMP + FSM.
> - **ANYmal** ANYdrive torque control → uses ESM-based safety limits.
> - **Disaster response robots** (e.g., for Fukushima inspection) → real-time SSM computation.

## 2.5 Static Walking Conditions (KTU-Relevant Theorems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 1 (Minimum Leg Count for Static Walking):** A legged robot can walk statically with **n ≥ 3** legs. With n = 3, the gait must be a **tripod gait** (alternating sets of 3 legs).
>
> **Theorem 2 (Maximum Liftable Legs):** A statically stable robot can lift up to **n − 3** legs simultaneously.
>
> **Theorem 3 (Insect Gaits with 6 legs):** A hexapod with 6 legs always has **at least 3 feet on the ground** during any well-defined wave, ripple, or tripod gait — ensuring continuous static stability.

## 2.6 Stability Margins — Deep Dive

The **Energy Stability Margin (ESM)** introduced by Messuri and Klein (1985) is considered the most physically meaningful metric because it directly relates to the *energy* required to topple the robot:

$$E_{sm} = m \cdot g \cdot d_{min}$$

where:
- $m$ = total robot mass (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \, m/s^2$)
- $d_{min}$ = shortest distance from COG projection to the support polygon edge (m)

> **Unit:** Joules (J). A robot with $E_{sm} = 50 \, J$ requires at least 50 J of external disturbance energy to tip over.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Stability Margin for a Quadruped Tripod

Consider a square-bodied quadruped robot with mass $m$ resting on 4 legs positioned at the corners of a square of side $a$. All four legs are in **stance phase** (on the ground). Find the static stability margin.

**Step 1 — Identify the Support Polygon.**

The four foot contact points in the ground plane are:

$$P_1 = (a/2, a/2), \quad P_2 = (-a/2, a/2), \quad P_3 = (-a/2, -a/2), \quad P_4 = (a/2, -a/2)$$

The support polygon is the square bounded by:

$$SP = \left\{(x,y) \mid |x| \leq a/2, \, |y| \leq a/2 \right\}$$

**Step 2 — Locate the COG Projection.**

Assuming uniform mass distribution, the COG lies at the geometric center of the body:

$$\vec{r}_{COG}^{proj} = (0, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Shortest Distance to the SP Edge.**

The distance from $(0,0)$ to the right edge $x = a/2$ is:

$$d_x = a/2 - 0 = a/2$$

The distance to the top edge $y = a/2$ is:

$$d_y = a/2 - 0 = a/2$$

**Step 4 — Take the Minimum.**

$$S_{sm} = d_{min} = \min\left(d_x, d_y\right) = a/2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Normalized SSM.**

The farthest support point from the COG projection is one of the corners, at distance:

$$L_{max} = \sqrt{(a/2)^2 + (a/2)^2} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$S_{nsm} = \frac{S_{sm}}{L_{max}} = \frac{a/2}{a/\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Energy Stability Margin.**

$$E_{sm} = m \cdot g \cdot S_{sm} = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{a}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $m = 25$ kg and $a = 0.5$ m, then $S_{sm} = 0.25$ m and $E_{sm} = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \approx 61.31$ J. The robot requires 61.31 J of energy to topple sideways.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability During a 3-Leg Stance (Hexapod Tripod Gait)

A hexapod has 6 legs at the corners of a regular hexagon of circumradius $R$. It walks using a **tripod gait**: at any instant, either set $\mathcal{A} = \{L_1, L_3, L_5\}$ or set $\mathcal{B} = \{L_2, L_4, L_6\}$ is in stance.

**Step 1 — Coordinates of all six feet.**

The six foot contact points, labeled in counter-clockwise order starting from the front:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= (R, 0) \\
P_2 &= (R/2, R\sqrt{3}/2) \\
P_3 &= (-R/2, R\sqrt{3}/2) \\
P_4 &= (-R, 0) \\
P_5 &= (-R/2, -R\sqrt{3}/2) \\
P_6 &= (R/2, -R\sqrt{3}/2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Tripod set $\mathcal{A}$ contact points.**

Tripod set $\mathcal{A}$ uses the alternating feet $\{P_1, P_3, P_5\}$, which form an **equilateral triangle**. The support polygon is the triangle with vertices:

$$P_1 = (R, 0), \quad P_3 = (-R/2, R\sqrt{3}/2), \quad P_5 = (-R/2, -R\sqrt{3}/2)$$

**Step 3 — Centroid of the support triangle.**

The centroid (geometric center) of an equilateral triangle lies at the average of its vertices:

$$\vec{C} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(R, 0) + (-R/2, R\sqrt{3}/2) + (-R/2, -R\sqrt{3}/2)\right] = (0, 0)$$

**Step 4 — Distance from centroid to the nearest edge.**

The inscribed circle radius (inradius) of an equilateral triangle with side $s$ is:

$$r_{in} = \frac{s}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

The side length of the tripod triangle is:

$$s = \sqrt{(R - (-R/2))^2 + (0 - R\sqrt{3}/2)^2}$$

$$s = \sqrt{(3R/2)^2 + (R\sqrt{3}/2)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{9R^2}{4} + \frac{3R^2}{4}} = \sqrt{3R^2} = R\sqrt{3}$$

Therefore, the inradius is:

$$r_{in} = \frac{R\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{R}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Static Stability Margin.**

The COG projection is at the body center $(0,0)$, which is at the centroid of the equilateral triangle. Thus:

$$S_{sm} = r_{in} = \frac{R}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Energy Stability Margin.**

$$E_{sm} = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{R}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** For the same circumradius $R$, a hexapod tripod gait has $S_{sm} = R/2$, which is *less* than a quadruped's $S_{sm} = a/2$ for comparable leg span ($a = R\sqrt{2}$). This shows the trade-off between mobility and stability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stability Comparison

| Robot Type | # Legs | # Stance Legs (during gait) | Support Polygon | $S_{sm}$ | Stability Class |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Monopod (hopping) | 1 | 1 (instantaneously) | Point | 0 | Dynamic only |
| Biped (walking) | 2 | 1 or 2 | Line / Point | 0 (point) | Dynamic only |
| Triped (tripod stand) | 3 | 3 | Triangle | $\dfrac{R}{2}$ | Static |
| Quadruped (4-leg stance) | 4 | 4 | Quadrilateral | $\dfrac{a}{2}$ (square case) | Static |
| Hexapod (tripod gait) | 6 | 3 | Equilateral triangle | $\dfrac{R}{2}$ | Static |
| Hexapod (wave gait) | 6 | 5 | Pentagon | $\sim \dfrac{R}{2}$ | Highly static |
| Octopod (wave gait) | 8 | 6 or 7 | Hexagon / heptagon | $\sim \dfrac{R}{2}$ | Extremely stable |

> **Conclusion of the matrix:** The **hexapod with wave gait** is the *gold standard* for guaranteed static stability in field robotics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Controller Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *control pipeline* by which a legged robot computes and maintains stability during locomotion. It does not attempt a physical sketch of a robot, but rather maps the **information flow** between subsystems — which is the most exam-relevant aspect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Suite IMU Force Sensors Vision] --> B[State Estimator]
    B --> C[Center of Mass Estimator]
    C --> D[Support Polygon Calculator]
    D --> E[Foot Contact Detection Module]
    E --> F[Stability Margin Calculator SSM FSM ESM]
    F --> G{Is Margin Within Safe Threshold}
    G -- Yes --> H[Continue Current Gait]
    G -- No --> I[Reactive Gait Adjustment Module]
    I --> J[Leg Trajectory Planner]
    J --> K[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    K --> L[Joint Torque and Velocity Commands]
    L --> M[Leg Actuators Servos Motors]
    M --> N[Ground Reaction Forces]
    N --> A
    H --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Layer 1 (Sensing):** IMU measures body orientation; force sensors measure ground reaction; vision detects terrain features.
- **Layer 2 (Perception):** State estimator fuses sensor data → COG position.
- **Layer 3 (Reasoning):** Support polygon is computed from foot contacts; stability margins are evaluated.
- **Layer 4 (Action):** If margin is unsafe, the reactive module triggers gait adjustment; otherwise, the planned gait continues.
- **Layer 5 (Execution):** Inverse kinematics converts foot trajectories into joint commands.
- **Loop closure:** Ground reaction forces feed back into the sensor layer.

## 4.2 Stability State Machine for a Quadruped

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AllStance
    AllStance --> DiagonalPair: Begin Diagonal Gait
    DiagonalPair --> AllStance: Both Pairs Landed
    AllStance --> LateralPair: Begin Lateral Gait
    LateralPair --> AllStance: Both Pairs Landed
    AllStance --> Tripod: Begin Dynamic Tripod
    Tripod --> AllStance: Recovery
    AllStance --> Unstable: COG outside SP
    Unstable --> EmergencyStabilize: Activate Reflex Controller
    EmergencyStabilize --> AllStance: Recovery via Leg Placement
    Unstable --> Fall: Recovery Failed
    Fall --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Support Polygon Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Support Polygon Formation]
        P1[Foot 1 Contact Point] --- P2[Foot 2 Contact Point]
        P2 --- P3[Foot 3 Contact Point]
        P3 --- P4[Foot 4 Contact Point]
        P4 --- P1
        COG[COG Projection] -.->|d_min| P3
        COG -.->|d_min| P1
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** The four corners $P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$, $P_4$ are the foot contact points. The convex hull forms the support polygon. The dotted lines indicate the **shortest distance** from the COG projection to the polygon edges — this is the Static Stability Margin $S_{sm}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between mammalian and reptilian leg configurations in legged mobile robots. Give one example of a field robot for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Mammalian Configuration:** Knee bends forward; legs are positioned directly beneath the body. Center of mass is low, providing high static stability at rest. *Example: Boston Dynamics Spot (quadruped).*
>
> **Reptilian Configuration:** Knee bends outward (sprawled); legs are positioned laterally to the body. Center of mass is higher, allowing lower body profile but lower stability. *Example: RHex hexapod; cockroach-inspired robots.*
>
> **Key Distinction:** Mammalian is preferred for static stability and load carrying; reptilian is preferred for high-speed dynamic gaits and confined-space navigation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term *Support Polygon* in legged robot stability. What is its relationship with the *Center of Gravity* projection for static stability?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Support Polygon (SP)** is the convex hull formed by connecting all the foot contact points of the legs currently in stance phase. For a robot standing on 4 legs at the corners of a rectangle, the SP is that rectangle.
>
> A robot is **statically stable** if and only if the vertical projection of the **Center of Gravity (COG)** onto the ground plane lies **strictly within** the interior of the support polygon. If the COG projection moves to the boundary, the robot is in marginal stability. If it moves outside, the robot will tip over (without dynamic recovery).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**
> *Module Internal Choice A:*
> **(a)** With neat architectural classification, explain the various **leg configurations** of legged mobile robots. Compare the stability, mobility, and mechanical complexity of biped, quadruped, and hexapod configurations. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Define **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** and **Energy Stability Margin (ESM)**. A quadruped robot of mass **30 kg** stands with all four legs on the ground, positioned at the corners of a square of side **0.4 m**. The COG lies exactly at the center. Compute the SSM, the Normalized SSM, and the ESM. Take **g = 9.81 m/s²**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to (a):

| Configuration | Biped | Quadruped | Hexapod |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **# Legs** | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **DOF / Leg (typical)** | 5–6 | 3 | 3 |
| **Total DOF** | ~12 | 12 | 18 |
| **Static Stability** | No (dynamic only) | Yes (≥ 3 legs in stance) | Yes (≥ 3 legs always) |
| **Mobility** | High (stairs, ladders) | High | Moderate (slower) |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | High (active balancing) | Moderate | High (more actuators) |
| **Computational Complexity** | Very high (ZMP control) | Moderate | Low (static gaits) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Example** | Atlas, Cassie | Spot, ANYmal | Messor, RHex |

> **Conclusion:** Bipeds offer best mobility but worst stability; hexapods offer best stability and computational simplicity but lowest speed; quadrupeds strike a balance and are the most common field robot architecture.

### Model Solution to (b):
