## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Block Architecture of a Fixed-Wing UAV System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Power Plant: Electric Motor or Piston Engine] --> B1[Propeller / Ducted Fan]
    B1 --> C1[Thrust Generation T]
    A1 --> A2[Energy Storage: Li-Po Battery or Fuel Tank]
    A2 --> A1
    C1 --> D1[Aerofoil Wing: Fixed Geometry]
    D1 --> E1[Lift Generation L equals 1/2 rho V squared S CL]
    D1 --> F1[Control Surfaces]
    F1 --> F2[Ailerons: Roll Control]
    F1 --> F3[Elevator: Pitch Control]
    F1 --> F4[Rudder: Yaw Control]
    G1[Flight Controller: Pixhawk / Cube Orange] --> F1
    G1 --> A1
    G1 <--> G2[IMU: 9-DOF Inertial Measurement Unit]
    G1 <--> G3[GPS / GNSS Receiver]
    G1 <--> G4[Telemetry Radio: 2.4 GHz or 900 MHz]
    G1 <--> G5[Airspeed Sensor: Pitot-Static Probe]
    H1[Payload Bay: Camera / LiDAR / Multispectral] --> D1
    H1 --> G6[Data Link to Ground Station]
    I1[Launch System: Catapult or Bungee] --> D1
    J1[Recovery System: Parachute or Net] --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The fixed-wing UAV is a *closed-loop cyber-physical system*. The flight controller reads sensor data (IMU, GPS, airspeed), computes the *error* between desired and measured attitude, and commands the control surfaces to deflect. This is a classic **PID-controlled mechatronic system** — directly relevant to KTU's PCRAT601 Field Robotics framing.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Launch --> Climb: Climb to cruise altitude

    Parachute --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Field-robotics integration:** A modern fixed-wing UAV is *not* just a flying camera. It is a *mobile edge-AI node* that performs onboard perception, decision-making, and adaptive trajectory planning — the very definition of a "field robot" in the KTU Module 1 framing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers below are tuned to a **2-page, 3-mark KTU answer booklet style** with crisp technical content and one supporting diagram or equation.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCRAT601]**

**"List and briefly explain the key characteristics of a fixed-wing UAV. Why is it preferred over a multirotor for large-area survey missions?"**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A fixed-wing UAV is an unmanned aerial platform whose **rigid wings generate lift through forward motion** in accordance with aerodynamic principles. Its defining characteristics are:

1. **Lift generation by forward airspeed** — requires a minimum stall speed $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L,max})}$.
2. **No hovering capability** — cannot remain stationary; must maintain cruise.
3. **Long endurance** — 1 to 40 hours depending on propulsion; Breguet range equation governs mission duration.
4. **High $L/D$ ratio** — typically 15–25, far exceeding multirotor's 3–5.
5. **Launch/recovery infrastructure** — catapult, bungee, or runway for launch; net, parachute, or belly-landing for recovery.
6. **Three-axis control** — ailerons (roll), elevator (pitch), rudder (yaw).

**Why preferred for survey:** The high lift-to-drag ratio of 15–25 (versus 3–5 for multirotors) directly multiplies the range in the Breguet equation. A 5-kg fixed-wing UAV can map 500–1000 hectares per flight, whereas a multirotor of the same weight covers only 20–40 hectares per battery cycle. Hence for **large-area, time-critical field-robotics missions**, the fixed-wing UAV is the energy-optimal choice.

*(Keywords: stall speed, L/D ratio, Breguet range, energy efficiency — 1 mark per characteristic cluster.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCRAT601]**

**"Explain the function of the three primary control surfaces of a fixed-wing UAV. How do they collectively control the aircraft's attitude?"**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A fixed-wing UAV has three primary hinged control surfaces mounted on the trailing edges of its wings and tail:

1. **Ailerons (Roll axis):** Mounted on the *outer trailing edge* of each wing, they move in *opposite directions* (one up, one down). This creates a *differential lift* that rolls the airframe about its longitudinal (x) axis.
2. **Elevator (Pitch axis):** Mounted on the *rear* of the horizontal stabiliser. A trailing-edge-up deflection increases downward tail force, pitching the nose down; the reverse pitches the nose up.
3. **Rudder (Yaw axis):** Mounted on the *rear* of the vertical stabiliser. Deflecting it sideways produces a side-force on the tail, yawing the nose left or right.

**Collective effect:** The three surfaces act in a *coordinated* manner to control the three rotational degrees of freedom (roll, pitch, yaw). A coordinated turn, for instance, requires simultaneous aileron (bank) + rudder (coordinated yaw) + elevator (maintain altitude). In a fly-by-wire / autopilot UAV, the **flight controller (e.g., Pixhawk)** computes the required deflections using PID loops running at 400 Hz on the IMU feedback.

*(1 mark for naming the three surfaces, 1 mark for the axis each controls, 1 mark for the coordinated-control concept.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and may be split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, **OR** (a) 4 + (b) 10, etc. We present the standard 7+7 split. **Two full alternative questions are provided — answer ANY ONE.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Aerodynamics and Performance Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, PCRAT601, Module 1]**

**(a) Derive the lift equation for a fixed-wing UAV starting from Bernoulli's principle. State the steady-cruise condition and derive the expression for stall speed. (7 marks)**

**(b) A fixed-wing survey UAV has the following parameters:**
- Total weight $W = 100$ N
- Wing area $S = 1.0 \text{ m}^{2}$
- Wing aspect ratio $AR = 9$
- Oswald efficiency $e = 0.85$
- Zero-lift drag coefficient $C_{D0} = 0.022$
- Maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,max} = 1.5$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Compute: (i) the stall speed, (ii) the lift coefficient for maximum $L/D$, (iii) the maximum $L/D$ ratio, and (iv) the corresponding cruise speed. Comment on whether this UAV can fly at 12 m/s without stalling. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Lift Equation and Stall Speed (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Bernoulli's Equation (1 mark)**

For steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline, the energy equation between a free-stream point ($\infty$) and a point on the upper surface of the aerofoil ($u$) is:

$$p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_u + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_u^{2}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the suction pressure on the upper surface (1 mark)**

$$p_u = p_{\infty} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_u^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$$

The pressure drop (suction) is:

$$\Delta p = p_{\infty} - p_u = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_u^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$$

**Step 3: Integrate the pressure differential over the wing planform area (2 marks)**

The net upward force (lift) is the surface integral of the local $\Delta p$ over the wing reference area $S$. The lift coefficient $C_L$ is defined as the *dimensionless ratio* of the integrated lift to the dynamic pressure times area:

$$L = \int_S \Delta p \, dS = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$$

$$\boxed{L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L}$$

**Step 4: Steady-cruise condition and stall speed (3 marks)**

In steady, unaccelerated level cruise, the four forces are in equilibrium:

$$\sum F_z = 0 \Rightarrow L = W$$

Substituting the lift equation:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L = W \quad\Rightarrow\quad V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L}}$$

The *stall* condition occurs at the maximum achievable lift coefficient $C_{L,max}$, beyond which the airflow separates from the upper surface and lift collapses. Hence:

$$\boxed{V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,max}}}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

**(i) Stall speed (1.5 marks)**

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 100}{1.225 \times 1.0 \times 1.5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200}{1.8375}} = \sqrt{108.84}$$

$$\boxed{V_{stall} \approx 10.43 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Lift coefficient for maximum $L/D$ (2 marks)**

From the standard result derived in Section 3.3:

$$C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e \cdot C_{D0}} = \sqrt{\pi \times 9 \times 0.85 \times 0.022}$$

$$\pi \times 9 \times 0.85 \times 0.022 = 0.5285$$

$$C_L^{*} = \sqrt{0.5285} \approx 0.727$$

$$\boxed{C_L^{*} \approx 0.727}$$

**(iii) Maximum $L/D$ ratio (1.5 marks)**

$$(L/D)_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}{C_{D0}}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \times 9 \times 0.85}{0.022}}$$

$$\dfrac{\pi \times 9 \times 0.85}{0.022} = \dfrac{24.05}{0.022} = 1093$$

$$\sqrt{1093} = 33.06$$

$$(L/D)_{max} = 0.5 \times 33.06 = 16.53$$

$$\boxed{(L/D)_{max} \approx 16.5}$$

**(iv) Cruise speed and stall check (2 marks)**

The cruise speed corresponding to $C_L^{*}$ is:

$$V_{cruise} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L^{*}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200}{1.225 \times 1.0 \times 0.727}} = \sqrt{224.6}$$

$$\boxed{V_{cruise} \approx 14.99 \text{ m/s} \approx 15.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Stall check at 12 m/s:**

Since $V_{stall} \approx 10.4$ m/s and $V = 12$ m/s, the UAV is flying with a stall margin of $(12 - 10.4)/10.4 = 15.4\%$, which is **safe** (a 15% margin is within the standard design range of 15–40%). However, the UAV would not be operating at *maximum L/D* — for best endurance, the autopilot should command $V \approx 15$ m/s.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Mission Profile and Engineering Application*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, PCRAT601, Module 1]**

**(a) With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the typical mission phases of a fixed-wing UAV. Highlight the differences in aerodynamic requirements between the *climb*, *cruise*, and *landing* phases. (7 marks)**

**(b) A field-robotics startup intends to deploy a fixed-wing UAV for **crop-health monitoring over 800 hectares of farmland**. The required ground-sample distance (GSD) is 5 cm/pixel, and the camera footprint at 120 m altitude is $120 \times 80$ m. Compute:**
- (i) The total number of flight lines required if each line is 4 km long.
- (ii) The total survey distance.
- (iii) The minimum cruise speed (assuming a 30-minute endurance budget) and the total mission time.
- (iv) Justify the choice of fixed-wing over multirotor for this mission, citing the Breguet range equation. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Mission Phases (7 marks)**

A typical fixed-wing UAV mission has the following sequential phases:

| Phase | Duration (typical) | Aerodynamic State | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-flight** | 5–10 min | N/A | Battery, GPS lock, sensor calibration |
| **2. Launch** | 2–5 s | Transient acceleration | $V > V_{stall}$ by end of launch |
| **3. Climb** | 1–5 min | $T > D$, $L > W \cos \gamma$ | Excess thrust for $\gamma$ climb angle |
| **4. Cruise** | 10–60 min | $T = D$, $L = W$ | **Optimised for $L/D_{max}$** |
| **5. Mission (survey)** | 20–120 min | $T = D$, $L = W$, payload ON | Camera trigger / LiDAR scan |
| **6. Descent** | 2–5 min | $T < D$ (idle / glide) | Energy management |
| **7. Approach** | 30–60 s | $T = D$, $L = W$, low altitude | Precise alignment with runway / net |
| **8. Landing / Flare** | 5–10 s | $T \downarrow$, $\alpha \uparrow$ | Sink rate < 1 m/s |

**Climb phase (2 marks):** The UAV operates in a non-equilibrium state with **excess thrust ($T > D$)** and **excess lift ($L > W \cos \gamma$)** to gain altitude. The flight-path angle $\gamma$ is given by $\sin \gamma = (T - D)/W$. The propeller typically runs at *maximum RPM* to maximise thrust.

**Cruise phase (2 marks):** The UAV operates at the **aerodynamically optimal** point where $T = D$ and $L = W$, *and* $C_L = C_L^{*}$ for *maximum L/D ratio*. This minimises fuel/battery consumption per unit distance, directly maximising the Breguet range.

**Landing phase (1 mark):** The UAV must *lose altitude* while retaining *enough airspeed* to avoid stall. Power is reduced ($T \ll D$) so the aircraft glides. In the final *flare*, the elevator is pulled up to increase $\alpha$ and $\C_L$ to bleed off speed, settling onto the runway / net.

---

**Part (b) — Mission Planning Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** Area = 800 ha = 8,000,000 m². Camera footprint = 120 m × 80 m at 120 m altitude. Flight line length = 4 km.

**(i) Number of flight lines (2 marks)**

Each flight line covers a strip of width 120 m (assuming 60% lateral overlap, effective coverage width = 120 × 0.4 = 48 m; using 30% overlap, effective width = 120 × 0.7 = 84 m). Using the *conservative* 60% overlap:

Effective strip width = 120 × (1 − 0.6) = 48 m

Number of lines = total swath / line spacing. If the farm is approximately square: side length = $\sqrt{8{,}000{,}000} \approx 2828$ m.

$$N_{lines} = \dfrac{2828}{48} \approx 59 \text{ lines}$$

For a 4-km long line, this is geometrically feasible (line length ≥ farm diagonal/2). Rounding up:

$$\boxed{N_{lines} \approx 60 \text{ lines}}$$

**(ii) Total survey distance (1 mark)**

$$D_{total} = 60 \times 4000 \text{ m} = 240{,}000 \text{ m} = 240 \text{ km}$$

Plus climb/turn/landing overhead (~10%): $\approx 264$ km total mission distance.

$$\boxed{D_{total} \approx 240 \text{ km} \text{ (excluding overheads)}}$$

**(iii) Minimum cruise speed and mission time (2 marks)**

Endurance budget: $E = 30 \text{ min} = 1800$ s. Including 10% reserve for launch + landing + 20% reserve: usable mission time = $0.7 \times 1800 = 1260$ s.

$$V_{min} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{t_{mission}} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{1260} \approx 190 \text{ m/s}$$

*But $190$ m/s is unrealistic for a small UAV.* Let us re-check: 240 km in 30 min would require 480 km/h — which is *not* feasible for a survey UAV (typical cruise is 15–25 m/s).

**Reality check:** 30 minutes endurance is **insufficient** for an 800-hectare survey at high GSD. The required endurance is:

$$t_{mission} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{18 \text{ m/s}} = 13{,}333 \text{ s} \approx 222 \text{ min} \approx 3.7 \text{ hours}$$

Therefore, the UAV must be **fuel-engine (piston)** for this mission — electric Li-Po endurance of 30 min is grossly insufficient. The minimum cruise speed is set by the *stall margin* (typically 1.4× stall speed) and the *image-motion blur constraint*.

For GSD = 5 cm/px, exposure time $t_{exp} \leq 1/1000$ s, and the ground-projected motion = $V \cdot t_{exp}$ must be ≤ 0.5 × GSD:

$$V \leq \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.05}{0.001} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

So a **piston-engine fixed-wing UAV with $V_{cruise} = 18$–$25$ m/s and endurance ≥ 4 hours** is the only feasible platform.

$$\boxed{V_{min} \approx 18 \text{ m/s}, \quad t_{mission} \approx 3.7 \text{ hours}}$$

**(iv) Fixed-wing vs Multirotor Justification (2 marks)**

Applying the Breguet range equation with realistic values ($L/D$ = 18 for fixed-wing, $L/D$ = 4 for multirotor, fuel fraction = 0.3):

$$R_{fixed} = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T} \cdot 18 \cdot \ln(1/0.7) \approx 5.7 \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T}$$

$$R_{multi} = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T} \cdot 4 \cdot \ln(1/0.7) \approx 1.27 \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T}$$

$$\dfrac{R_{fixed}}{R_{multi}} = \dfrac{18}{4} = 4.5 \times$$

The fixed-wing UAV has **4.5× the range** of a multirotor of the same weight class. Coupled with the **15–25× endurance** advantage (multirotor endurance is limited by hover power, not Breguet equation), the fixed-wing is the only platform that can cover 800 ha in a single sortie. Multirotors would require ≥ 15 battery swaps, which is operationally infeasible for a field-robotics deployment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the steady-cruise condition** $L = W$ before solving for $V$ — partial credit lost on lift-equation problems.
> 2. **Confusing $C_L$ (coefficient) with $L$ (force)** — symbolic mix-up loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the assumptions** of Bernoulli's derivation (steady, incompressible, inviscid) — 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — typically 0.5-mark deduction per missing unit.
> 5. **Omitting the Breguet-equation range-formula derivation step** — 2 marks lost on Part B questions.
> 6. **Confusing ailerons with rudder** in 3-mark Part A questions — 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Not stating the stall condition** as $C_{L,max}$ — examiners specifically look for this phrase.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> Read this section **30 minutes before the exam** as a final revision pass. Every bullet below is a *high-yield* KTU Module 1 fact.

- ✅ A **fixed-wing UAV** has *rigid wings* and generates lift via *forward motion* (no hovering).
- ✅ **Lift equation:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$ — must be derived from Bernoulli on the exam.
- ✅ **Stall speed:** $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L,max})}$ — lower bound of safe flight.
- ✅ **Steady-cruise:** $L = W$ and $T = D$ — single equilibrium the examiner tests.
- ✅ **Three control surfaces:** aileron (roll), elevator (pitch), rudder (yaw) — never confuse them.
- ✅ **Maximum $L/D$:** $(L/D)_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\pi AR e / C_{D0}}$ — occurs at $C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\pi AR e C_{D0}}$.
- ✅ **Breguet range:** $R = (\eta_{prop}/c_T) \cdot (L/D) \cdot \ln(W_i/W_f)$ — backbone of mission planning.
- ✅ **Wing loading** $W/S$ determines stall speed and cruise speed; high $AR$ gives high efficiency.
- ✅ **Fixed-wing advantages:** long endurance (1–40 h), high speed, long range, high payload.
- ✅ **Fixed-wing disadvantages:** cannot hover, needs launch/landing, large turning radius, wind-sensitive.
- ✅ **Five variants:** conventional, flying-wing, delta, blended-wing-body, hybrid-VTOL.
- ✅ **Mission phases:** pre-flight → launch → climb → cruise → survey → descent → approach → landing.
- ✅ **Atmospheric constants:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $a = 340.3$ m/s at sea level.
- ✅ **Field-robotics applications:** precision agriculture, mining survey, disaster response, BVLOS logistics, wind-farm inspection.
- ✅ **Multirotor vs fixed-wing:** multirotor $L/D \approx 3$–$5$, endurance 15–40 min; fixed-wing $L/D \approx 15$–$25$, endurance 1–40 h — the 4.5×–6× range advantage is decisive for large-area missions.
- ✅ **Examiner mantra:** *"Always state the assumptions, always state the units, always write the formula before substituting the numbers."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary-wing UAV - Multi-rotor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Rotary-Wing UAV: Multi-Rotor — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rotary-wing UAV - Multi-rotor

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multi-Rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)**, also called a **multirotor** or **multicopter**, is a rotary-wing aerial robot that generates lift and thrust using **three or more motor-driven rotors** with fixed-pitch blades. Unlike fixed-wing UAVs, multirotors achieve flight by varying the **angular velocities (RPM)** of their symmetrically placed rotors, exploiting differential thrust and reactive torques to maneuver through six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) in three-dimensional space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A multirotor UAV is an *under-actuated, mechanically simple* aerial platform whose only actuators are fixed-pitch rotors. The vehicle has *more DOF than independent control inputs*, making flight control an inherently **nonlinear, multivariable control problem**.

### Key Terminology (KTU-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **UAV** | Unmanned Aerial Vehicle — an aircraft without a human pilot onboard |
| **RPA** | Remotely Piloted Aircraft — a subset of UAVs under continuous ground control |
| **VTOL** | Vertical Take-Off and Landing — the ability to hover, ascend, and descend vertically |
| **Rotor** | A rotating assembly of blades that produces aerodynamic lift |
| **Fixed-Pitch** | Blade angle cannot be changed in flight; thrust is controlled only by RPM |
| **Hexacopter / Quadcopter** | Multirotor configurations with 6 and 4 rotors respectively |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are balancing a **broomstick on the palm of your hand**. You must constantly make tiny adjustments with your fingers to keep it from falling. Now imagine attaching **four small helicopter blades** to the ends of the broomstick and spinning them at high speed — the spinning blades push air down, and the broomstick (the body) floats in the air.

**Real-world analogy:** A multirotor is essentially a **flying chandelier** — the rotors are like rotating ceiling fans, and the central body is the chandelier's frame. To move forward, the "fans" at the back spin slightly faster than the ones in the front, tilting the chandelier forward. To turn, the diagonal fans change speeds in opposite directions, like a ballet dancer spinning by moving their arms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A multirotor is a *floating platform* held in the air by the **collective thrust** of its spinning rotors. It has no wings, no rudders, and no flaps — every motion is achieved purely by **rotor speed modulation**.

---

## 1.3 Why Multi-Rotors Dominate Field Robotics

- **Hovering capability** — essential for inspection, mapping, and precision agriculture.
- **Mechanical simplicity** — no swashplate, no variable-pitch mechanisms.
- **Omnidirectional agility** — 6-DOF maneuverability from a single thrust source.
- **Modular payload** — easily mounts cameras, LiDAR, multispectral sensors.
- **Indoor/Outdoor versatility** — can fly in GPS-denied environments with onboard SLAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **trade-off** is *limited endurance* (typically **15–30 minutes**) and *low payload fraction* (usually < 30% of total mass) due to high power consumption of multiple rotors.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Typical multirotor hover thrust-to-weight ratio: **T/W ≈ 2:1 to 3:1**
- Standard operating voltage: **11.1 V (3S LiPo)** to **22.2 V (6S LiPo)**
- Rotor blade tip speed (limit): **< 0.7 Mach** (subsonic)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Classification of Multi-Rotors

Multi-rotor UAVs are primarily classified by their **rotor count** and **rotor configuration**:

### 2.1.1 By Rotor Count

| Type | Rotor Count | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Tricopter** | 3 | Hobby / research |
| **Quadcopter** | 4 | Commercial, agricultural, research |
| **Hexacopter** | 6 | Industrial inspection, heavy payloads |
| **Octocopter** | 8 | Cinematography, heavy lift |

### 2.1.2 By Rotor Orientation

- **Coaxial** — two rotors stacked on the same axis (counter-rotating). High thrust density but mechanically complex.
- **Conventional / Flat** — rotors mounted in a single plane on outriggers.
- **Tilt-rotor** — rotors that physically tilt to redirect thrust (hybrid concept).

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — How a Multirotor Flies

A multirotor achieves **6-DOF control** using only **4 control inputs** (roll, pitch, yaw, throttle), making it a **strictly under-actuated system**. The mapping is:

### 2.2.1 The Four Control Inputs

1. **Throttle (Collective Thrust, $U_1$)** — Increases or decreases all rotor speeds equally → vertical climb/descend.
2. **Roll (Lateral Tilt, $U_2$)** — Differential speed between left and right rotors → sideways motion.
3. **Pitch (Longitudinal Tilt, $U_3$)** — Differential speed between front and rear rotors → forward/backward motion.
4. **Yaw (Heading Rotation, $U_4$)** — Differential speed between counter-rotating rotors → rotation about the vertical axis.

### 2.2.2 The Six Degrees of Freedom

| DOF | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $x$ | Surge | Forward/backward translation |
| $y$ | Sway | Left/right translation |
| $z$ | Heave | Up/down translation |
| $\phi$ | Roll | Rotation about $x$-axis |
| $\theta$ | Pitch | Rotation about $y$-axis |
| $\psi$ | Yaw | Rotation about $z$-axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Actuation Mismatch:** 4 control inputs ≠ 6 DOF. Two degrees ($\phi, \theta$) are *coupled* to lateral translations $(x, y)$ — a small tilt automatically produces lateral motion. This is the *under-actuation principle* of multirotors.

---

## 2.3 Aerodynamic Foundation

### 2.3.1 Momentum Theory (Disk Actuator Model)

Each rotor acts as an **actuator disk** that accelerates air downward. The thrust produced by a single rotor is:

$$T_i = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot \Omega_i^2 \cdot R^2$$

Where:
- $T_i$ = thrust of $i$-th rotor (Newtons)
- $C_T$ = thrust coefficient (dimensionless, ~ 0.1 for typical propellers)
- $\rho$ = air density ($1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- $A$ = disk area $\pi R^2$ (m²)
- $\Omega_i$ = rotor angular velocity (rad/s)
- $R$ = rotor radius (m)

### 2.3.2 Blade Element Theory (BEM) — Simplified

$$T_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^2$$

Where $C_L$ is the lift coefficient and $V$ is the relative air velocity over the blade.

### 2.3.3 Torque and Reaction

By Newton's third law, every rotor producing upward thrust generates an equal and opposite **reaction torque** on the airframe:

$$\tau_i = C_M \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot \Omega_i^2 \cdot R^3$$

Where $C_M$ is the moment coefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To cancel net yaw torque, adjacent rotors must **counter-rotate** (e.g., in a quadcopter: two spin CW, two spin CCW). This is the foundational principle of multirotor stability.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_i = C_T \rho A \Omega_i^2 R^2$ | Single rotor thrust | N |
| 2 | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Total thrust of $n$ rotors | N |
| 3 | $\tau_i = C_M \rho A \Omega_i^2 R^3$ | Reaction torque on airframe | N·m |
| 4 | $P_i = T_i \cdot v_{\text{induce}}$ | Induced power per rotor | W |
| 5 | $T/W = \frac{n \cdot T_i}{m \cdot g}$ | Thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_{\text{hover}} = mg$ | Hover thrust equals weight | N |
| 7 | $\omega_{\text{hov}} = \sqrt{\frac{mg}{n \cdot C_T \rho A R^2}}$ | Hover rotor speed | rad/s |
| 8 | $U_1 = b(\Omega_1^2 + \Omega_2^2 + \Omega_3^2 + \Omega_4^2)$ | Collective thrust input (quad) | N |
| 9 | $U_2 = lb(-\Omega_2^2 + \Omega_4^2)$ | Roll torque (quad) | N·m |
| 10 | $U_3 = lb(\Omega_1^2 - \Omega_3^2)$ | Pitch torque (quad) | N·m |
| 11 | $U_4 = d(-\Omega_1^2 + \Omega_2^2 - \Omega_3^2 + \Omega_4^2)$ | Yaw torque (quad) | N·m |
| 12 | $\eta = \frac{T \cdot v}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | Propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |

Where $b$ = thrust coefficient, $d$ = drag/torque coefficient, $l$ = arm length.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Figure of Merit (FoM)** in rotorcraft is: $\text{FoM} = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{actual}}}$, typically 0.6–0.8 for multirotors.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The multirotor concept is the workhorse of modern field robotics:
- **Precision agriculture** — DJI Agras series for spraying.
- **Infrastructure inspection** — power lines, wind turbines, bridges.
- **Search and rescue** — thermal imaging in disaster zones.
- **Surveying and mapping** — photogrammetry, LiDAR scanning.
- **Last-mile delivery** — Amazon Prime Air, medical supply delivery.
- **Indoor robotics** — SLAM-based exploration of warehouses and caves.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Hover Condition and Required Rotor Speed

### Problem Statement
A **quadcopter** of total mass $m = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ uses four identical rotors, each with $C_T = 0.005$, $R = 0.127 \text{ m}$ (5-inch propeller), in sea-level air ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Determine the **hover rotor angular velocity** $\Omega_{\text{hover}}$ for each motor.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Hover Condition

For a multirotor to hover, the **total upward thrust** must exactly balance the **gravitational weight**:

$$T_{\text{total}} = mg$$

For a quadcopter with four equal rotors, by symmetry, each rotor provides thrust:

$$T_i = \frac{mg}{4}$$

This is the **fundamental equilibrium equation** for hover.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Thrust Equation

Substitute the general thrust equation $T_i = C_T \rho A \Omega^2 R^2$:

$$\frac{mg}{4} = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot \Omega_{\text{hover}}^2 \cdot R^2$$

---

### Step 3 — Solve for $\Omega_{\text{hover}}$

Rearrange algebraically:

$$\Omega_{\text{hover}}^2 = \frac{mg}{4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot R^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\Omega_{\text{hover}} = \sqrt{\frac{mg}{4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot R^2}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Numerical Value

**Convert units carefully** (KTU expects SI):
- $m = 1.5 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $C_T = 0.005$ (dimensionless)
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $R = 0.127 \text{ m}$, so $R^2 = 0.01613 \text{ m}^2$
- $A = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.127)^2 = 0.0507 \text{ m}^2$

Plug in the numbers:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (1.5)(9.81) = 14.715 \text{ N} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 4 \times 0.005 \times 1.225 \times 0.0507 \times 0.01613 \\
&= 4 \times 0.005 \times 1.225 \times 0.000818 \\
&= 4 \times 5.01 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 2.004 \times 10^{-5} \\
\Omega_{\text{hover}}^2 &= \frac{14.715}{2.004 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.34 \times 10^{5} \text{ (rad/s)}^2 \\
\Omega_{\text{hover}} &= \sqrt{7.34 \times 10^{5}} \approx 856.7 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 5 — Convert to RPM

$$\text{RPM}_{\text{hover}} = \Omega_{\text{hover}} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 856.7 \times 9.549 \approx 8180 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is consistent with hobby-grade quadcopters using 5-inch propellers on 2206 motors, which typically hover at **7000–9000 RPM**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equations of Motion (Newton-Euler)

For a multirotor, the **6-DOF rigid body equations of motion** in the body frame are:

### 3.2.1 Translational Dynamics

$$\begin{aligned}
m \dot{u} &= -mg\sin\theta + (U_1) \\
m \dot{v} &= mg\sin\phi \cos\theta + (0) \\
m \dot{w} &= mg\cos\phi \cos\theta - U_1
\end{aligned}$$

Where $u, v, w$ are body-frame velocities, and $U_1$ is the total thrust.

### 3.2.2 Rotational Dynamics

$$\begin{aligned}
I_x \dot{p} &= (I_y - I_z) q r + U_2 \\
I_y \dot{q} &= (I_z - I_x) p r + U_3 \\
I_z \dot{r} &= (I_x - I_y) p q + U_4
\end{aligned}$$

Where $I_x, I_y, I_z$ are principal moments of inertia and $p, q, r$ are body angular rates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Control Allocation (Quadcopter)

For a quadcopter in **"X" configuration**, with motors numbered 1 (front-right, CW), 2 (rear-right, CCW), 3 (rear-left, CW), 4 (front-left, CCW), the **control mixing matrix** is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} b & b & b & b \\ -bl & 0 & 0 & bl \\ 0 & 0 & bl & -bl \\ -d & d & -d & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Omega_1^2 \\ \Omega_2^2 \\ \Omega_3^2 \\ \Omega_4^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Inverting** this matrix yields the **motor speed commands** for any desired $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$. This matrix is the **heart of the flight controller** (e.g., PX4, ArduPilot, Betaflight).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence of axes:** Notice that $U_1$ (throttle) appears in the first row only. This is because the control inputs are **linearly independent** under the small-angle assumption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Configuration | Hover Efficiency | Payload | Reliability | Mechanical Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tricopter** | Low | Low | Low (single tilt servo) | High |
| **Quadcopter** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Hexacopter (X6)** | Good | High | High (3-fail safe) | Medium |
| **Octocopter (X8)** | Low | Very High | Very High (4-fail safe) | High |
| **Coaxial Quad** | Good | High | High | Very High |

---

## 3.5 Power & Endurance Analysis

The **hover power** can be approximated as:

$$P_{\text{hover}} = \frac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A n}}$$

Where $n$ is the number of rotors. This shows that **adding rotors reduces total hover power** (at the cost of higher structural mass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Endurance** $\tau_{\text{flight}} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{hover}}}$ — modern LiPo batteries offer **150–250 Wh/kg**, the dominant limitation on multirotor endurance.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a multi-rotor UAV. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of using multi-rotor UAVs in field robotics applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A multi-rotor UAV is a rotary-wing unmanned aerial vehicle that uses three or more motor-driven fixed-pitch rotors to achieve lift and maneuverability. Thrust and torque are modulated by varying the rotational speed of the rotors.

**Advantages:**
1. **VTOL capability** — enables vertical take-off and landing in confined spaces, essential for field operations in uneven terrain.
2. **Hover stability** — can remain stationary at a fixed point, ideal for inspection and aerial photography.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Limited endurance** — typically 15–30 minutes due to high power consumption of multiple rotors.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of counter-rotating rotors in a quadcopter. What happens if all four rotors rotate in the same direction?**

**Model Answer:**

In a quadcopter, adjacent rotors rotate in **opposite directions** (two CW and two CCW). This counter-rotation is essential because:
- Each spinning rotor produces a **reaction torque** on the airframe (Newton's third law).
- By pairing CW and CCW rotors, the reaction torques **cancel out**, keeping the airframe stable in yaw.

**If all four rotors rotate in the same direction**, the net reaction torque would accumulate, causing the entire quadcopter to **spin uncontrollably about the vertical axis**, making controlled flight impossible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1]`
**(a)** Derive the thrust equation for a single rotor of a multirotor using **momentum theory**. State clearly the assumptions used in your derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hexacopter has a total mass of $3.0 \text{ kg}$. Each rotor has $C_T = 0.005$, radius $R = 0.15 \text{ m}$, and the air density is $1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate:
  - (i) The total thrust required for hover. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The hover angular velocity of each rotor in rad/s and RPM. **(3 marks)**
  - (iii) The thrust-to-weight ratio (T/W) and state whether the value is suitable for a stable multirotor. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Thrust Equation Derivation

**Assumptions of Momentum Theory (Actuator Disk Model):**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
2. Uniform velocity across the disk.
3. Far upstream and downstream, the pressure equals atmospheric.
4. The rotor is a single infinite number of blades (no swirl).

**Derivation:**

Consider a control volume enclosing the rotor disk of area $A$. Let:
- $V_\infty$ = far-field air velocity (≈ 0 in hover)
- $v_i$ = induced velocity at the disk
- $v_\infty'$ = wake velocity far downstream
- $T$ = thrust
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate through the disk

By **conservation of mass**:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_i$$

By **Bernoulli's equation** (upstream and downstream):

$$p_+ - p_- = \rho (v_\infty'^2 - V_\infty^2)/2$$

Since $V_\infty = 0$ (hover) and $v_\infty' = 2 v_i$ (from continuity):

$$\Delta p = \frac{1}{2} \rho (2 v_i)^2 = 2 \rho v_i^2$$

Thrust is the pressure difference times area:

$$T = A \Delta p = 2 \rho A v_i^2$$

Solving for $v_i$:

$$v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

Substituting back into the **momentum equation** $T = \dot{m} (v_\infty' - V_\infty) = \rho A v_i \cdot 2 v_i$:

$$\boxed{T = 2 \rho A v_i^2 = C_T \rho A \Omega^2 R^2}$$

where $C_T$ is the thrust coefficient determined experimentally.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Hexacopter Hover Calculation

**Given:**
- $m = 3.0 \text{ kg}$, $n = 6$ rotors
- $C_T = 0.005$, $R = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Total thrust for hover:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = mg = 3.0 \times 9.81 = \boxed{29.43 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Hover angular velocity:**

Per rotor: $T_i = T_{\text{total}} / 6 = 29.43 / 6 = 4.905 \text{ N}$

Disk area: $A = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.15)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$

From $T_i = C_T \rho A \Omega^2 R^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Omega^2 &= \frac{T_i}{C_T \rho A R^2} = \frac{4.905}{0.005 \times 1.225 \times 0.0707 \times 0.0225} \\
&= \frac{4.905}{9.74 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 5.04 \times 10^{5} \\
\Omega &= \sqrt{5.04 \times 10^{5}} \approx \boxed{710 \text{ rad/s}}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to RPM:

$$\text{RPM} = 710 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \boxed{6780 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) T/W ratio and assessment:**

$$\frac{T}{W} = \frac{29.43}{29.43} = 1.0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A T/W ratio of 1.0 is BARELY enough for hover and is NOT suitable for a stable multirotor.** A practical multirotor should have a T/W ratio of **2.0 to 3.0** to allow for:
> - Payload mounting
> - Wind disturbance rejection
> - Maneuvering (banking turns require extra thrust)
> - Battery degradation margin

**Recommended action:** Increase the rotor size or use motors with higher $C_T$ to achieve T/W ≈ 2.5.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a)** Explain the **control allocation strategy** for a quadcopter in X-configuration. Derive the **control mixing matrix** relating motor speeds to the four control inputs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **stability and control challenges** unique to multi-rotor UAVs. Compare the dynamic response of a quadcopter, hexacopter, and octocopter in terms of redundancy, payload, and agility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Control Allocation Strategy

In an **X-configuration quadcopter**, motors are placed at the four corners of a square frame, with motors **1 and 3 rotating clockwise (CW)** and motors **2 and 4 rotating counter-clockwise (CCW)**.

**The four control inputs are:**

- $U_1$ — Total thrust (vertical)
- $U_2$ — Roll torque (about $x$-axis)
- $U_3$ — Pitch torque (about $y$-axis)
- $U_4$ — Yaw torque (about $z$-axis)

**Force/torque contribution of each motor:**

| Motor | Location | Spin | Thrust $T_i$ | Torque $\tau_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Front-Right | CW | $b\Omega_1^2$ | $-d\Omega_1^2$ |
| 2 | Rear-Right | CCW | $b\Omega_2^2$ | $+d\Omega_2^2$ |
| 3 | Rear-Left | CW | $b\Omega_3^2$ | $-d\Omega_3^2$ |
| 4 | Front-Left | CCW | $b\Omega_4^2$ | $+d\Omega_4^2$ |

Where $b$ is the thrust constant and $d$ is the drag constant.

**The control mixing matrix:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} b & b & b & b \\ -\frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{bl}{\sqrt{2}} \\ -d & d & -d & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Omega_1^2 \\ \Omega_2^2 \\ \Omega_3^2 \\ \Omega_4^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The **$\sqrt{2}$** factor accounts for the diagonal arm length in X-configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To find individual motor speeds, this matrix must be **inverted** for any desired $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$ command.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Stability and Control Challenges

**Stability Challenges in Multi-Rotors:**

1. **Inherent instability** — A multirotor with no controller is **unstable** in all six DOF; it requires **continuous active feedback** to remain airborne.
2. **Coupling between axes** — Roll motion couples to lateral translation, pitch to longitudinal motion.
3. **Under-actuation** — 4 inputs for 6 DOF, requiring coordinated control.
4. **Sensor noise** — IMU drift, GPS latency, vibration from rotors.
5. **Wind disturbance** — Highly susceptible due to low inertia.
6. **Actuator saturation** — Motor speed limits during aggressive maneuvers.
7. **Ground effect** — Increased lift near ground can cause unstable hovering below ~0.5 m altitude.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Quadcopter | Hexacopter | Octocopter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotors** | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| **Max Fail-Safe Rotor Loss** | 0 (no redundancy) | 2 | 4 |
| **Typical Payload** | 0.5–2 kg | 2–5 kg | 5–15 kg |
| **Hover Efficiency** | Medium | High | Medium-Low |
| **Agility** | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Field Application** | Survey, light inspection | Industrial inspection | Heavy lift, cinematography |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Adding rotors *increases redundancy* and *payload*, but *decreases efficiency* because more rotors operate at off-hover conditions. The **hexacopter is the engineering sweet spot** for most field robotics applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for missing or wrong units (e.g., writing 856 instead of **856 rad/s**).
> 2. **Confusing $C_T$ and $C_L$** — $C_T$ is the *thrust coefficient* of the rotor; $C_L$ is the *lift coefficient* of a blade airfoil. Do not interchange.
> 3. **Mixing CW and CCW signs** — In the yaw equation $U_4$, the signs of $d$ coefficients depend on rotor spin direction. Get this wrong and the entire mixing matrix is incorrect.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — In derivation questions, **always list the assumptions** of momentum theory (steady, incompressible, axisymmetric flow) before starting.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in X-configuration — The diagonal arm length is $\sqrt{2} \times$ the half-arm, which students frequently omit.
> 6. **T/W ratio for stability** — A T/W of 1.0 is *theoretically* sufficient for hover, but examiners expect students to recognize that **2.0–3.0 is the engineering standard** for practical multirotors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

### Conceptual
- ✅ A multirotor uses **3+ fixed-pitch rotors** and is **under-actuated** (4 inputs for 6 DOF).
- ✅ **Counter-rotating rotors** cancel net yaw torque — fundamental to stability.
- ✅ **Throttle** changes altitude; **differential thrust** causes rotation/translation.

### Mathematical
- ✅ Thrust equation: $T = C_T \rho A \Omega^2 R^2$
- ✅ Hover condition: $T_{\text{total}} = mg$
- ✅ Per-rotor hover speed: $\Omega = \sqrt{\frac{mg}{n C_T \rho A R^2}}$
- ✅ Induced power: $P_i = T v_i$, where $v_i = \sqrt{T/(2\rho A)}$

### Design
- ✅ T/W ratio should be **2.0–3.0** for a practical multirotor.
- ✅ **Hexacopter** = best efficiency-redundancy trade-off.
- ✅ **Coaxial** = high thrust density but mechanically complex.

### Control
- ✅ Control mixing matrix is the **inverse problem** of allocating $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$ to motor speeds.
- ✅ PID cascade: outer loop (position) → inner loop (attitude).
- ✅ X-configuration uses $\sqrt{2}$ factor in arm length; "+" configuration uses simple arm length $l$.

### Sensors
- ✅ **IMU** (gyro + accel) is the heart of stabilization.
- ✅ **GPS** for outdoor position; **optical flow / LiDAR** for indoor.

### Limitations
- ✅ Limited endurance (15–30 min) due to high power draw.
- ✅ Low payload fraction (< 30% of total mass).
- ✅ Inherently unstable without active control.

### KTU Examiner's Final Tip
> [!NOTE]
> When asked to **"explain the working principle"** of a multirotor, always structure your answer in this order: **(1) Thrust generation, (2) Torque cancellation by counter-rotation, (3) Four control inputs and their effect on 6-DOF, (4) Hover equation, (5) One practical example**. This structure guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tricopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tricopter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tricopter** (also termed a **trirotor UAV** or **Y3-configuration rotorcraft**) is a multi-rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) that uses **three independent brushless DC (BLDC) motors** coupled to fixed-pitch rotors to generate the combined lift, attitude, and yaw control required for stable hover and agile flight. It is classified as a *rotary-wing VTOL (Vertical Take-Off and Landing)* platform under the broader taxonomy of Field Robotics mobile platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Aerial Field Robotics):**
> Tricopters are studied as a *minimal-actuator* aerial platform, where a single tilting servo replaces the conventional fourth rotor, making them a canonical case study for **under-actuated control systems**.

The three rotors are typically arranged in a **Y-shaped (120° apart)** or **T-shaped (two front, one rear)** configuration. Two rotors (left and right) provide symmetric thrust for **collective lift** and **roll/pitch control**, while the **third rotor is mounted on a tilting mechanism** (servo-actuated hinge) that produces differential **reaction torque** for **yaw control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Principle:**
> A rigid body in 3D space has **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** — 3 translational (surge, sway, heave) and 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw). With only 4 control inputs (3 motor speeds + 1 tilt servo angle), a tricopter is an **under-actuated system** (4 inputs < 6 DOF).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are balancing a **flat tray carrying a cup of water** on the tip of one finger. The tray has 6 ways it can wobble (forward, back, side-to-side, twist, tilt, drop). Yet you only have **one finger** and **a few muscles** to keep it steady. Your brain automatically *couples* certain motions — for example, leaning forward is achieved by moving your whole arm, not by a separate "lean" muscle. This is exactly what an under-actuated tricopter does: it has fewer controls than freedoms, so the controller must *smartly coordinate* them.

> **Real-World Analogy — The "Three-Wheeled Steering" Bicycle:**
> A bicycle with two rear wheels (fixed) and one front wheel (steerable) is mechanically the ground analog of a tricopter. The single steerable element controls an entire rotational axis (yaw), while differential forces on the fixed wheels control the others.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of rotors** | **3** | Fixed-pitch, BLDC-driven |
| **Rotor disk diameter** | 5″ – 12″ | Application-dependent |
| **Hover thrust-to-weight ratio** | **≥ 2.0** | For aggressive maneuverability |
| **Maximum takeoff weight (MTOW)** | 0.5 – 5 kg | Hobbyist to research-grade |
| **Endurance** | 8 – 25 minutes | Battery-limited |
| **Operating envelope** | ±45° attitude | Within Euler-angle safe region |
| **Air density (ρ) at sea level** | **1.225 kg/m³** | ISA standard |
| **Gravitational acceleration (g)** | **9.81 m/s²** | Used in weight computation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — The Six-Step Control Logic

A tricopter achieves stable flight through the following orchestrated control layers:

1. **State Estimation** — The onboard Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — comprising a 3-axis gyroscope, 3-axis accelerometer, and 3-axis magnetometer — measures the body's angular velocity, linear acceleration, and heading.
2. **Reference Generation** — The pilot (via RC transmitter) or autonomous planner specifies a desired setpoint: position $(x_d, y_d, z_d)$ and yaw $\psi_d$.
3. **Outer-Loop Control (Position Controller)** — A cascaded **PID** or **LQR** controller converts position error into desired attitude (roll, pitch) and total thrust.
4. **Inner-Loop Control (Attitude Controller)** — A high-bandwidth PID converts attitude error into motor speed commands.
5. **Mixing & Allocation** — The control mixer mathematically maps the four virtual control inputs ($T_{total}, \tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi$) to the four physical actuators (M1, M2, M3, servo $\alpha$).
6. **Actuator Dynamics** — Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) drive the BLDC motors at commanded RPM; the servo positions the rear rotor mount.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Under-Actuation:**
> Heave (altitude) is directly controlled by **total thrust**. Roll and pitch are controlled by **differential thrust**. Yaw, however, has *no symmetric counterpart* on a 3-rotor platform — it requires **asymmetric reaction torque**, which is exactly why the tilt servo exists.

## 2.2 The Y3 (Triangular) Configuration

In the most common layout, the three motor arms are spaced **120° apart** around a central hub.

- **Motor positions** in the body frame (arm length $L$):
  - $M_1$: front-right at angle $0°$
  - $M_2$: rear-left at angle $120°$
  - $M_3$: rear-right at angle $240°$
- The **tilt servo** is mounted on the arm of one rotor (typically the rear, $M_2$ or $M_3$).

## 2.3 Force and Torque Model

Let $T_i$ denote the thrust produced by rotor $i$ (where $i \in \{1,2,3\}$) and $Q_i$ denote the **reaction torque** (anti-torque) produced by rotor $i$. The thrust of a single rotor follows the **momentum-theory equation**:

$$
T_i = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n_i^2 \cdot D^4
$$

where:
- $C_T$ is the thrust coefficient of the rotor (typically $\mathbf{0.1 \le C_T \le 0.12}$ for small propellers),
- $n_i$ is the rotational speed in **rev/s**,
- $D$ is the propeller diameter in meters,
- $\rho$ is the air density.

The reaction torque (drag torque) is:

$$
Q_i = C_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n_i^2 \cdot D^5
$$

with $C_Q$ the torque coefficient (typically $C_Q \approx C_T / 8$ for small rotors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Thrust scales with $n^2$ (quadratic in RPM), which is why flight controllers use **square-root mixing** ($n \propto \sqrt{T}$) when converting desired thrust to PWM commands.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Critical formatting note:** All absolute-value and set-membership symbols are written as `\vert` / `\mid` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rotor thrust | $T_i = C_T \rho n_i^2 D^4$ | N | Momentum theory |
| 2 | Rotor torque | $Q_i = C_Q \rho n_i^2 D^5$ | N·m | Reaction (drag) torque |
| 3 | Total heave force | $T = T_1 + T_2 + T_3$ | N | Controls altitude |
| 4 | Roll torque (about body x-axis) | $\tau_\phi = L \left( T_1 - \tfrac{1}{2} T_2 - \tfrac{1}{2} T_3 \right)$ | N·m | Differential front thrust |
| 5 | Pitch torque (about body y-axis) | $\tau_\theta = L \cos(30°) ( T_3 - T_2 )$ | N·m | Differential side thrust |
| 6 | Yaw torque (about body z-axis) | $\tau_\psi = \alpha L_t (T_1 + T_2 + T_3)$ | N·m | $\alpha$ is servo tilt |
| 7 | Direction of rotation | $Q_i = (-1)^{i+1} \cdot \vert Q_i \vert$ | N·m | Alternating CW/CCW |
| 8 | Hover condition | $T_{hover} = mg / (3 C_T \rho n^2 D^4)$ | N | Equilibrium thrust |
| 9 | Power consumption | $P_i = C_P \rho n_i^3 D^5$ | W | Cubic in RPM |
| 10 | Inertia tensor (approx.) | $J = \text{diag}(J_x, J_y, J_z)$ | kg·m² | Principal axes |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tricopters are deployed in:

- **Precision agriculture** — narrow-row crop monitoring where maneuverability > payload.
- **Indoor inspection** — confined spaces (tunnels, ducts) where quadrotor downwash is undesirable.
- **Research testbeds** — minimal complexity for **novel control-law validation** (adaptive control, sliding-mode, MPC).
- **Educational platforms** — teaching UAV kinematics without the cost of a 4th ESC/motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tricopter is a *minimal-complexity* aerial platform — every additional rotor would add weight, cost, and failure modes. Field robotics favors the **simplest platform that satisfies mission constraints**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Yaw Control via Tilting Servo

The yaw axis is the most distinctive feature of a tricopter. On a quadrotor, yaw is controlled by *differential motor speed* (because each motor's reaction torque can be independently balanced). On a tricopter, with only one "free" control axis once collective thrust is set, yaw must be generated **mechanically** by tilting the thrust vector of one rotor.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Let the tilting rotor be $M_1$ (front), and let $\alpha$ be the servo tilt angle measured from the vertical. The thrust vector of $M_1$ is then tilted by $\alpha$ in the body $x$-$z$ plane.

**Step 2 — Decompose the thrust vector.**

$$
\vec{T}_1 = T_1 \sin(\alpha)\,\hat{x} + T_1 \cos(\alpha)\,\hat{z}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the moment arm.** The rotor is offset from the CG by $L$ along the body $x$-axis. The cross product $\vec{r} \times \vec{T}_1$ gives the torque about the CG.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the cross product.**

$$
\vec{\tau}_{\psi}^{(1)} = \vec{r} \times \vec{T}_1 = (L\,\hat{x}) \times (T_1 \sin\alpha\,\hat{x} + T_1 \cos\alpha\,\hat{z})
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the right-hand rule.** Recall that $\hat{x} \times \hat{x} = 0$ and $\hat{x} \times \hat{z} = -\hat{y}$ (since $\hat{x} \times \hat{z} = -\hat{y}$ in a right-handed frame, or $+\hat{y}$ depending on convention — here we adopt aerospace convention with $\hat{y}$ pointing right).

$$
\vec{\tau}_{\psi}^{(1)} = L T_1 \cos(\alpha) \cdot (\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) = -L T_1 \cos(\alpha)\,\hat{y}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* A positive $\alpha$ (tilt to the right) produces a moment about the lateral axis. To generate a **pure yaw** moment, the tilt must produce an *asymmetric reaction torque* — which is achieved by the fact that tilting the rotor also changes the **moment arm of its reaction torque $Q_1$** about the CG.

**Step 6 — Yaw torque from tilted reaction torque.** The reaction torque of $M_1$ acts along the rotor's shaft, which is now tilted. Its component about the body $z$-axis (yaw) is:

$$
\tau_\psi^{(Q)} = Q_1 \cos(\alpha) = C_Q \rho n_1^2 D^5 \cos(\alpha)
$$

**Step 7 — The complete yaw authority.**

$$
\tau_\psi = Q_1 \cos(\alpha) + (T_1 \cos\alpha) \cdot (L \sin\alpha) - (T_2 + T_3) \cdot d_{cg}
$$

For small angles $\alpha$, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$ and $\sin\alpha \approx \alpha$, so:

$$
\tau_\psi \approx Q_1 + T_1 L \alpha - (T_2 + T_3) d_{cg}
$$

**Step 8 — Yaw equation of motion.**

$$
J_z \ddot{\psi} = \tau_\psi = C_Q \rho n_1^2 D^5 + T_1 L \alpha - (T_2 + T_3) d_{cg}
$$

**Conversion logic of the final result:** Yaw acceleration is driven by *three terms*: the direct anti-torque of the tilting rotor, a thrust-coupling term proportional to tilt angle, and a parasitic term from off-center CG placement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Complete Newton–Euler Dynamic Model (Tricopter Body Frame)

**Step 1 — Translational dynamics (Newton's 2nd law in body frame).**

Let $\vec{F}_b$ be the total aerodynamic force and $m$ the mass. In the body frame:

$$
m \dot{\vec{v}}_b + m \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_b = \vec{F}_b - m R^T \vec{g}
$$

Expanding component-wise for $u$ (surge), $v$ (sway), $w$ (heave):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{u} - v r + w q) &= F_x \\
m(\dot{v} - w p + u r) &= F_y \\
m(\dot{w} - u q + v p) &= F_z - mg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotational dynamics (Euler's equations).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_x \dot{p} + (J_z - J_y) q r &= \tau_\phi \\
J_y \dot{q} + (J_x - J_z) p r &= \tau_\theta \\
J_z \dot{r} + (J_y - J_x) p q &= \tau_\psi
\end{aligned}
$$

where $p, q, r$ are the body angular rates (roll, pitch, yaw).

**Step 3 — Control allocation matrix (the "Mixer").**

Define the virtual control input vector:

$$
\vec{u} = \begin{bmatrix} T_{total} \\ \tau_\phi \\ \tau_\theta \\ \tau_\psi \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is mapped to the actuator vector $\vec{\omega}_{cmd} = [\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \alpha]^T$ via the **control-effectiveness matrix** $B$:

$$
\vec{u} = B(\vec{\omega}_{cmd})
$$

**Step 4 — Construct $B$ for the Y3 tricopter.**

The mixer is derived by inverting the geometric relationship derived in §2.3 and §3.1:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} T_{total} \\ \tau_\phi \\ \tau_\theta \\ \tau_\psi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ L_x & -L_x/2 & -L_x/2 & 0 \\ 0 & -L_y\sqrt{3}/2 & L_y\sqrt{3}/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & L_t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ T_1 \alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Actuator saturation constraint.**

Each motor's thrust is bounded:

$$
0 \le T_i \le T_{max}, \quad -\alpha_{max} \le \alpha \le +\alpha_{max}
$$

with $T_{max}$ defined by the motor's KV rating, propeller pitch, and battery voltage.

**Step 6 — Closed-loop linearization (hover condition).**

At hover, $\vec{v}_b = 0$, $\vec{\omega} = 0$, and $\phi = \theta = 0$. The nonlinear terms $qr, pr, pq$ vanish. The linearized model becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{z} &= -mg + T_{total} \\
J_x \ddot{\phi} &= \tau_\phi \\
J_y \ddot{\theta} &= \tau_\theta \\
J_z \ddot{\psi} &= \tau_\psi
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **decoupled double-integrator** in each axis — the foundation for PID design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The decoupled linearized dynamics justify using four independent PID loops (one per axis), which is the standard autopilot architecture in ArduPilot and PX4 for tricopter mode.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A tricopter has total mass $m = 1.5$ kg, arm length $L = 0.25$ m, propeller diameter $D = 0.25$ m, $C_T = 0.11$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Find the required RPM for hover.

**Solution:**

Required total thrust = $mg = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715$ N. Per rotor (assuming equal distribution):

$$
T_i = \frac{mg}{3} = \frac{14.715}{3} = 4.905 \text{ N}
$$

Solve for $n$ (rev/s):

$$
n = \sqrt{\frac{T_i}{C_T \rho D^4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.905}{0.11 \times 1.225 \times (0.25)^4}}
$$

Evaluate the denominator:

$$
0.11 \times 1.225 \times 0.003906 = 0.000527
$$

Therefore:

$$
n = \sqrt{\frac{4.905}{0.000527}} = \sqrt{9307} \approx 96.5 \text{ rev/s} \approx 5790 \text{ RPM}
$$

> **Result:** Each motor must spin at approximately **5790 RPM** to maintain hover.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Y3 Tricopter Top-View Configuration (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB["M1 - Front Rotor 1200g Thrust CW"]:::rotor

    nodeD["M3 - Rear Right Rotor 1200g Thrust CW"]:::rotor
    nodeE["Tilt Servo on M1 Arm Range -25 to +25 deg"]:::servo
    nodeF["Flight Controller Pixhawk-class IMU + Baro"]:::fc
    nodeG["RC Receiver 2.4 GHz PPM SBUS"]:::rc
    nodeH["Battery 3S 11.1V 2200mAh LiPo"]:::batt
    nodeI["ESCs 3x 30A BLDC Drivers"]:::esc
    nodeJ["Telemetry 915 MHz Optional"]:::tele

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD

    nodeI --- nodeB
    nodeI --- nodeC

    nodeF --- nodeJ

    classDef hub fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Functional Control-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmd1["Pilot RC Input"] --> cmd2["Desired Setpoint"]
    cmd2 --> loop1["Outer Loop Position PID"]
    loop1 --> loop2["Inner Loop Attitude PID"]
    loop2 --> loop3["Control Mixer B-matrix"]
    loop3 --> loop4["Actuator Commands PWM"]
    loop4 --> loop5["ESCs Servo"]
    loop5 --> loop6["Rotor Dynamics + Aerodynamics"]
    loop6 --> loop7["Rigid Body 6-DOF Dynamics"]
    loop7 --> loop8["IMU Sensor Feedback"]
    loop8 --> loop1
    loop8 -.-> loop2

    classDef ctrl fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class loop6,loop7,loop8 plant
```

## 4.3 Yaw-Control Mechanics (Tilt Servo Action)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ya["Yaw Command from Pilot"] --> yb["Compute Yaw Torque Demand tau_psi"]
    yb --> yc{"Sign of tau_psi"}
    yc -- Positive --> yd["Set Servo to +alpha_tilt"]
    yc -- Negative --> ye["Set Servo to -alpha_tilt"]
    yc -- Zero --> yf["Hold Servo at 0 deg"]
    yd --> yg["M1 Thrust Vector Tilts"]
    ye --> yg
    yf --> yg
    yg --> yh["Differential Reaction Torque Acts on Body"]
    yh --> yi["Body Yaw Rate Changes"]
    yi --> yj["IMU Measures New Yaw Rate"]
    yj --> yb

    classDef cmd fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class ya,yb,yc,yd,ye,yf cmd
    class yg,yh,yi act
    class yj sens
```
