A set of $k$ kinematic constraints can be written compactly in **Pfaffian form**:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$$

where $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})$ is a $k \times n$ matrix (with $n$ = total configuration variables). The **null space** of $\mathbf{A}$ gives the *admissible velocities*.

### Step 8 — Worked Conceptual Logic — Differential Drive Robot
For a 2-wheeled differential drive robot (very common in KTU problems):
- Left wheel angular velocity: $\omega_L$, Right wheel angular velocity: $\omega_R$
- Linear velocity of robot center: $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$
- Angular velocity of robot: $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- Where $L$ = wheelbase (distance between wheels), $r$ = wheel radius.

The **non-holonomic constraint** ("wheel cannot slide sideways") is mathematically:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is non-integrable: there is no position-only function $f(x,y,\theta)=0$ from which this can be derived. Hence the robot can be *parallel-parked* despite the constraint.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | 2D Position Vector | $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | 2D Rotation Matrix | $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{psmallmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{psmallmatrix}$ | $SO(2)$, dimensionless |
| 3 | 2D Pose HTM | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{psmallmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{psmallmatrix}$ | $SE(2)$ |
| 4 | 3D Position Vector | $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ | meters (m) |
| 5 | Roll-Pitch-Yaw Rotation | $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\psi)\mathbf{R}_y(\theta)\mathbf{R}_x(\phi)$ | $SO(3)$ |
| 6 | 3D Pose HTM | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{psmallmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{psmallmatrix}$, $4\times 4$ | $SE(3)$ |
| 7 | Holonomic Constraint | $f(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | — |
| 8 | Non-holonomic Constraint | $g(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = 0$, non-integrable | — |
| 9 | Pfaffian Form | $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ | — |
| 10 | Differential Drive Linear Vel. | $v = (r/2)(\omega_L + \omega_R)$ | m/s |
| 11 | Differential Drive Angular Vel. | $\dot{\theta} = (r/2L)(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | rad/s |
| 12 | No-Slip Constraint (diff. drive) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | — |
| 13 | Point Transformation | $\mathbf{p}^A = \mathbf{R}_{B}^{A}\,\mathbf{p}^B + \mathbf{p}_{B\,\text{org}}^{A}$ | m |
| 14 | Pose Composition | $\mathbf{T}_{C}^{A} = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}$ | $SE(2)$ / $SE(3)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility** — In real-world field robotics, these models power **autonomous navigation** of warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), **agricultural tractors** (constrained by row alignment), **planetary rovers** (with rocker-bogie suspensions), and **search-and-rescue robots** (constrained by rubble-strewn terrain).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — 2D Rotation Matrix from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ from geometric projection.

**Step 1:** Consider a unit vector along the x-axis of frame $\{B\}$: $\hat{x}_B = [1, 0]^T$ (in $\{B\}$).

**Step 2:** Express $\hat{x}_B$ in frame $\{A\}$ after a CCW rotation by $\theta$:

$$\hat{x}_B \text{ in } \{A\} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Explanation:** The original x-axis now makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis of $\{A\}$, so its projections are $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.

**Step 3:** Similarly, the unit vector along the y-axis of $\{B\}$: $\hat{y}_B = [0, 1]^T$.

**Step 4:** Express $\hat{y}_B$ in frame $\{A\}$ after CCW rotation by $\theta$:

$$\hat{y}_B \text{ in } \{A\} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Explanation:** The original y-axis now makes angle $\theta + 90°$ with $\{A\}$'s x-axis. Its projections are $\cos(\theta+90°) = -\sin\theta$ and $\sin(\theta+90°) = \cos\theta$.

**Step 5:** Stack these two column vectors side-by-side to form the rotation matrix:

$$\mathbf{R}_{B}^{A}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \vert & \vert \\ \hat{x}_B & \hat{y}_B \\ \vert & \vert \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (Verification — Orthogonality):**

$$\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 (Determinant):**

$$\det(\mathbf{R}) = \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta - (-\sin\theta)(\sin\theta) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

This confirms $\mathbf{R} \in SO(2)$. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — 2D Pose Transformation (Point in $\{B\}$ → Point in $\{A\}$)

**Given:** A point $P$ with coordinates $\mathbf{p}^B$ in the robot frame, the robot origin is at $\mathbf{p}_{B\,\text{org}}^A$ in the world frame, and the robot frame is rotated by $\theta$ with respect to the world frame.

**Goal:** Find $\mathbf{p}^A$, the coordinates of $P$ in the world frame.

**Step 1 — Rotate the point from $\{B\}$ to be aligned with $\{A\}$:**

$$\mathbf{p}_{\text{rotated}}^A = \mathbf{R}_{B}^{A}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^B$$

**Step 2 — Translate by the origin of $\{B\}$ expressed in $\{A\}$:**

$$\mathbf{p}^A = \mathbf{R}_{B}^{A}(\theta)\,\mathbf{p}^B + \mathbf{p}_{B\,\text{org}}^A$$

**Step 3 — Convert to homogeneous (augmented) form** $\tilde{\mathbf{p}} = [x, y, 1]^T$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x^A \\ y^A \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_{B,\text{org}} \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_{B,\text{org}} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x^B \\ y^B \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore the homogeneous transformation matrix** that maps points from $\{B\}$ to $\{A\}$ is:

$$\mathbf{T}_{B}^{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_{B,\text{org}} \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_{B,\text{org}} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(2)$$

∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Differential Drive Robot Kinematic Model (Standard KTU Numericals)

**Setup:** A differential drive robot has two independently driven wheels on a common axle. Let:
- $r$ = wheel radius (m)
- $L$ = distance between wheel centers (wheelbase) (m)
- $\omega_L$, $\omega_R$ = angular velocities of left and right wheels (rad/s)
- $(x, y)$ = position of robot center in world frame
- $\theta$ = heading angle of robot (rad)

**Step 1 — Linear velocity at each wheel contact point:**
The contact point of each wheel has linear velocity $v_{\text{contact}} = r \omega$.

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of left wheel:** $v_L = r\,\omega_L$ (m/s)
**Step 3 — Linear velocity of right wheel:** $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ (m/s)

**Step 4 — Linear velocity of robot center $v$** (average of both wheel velocities, since both wheels are on the same axle):

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$$

**Step 5 — Angular velocity of robot $\dot{\theta}$** (instantaneous rotation about the Instantaneous Center of Curvature ICC):

The difference $(v_R - v_L)$ produces a yaw rate. Since the two wheels are separated by $L$:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

**Step 6 — World-frame velocity components** (projecting $v$ along world x and y axes):

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$

**Step 7 — Substituting $v$ and $\dot{\theta}$**, the complete forward kinematic model is:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Non-holonomic constraint in Pfaffian form:**

Eliminate $\omega_L, \omega_R$ using the no-sideways-slip rule. A point on the axle at distance $L/2$ from the center cannot have lateral velocity. The no-slip constraint is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

In Pfaffian form $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ with $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

This is a **first-order non-holonomic constraint** — non-integrable. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Pose Composition (Chained Transformations)

**Given:** A point $P$ has coordinates $\mathbf{p}^C$ in frame $\{C\}$. Frame $\{C\}$ is described relative to $\{B\}$ by $\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}$, and frame $\{B\}$ is described relative to $\{A\}$ by $\mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}$. Find $\mathbf{p}^A$.

**Step 1 — First transform from $\{C\}$ to $\{B\}$:**
$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B = \mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^C$

**Step 2 — Then from $\{B\}$ to $\{A\}$:**
$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^A = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^A = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,(\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^C)$

**Step 4 — Group the matrices using associativity:**

$$\boxed{\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^A = \mathbf{T}_{C}^{A}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^C, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{T}_{C}^{A} = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}}$$

This is the **pose composition rule**, used heavily in KTU numerical problems involving multi-link manipulators and sensor-to-robot calibration. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coordinate Frame Relationship Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical relationship between coordinate frames, position, orientation, and the resulting pose — the conceptual architecture of representing robot position.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Frame World W] --> B[Robot Body Frame B]
    B --> C[Sensor Frame S LiDAR Camera]
    B --> D[Wheel Contact Frame L]
    B --> E[Wheel Contact Frame R]
    
    A1[Position Vector x y] --> P[Robot Pose]
    A2[Orientation Matrix R theta] --> P
    P --> P1[2D Pose SE 2 Homogeneous Transform 3x3]
    P --> P2[3D Pose SE 3 Homogeneous Transform 4x4]
    
    F1[Holonomic Constraint f of q equals 0] --> K[Kinematic Constraints]
    F2[Non-holonomic Constraint g of q dot q equals 0] --> K
    F3[Pfaffian Form A of q times dot q equals 0] --> K
    
    P1 --> G[Forward Kinematic Model]
    P2 --> G
    K --> G
    G --> H[Wheel Angular Velocities omega L omega R]
    H --> I[Robot Linear Velocity v and Angular Velocity dot theta]
    
    classDef frameStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef poseStyle fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef constraintStyle fill:#9d0208,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef outputStyle fill:#ee9b00,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    
    class A,B,C,D,E frameStyle
    class P,P1,P2,A1,A2 poseStyle
    class F1,F2,F3,K constraintStyle
    class G,H,I outputStyle
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The top flow (blue) shows the **frame hierarchy** — sensor and wheel frames are children of the body frame, which is described relative to the global frame.
- The middle flow (green) shows how **position + orientation combine** into a 2D or 3D pose using homogeneous transformation matrices.
- The red flow shows the **three mathematical forms of kinematic constraints** (holonomic, non-holonomic, Pfaffian).
- The orange flow at the bottom shows the **final kinematic output**: linear and angular velocities of the robot in the world frame.

---

## 4.2 Wheel-Ground Contact Schematic (Differential Drive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Robot Center x y theta] --> V[Linear Velocity v]
    R --> W[Angular Velocity dot theta]
    
    WL[Left Wheel radius r omega L] --> V
    WR[Right Wheel radius r omega R] --> V
    WL --> W
    WR --> W
    
    V --> M1[v equals r divided by 2 times omega L plus omega R]
    W --> M2[dot theta equals r divided by 2L times omega R minus omega L]
    
    M1 --> OUT[World Frame Velocities dot x and dot y]
    M2 --> OUT
    
    C[No-Slip Constraint dot x sin theta minus dot y cos theta equals 0] --> OUT
    
    classDef wheelStyle fill:#5a189a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef velStyle fill:#0077b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef eqStyle fill:#06a77d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef constraintStyle fill:#d62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    
    class R,WL,WR wheelStyle
    class V,W,OUT velStyle
    class M1,M2 eqStyle
    class C constraintStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two wheels' angular velocities feed into both the linear and angular velocity equations of the robot. The non-holonomic constraint acts as a **filter** on the world-frame velocities, forbidding sideways motion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the terms *pose*, *configuration*, and *degree of freedom* as applied to a field robot. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Pose (1 mark):** The pose of a robot is the complete specification of its position *and* orientation in a given reference frame. In 2D, pose is $\mathbf{q} = (x, y, \theta)^T \in SE(2)$; in 3D, pose is $\mathbf{q} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T \in SE(3)$.

- **Configuration (1 mark):** The configuration of a robot is the set of all independent parameters (generalized coordinates) that completely specify the geometric state of the robot, including the positions of all its links, wheels, and joints.

- **Degree of Freedom — DOF (1 mark):** The number of independent parameters required to fully specify the robot's configuration. For example, a free rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF**, a differential drive robot in 2D has **3 DOF**.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between *holonomic* and *non-holonomic* constraints with one example each. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Holonomic | Non-holonomic |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Mathematical form (1 mark)** | $f(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ — function of position only | $g(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = 0$ — involves velocities, **non-integrable** |
| **Effect on DOF (1 mark)** | Reduces the *reachable configurations* | Reduces only the *instantaneous velocities*, not the reachable workspace |
| **Example (1 mark)** | A pendulum constrained by a rigid rod of fixed length | A car / differential drive robot — cannot move sideways |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**A differential drive field robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. It is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (2.0, 1.0, \pi/4)$ in the world frame.**

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic model of the differential drive robot. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s, compute the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ of the robot, and then find the world-frame velocity components $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$. Verify whether the no-slip constraint is satisfied. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the robot have two independently driven wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$. Let the contact point linear velocities be $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$.

The linear velocity of the robot center is the average:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

The angular velocity about the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC):

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Projecting $v$ onto the world frame axes:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\cos\theta \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\sin\theta \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

State vector form:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ -\frac{r}{2L} & \frac{r}{2L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

This is the **forward kinematic model** of the differential drive robot. ∎

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute linear velocity $v$:**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R) = \frac{0.05}{2}(2 + 4) = 0.025 \times 6 = 0.15 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$:**

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2 \times 0.30}(4 - 2) = \frac{0.05}{0.60} \times 2 = \frac{0.10}{0.60} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute world-frame velocity components** with $\theta = \pi/4$:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.15 \times \cos(\pi/4) = 0.15 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.1061 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.15 \times \sin(\pi/4) = 0.15 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.1061 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the no-slip constraint** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = (0.15\cos\theta)\sin\theta - (0.15\sin\theta)\cos\theta$$

$$= 0.15(\cos\theta\sin\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta) = 0 \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

The constraint is satisfied, confirming the kinematic model is consistent. ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice — KTU Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a homogeneous transformation matrix, explain how the pose of a 2D field robot is represented. Given a robot with origin at $(3, 4)$ m in the world frame and heading angle $\theta = 60°$, write the corresponding transformation matrix $\mathbf{T}_{B}^{W}$ and find the world coordinates of a point $P$ located at $(1, 0)$ m in the robot frame. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define a *Pfaffian constraint*. For a unicycle-type mobile robot with state $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$, derive the Pfaffian form of the non-holonomic constraint and comment on whether it can be integrated to a holonomic form. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 2D Pose Representation and Coordinate Mapping (7 marks):**

A 2D pose is a member of $SE(2)$ represented by a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix (1 mark):

$$\mathbf{T}_{B}^{W} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Substitute $x = 3$, $y = 4$, $\theta = 60°$, so $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$ (1 mark):

$$\mathbf{T}_{B}^{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 3 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Convert point $P$ in robot frame to homogeneous form $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B = [1, 0, 1]^T$ (1 mark).

Compute world coordinates $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^W = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{W}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B$ (2 marks):

$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 3 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5(1) + (-0.866)(0) + 3(1) \\ 0.866(1) + 0.5(0) + 4(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 4.866 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore the point $P$ in world coordinates is $(x^W, y^W) = (3.5, 4.866)$ m (2 marks). ∎

---

**Part (b) — Pfaffian Form of Non-holonomic Constraint (7 marks):**

A **Pfaffian constraint** is a linear constraint in velocities of the form (1 mark):

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$$

For a unicycle mobile robot, the state vector is $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and the velocities are $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$. The unicycle cannot slide sideways — the lateral velocity in the body frame must vanish (2 marks):

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is the **rolling-without-slipping** constraint, and in Pfaffian matrix form (2 marks):

$$\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}}_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}} = 0$$

**Integrability check (2 marks):** For the constraint to be holonomic, there must exist a scalar function $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ such that $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})$ is the gradient $\nabla f$. But

$$\frac{\partial A_1}{\partial y} = 0 \neq -\cos\theta = \frac{\partial A_2}{\partial x}$$

The mixed partial derivatives are unequal, so the constraint is **non-integrable**, hence **non-holonomic**. The robot's workspace is still the entire 2D plane — it just cannot move sideways. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of angular velocity** (must be **rad/s**, not RPM). KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** per wrong unit.
> 2. **Confusing *non-holonomic* with *under-actuated***. A quadrotor is under-actuated (4 inputs, 6 DOF) but it is holonomic in position. They are not the same!
> 3. **Mixing up intrinsic vs. extrinsic Euler angles**. KTU questions use intrinsic **Z-Y-X** (Roll-Pitch-Yaw) by default.
> 4. **In pose composition $\mathbf{T}_{C}^{A} = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}$** — many students write the order reversed, leading to a completely wrong answer.
> 5. **Skipping the integrability proof** in non-holonomic constraint problems — KTU expects explicit $\partial A_i / \partial q_j \neq \partial A_j / \partial q_i$ verification for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Pose = Position + Orientation.** In 2D: $(x, y, \theta)$. In 3D: $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$.
- [x] **Rotation matrix** belongs to $SO(n)$; **Pose matrix** belongs to $SE(n)$.
- [x] 2D HTM is $3 \times 3$; 3D HTM is $4 \times 4$. Last row is always $[0, 0, ..., 1]$.
- [x] **Pose composition rule:** $\mathbf{T}_{C}^{A} = \mathbf{T}_{B}^{A}\,\mathbf{T}_{C}^{B}$ — order matters!
- [x] **Holonomic constraint** $f(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ reduces the *configuration* space.
- [x] **Non-holonomic constraint** $g(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = 0$ reduces the *velocity* space but not the *configuration* space.
- [x] **Pfaffian form:** $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ — the **null space** of $\mathbf{A}$ gives admissible velocities.
- [x] **Differential drive linear velocity:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$.
- [x] **Differential drive angular velocity:** $\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- [x] **No-slip constraint (differential drive):** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- [x] **Test for non-holonomic:** show $\frac{\partial A_i}{\partial q_j} \neq \frac{\partial A_j}{\partial q_i}$ — i.e., non-integrability.
- [x] **Units to remember:** position in **m**, angles in **rad**, angular velocity in **rad/s**, linear velocity in **m/s**.
- [x] **KTU exam-favorite topics:** (i) Derive forward kinematics of differential drive; (ii) Prove a given constraint is non-holonomic; (iii) Compose HTMs; (iv) Compute world coordinates via HTM multiplication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Kinematic Models and Constraints: Representing Robot Position.**
> Master the HTM composition rule, the differential drive kinematic equations, and the holonomic vs. non-holonomic distinction — these three are **guaranteed high-yield topics** for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward Kinematic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forward Kinematic Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forward Kinematic Model (FKM)** in the context of field robotics is the mathematical mapping that describes the robot's **pose** (position and orientation) in a global/world reference frame as a function of its **wheel velocities**, **steering angles**, and **geometric parameters** (wheel radius, wheelbase, track width). It answers the fundamental question:

> *"If I command a specific set of wheel rotational speeds to a mobile robot, what trajectory and orientation will the robot trace in the 2D plane?"*

Formally, for a mobile robot with generalized velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\dot{q}} = [v, \omega]^T$ (linear and angular velocity) or wheel control inputs $\boldsymbol{u} = [\dot{\varphi}_1, \dot{\varphi}_2, \ldots, \dot{\varphi}_n]^T$, the forward kinematic model is expressed as:

$$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = f(\boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{\theta}_{params})$$

where $\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ is the rate of change of the robot's pose in the world frame $\{W\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Forward Kinematics is the **first pillar** of mobile robot motion modeling. It is a *kinematic* (not dynamic) model — it ignores forces, masses, and torques. For field robots operating in agricultural fields, mines, and outdoor terrain, FKM forms the basis of **dead reckoning** and **odometry** before higher-level dynamic and sensor fusion layers are applied.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are steering a **shopping cart** in a supermarket. Your two hands act like the two driven wheels of a differential-drive robot:
- If **both hands push equally fast**, the cart moves straight forward.
- If **your right hand pushes faster** than the left, the cart **curves to the left**.
- If one hand pushes and the other holds, the cart **pivots** around the stationary wheel.

**Forward Kinematics** is the mathematics that, given the speed of each hand (wheel), tells you the *exact* curved path the cart (robot) takes across the floor (world frame). You don't need to know how heavy the groceries are (mass) or how hard you're pushing (force) — only the rotational speeds and the cart's wheel geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Field Metric:** The robot's pose in field robotics is almost universally represented as a vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta)$ in the Special Euclidean group $\mathbb{SE}(2)$ — the 2D pose space. The parameter $\theta$ is the **yaw angle**, measured counter-clockwise from the world X-axis.

### Classification of Field Robots by Kinematic Type

| Robot Class | Steering Principle | Key Geometric Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive** | Two independently driven wheels | Track width $L$ (or $2b$), wheel radius $r$ |
| **Ackermann (Car-like)** | Single front steering + rear drive | Wheelbase $d$, steering angle $\alpha$ |
| **Skid-Steer (e.g., agricultural tractor)** | Tracked or 4-wheel independent | Track gauge $L$, wheelbase $d$ |
| **Omnidirectional (Mecanum)** | Four Mecanum wheels at 45° | Wheel radius $r$, half-diagonal $a, b$ |
| **Tracked Field Robot** | Two parallel tracks | Track gauge $L$, sprocket radius $r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Field robots in KTU context (PCRAT601) most often deal with **non-holonomic** systems — vehicles that can move forward/backward but cannot instantly translate sideways. The **unicycle model** and **differential-drive model** are the canonical non-holonomic FKM forms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Posture / Pose Vector in 2D

The configuration of a mobile robot in a planar field is fully defined by three quantities:
- $x$ : X-coordinate of a reference point on the robot (often the center of the axle — the Instantaneous Center of Rotation, ICR) in the world frame.
- $y$ : Y-coordinate of the same reference point.
- $\theta$ : Orientation angle of the robot's forward axis (longitudinal axis) measured from the world X-axis.

This is bundled into the **pose vector**:

$$\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1} \equiv \text{Configuration Space}$$

### 2.2 General Kinematic Equation (Unicycle Template)

For virtually all field robots, the fundamental kinematic equation in the **robot frame** $\{R\}$ is:

$$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}}_{R} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v$ is the forward linear velocity and $\omega$ is the angular velocity. Transforming this into the **world frame** $\{W\}$ via the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}}_{W} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **unicycle model** — the foundational FKM of nearly all KTU field-robot problems. Memorize the structure: $\cos\theta$ couples to $\dot{x}$, $\sin\theta$ couples to $\dot{y}$, and $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ directly.

### 2.3 Why "Non-Holonomic"? — The Pfaffian Constraint

A key KTU 2024 learning outcome is recognizing that field robots obey a **non-holonomic velocity constraint** (cannot move sideways):

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is a **Pfaffian constraint** of the form $\boldsymbol{A}(\boldsymbol{\xi})\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = 0$, meaning the robot's velocity is always aligned with its forward axis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the high-yield formula bank for Forward Kinematic Models. Master these for ESE problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pose vector | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | meters, meters, radians |
| 2 | Unicycle model (world frame) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | 3 coupled ODEs |
| 3 | Differential-drive linear velocity | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)$ | $r$: wheel radius |
| 4 | Differential-drive angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$ | $L$: track width |
| 5 | ICurvature radius (diff-drive) | $R_{ICR} = \frac{L(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)}{2(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)}$ | meters; $\pm\infty$ when wheels equal |
| 6 | Ackermann linear velocity | $v = \frac{r \dot{\varphi}_r}{2}(\cos\alpha_R + \cos\alpha_F)$ | combined drive + steer |
| 7 | Ackermann steering (single) | $\tan\alpha = \frac{d}{R_{turn}}$ | $d$: wheelbase, $R_{turn}$: turn radius |
| 8 | Bicycle model FKM | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{d}\tan\alpha$ | KTU favourite |
| 9 | Tracked robot FKM | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$ | identical to diff-drive, no slip |
| 10 | Mecanum wheel velocity | omnidirectional — combine 4 rollers at 45° | decoupled $v_x, v_y, \omega$ |
| 11 | Jacobian (mobile robot) | $J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | 3×2 for unicycle |
| 12 | Holonomic constraint (non-holon.) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Pfaffian form |
| 13 | Rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | $\in SO(2)$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Sprayers / Tractors:** Use Ackermann FKM to predict spray path on rows of crops, ensuring even coverage and no overlap.
- **Autonomous Lawn Mowers & Mining Vehicles:** Differential-drive FKM is embedded in their odometry so the robot can estimate position when GPS drops out.
- **Warehouse / Hospital Field Robots:** Mecanum FKM enables lateral parking and rotation, vital in cluttered environments.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use rocker-bogie + Ackermann-style FKM to reconstruct traversable paths on rocky terrain.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The forward kinematic model is a *necessary but not sufficient* tool. In KTU's full syllabus, you will later combine FKM with **dynamic models** (forces, slip, terrain) and **sensor models** (IMU, GPS, LiDAR) for a complete field robot. FKM is the *first* layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Unicycle Model from First Principles

**Setup:** A field robot has a reference point $P$ at coordinates $(x,y)$ in the world frame, with its forward axis making angle $\theta$ with the world X-axis. The robot moves with linear velocity $v$ along its forward axis and angular velocity $\omega$ about the vertical axis.

**Step 1 — Identify the velocity vector in the robot frame $\{R\}$:**
The robot's instantaneous linear velocity is along its own $\hat{x}_R$ axis. In the robot frame, the velocity vector is:
$$\boldsymbol{v}_R = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
and the angular velocity scalar is $\omega$ about the $\hat{z}$ axis.

**Step 2 — Express the robot frame in terms of the world frame using the rotation matrix:**
The robot's forward axis unit vector in the world frame is:
$$\hat{x}_R^W = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad \hat{y}_R^W = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$
This means the rotation matrix from $\{R\}$ to $\{W\}$ is:
$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Transform the linear velocity to the world frame:**
$$\boldsymbol{v}_W = R(\theta)\,\boldsymbol{v}_R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Write the rates of change of position:**
By definition, $\boldsymbol{v}_W = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}]^T$. Therefore:
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Angular velocity is invariant under 2D rotation:**
In 2D, the orientation rate is independent of the reference frame:
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the full unicycle FKM:**
$$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Differential-Drive FKM

**Setup:** Two wheels of identical radius $r$ are mounted on a rigid axle, separated by a track width $L$ (also called $2b$ in some texts). The left wheel has rotational velocity $\dot{\varphi}_L$ and the right wheel has rotational velocity $\dot{\varphi}_R$, both measured in rad/s. We assume the wheels roll without slipping (pure rolling condition).

**Step 1 — Velocity of each wheel contact point in the robot frame:**
The contact point of the left wheel (in the robot frame) is at $(-b, 0) = (-L/2, 0)$ — wait, more carefully, the wheel center is at $(\pm b, 0)$ with $b = L/2$. Each wheel rolls without slipping, so the linear speed of the wheel's contact point along the ground is $r\dot{\varphi}$ along the wheel's rolling direction.

The linear velocity of the right wheel center in the robot frame:
$$v_R = r\dot{\varphi}_R$$
The linear velocity of the left wheel center in the robot frame:
$$v_L = r\dot{\varphi}_L$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) concept:**
The robot, being a rigid body in 2D, rotates about an instantaneous center $C$ at perpendicular distance $R_{ICR}$ from the contact point of each wheel.

**Step 3 — Relate $v_R$, $v_L$ to $R_{ICR}$ and $\omega$:**
$$v_R = \omega (R_{ICR} + b) \quad \text{and} \quad v_L = \omega (R_{ICR} - b)$$
Here $b = L/2$ is the half-track.

**Step 4 — Subtract to solve for $\omega$:**
$$v_R - v_L = \omega(R_{ICR} + b) - \omega(R_{ICR} - b) = 2b\,\omega = L\,\omega$$
$$\therefore \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Add to solve for $v$ (the robot's center velocity):**
$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R_{ICR} \Rightarrow R_{ICR} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2\omega}$$
But the robot's center linear velocity is:
$$v = \omega R_{ICR} = \omega \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{2\omega} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$
$$\therefore \quad v = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compose the full FKM using the unicycle template:**
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Bicycle (Ackermann) Model

**Setup:** A simplified car model where the front wheel can be steered by angle $\alpha$ (measured from the longitudinal axis), and the rear wheel is fixed. The wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles) is $d$. The car moves forward with velocity $v$ at the rear axle.

**Step 1 — Ackermann constraint for non-slipping front wheel:**
The velocity of the front wheel contact point must be perpendicular to the front wheel's axle. This yields the classical Ackermann relation:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{d}{R_{turn}}$$
where $R_{turn}$ is the turning radius (distance from ICR to rear axle).

**Step 2 — Express the angular velocity in terms of $v$ and $R_{turn}$:**
Since the rear axle center traces a circle of radius $R_{turn}$ about the ICR:
$$\omega = \frac{v}{R_{turn}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Ackermann relation:**
From $\tan\alpha = d / R_{turn}$, we get $R_{turn} = d / \tan\alpha$. Therefore:
$$\omega = \frac{v}{d/\tan\alpha} = \frac{v}{d}\tan\alpha$$
$$\therefore \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{d}\tan\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Compose the full bicycle-model FKM:**
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{d}\tan\alpha \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compact matrix form of bicycle model FKM:**
> $$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{d} & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\alpha \;\text{? (single input is }v\text{)}$$
> More cleanly, with control input $\boldsymbol{u} = [v, \alpha]^T$:
> $$\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{d} & \dfrac{v}{d\cos^2\alpha} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of FKM for KTU Problems

| Field Robot | Input Vector $\boldsymbol{u}$ | Output Pose Rate $\boldsymbol{\dot{\xi}}$ | When Used in KTU Paper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle (abstract) | $[v, \omega]^T$ | $[\cos\theta\cdot v, \sin\theta\cdot v, \omega]^T$ | Conceptual / ESE theory |
| Differential Drive | $[\dot{\varphi}_R, \dot{\varphi}_L]^T$ | Combined via $v,\omega$ formulae | Most ESE numerical problems |
| Bicycle (Ackermann) | $[v, \alpha]^T$ | $[\cos\theta\cdot v, \sin\theta\cdot v, (v/d)\tan\alpha]^T$ | Car / tractor problems |
| Tracked Robot | $[\dot{\varphi}_R, \dot{\varphi}_L, \dot{\varphi}_{track}]^T$ | Diff-drive equivalent | Mining / terrain robots |
| Mecanum (4-wheel) | $[\dot{\varphi}_1, \dot{\varphi}_2, \dot{\varphi}_3, \dot{\varphi}_4]^T$ | Decoupled $v_x, v_y, \omega$ | Omnidirectional problems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forward Kinematic Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CONTROL INPUT VECTOR u"] --> B["INPUT MODULE\nWheel Angular Velocities or v, omega"]
    B --> C["GEOMETRIC PARAMETER MODULE\nWheel Radius r, Track Width L, Wheelbase d"]
    C --> D["KINO GEOMETRIC EQUATION SOLVER\nApply Pure Rolling and No-Slip Constraints"]
    D --> E["ROBOT FRAME VELOCITY VECTOR\nv_R, omega_R"]
    E --> F["ROBOT TO WORLD ROTATION MATRIX\nR of theta = cos -sin / sin cos"]
    F --> G["WORLD FRAME POSE RATE dxi dt\nx dot, y dot, theta dot"]
    G --> H{"INTEGRATION BLOCK\nEuler or RK4"}
    H --> I["GLOBAL POSE xi of t\nx of t, y of t, theta of t"]
    I --> J["FEEDBACK TO PATH PLANNER\nand Mapping Module"]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style H fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
```

### 4.2 Differential-Drive Kinematic Geometry (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["phi_R dot\nRight Wheel Speed"] --> C["v equals r over 2 times phi_R + phi_L"]
    B["phi_L dot\nLeft Wheel Speed"] --> C
    A --> D["omega equals r over L times phi_R minus phi_L"]
    B --> D
    C --> E["FORWARD VELOCITY v\nin robot frame"]
    D --> F["ANGULAR VELOCITY omega\nabout z axis"]
    E --> G["R of theta Block"]
    F --> H["Direct Pass Through"]
    G --> I["x dot and y dot in WORLD frame"]
    H --> J["theta dot equals omega"]
    I --> K["POSE UPDATE\nxi t plus delta t"]
    J --> K
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#fff59d
    style D fill:#fff59d
    style K fill:#a5d6a7
```

### 4.3 Ackermann Bicycle Model Geometry Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["v : Forward Velocity\nat Rear Axle"] --> B["v cos theta\nX rate in World"]
    A --> C["v sin theta\nY rate in World"]
    A --> D["v tan alpha over d\nYaw Rate theta dot"]
    E["alpha : Steering Angle"] --> D
    B --> F["STATE VECTOR dxi dt"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G["INTEGRATOR\nxi t"]

    style E fill:#c5cae9
    style D fill:#ffe082
    style H fill:#b2dfdb
```

### 4.4 Pose Frame Schematic (Robot vs. World)

```
        WORLD FRAME {W}                        ROBOT FRAME {R}
        +Y ↑                                  +Y_R ↑
           |                                     |
           |   _______                           |       θ
           |  /   x   \                          |      ↗
           | /  (x,y,θ) → P                      |     /
           |/          / \  +X                   |    / +X_R
           +---------/----→                     +---+
                  +X

    Legend:
    - {W} : Global / Inertial / World frame (fixed in field)
    - {R} : Body / Robot frame attached to the vehicle
    - P   : Reference point (often midpoint of driven axle)
    - θ   : Yaw angle from world +X to robot +X_R (counter-clockwise +ve)
    - The transformation between frames is the rotation R(θ) ∈ SO(2)
```
