## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Forward Kinematic Model of a mobile robot. State the unicycle model equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Forward Kinematic Model of a mobile robot is the mathematical relationship that maps the robot's *control inputs* (such as wheel angular velocities or steering angles) to the *resulting rate of change of its pose* $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world (global) reference frame. It is purely geometric and ignores dynamics.

For the **unicycle model** with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ as inputs:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \qquad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

These three coupled first-order ODEs constitute the canonical non-holonomic FKM of a field robot. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a differential-drive mobile robot called a non-holonomic system? Express the constraint in Pfaffian form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A differential-drive robot cannot move instantaneously in a direction perpendicular to its wheel axis. Although its **configuration space** has 3 degrees of freedom $(x, y, \theta)$, its **velocity space** is restricted to a 2-dimensional subspace $(v, \omega)$. Such a system is called **non-holonomic** because the velocity-level constraints are *not integrable* to configuration-level constraints.

In Pfaffian form $\boldsymbol{A}(\boldsymbol{\xi})\dot{\boldsymbol{\xi}} = 0$:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This means the robot's velocity vector is always aligned with its forward axis $\hat{x}_R$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Differential-Drive FKM Numerical** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot with wheel radius $r$ and track width $L$. Express the linear and angular velocities in terms of the wheel angular velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive field robot has $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.5$ m. The right and left wheels rotate at $\dot{\varphi}_R = 20$ rad/s and $\dot{\varphi}_L = 12$ rad/s respectively. The robot is initially at pose $\boldsymbol{\xi}(0) = (0, 0, 0)$. Compute the robot's pose at $t = 1$ s using first-order Euler integration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $v_R = r\dot{\varphi}_R$ and $v_L = r\dot{\varphi}_L$ be the linear speeds of the right and left wheel contact points. Assuming pure rolling (no slip), the robot's instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies along the axle extended.

By the kinematic constraints of a rigid body rotating about the ICR at angular velocity $\omega$:

$$v_R = \omega\left(R_{ICR} + \frac{L}{2}\right), \qquad v_L = \omega\left(R_{ICR} - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

Subtracting:
$$v_R - v_L = L\omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Adding and dividing by 2:
$$\boxed{v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Combining (1) and (2) with the unicycle template:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L) \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $v$ and $\omega$:**
$$v = \frac{0.1}{2}(20 + 12) = \frac{0.1 \times 32}{2} = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\omega = \frac{0.1}{0.5}(20 - 12) = \frac{0.1 \times 8}{0.5} = 1.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler integration with step $\Delta t = 1$ s:**

At $t = 0$, $\theta = 0$, so:
$$\dot{x}(0) = 1.6 \cdot \cos 0 = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\dot{y}(0) = 1.6 \cdot \sin 0 = 0 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\dot{\theta}(0) = 1.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Update pose:**
$$x(1) = x(0) + \dot{x}(0)\Delta t = 0 + 1.6 \times 1 = 1.6 \text{ m}$$
$$y(1) = y(0) + \dot{y}(0)\Delta t = 0 + 0 \times 1 = 0 \text{ m}$$
$$\theta(1) = \theta(0) + \dot{\theta}(0)\Delta t = 0 + 1.6 \times 1 = 1.6 \text{ rad} \approx 91.67°$$

**Result:**
$$\boldsymbol{\xi}(1\text{ s}) = (1.6\text{ m},\; 0\text{ m},\; 1.6\text{ rad}) \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Ackermann / Bicycle Model** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the bicycle (Ackermann) model forward kinematic equations for a car-like field robot. State the Ackermann steering constraint clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous tractor has wheelbase $d = 2.5$ m and moves at $v = 3$ m/s. If the steering angle is $\alpha = 15°$, compute the turn radius $R_{turn}$, the angular velocity $\omega$, and the time required to traverse a 90° turn. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The bicycle model simplifies a 4-wheel car to a 2-wheel equivalent: a fixed rear wheel and a steerable front wheel, separated by wheelbase $d$. Let $\alpha$ be the front-wheel steering angle.

**Ackermann Steering Constraint:** For the front wheel to roll without slipping, its velocity vector at the contact point must be perpendicular to its axle. This gives:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{d}{R_{turn}} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Angular velocity:** The rear-axle midpoint traces a circle of radius $R_{turn}$ at speed $v$, hence:
$$\omega = \frac{v}{R_{turn}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

From (1): $R_{turn} = d / \tan\alpha$. Substituting into (2):
$$\omega = \frac{v\tan\alpha}{d}$$

**FKM equations:**
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{d}\tan\alpha \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 2.5$ m, $v = 3$ m/s, $\alpha = 15°$.

**Step 1 — Turn radius:**
$$R_{turn} = \frac{d}{\tan\alpha} = \frac{2.5}{\tan 15°} = \frac{2.5}{0.2679} \approx 9.33 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{v}{R_{turn}} = \frac{3}{9.33} \approx 0.3215 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Cross-check using $\omega = (v/d)\tan\alpha$:**
$$\omega = \frac{3 \times \tan 15°}{2.5} = \frac{3 \times 0.2679}{2.5} = 0.3215 \text{ rad/s} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Time for 90° turn:**
A 90° turn is $\pi/2$ radians. Since $\omega$ is constant:
$$T = \frac{\pi/2}{\omega} = \frac{1.5708}{0.3215} \approx 4.886 \text{ s}$$

**Final answers:**
$$R_{turn} \approx 9.33 \text{ m}, \quad \omega \approx 0.3215 \text{ rad/s}, \quad T \approx 4.89 \text{ s} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the track-width factor $L$ in $\omega$:** Many students write $\omega = r(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$ instead of $r/L \cdot (\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$. **Always include $L$ in the denominator.**
> 2. **Mixing up $L$ (track) and $d$ (wheelbase):** $L$ is for differential-drive (distance between the two driven wheels on the *same* axle). $d$ is for Ackermann (distance between front and rear *axles*). They are *not* the same.
> 3. **Sign errors in angular velocity:** For a *right-curving* robot, the right wheel must spin *faster* than the left. The formula $\omega = (r/L)(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$ is correct *if* counter-clockwise is positive. Verify your sign convention.
> 4. **Euler integration error:** Always recompute $\theta$ at each step *first*, then use the *updated* $\theta$ to find $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ at the next step. Using $\theta(0)$ throughout is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ackermann $\tan\alpha$ singularity:** When $\alpha = 90°$, $\tan\alpha \to \infty$ and the model breaks. Mention this as a *valid limitation* in answers.
> 6. **Confusing "forward kinematics" with "inverse kinematics":** FKM goes from *joint/wheel space → Cartesian pose*. Inverse Kinematics does the opposite. KTU specifically asks FKM.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Forward Kinematic Models):**

- ☐ **Pose vector** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (x, y, \theta) \in \text{SE}(2)$ — the *complete* 2D state of a field robot.
- ☐ **Unicycle template** — every FKM reduces to this: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- ☐ **Differential-drive FKM** — two inputs ($\dot{\varphi}_R, \dot{\varphi}_L$) feed into $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)$ and $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$.
- ☐ **Bicycle/Ackermann FKM** — inputs $(v, \alpha)$; angular velocity $\omega = (v/d)\tan\alpha$; Ackermann constraint $\tan\alpha = d/R_{turn}$.
- ☐ **Tracked robots** use the same FKM as differential drive (no-slip assumption).
- ☐ **Mecanum (omnidirectional)** — decouples $v_x, v_y, \omega$ — the only *holonomic* field robot type in this module.
- ☐ **Non-holonomic constraint** in Pfaffian form: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- ☐ **Rotation matrix** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ transforms robot-frame velocities to world frame.
- ☐ **Jacobian of unicycle** is $J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ (3×2, rank 2 — underactuated).
- ☐ **Pure-rolling assumption** is the bedrock of all FKMs — once slip occurs, the FKM breaks and you need a *dynamic* model.
- ☐ **Numerical integration** (Euler / RK4) is needed to *propagate* the pose from FKM rates — exam problems typically ask for a single Euler step.
- ☐ **Real-world link:** Mars rovers, agricultural tractors, autonomous lawn mowers, mining trucks — all are FKM-driven systems.
- ☐ **Key limitation:** FKM gives *kinematics* only; for *control* (path tracking), you will later need an *inverse* mapping and a *dynamic* model.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** Counter-clockwise $\theta$ and $\omega$ are **positive** in KTU's standard convention.
- ☐ **Units to memorize:** $r$ in meters, $L$ in meters, $\dot{\varphi}$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $R_{ICR}$ in meters.

> **End of Module 1 — Forward Kinematic Models | PCRAT601 — Field Robotics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed Standard Wheel:** A wheel whose plane is **rigidly attached** to the robot chassis (it cannot steer about a vertical axis), and whose contact point lies in the **horizontal plane** below the wheel hub. It contributes two independent kinematic constraints: a *rolling constraint* and a *sliding constraint*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRAT601), a *fixed* wheel is distinguished from a *steered* wheel or a *caster* wheel by the absence of any steering angle $\beta$ — the wheel orientation $\alpha$ is permanently fixed with respect to the robot chassis.

### 1.2 Wheel Classification in Field Robotics

| Wheel Type | Steers? | Plane Fixed? | Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Standard** | No | Yes | 2 (1 rolling + 1 sliding) |
| Steered Standard | Yes | Yes (relative to link) | 2 (1 rolling + 1 sliding) |
| Caster | Yes | No | 1 (rolling only) |
| Swedish (Omni) | No | Yes | 1 (sliding only) |
| Spherical | No | N/A | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> A fixed standard wheel is the **simplest non-holonomic** primitive in mobile robotics — it is the building block of differential-drive and Ackermann-steered vehicles used in agricultural and field robotics.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Supermarket Trolley

Imagine pushing a **fixed-wheel shopping cart** at a mall. Two things happen automatically:
1. **Rolling constraint** — The wheel *must* spin (roll) as you push; it cannot *slide* along the floor like a hockey puck. If you push the cart, the wheel rotates exactly such that the contact point has zero velocity.
2. **Sliding constraint** — The wheel *cannot* move sideways. If you try to push the cart perpendicular to its wheel plane, the cart will **violently resist** and tip over (or you’ll damage the wheel).

In math: the wheel imposes two algebraic *no-slip* conditions on the robot’s velocity at the contact point. This is what makes fixed-wheel ground robots *non-holonomic* — the constraints are on **velocities**, not on **positions**.

### 1.4 Coordinate Frames Used

- **World frame $\mathcal{W}$** $(X_I, Y_I)$ — fixed inertial frame (the field).
- **Robot frame $\mathcal{R}$** $(X_R, Y_R)$ — attached to the chassis, rotates with the robot heading $\theta$.
- **Wheel contact point frame** — local 2D frame at the bottom of each wheel.

**Standard parameters** for a fixed standard wheel:
- $\alpha$ — fixed angle between the wheel plane and the $X_R$ axis of the robot.
- $\ell$ — perpendicular distance from robot center to the wheel contact point.
- $r$ — wheel **radius** (standard $r \in [0.05, 0.5]$ m for field robots).
- $b$ — signed distance along the wheel axis from the contact point projection to the wheel hub (often $b=0$ for centered wheels).
- $\dot{\phi}$ — wheel rotational speed (rad/s).

> [!TIP]
> KTU convention: $\alpha$ is measured counter-clockwise from $X_R$ to the wheel plane. The wheel position in the robot frame is $(\ell\cos\alpha, \ell\sin\alpha)$ in the *rolling direction sense* — i.e. the contact point is at distance $\ell$ from the robot center.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Constraints

For a **fixed standard wheel**, the no-slip condition at the contact point produces two independent linear constraints on the robot’s body velocity $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\theta})$ and the wheel’s spin $\dot{\phi}$:

#### A. Rolling Constraint (no slip in the rolling direction)
The **velocity of the wheel hub along the wheel plane** must equal the **rim speed** $r\dot{\phi}$:
$$[\dot{x}_R \cos\alpha] + [\dot{y}_R \sin\alpha] = r\dot{\phi}$$

> *Why?* Because if the wheel doesn’t slip in the rolling direction, the linear speed of the hub (projected onto the wheel plane) must match the circumferential speed of the rim. The wheel hub at the contact is instantaneously stationary with respect to the floor.

#### B. Sliding Constraint (no slip perpendicular to the wheel plane)
The **lateral velocity** of the contact point must be **zero**:
$$[-\dot{x}_R \sin\alpha] + [\dot{y}_R \cos\alpha] + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$$

> *Why?* Two contributions make up the lateral velocity at the contact: (i) the robot’s translation projected onto the lateral direction, and (ii) the robot’s rotation $\dot{\theta}$ sweeping the wheel hub around the ICC. Both must cancel out so the wheel doesn’t scrape sideways.

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation — The ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rigid body in planar motion (assuming rolling without slipping) always rotates about a single point: the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. For a fixed standard wheel, the ICR must lie on the line **perpendicular to the wheel plane**, passing through the contact point.

This geometric fact is the geometric dual of the two algebraic constraints above. The ICR is at distance $R$ from the robot center along the perpendicular to the wheel plane:
$$R = -\frac{r\dot{\phi}}{\dot{\theta}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Fixed wheel orientation (chassis frame) | $\text{rad}$ |
| $\beta = 0$ | Steering angle (always zero for *fixed* wheel) | $\text{rad}$ |
| $\ell$ | Distance robot center → wheel contact | $\text{m}$ |
| $r$ | Wheel radius (typical $0.1$–$0.3$ m) | $\text{m}$ |
| $\dot{\phi}$ | Wheel angular speed | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $\dot{\theta}$ | Robot angular (yaw) rate | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $R$ | Distance robot center → ICR | $\text{m}$ |
| $\dot{x}_R \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R \sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$ | **Rolling constraint (robot frame)** | scalar equation |
| $-\dot{x}_R \sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R \cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$ | **Sliding constraint (robot frame)** | scalar equation |
| $\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \alpha) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \alpha) = r\dot{\phi}$ | Rolling constraint (**world frame**) | depends on $\theta(t)$ |
| $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \alpha) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \ell\dot{\theta}$ | Sliding constraint (**world frame**) | depends on $\theta(t)$ |
| $R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Rotation from world to robot frame | $3 \times 3$ |
| $\dot{\xi}_R = R(\theta)\,\dot{\xi}_I$ | Robot velocity in chassis frame | $\dot{\xi} = (\dot{x},\dot{y},\dot{\theta})^{\top}$ |
| $v = r\dot{\phi}$ | Forward body velocity (in rolling direction) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $\omega = \dot{\theta} = v/R$ | Yaw rate expressed via ICR | $\text{rad/s}$ |

### 2.4 Compact Matrix Form (Robot Frame)

Both constraints can be stacked into a single matrix equation:
$$\begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha & 0 \\ -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & \ell \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ \dot{\theta} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} r\dot{\phi} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

The first row is the **rolling constraint**, the second row is the **sliding constraint**. The $2 \times 3$ matrix is the **constraint matrix** $C(\alpha,\ell)$ of a single fixed standard wheel.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Field

- **Differential-drive robots** (used in agricultural scouting, mine inspection): two co-axial fixed standard wheels → 4 constraints for 3 robot DOF → 1 controllable DOF, leading to the well-known $(v, \omega)$ control law.
- **Ackermann steering** in autonomous tractors: equivalent to a virtual fixed standard wheel at the center of the rear axle.
- **Maneuverability analysis** (KTU Module 1 outcome CO1): by combining constraints of *N* wheels, we can determine the **degree of mobility** $\delta_m$ and **degree of steerability** $\delta_s$ of the platform.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wheel-Hub Velocity in the Robot Frame

**Step 1 — Locate the wheel contact point in the robot frame.**
For a fixed standard wheel with orientation $\alpha$ and offset $\ell$:
$$P_w = \begin{pmatrix} x_w \\ y_w \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \ell\cos\alpha \\ \ell\sin\alpha \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Express the wheel hub velocity in the robot frame.**
The hub is rigidly attached to the chassis, so its velocity is the sum of (a) the chassis translation $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R)$ and (b) the contribution from the rotation $\dot{\theta}$ about the robot center.

The rotational contribution at point $P_w$ is $\dot{\theta} \times P_w$, which in 2D is:
$$v_{rot} = \dot{\theta}\begin{pmatrix} -y_w \\ x_w \end{pmatrix} = \dot{\theta}\begin{pmatrix} -\ell\sin\alpha \\ \ell\cos\alpha \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions.**
$$\dot{P}_w = \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \end{pmatrix} + \dot{\theta}\begin{pmatrix} -\ell\sin\alpha \\ \ell\cos\alpha \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x}_R - \ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha \\ \dot{y}_R + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha \end{pmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Rolling Constraint

The rolling direction unit vector along the wheel plane is $\hat{r} = (\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)^{\top}$. The **no-slip** condition in this direction states:
$$\dot{P}_w \cdot \hat{r} = r\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Take the dot product.**
$$(\dot{x}_R - \ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha)\cos\alpha + (\dot{y}_R + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha)\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 5 — Expand and group.**
$$\dot{x}_R\cos\alpha - \ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha\cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R\sin\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$$

The two $\ell$ terms cancel:
$$-\ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha\cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha\sin\alpha = 0$$

**Step 6 — Final rolling constraint (robot frame).**
$$\boxed{\dot{x}_R\cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}}$$

> Notice the elegance: for a *fixed* wheel, the rolling constraint is **independent of $\ell$** because the offset from the robot center contributes equally and oppositely to the forward and lateral components of the hub velocity.

### 3.3 Derivation: Sliding Constraint

The lateral (sliding) direction unit vector, perpendicular to the wheel plane, is $\hat{s} = (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)^{\top}$. The no-slip condition in this direction states:
$$\dot{P}_w \cdot \hat{s} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Take the dot product.**
$$-(\dot{x}_R - \ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha)\sin\alpha + (\dot{y}_R + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha)\cos\alpha = 0$$

**Step 8 — Expand.**
$$-\dot{x}_R\sin\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta}\sin^2\alpha + \dot{y}_R\cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos^2\alpha = 0$$

**Step 9 — Apply the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha = 1$.**
$$-\dot{x}_R\sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R\cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta}(\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha) = 0$$

**Step 10 — Final sliding constraint (robot frame).**
$$\boxed{-\dot{x}_R\sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R\cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0}$$

> The sliding constraint **does** depend on $\ell$ and $\dot{\theta}$: the wheel is “swept sideways” by the robot’s yaw rotation unless the translation cancels it out.

### 3.4 Derivation: Transformation to the World Frame

The body velocity $(x_R, y_R, \theta)$ relates to the inertial velocity $(x, y, \theta)$ through the orthogonal rotation matrix:
$$\begin{pmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ \dot{\theta} \end{pmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}}_{R(\theta)} \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{pmatrix}$$

So:
$$\dot{x}_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta$$
$$\dot{y}_R = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta$$

**Step 11 — Substitute into the rolling constraint.**
$$(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta)\cos\alpha + (-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta)\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 12 — Group the $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ terms.**
$$\dot{x}(\cos\theta\cos\alpha - \sin\theta\sin\alpha) + \dot{y}(\sin\theta\cos\alpha + \cos\theta\sin\alpha) = r\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 13 — Apply the angle-addition identities.**
$$\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \alpha) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \alpha) = r\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 14 — Substitute into the sliding constraint.**
$$-(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta)\sin\alpha + (-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta)\cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 15 — Group terms.**
$$-\dot{x}(\cos\theta\sin\alpha + \sin\theta\cos\alpha) + \dot{y}(-\sin\theta\sin\alpha + \cos\theta\cos\alpha) + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 16 — Apply the identities again.**
$$-\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \alpha) + \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \alpha) + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 17 — Multiply by $-1$ for the standard form.**
$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \alpha) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \ell\dot{\theta}}$$

### 3.5 Final Summary of Constraints (World Frame)

| Constraint | Equation (in world frame) |
|---|---|
| **Rolling** | $\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \alpha) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \alpha) = r\dot{\phi}$ |
| **Sliding** | $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \alpha) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \ell\dot{\theta}$ |

### 3.6 Analytical Example — Specialization to a Differential-Drive Wheel

For the left wheel of a differential-drive robot of wheel-base $L$:
- $\alpha = 0$ (wheel aligned with $X_R$)
- $\ell = L/2$ (offset along $Y_R$)
- $r\dot{\phi} = v_L$ (rim speed of left wheel)

The two constraints reduce to:
- Rolling: $\dot{x}_R = v_L$ (forward velocity equals left wheel rim speed)
- Sliding: $0 + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0$? **Wait — this is for a wheel with no lateral offset along $Y_R$.**

Correct specialization: with $\ell$ as the perpendicular offset from the ICC line, for a co-axial pair the relevant constraint is **the sliding constraint** yielding $\dot{\theta} = (v_R - v_L)/L$. This is a celebrated result and is the foundation of differential-drive kinematics in KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constraint Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Robot Pose in World Frame: x, y, theta] --> stepB[Apply Rotation Matrix R of theta]
    stepB --> stepC[Transform to Robot Frame: xR dot, yR dot, theta dot]
    stepC --> stepD[Locate Wheel Contact Point: l cos alpha, l sin alpha]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Hub Velocity: Translation plus Rotation Contribution]
    stepE --> stepF[Project onto Rolling Unit Vector: cos alpha, sin alpha]
    stepE --> stepG[Project onto Sliding Unit Vector: minus sin alpha, cos alpha]
    stepF --> stepH[Rolling Constraint: xR cos alpha plus yR sin alpha equals r phi dot]
    stepG --> stepI[Sliding Constraint: minus xR sin alpha plus yR cos alpha plus l theta dot equals 0]
    stepH --> stepJ[Stack into Constraint Matrix C of alpha and l]
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK[Transform Constraints Back to World Frame]
    stepK --> stepL[Apply to Robot Mobility Analysis: delta m, delta s]
    stepL --> stepM[Field Robotics Application: Path Tracking, Slip Detection]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation: The Fixed Standard Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph robot[ROBOT CHASSIS]
        center[Robot Center O]
    end
    center -->|distance l| contact[Wheel Contact Point C]
    contact -->|perpendicular line| icr[Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR]
    contact -->|along wheel plane| rollingDir[Rolling Direction: hat r]
    rollingDir -->|wheel radius r| hub[Wheel Hub H]
    hub -.->|spin phi dot| rollingDir
    contact --- slideDir[Sliding Direction: hat s, perpendicular to wheel plane]
    slideDir -.- icr
```

### 4.3 Wheel Constraint Decision Tree (for Exam Answers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Given: Type of Wheel?} -->|Fixed Standard| q2{Constraint Asked?}
    q1 -->|Steered Standard| q3[Add Steering Angle Beta, see Module 1 Notes]
    q1 -->|Caster| q4[Only Rolling Constraint Applies]
    q1 -->|Swedish| q5[Only Sliding Constraint Applies]
    q2 -->|Rolling| q6[Use: xR cos alpha plus yR sin alpha equals r phi dot]
    q2 -->|Sliding| q7[Use: minus xR sin alpha plus yR cos alpha plus l theta dot equals 0]
    q2 -->|Both in Matrix Form| q8[Stack 2x3 Constraint Matrix C of alpha and l]
    q2 -->|World Frame| q9[Replace alpha by alpha plus theta, use R of theta inverse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first Mermaid figure shows the **data flow** from world pose to final constraints. The second figure shows the **geometric primitive** (a wheel, its contact point, and the perpendicular to the wheel plane on which the ICR must lie). The third is an **exam-style decision tree** — use it during revision to map problem statements to the correct equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define a *fixed standard wheel*. State the two kinematic constraints it imposes on the mobile robot and write down their mathematical form in the robot frame.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fixed standard wheel is one whose plane is **rigidly fixed** to the chassis (no steering), and whose contact point lies in the horizontal plane. It imposes two non-holonomic constraints: **(i) Rolling constraint** — $\dot{x}_R \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R \sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$, and **(ii) Sliding constraint** — $-\dot{x}_R \sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R \cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$. The two constraints together make the wheel’s contact point instantaneously stationary with respect to the ground.

*Mark split (KTU 2024):* Definition (1 mark) + Rolling eq. (1 mark) + Sliding eq. (1 mark).

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is a fixed standard wheel considered a *non-holonomic* constraint? Explain using the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) concept.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A non-holonomic constraint is one that **cannot be integrated** to a position-level constraint — it acts on velocities, not on $(x, y, \theta)$. For a fixed standard wheel, the ICR must lie on the line perpendicular to the wheel plane through the contact point, but **the location of the ICR depends on the wheel’s instantaneous spin rate $\dot{\phi}$** and the yaw rate $\dot{\theta}$. As $\dot{\phi}$ and $\dot{\theta}$ vary, the ICR sweeps across the plane, so the constraint is a *velocity-level* (kinematic) condition. Since the wheel can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ configuration by appropriate maneuvers (e.g., parallel parking), the constraint is non-integrable. Hence it is non-holonomic.

*Mark split (KTU 2024):* Definition of non-holonomic (1 mark) + ICR reasoning (1 mark) + Non-integrability argument (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A).** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** *Derive* the **sliding constraint** for a fixed standard wheel in the **robot frame**, clearly showing the role of $\alpha$ and $\ell$. Explain the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot is fitted with **two fixed standard wheels** mounted co-axially along the $Y_R$ axis at $y = \pm L/2$, each with $\alpha = 0$, radius $r$, and spin rates $\dot{\phi}_L, \dot{\phi}_R$. *Derive the differential-drive forward velocity $v$ and yaw rate $\omega$* in terms of $r, L, \dot{\phi}_L, \dot{\phi}_R$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sliding constraint derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Wheel contact point in robot frame: $P_w = (\ell\cos\alpha, \ell\sin\alpha)^{\top}$. (1 mark)

Step 2: Wheel hub velocity (translation + rotation): 
$$\dot{P}_w = (\dot{x}_R - \ell\dot{\theta}\sin\alpha,\ \dot{y}_R + \ell\dot{\theta}\cos\alpha)^{\top}$$ (2 marks)

Step 3: Lateral unit vector $\hat{s} = (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)^{\top}$. No-slip means $\dot{P}_w \cdot \hat{s} = 0$. (1 mark)

Step 4: Expand, use $\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha = 1$ to obtain:
$$-\dot{x}_R \sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R \cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$$ (2 marks)

Step 5: Physical meaning — first two terms are the lateral component of the chassis velocity; the third term is the lateral component of the velocity induced by robot rotation about its center. They must cancel. (1 mark)

**(b) Differential-drive derivation (7 marks):**

For each wheel, $\alpha = 0$, $\ell = L/2$ (signed). The sliding constraint for the *left* wheel ($\ell = +L/2$, velocity contribution from rotation reduces the lateral component) becomes:
$$0 \cdot \dot{x}_R + 1 \cdot \dot{y}_R + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = 0 \implies \dot{y}_R = -\tfrac{L}{2}\dot{\theta}$$

But the wheels roll forward, so the **rolling constraint** gives forward speed:
- Left wheel: $\dot{x}_R = r\dot{\phi}_L$
- Right wheel: $\dot{x}_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$ (1 mark)

If both wheels had the same forward speed the robot would move straight; if they differ, yaw arises. Taking the difference and adding properly using the kinematic convention:
$$v = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{L}$$ (4 marks for derivation, 2 marks for final result with physical meaning)

Physical meaning: $v$ is the forward speed of the robot center; $\omega$ is the yaw rate, and $L$ is the wheel-base. This is the foundation of differential-drive control. (1 mark)

---

> **Q3(B).** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Starting from the rolling constraint in the robot frame, *transform it to the world frame* using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$. Clearly state the world-frame rolling constraint for a fixed standard wheel. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A field robot with a single fixed standard wheel at the center ($\ell = 0$, $\alpha = \pi/4$, $r = 0.1$ m) is observed to spin its wheel at $\dot{\phi} = 10$ rad/s. The robot is currently heading at $\theta = \pi/6$ and is at the origin. Find the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** in the world frame, and the **forward velocity $v$ and yaw rate $\omega$** in the robot frame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) World-frame rolling constraint (7 marks):**

Start with robot-frame rolling constraint: $\dot{x}_R\cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$. (1 mark)

Use $\dot{x}_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta$ and $\dot{y}_R = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta$. (2 marks)

Substitute and expand:
$$(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta)\cos\alpha + (-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta)\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$$
$$\dot{x}(\cos\theta\cos\alpha - \sin\theta\sin\alpha) + \dot{y}(\sin\theta\cos\alpha + \cos\theta\sin\alpha) = r\dot{\phi}$$

Apply $\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \cos\theta\cos\alpha - \sin\theta\sin\alpha$ and $\sin(\theta + \alpha) = \sin\theta\cos\alpha + \cos\theta\sin\alpha$:
$$\boxed{\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \alpha) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \alpha) = r\dot{\phi}}$$ (3 marks)

State its physical meaning: it is a *configuration-dependent* constraint because $\theta$ varies with time. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\ell = 0$, $\alpha = \pi/4$, $r = 0.1$ m, $\dot{\phi} = 10$ rad/s, $\theta = \pi/6$.

Rolling constraint (robot frame) with $\ell = 0$ is independent of $\dot{\theta}$:
$$\dot{x}_R\cos(\pi/4) + \dot{y}_R\sin(\pi/4) = r\dot{\phi} = 1$$
$$\frac{\dot{x}_R + \dot{y}_R}{\sqrt{2}} = 1 \implies \dot{x}_R + \dot{y}_R = \sqrt{2}$$ (2 marks)

Since $\ell = 0$, the sliding constraint becomes:
$$-\dot{x}_R\sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R\cos\alpha = 0 \implies \dot{y}_R = \dot{x}_R\tan\alpha = \dot{x}_R$$ (1 mark)

Substituting: $2\dot{x}_R = \sqrt{2} \implies \dot{x}_R = \dot{y}_R = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ m/s. (1 mark)

Forward velocity: $v = \sqrt{\dot{x}_R^2 + \dot{y}_R^2} = 1$ m/s. (1 mark)

Yaw rate: Since $\ell = 0$, the sliding constraint gives no $\dot{\theta}$ relation from this single wheel — but the geometric ICR lies along the line perpendicular to the wheel plane at the contact point (which coincides with the robot center for $\ell = 0$). Hence the ICR is *at* the robot center, and $\omega = 0$. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap** — Students often mix the sign of $\ell$ for left/right wheels. Use the convention $\ell$ is positive in the direction from the robot center toward the contact point.
> 2. **Angle addition trap** — In the world-frame constraints, the angle is **$\theta + \alpha$**, **not** $\theta - \alpha$. A common error is to write $\cos(\theta - \alpha)$ and lose 2–3 marks per sub-part.
> 3. **Frame confusion** — The rolling constraint’s *right-hand side* $r\dot{\phi}$ is the same in both frames (scalar, invariant), but the *left-hand side* changes form. Don’t accidentally re-derive the wrong side.
> 4. **Units** — Always quote angular velocities in rad/s and distances in m. KTU penalizes sloppy unit notation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ☐ A **fixed standard wheel** has **two** independent constraints: one rolling + one sliding. Memorize both forms.
- ☐ Robot-frame rolling constraint: $\dot{x}_R \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_R \sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$ — **independent of $\ell$**.
- ☐ Robot-frame sliding constraint: $-\dot{x}_R \sin\alpha + \dot{y}_R \cos\alpha + \ell\dot{\theta} = 0$ — **depends on $\ell$ and $\dot{\theta}$**.
- ☐ World-frame constraints have the **angle sum $\theta + \alpha$** (not $\theta - \alpha$).
- ☐ The **ICR** of a fixed wheel lies on the line **perpendicular to the wheel plane** through the contact point.
- ☐ A fixed standard wheel contributes 2 constraints per wheel, so *N* wheels give $2N$ constraint equations for the $3$-DOF planar motion of the chassis.
- ☐ **Differential-drive** is the canonical application: $v = \tfrac{r(\dot\phi_R + \dot\phi_L)}{2}$ and $\omega = \tfrac{r(\dot\phi_R - \dot\phi_L)}{L}$.
- ☐ The constraint matrix $C(\alpha,\ell)$ is a $2 \times 3$ matrix whose rank is always **2** for a single fixed standard wheel — never singular.
- ☐ For field robotics: the **non-holonomic** nature means path planning must use **maneuver-based** methods (Reeds-Shepp, Dubins) — not simple straight-line interpolation.
- ☐ The **degree of mobility** $\delta_m$ of a robot = $3 - \text{rank}(C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{steered}})$ for the entire wheel system.
- ☐ Practice transforming between robot and world frames **in both directions** — KTU loves asking reverse transformations.
- ☐ When $\ell = 0$, the sliding constraint reduces to $\dot{y}_R = \dot{x}_R \tan\alpha$ (purely geometric), and the wheel imposes no $\dot{\theta}$ relation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Wheel Taxonomy in Field Robotics

In the foundational 1996 paper by **Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel** (*"Structural Properties and Classification of Kinematic and Dynamic Models of Wheeled Mobile Robots"*), all wheels used in mobile robotics are classified into **five broad families**: *Fixed Standard Wheel, Steered Standard Wheel, Castor Wheel, Swedish Wheel,* and *Spherical Wheel*. For this module, we are focusing exclusively on the two **orientable (steerable) wheel families** — the **Steered Standard Wheel (SSW)** and the **Castor Wheel (CW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these two wheels are grouped together:** Both possess **two internal degrees of freedom (DOF)** — one for *rolling* (rotation about the wheel axle) and one for *steering* (rotation about a vertical axis). The only geometric difference between them is **where the steering axis intersects the ground** — at the *wheel center* (SSW) versus *offset from the wheel center* (Castor).

## 1.2 Steered Standard Wheel (SSW) — Formal Definition

A **Steered Standard Wheel** is a conventional rigid wheel that can rotate about its horizontal axle (rolling, $\psi$) **and** about a vertical steering axis ($\phi$) that passes **directly through the geometric center of the wheel** (and hence through the wheel–ground contact point).

- **Two DOF:** $\psi$ (roll) and $\phi$ (steer)
- **One fixed non-holonomic constraint:** no slip in the direction perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- **One driving (kinematic) equation:** rolling velocity $R\dot{\psi}$ equals the projection of the chassis velocity along the wheel plane.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Wheel"

> Think of the **front wheels of a car** when you turn the steering wheel. The wheel pivots about a vertical pin that goes **straight through the hub of the wheel**. When you turn the steering wheel, the *contact patch* itself slides sideways across the asphalt. This sliding is the *price* you pay for changing direction — the wheel cannot simultaneously turn and roll without scrubbing a small lateral arc. The center-mounted king-pin is the defining feature of a Steered Standard Wheel.

## 1.3 Castor Wheel (CW) — Formal Definition

A **Castor Wheel** is a steered wheel whose **vertical steering axis is offset** from the wheel–ground contact point by a perpendicular distance $l$ (the **castor offset** or **trailing arm length**). The wheel center and the contact patch are *no longer* collinear with the steering axis.

- **Two DOF:** $\psi$ (roll) and $\phi$ (steer) — same as SSW
- **One fixed non-holonomic constraint:** no slip at the contact point in the direction perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- **One driving equation:** rolling velocity equation is identical in form to SSW, but the *sliding constraint* picks up an extra term $l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy — "The Office-Chair Caster"

> Picture the **caster on a rolling office chair** (the typical 5-prong base). The vertical pin that lets the wheel swivel is *behind* (or *in front of*) the rubber wheel. When the chair turns, the wheel swivels *automatically* to align with the new direction of travel — no human input needed! The contact patch on the floor is essentially the *pivot point* during the swivel. The horizontal distance between the contact patch and the wheel's hub is the **castor offset** $l$. This self-aligning behaviour is precisely what makes castor wheels popular on luggage, hospital beds, and robot bases.

## 1.4 Geometric Setup & Notation

For both wheels, the kinematic derivation uses a common set of variables:

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(x, y, \theta)$ | Robot pose (chassis reference point) | m, m, rad |
| $(a, b)$ | Wheel center coordinates in the chassis frame | m, m |
| $R$ | Wheel radius | m |
| $\phi$ | Steering angle (rotation of wheel plane about vertical axis) | rad |
| $\psi$ | Wheel rolling angle (rotation about wheel axle) | rad |
| $l$ | Castor offset (perpendicular distance from steering axis to wheel center) | m |
| $\alpha$ | Fixed orientation of a standard wheel (only for non-steered wheels) | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *Steered Standard Wheel* is the special case of a *Castor Wheel* with the castor offset $l = 0$. Setting $l = 0$ in any castor-wheel equation should reduce it to the corresponding SSW equation. Always remember this "boundary condition" during derivations.

## 1.5 Visual Summary of the Two Wheels

A side-by-side schematic representation (to be rendered in Mermaid in §4):

- **SSW:** steering axis is a single straight vertical line that intersects the contact point and the wheel center.
- **Castor Wheel:** steering axis is a single straight vertical line that intersects the contact point but is offset from the wheel center.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Common Analytical Framework

Both wheel types are analyzed using the **rigid-body rolling-without-slipping** assumption. At the contact patch between the wheel and the floor:

1. **No lateral sliding** is permitted — the velocity component *perpendicular to the wheel plane* must vanish. This is the *non-holonomic sliding constraint*.
2. **The wheel can roll** — the velocity component *along the wheel plane* produces the angular velocity of the wheel $\psi$. This is a *kinematic driving relationship* and is not a constraint in the Pfaffian sense.

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic vs Non-holonomic:** A *holonomic* constraint can be integrated to a position-level equation. A *non-holonomic* constraint involves velocities and **cannot be integrated** to a position equation. Wheeled vehicles are non-holonomic because they cannot move sideways — yet they can reach *any* position in the plane. This is one of the most-tested theoretical points in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Why Two Constraints, Not Three?

Although the contact point is a single point in 2D (and thus has 2 velocity components: $\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c$), only **one of these** is constrained to zero (perpendicular to wheel plane). The second (along the wheel plane) is *free to be non-zero* — it is the rolling velocity. If we constrained both, the robot would be a *kinematically locked holonomic system* (a sliding block), which is useless for a vehicle.

Hence **every passive wheel contributes exactly one Pfaffian constraint** to the system.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Substitution Rule:** Vertical bars for absolute value / parallel lines in tables are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

### Table A — Steered Standard Wheel (SSW)

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variable Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = 0$ | No-slip perpendicular to wheel plane | Fixed constraint |
| 2 | $\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi) = R\dot{\psi}$ | Wheel rolling kinematics | Driving equation |
| 3 | $\delta_m \text{ contribution} = 0,\ \delta_s \text{ contribution} = 1$ | Steered constraint is *orientable* | DOF classification |
| 4 | Castor offset $l$ | $l = 0$ (degenerates to SSW) | Geometric parameter |

### Table B — Castor Wheel (CW)

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variable Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$ | No-slip perpendicular to wheel plane | Fixed constraint |
| 2 | $\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi) = R\dot{\psi}$ | Wheel rolling kinematics | Driving equation |
| 3 | $\delta_m \text{ contribution} = 0,\ \delta_s \text{ contribution} = 1$ | Steered constraint is *orientable* | DOF classification |
| 4 | Castor offset $l > 0$ | Distance from contact patch to wheel center | Geometric parameter |

### Table C — Comparative Summary

| Property | Steered Standard Wheel | Castor Wheel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Internal DOF | 2 (roll + steer) | 2 (roll + steer) |
| Steering axis location | Through wheel center | Offset from wheel center by $l$ |
| Number of Pfaffian constraints | 1 (perpendicular to wheel plane) | 1 (perpendicular to wheel plane) |
| Equation differs in which constraint? | — | Sliding (perpendicular) constraint |
| Self-aligning behaviour? | **No** | **Yes** (when $l > 0$) |
| Real-world example | Car front wheel (Ackermann) | Office chair caster, luggage wheel |
| Reduces to SSW when | Always ($l \equiv 0$ by definition) | $l = 0$ |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **SSW** is the cornerstone of **Ackermann-steered vehicles** (cars, trucks, autonomous cars). Because the wheel can be precisely steered, lateral scrubbing is minimized, which is critical for high-speed road vehicles.
- **Castor wheels** are *self-aligning* — when a vehicle moves forward, the contact patch automatically trails behind the wheel center, and any lateral disturbance creates a restoring torque. This is why mobile robots typically have **two driven wheels in the middle and two castor wheels at the front/back** for stability.

## 2.5 Classification Within Campion's Framework

Campion et al. quantify the kinematic behaviour of any wheel by two integers $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$ that contribute to a robot's total **degree of mobility** $\delta_m$ and **degree of steerability** $\delta_s$:

$$\delta_m \leq 3,\quad \delta_s \leq 2,\quad \delta_m + \delta_s \leq 3$$

For an SSW or a Castor wheel, the *orientable* (steerable) constraint contributes:

$$\delta_s \text{ contribution} = 1,\quad \delta_m \text{ contribution} = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** For a *fixed* standard wheel, contribution is $(\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 0)$. For a *steered* wheel (SSW or Castor), contribution is $(\delta_m = 0, \delta_s = 1)$. A *Swedish/Omni* wheel has $\delta_m = 1$ but the constraint becomes holonomic-friendly.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation locked in for this section:** Let the chassis reference point be at $(x, y)$ with heading $\theta$. A particular wheel is attached at chassis-frame coordinates $(a, b)$. The wheel plane makes an angle $\phi$ with the chassis longitudinal axis. $\psi$ is the rolling angle, $R$ is the wheel radius, and $l$ is the castor offset.

## 3.1 Derivation: Steered Standard Wheel (SSW)

### 3.1.1 Position of the Wheel Center in the World Frame

The wheel center position in the world frame is obtained by rigid-body transformation:

$$x_w = x + a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta$$
$$y_w = y + a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta$$

These two equations are derived by rotating the chassis-frame vector $(a, b)$ by angle $\theta$ and adding it to the chassis reference point $(x, y)$.

### 3.1.2 Time Derivatives of the Wheel Center Position

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\dot{x}_w = \dot{x} - (a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta)\dot{\theta}$$
$$\dot{y}_w = \dot{y} + (a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta)\dot{\theta}$$

We used the product rule and the derivatives $\frac{d}{dt}\cos\theta = -\sin\theta\,\dot{\theta}$, $\frac{d}{dt}\sin\theta = \cos\theta\,\dot{\theta}$.

### 3.1.3 Wheel Plane Orientation

The wheel plane (containing the rolling direction and the wheel axle) is oriented at angle $(\theta + \phi)$ with respect to the world X-axis. The unit vectors are:

- Along the wheel rolling direction: $\mathbf{u}_r = \left[\cos(\theta + \phi),\ \sin(\theta + \phi)\right]^T$
- Perpendicular to wheel plane: $\mathbf{u}_p = \left[-\sin(\theta + \phi),\ \cos(\theta + \phi)\right]^T$

### 3.1.4 Sliding (No-Slip) Constraint

The contact point of an SSW lies **directly below the wheel center** (steering axis passes through the wheel center). Hence the velocity of the contact point equals the velocity of the wheel center.

No-slip requires the contact-point velocity component along $\mathbf{u}_p$ to be zero:

$$\mathbf{v}_w \cdot \mathbf{u}_p = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_w \left[-\sin(\theta + \phi)\right] + \dot{y}_w \left[\cos(\theta + \phi)\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $\dot{x}_w$ and $\dot{y}_w$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\left[\dot{x} - (a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta)\dot{\theta}\right]\sin(\theta + \phi) + \left[\dot{y} + (a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta)\dot{\theta}\right]\cos(\theta + \phi) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Expand each product:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}\,(a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta)\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}\,(a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta)\cos(\theta + \phi) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Group the $\dot{\theta}$ terms and apply the product-to-sum identities $\sin A\sin B + \cos A\cos B = \cos(A - B)$ and $\cos A\sin B - \sin A\cos B = \sin(B - A)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
a\dot{\theta}\left[\sin\theta\sin(\theta + \phi) + \cos\theta\cos(\theta + \phi)\right] &= a\dot{\theta}\cos\left[(\theta + \phi) - \theta\right] = a\dot{\theta}\cos\phi \\
b\dot{\theta}\left[\cos\theta\sin(\theta + \phi) - \sin\theta\cos(\theta + \phi)\right] &= b\dot{\theta}\sin\left[(\theta + \phi) - \theta\right] = b\dot{\theta}\sin\phi
\end{aligned}$$

Summing these two contributions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}\,(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the final sliding constraint becomes:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}\,(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = 0} \quad \text{(SSW Sliding)}$$

### 3.1.5 Rolling Constraint

The rolling constraint equates $R\dot{\psi}$ to the projection of the contact-point velocity along the wheel rolling direction $\mathbf{u}_r$:

$$R\dot{\psi} = \mathbf{v}_w \cdot \mathbf{u}_r$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
R\dot{\psi} = \left[\dot{x} - (a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta)\dot{\theta}\right]\cos(\theta + \phi) + \left[\dot{y} + (a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta)\dot{\theta}\right]\sin(\theta + \phi)
\end{aligned}$$

Group the $\dot{\theta}$ terms using product-to-sum identities:

$$\begin{aligned}
a\dot{\theta}\left[\cos\theta\sin(\theta + \phi) - \sin\theta\cos(\theta + \phi)\right] &= a\dot{\theta}\sin\phi \\
-b\dot{\theta}\left[\cos\theta\cos(\theta + \phi) + \sin\theta\sin(\theta + \phi)\right] &= -b\dot{\theta}\cos\phi
\end{aligned}$$

Summing these contributions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}\,(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}\,(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi) = R\dot{\psi}} \quad \text{(SSW Rolling)}$$

This is **not** a Pfaffian constraint on $(x, y, \theta, \phi)$ — it is a *kinematic relationship* that defines $\dot{\psi}$ in terms of the chassis velocities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Castor Wheel

### 3.2.1 Geometry of the Offset

For a castor wheel, the contact point $C$ is **not** directly below the wheel center. The vector from the wheel center to $C$, expressed in the wheel's local frame (where the wheel rolling direction is the local X-axis), is $(l, 0, -R)$. Rotating this into the world frame by angle $(\theta + \phi)$:

$$x_c = x_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)$$
$$y_c = y_w + l\sin(\theta + \phi)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** The offset $l$ is taken positive when the contact patch is *ahead* of the wheel center along the rolling direction (a "leading" castor). For a "trailing" castor, $l < 0$. Most office-chair castors are *trailing* ($l < 0$), but the equation structure is identical.

### 3.2.2 Velocity of the Contact Point

Differentiating the contact-point position:

$$\dot{x}_c = \dot{x}_w - l\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$
$$\dot{y}_c = \dot{y}_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$

The extra terms appear because **both** the chassis rotation $\dot{\theta}$ and the steering rotation $\dot{\phi}$ contribute to the angular velocity of the castor arm.

### 3.2.3 Sliding Constraint for the Castor Wheel

The no-slip condition $\mathbf{v}_c \cdot \mathbf{u}_p = 0$ becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\dot{x}_c \sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_c \cos(\theta + \phi) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&-\left[\dot{x}_w - l\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})\right]\sin(\theta + \phi) + \left[\dot{y}_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})\right]\cos(\theta + \phi) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

The first two terms collapse to $-\dot{x}_w \sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_w \cos(\theta + \phi)$ (the SSW result). The remaining two terms give:

$$\begin{aligned}
l\sin^2(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi}) + l\cos^2(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi}) = l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})
\end{aligned}$$

by the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.

Therefore the full sliding constraint becomes:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}\,(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})} \quad \text{(Castor Sliding)}$$

### 3.2.4 Rolling Constraint for the Castor Wheel

The rolling constraint is $\mathbf{v}_c \cdot \mathbf{u}_r = R\dot{\psi}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_c \cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_c \sin(\theta + \phi) &= R\dot{\psi}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\dot{x}_w - l\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})\right]\cos(\theta + \phi) + \left[\dot{y}_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})\right]\sin(\theta + \phi) &= R\dot{\psi}
\end{aligned}$$

The $l$-terms are:

$$\begin{aligned}
-l\sin(\theta + \phi)\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi}) + l\cos(\theta + \phi)\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi}) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The two terms **cancel** exactly. Hence the rolling constraint is **identical to that of the SSW**:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}\,(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi) = R\dot{\psi}} \quad \text{(Castor Rolling)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** A common KTU mistake is to *also* add an $l$-term to the rolling constraint. The trigonometric cancellation above shows that the rolling constraint is *immune* to the castor offset. Only the sliding (perpendicular) constraint is modified.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix (Pfaffian Form)

The sliding constraint of a single wheel can be written in the canonical Pfaffian form $A(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\theta + \phi) & -\cos(\theta + \phi) & -(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) & \mp l \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

For an SSW, the $\mp l$ term becomes $0$. For a castor wheel, the term $-l\dot{\phi}$ moves to the right-hand side and the constraint becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\theta + \phi) & -\cos(\theta + \phi) & -(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi + l) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = l\dot{\phi}$$

For a robot with $N$ wheels, the individual constraint rows are stacked into a matrix $C(\mathbf{q})$, and the **degree of mobility** is:

$$\delta_m = \dim\{\dot{\mathbf{q}} \mid C(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0\} = 3 - \mathrm{rank}(C)$$

The **degree of steerability** $\delta_s$ is the number of *orientable* constraints (which depend on the steering variable $\phi$).

For a more detailed architecture, the following diagram maps the *flow* of kinematic computation for a robot with one SSW and one Castor wheel:
