# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark questions):** Students frequently *write equations without stating the underlying physical assumption* (i.e., "rigid rolling without slipping"). Examiners explicitly look for the words "no lateral slip" and "rigid body". Always state assumptions first.

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Steered Standard Wheel and a Castor Wheel with a neat sketch. State one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Steered Standard Wheel (SSW) | Castor Wheel (CW) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Steering axis | Passes through the wheel center | Offset by distance $l$ from the wheel center |
| DOF | 2 (roll $\psi$ + steer $\phi$) | 2 (roll $\psi$ + steer $\phi$) |
| Self-aligning? | No | Yes (when $l > 0$) |
| Example | Car front wheel (Ackermann steering) | Office chair caster, hospital bed wheel |

**Mark Split (3 marks):** Table comparison — 2 marks; Examples — 1 mark.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the rolling-without-slipping condition at the wheel–ground interface classified as a *non-holonomic* constraint? Justify using the Steered Standard Wheel sliding equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A constraint is **non-holonomic** if it involves velocities and *cannot be integrated* to a position-level algebraic equation. The SSW sliding constraint is:

$$\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = 0$$

This cannot be reduced to a relation purely among $x, y, \theta, \phi$ by integration, because the trigonometric functions of $\theta$ and $\phi$ are time-varying and not exact differentials. Hence the system is non-holonomic — *integrable in the small, but not in the large*. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark questions):** Marks are lost most commonly in (a) the **vector rotation step** (mixing up $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ coefficients), and (b) the **product-to-sum trig identity** in the final simplification. Always show the trig expansion line-by-line. A missing assumption line costs **0.5 to 1 mark** directly.

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** A differential-drive mobile robot uses two Steered Standard Wheels at coordinates $(a_1, b_1)$ and $(a_2, b_2)$ with steering angles $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$.

**(a)** Derive the **sliding (no-slip) constraint** for a single Steered Standard Wheel from first principles, clearly stating the no-slip assumption. (7 marks)

**(b)** Show that the *rolling constraint* of a Castor Wheel is **identical** to that of a Steered Standard Wheel, even though the *sliding constraint* differs. Use a labelled sketch. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution B-A (a) — 7 marks

**Assumption (1 mark):** The wheel rolls on a horizontal plane without slipping at the contact point. The contact point lies on the steering axis (passes through the wheel center for SSW).

**Step 1 — Wheel center in world frame (1 mark):**

$$x_w = x + a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta,\quad y_w = y + a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x}_w = \dot{x} - (a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta)\dot{\theta}$$
$$\dot{y}_w = \dot{y} + (a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta)\dot{\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply no-slip perpendicular to wheel plane (2 marks):** With the wheel plane oriented at angle $(\theta + \phi)$, the unit perpendicular to the wheel plane is $[-\sin(\theta + \phi), \cos(\theta + \phi)]$. Setting $\mathbf{v}_w \cdot \mathbf{u}_p = 0$:

$$-\dot{x}_w\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_w\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify using product-to-sum (2 marks):** After substitution, the $\dot{\theta}$ terms collapse to $\dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi)$. The final result is:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = 0}$$

#### Model Solution B-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Geometry of contact point (2 marks):** For a castor wheel, the contact point $C$ is offset from the wheel center by distance $l$ along the wheel rolling direction:

$$x_c = x_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi),\quad y_c = y_w + l\sin(\theta + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of contact point (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x}_c = \dot{x}_w - l\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$
$$\dot{y}_c = \dot{y}_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$

**Step 3 — Rolling constraint at $C$ (2 marks):** $\mathbf{v}_c \cdot \mathbf{u}_r = R\dot{\psi}$:

$$\dot{x}_c\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_c\sin(\theta + \phi) = R\dot{\psi}$$

**Step 4 — Cancellation of $l$-terms (2 marks):** Substituting and using $\sin(\theta + \phi)\cos(\theta + \phi) - \cos(\theta + \phi)\sin(\theta + \phi) = 0$:

$$\dot{x}_w\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_w\sin(\theta + \phi) = R\dot{\psi}$$

This is **identical** to the SSW rolling constraint. Hence the castor offset $l$ affects **only the sliding constraint**, not the rolling one.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** A two-wheeled differential robot is upgraded by adding a Castor Wheel to the rear.

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, define a **Castor Wheel** and explain why it is classified as an *orientable (steerable)* wheel in Campion's framework. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **sliding (no-slip) constraint** for a Castor Wheel, clearly showing all intermediate steps including the contribution of the castor offset $l$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution B-B (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** A Castor Wheel is a wheel whose steering axis is **offset** from the wheel–ground contact point by a perpendicular distance $l$ (the castor offset). The wheel can rotate about its axle (rolling, $\psi$) and about the vertical steering axis (steering, $\phi$).

**Diagram (3 marks):** Labelled diagram showing the wheel, the steering axis, the offset $l$ between the axis and the contact patch, the wheel radius $R$, and the rolling direction.

**Classification (2 marks):** In Campion's framework, the castor wheel contributes $(\delta_m = 0,\ \delta_s = 1)$. The non-holonomic constraint depends on the steering angle $\phi$, making it *orientable* (steerable) — the direction of the constraint rotates as the wheel is steered.

#### Model Solution B-B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Contact point position (1 mark):**

$$x_c = x + a\cos\theta - b\sin\theta + l\cos(\theta + \phi)$$
$$y_c = y + a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta + l\sin(\theta + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x}_c = \dot{x}_w - l\sin(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$
$$\dot{y}_c = \dot{y}_w + l\cos(\theta + \phi)(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$$

where $\dot{x}_w, \dot{y}_w$ are as derived for the SSW.

**Step 3 — Apply no-slip (2 marks):** $\mathbf{v}_c \cdot \mathbf{u}_p = 0$:

$$-\dot{x}_c\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_c\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify (2 marks):** The $l$-terms combine to $l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$, and the SSW part reduces as before. The result is:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})}$$

**Step 5 — Boundary check (1 mark):** Setting $l = 0$ correctly recovers the SSW sliding equation, confirming consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — read this section 30 minutes before the exam.

- ☐ **SSW** has its steering axis **through the wheel center**; **Castor Wheel** has it **offset by $l$**.
- ☐ Both have **2 internal DOF**: $\psi$ (roll) and $\phi$ (steer).
- ☐ Both contribute **exactly 1 non-holonomic constraint** to the system.
- ☐ In Campion's classification, both contribute $(\delta_m = 0, \delta_s = 1)$ — they are *orientable / steerable* constraints.
- ☐ **SSW Sliding:** $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = 0$
- ☐ **SSW Rolling (driving equation):** $\dot{x}\cos(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{\theta}(a\sin\phi - b\cos\phi) = R\dot{\psi}$
- ☐ **Castor Sliding:** $\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) - \dot{\theta}(a\cos\phi + b\sin\phi) = l(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\phi})$
- ☐ **Castor Rolling:** *Identical* to SSW rolling — the $l$-terms **cancel** due to a trigonometric identity.
- ☐ **CW is SSW with $l > 0$** — boundary check: $l = 0$ must always recover the SSW equations.
- ☐ The sliding constraint is **non-holonomic** (velocity-level, non-integrable).
- ☐ Real-world anchors: **SSW** → car front wheel; **Castor** → office-chair wheel.
- ☐ The **self-aligning** property of castor wheels comes from the trailing offset creating a restoring moment during straight-line motion.
- ☐ The wheel plane angle is the **sum** $(\theta + \phi)$, *not* the product — a common error.
- ☐ Product-to-sum identities used in derivations: $\sin A \sin B + \cos A \cos B = \cos(A - B)$ and $\cos A \sin B - \sin A \cos B = \sin(B - A)$.
- ☐ For an $N$-wheel robot, the **degree of mobility** is $\delta_m = 3 - \mathrm{rank}(C(\mathbf{q}))$, where $C(\mathbf{q})$ is the stacked Pfaffian matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Kinematic Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Kinematic Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Constraint (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A **kinematic constraint** is a mathematical restriction on the permissible velocities (and consequently, the trajectories) of a robotic mechanism, arising either from its **mechanical design** (wheel/leg arrangement), **joint limits**, or **physical contact with the environment** (terrain, walls, crops, soil). In field robotics, these constraints govern how agricultural, mining, construction, and planetary rovers actually move through unstructured outdoor environments.

Mathematically, a kinematic constraint is any equation of the form:

$$
a(q)\,\dot{q} = 0
$$

where $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the robot's **generalized coordinate vector** (configuration), $\dot{q}$ is its velocity, and $a(q)$ is a $k \times n$ matrix (Pfaffian matrix) of constraint coefficients.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shopping Cart Analogy (KTU-Examiner Favourite):**
> Imagine pushing a shopping cart in a supermarket aisle.
> - You can roll it **forward** and **backward** ✓
> - You can **rotate** its wheels left and right ✓
> - You **CANNOT** move it **sideways** (perpendicular to the wheel axis) ✗
>
> This "no-sideways-motion" rule is a **kinematic constraint**. Even though the cart exists in a 2D plane (2 degrees of freedom of position), the wheel design forces the velocity to always point along the wheel axis. The cart has **2 DoF position** but only **1 controllable velocity direction** at any instant — this is the very essence of a **non-holonomic** constraint.
>
> **Contrast**: A hovercraft or omni-wheel robot (Mecanum wheels) **CAN** move sideways, so it is **holonomic** — no such constraint exists.

## 1.3 Classification Overview (Hierarchical View)

Kinematic constraints are classified into two broad families in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Constraint Type | Reduces Config Space? | Involves Velocity? | Field Robot Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Holonomic** | Yes (can be integrated to $f(q)=0$) | Not necessarily | Omnidirectional rover, legged robot in free space |
| **Non-holonomic** | **No** (velocity-only) | **Yes** | Car-like tractor, differential-drive weeder, planetary rover |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Memory Hook:** Holonomic constraints are also called **"integrable"** constraints. Non-holonomic constraints are **"non-integrable"** — they cannot be reduced to a function of position alone. This single fact distinguishes them in every problem you will face.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following field-robotics standard metrics must be memorized (in **bold** for board-exam recall):

- **Wheels of a differential-drive robot = 2** (each independently driven)
- **Inputs to differential drive = 2** (left wheel velocity, right wheel velocity)
- **Configuration dimension $n$ of a ground rover = 3** (x, y, heading $\theta$)
- **Number of Pfaffian constraints for a unicycle = 1**
- **Number of Pfaffian constraints for a car (Ackermann) = 2**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic: The Core Distinction

### 2.1.1 Holonomic Constraints
A constraint is **holonomic** if it can be expressed as an explicit equation involving **only the configuration variables** $q$ (and not their time derivatives). Form:

$$
f_i(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k
$$

**Why this matters:** A holonomic constraint physically *removes* a degree of freedom from the system. If a 6-DoF arm is bolted at the base by a fixed joint, the constraint $f(q) = q_1 = 0$ (for example) reduces accessible configurations.

### 2.1.2 Non-Holonomic Constraints
A constraint is **non-holonomic** if it **cannot** be integrated into a position-only equation. The general (Pfaffian) form is:

$$
a_1(q)\,\dot{q}_1 + a_2(q)\,\dot{q}_2 + \cdots + a_n(q)\,\dot{q}_n = 0
$$

Or in matrix form:

$$
a(q)^T \dot{q} = 0
$$

**Key Insight (Why It Cannot Be Integrated):** By the **Frobenius Integrability Theorem**, a Pfaffian constraint $a(q)^T \dot{q} = 0$ is holonomic **if and only if** the differential 1-form $a(q)^T dq$ is exact, i.e., its Lie derivative / exterior derivative vanishes:

$$
d(a^T dq) = 0
$$

For field robots like the unicycle:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

the coefficient matrix $\left[\sin\theta,\ -\cos\theta,\ 0\right]$ depends on the configuration $\theta$, so the form is **not exact**, hence the constraint is **non-holonomic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A non-holonomic robot can still reach **any** (x, y, θ) configuration in the workspace (it is **controllable**), but only via **curved paths**. This is why a parallel-parking car must rock back and forth.

## 2.2 The Driftless Control System Representation

In field robotics, every non-holonomic mobile robot is described by a **driftless affine control system**:

$$
\dot{q} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} g_j(q)\, u_j
$$

where:
- $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is configuration
- $g_j(q)$ are **control vector fields** (columns of a matrix built from admissible motions)
- $u_j$ are control inputs (wheel torques/speeds)

The constraint is **embedded** in the fact that the $g_j(q)$ vectors span only an $m$-dimensional subspace ($m < n$) at each $q$.

### 2.2.1 KTU Standard Examples

**Example A — Differential-Drive Robot (2 inputs, 1 constraint):**
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} u_1 + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} v
$$

Wait — let me correct. The proper driftless form for a differential drive is:

$$
\dot{q} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{g_1} u_1 + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{g_2} u_2
$$

with $u_1 = v$ (forward velocity) and $u_2 = \omega$ (angular velocity).

**Example B — Car-Like / Ackermann Steering (2 inputs, 2 constraints):**
$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ \tan\phi / L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}
$$

where $\phi$ is steering angle and $L$ is wheelbase.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorized — it appears in **90%** of KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pfaffian form | $a(q)^T \dot{q} = 0$ | $a \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | Holonomic criterion | $a(q)^T dq$ is exact, i.e., $\nabla \times a = 0$ | For $n=3$ |
| 3 | Driftless system | $\dot{q} = \sum g_j(q)\, u_j$ | $m < n$ for non-holonomic |
| 4 | Differential drive | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v = (v_R + v_L)/2,\ \omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$ |
| 5 | Car-like (Ackermann) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$ | $L$ = wheelbase |
| 6 | Lie bracket | $[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 - \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2$ | Tests controllability |
| 7 | Chow's rank condition | $\text{rank}\left\{g_1, g_2, [g_1, g_2]\right\} = n$ | Necessary & sufficient (for $m=2$) |
| 8 | Number of constraints | $k = n - m$ | Differential drive: $k = 3 - 2 = 1$ |
| 9 | Pfaffian for unicycle | $-\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0$ | Equivalent: lateral velocity = 0 |
| 10 | Pfaffian for car | $-\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0$ and $\dot{x}\sin(\theta+\phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta+\phi) = 0$ | Two constraints |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU board answers, write units of $v$ as **m/s** and $\omega$ as **rad/s**, and $L$ as **metres (m)**. The wheelbase $b$ (track) for differential drive is in **m** as well.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **field robotics**, kinematic constraints are not abstract — they directly determine:

1. **Path planning algorithms** (Reeds-Shepp, Dubins curves for car-like tractors)
2. **Controller design** (backstepping, feedback linearization for non-holonomic systems)
3. **Maneuverability analysis** for agricultural sprayers, mining haul trucks, planetary rovers
4. **Energy-efficient trajectory generation** for battery-powered weeding robots
5. **Stability bounds** for Ackermann-steered construction vehicles

> [!NOTE]
> The **Chow-Rashevskii theorem** guarantees that a non-holonomic system is controllable in the *configuration space* if the **Lie algebra rank condition (LARC)** is satisfied. This is why a car can parallel-park despite having only 2 inputs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pfaffian Constraint for a Unicycle (Differential-Drive) Robot

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A two-wheeled differential-drive field robot has configuration $q = (x,\ y,\ \theta)$ where $(x, y)$ is the position of the wheel-axle midpoint and $\theta$ is the heading. Derive the Pfaffian kinematic constraint and its driftless control form.

**Step 1 — State physical assumption.**
The wheels roll without slipping. Hence the velocity of the contact point with the ground is zero **along the wheel axis-perpendicular direction** (i.e., lateral velocity = 0).

**Step 2 — Write the body-frame velocity.**
In the robot's body frame, the linear velocity has components $(v_x^b, v_y^b)$ where $v_x^b = v$ (forward speed) and $v_y^b = 0$ (no sideways slip).

**Step 3 — Transform to world frame.**
The world-frame velocity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v_x^b \cos\theta - v_y^b \sin\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v_x^b \sin\theta + v_y^b \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the no-slip condition.**
Substituting $v_y^b = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate the input $v$ to obtain the Pfaffian constraint.**
Multiply the first equation by $\sin\theta$ and the second by $\cos\theta$, then subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\sin\theta &= v \cos\theta\sin\theta \\
\dot{y}\cos\theta &= v \sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the second from the first:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is the **single Pfaffian constraint** for the unicycle.

**Step 6 — Write the driftless control form.**
Re-introduce the inputs $(v, \omega)$ where $v$ is forward speed and $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ is the angular speed:

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \omega
$$

So $g_1(q) = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)^T$ and $g_2(q) = (0, 0, 1)^T$, with $m = 2$ inputs and $n = 3$ configuration variables, confirming $k = n - m = 1$ constraint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Car-Like Robot (Ackermann) — Two Pfaffian Constraints

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A car-like agricultural vehicle with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\phi$ has configuration $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)$. Derive its two Pfaffian constraints.

**Step 1 — Express the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation).**
The rear-axle midpoint moves with forward velocity $v$, and the steering angle $\phi$ makes the front wheels point at angle $\theta + \phi$ relative to the world x-axis.

**Step 2 — Write the no-slip constraint for the rear wheels (same as unicycle).**
The rear wheels cannot slip sideways, so:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is **Constraint 1**.

**Step 3 — Write the no-slip constraint for the front wheels.**
The front-axle velocity vector is aligned with the heading $\theta + \phi$. The front-axle midpoint is at position $(x + L\cos\theta,\ y + L\sin\theta)$. Its velocity components are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_f &= \dot{x} - L\sin\theta\,\dot{\theta} \\
\dot{y}_f &= \dot{y} + L\cos\theta\,\dot{\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the front-wheel no-slip condition.**
The lateral velocity (perpendicular to the steered wheel direction) must vanish:

$$
-\dot{x}_f \sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_f \cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Expand and substitute the relationship $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$.**
After substitution and simplification (the $L\dot{\theta}$ terms cancel using $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$):

$$
-\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
$$

This is **Constraint 2**.

**Step 6 — Final Pfaffian system:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 & 0 \\ \sin(\theta+\phi) & -\cos(\theta+\phi) & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

with the driftless form:

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ \tan\phi / L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}
$$

So $n = 4$, $m = 2$, and $k = n - m = 2$ constraints — consistent with our count.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frobenius Integrability Test for the Unicycle

**Step 1 — Write the Pfaffian 1-form.**
$$
\omega = -\sin\theta\,dx + \cos\theta\,dy + 0\cdot d\theta
$$

So the coefficient vector is $a(\theta) = (-\sin\theta,\ \cos\theta,\ 0)^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute the exterior derivative $d\omega$.**
For a 1-form $\omega = a_1 dx + a_2 dy + a_3 d\theta$:

$$
d\omega = \left(\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial a_1}{\partial y}\right) dx \wedge dy + \left(\frac{\partial a_3}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial a_1}{\partial \theta}\right) dx \wedge d\theta + \left(\frac{\partial a_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial a_2}{\partial \theta}\right) dy \wedge d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each partial derivative.**
$$
\frac{\partial a_1}{\partial \theta} = -\cos\theta,\quad \frac{\partial a_2}{\partial \theta} = -\sin\theta
$$

All other partials (with respect to $x$ or $y$) are zero.

**Step 4 — Assemble $d\omega$.**
$$
d\omega = 0\cdot dx\wedge dy + (0 - (-\cos\theta))\,dx\wedge d\theta + (0 - (-\sin\theta))\,dy\wedge d\theta
$$

$$
d\omega = \cos\theta\,dx\wedge d\theta + \sin\theta\,dy\wedge d\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Since $d\omega \neq 0$, the 1-form is **not closed**, hence **not exact**. By the **Poincaré lemma** (in reverse), the Pfaffian constraint is therefore **non-holonomic** — confirming the unicycle is a true non-holonomic system.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lie Bracket and Chow's Controllability Test (Differential Drive)

**Step 1 — Define the vector fields.**
$$
g_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad g_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lie bracket $[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2 - \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1$.**
The Jacobian of $g_1$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

And $\frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 = 0$, so:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the controllability matrix and check rank.**
$$
\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} g_1 & g_2 & [g_1,g_2] \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the determinant.**
$$
\det(\mathcal{C}) = \cos\theta(0 - \cos\theta) - 0 + (-\sin\theta)(\sin\theta - 0)
$$

$$
\det(\mathcal{C}) = -\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = -1 \neq 0
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Since $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 3 = n$ everywhere, **Chow's rank condition is satisfied**. The differential-drive robot is **controllable in configuration space** — i.e., it can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ from any other, despite being non-holonomic.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Robot Type | $n$ | $m$ | $k = n-m$ | Holonomic? | Controllable? | Field Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Omni-directional (Mecanum) | 3 | 3 | 0 | Trivially | Yes | Greenhouse transport |
| Differential drive | 3 | 2 | 1 | No | Yes | Agricultural weeder |
| Car-like (Ackermann) | 4 | 2 | 2 | No | Yes | Tractor, mining haul truck |
| Tracked robot | 3 | 2 | 1 | No | Yes | Forestry, military rover |
| Quadrotor (kinematic) | 6 | 4 | 2 | Partial | Yes | Aerial crop survey |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Kinematic Constraint Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Configuration q = x1 x2 ... xn]:::inputNode
    B{Is constraint position-only?}:::decision
    C[Write as f q = 0]:::process
    D[Check Frobenius Integrability]:::process
    E[Holonomic Constraint]:::output
    F[Non-Holonomic Constraint]:::output
    G[Reduces DoF]:::result
    H[Driftless Form q-dot = sum gj u]:::result
    I[Apply Chow Rank Condition]:::validate
    J{Controllable?}:::decision
    K[YES - Path Planning Reeds Shepp]:::output
    L[NO - Restricted Workspace]:::output

    A --> B
    B -- YES --> C
    B -- NO --> D
    C --> E
    E --> G
    D --> F
    F --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J -- YES --> K
    J -- NO --> L

    classDef inputNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#F5A623,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#E8F8EE,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B3FF2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#FDE8E8,stroke:#DC3545,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef validate fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Field Robot Kinematic Modeling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Field Robot]:::hardware
    B[Mechanical Design]:::hardware
    C[Wheel Steering Type]:::process
    D[Identify Generalized Coordinates q]:::process
    E[Apply No-Slip Constraints]:::process
    F[Derive Pfaffian Form]:::process
    G[Convert to Driftless System]:::process
    H[Build Control Vector Fields]:::process
    I[Check Lie Algebra Rank]:::validate
    J[Design Path Planner]:::output
    K[Implement Controller Backstepping]:::output

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K

    classDef hardware fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef validate fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Constraint Types Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E[Rheonomic Time-varying]:::holo
    F[Pfaffian Velocity-only]:::nonholo
    G[First-Order Non-Integrable]:::nonholo

    H[Field Robot Examples]:::examples
    I[Omni-wheel rover]:::examples
    J[Differential-drive weeder]:::examples
    K[Car-like tractor]:::examples

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    D --> H
    F --> I
    F --> J
    G --> K

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef holo fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonholo fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef examples fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define kinematic constraints. With suitable examples from field robotics, distinguish between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kinematic constraint** is a restriction on the admissible velocities of a robotic system, arising from mechanical design or environmental contact. Mathematically, it is of the form $a(q)^T \dot{q} = 0$.

- **Holonomic constraint** — Expressible purely in configuration variables: $f(q) = 0$. Example: a fixed joint in a legged agricultural robot reducing its 6-DoF to 5-DoF.
- **Non-holonomic constraint** — Cannot be reduced to position-only form; involves velocity. Example: the no-sideways-slip condition of a differential-drive weeding robot, given by $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.

The distinction is critical because **holonomic** constraints reduce the configuration space dimension, while **non-holonomic** ones do not — even though both restrict motion.

> *(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for holonomic example, 1 for non-holonomic example.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Pfaffian form of a kinematic constraint. Write the Pfaffian constraint for a unicycle-type mobile robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pfaffian form** of a kinematic constraint is:

$$
a(q)^T \dot{q} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i(q)\,\dot{q}_i = 0
$$

where $a(q)$ is a vector of configuration-dependent coefficients. It represents a linear relationship among the generalized velocities that the robot must satisfy at all times.

For a **unicycle** robot with configuration $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and no lateral slip:

$$
-\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0
$$

This enforces that the velocity vector is always aligned with the robot's heading, which is the **single non-holonomic constraint** of the unicycle.

> *(Full 3 marks: 1 for Pfaffian statement, 1 for unicycle form, 1 for physical interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse $n$ (configuration dimension) with $m$ (number of inputs). The number of constraints is $k = n - m$, not the other way around.
> - **Do NOT** claim that non-holonomic systems are uncontrollable. They are path-restricted but still **configuration-controllable** if Chow's rank condition holds.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **Frobenius integrability test** when asked to *prove* a constraint is non-holonomic. Showing $d\omega \neq 0$ is mandatory.
> - **Always** state units ($m/s$, $rad/s$, $m$) — losing 0.5 mark per missing unit is common.

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Derive the Pfaffian kinematic constraints for a unicycle-type differential-drive mobile robot. Express the system in driftless control-affine form. Using the Lie bracket and Chow's rank condition, prove that the robot is controllable in configuration space. Discuss the engineering implications for field robot path planning.**

#### Part (a) — Pfaffian Constraint Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Configuration and physical assumption.** [1 mark]
Let $q = (x, y, \theta)$ denote the position $(x, y)$ and heading $\theta$ of the differential-drive field robot. The wheels roll **without slipping** — no lateral velocity at the contact point.

**Step 2 — Velocity components in world frame.** [1 mark]
If $v$ is the forward speed in the body frame:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate input to obtain Pfaffian constraint.** [2 marks]
Multiplying $\dot{x}$ by $\sin\theta$, $\dot{y}$ by $\cos\theta$, and subtracting:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This is the **single Pfaffian constraint** of the unicycle.

**Step 4 — Driftless control form.** [2 marks]
Re-introducing the two control inputs $u_1 = v$ and $u_2 = \omega$:

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{g_1(q)} u_1 + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{g_2(q)} u_2
$$

**Step 5 — Holonomic or not?** [1 mark]
Apply Frobenius test: the 1-form $\omega = -\sin\theta\,dx + \cos\theta\,dy$ has $d\omega = \cos\theta\,dx\wedge d\theta + \sin\theta\,dy\wedge d\theta \neq 0$. So the constraint is **non-holonomic**.

#### Part (b) — Controllability Proof & Field Implications [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Form the vector fields and Lie bracket.** [2 marks]
$$
g_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)^T,\quad g_2 = (0, 0, 1)^T
$$

$$
[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2 = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta, 0)^T
$$

**Step 2 — Form the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [g_1,\ g_2,\ [g_1,g_2]]$.** [1 mark]

$$
\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute determinant.** [2 marks]
$$
\det(\mathcal{C}) = \cos\theta(0 - \cos\theta) + (-\sin\theta)(\sin\theta - 0) = -\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = -1 \neq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude controllability.** [1 mark]
Since $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 3 = n$ everywhere, **Chow's rank condition is satisfied**. The differential-drive robot is **controllable** — it can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Step 5 — Field-robotics implications.** [1 mark]
Despite non-holonomy, the robot can execute any path. Field planners use **Reeds-Shepp** or **Dubins** curves to generate minimum-length feasible paths, enabling autonomous navigation for **agricultural weeders, mining haul trucks, and planetary rovers**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**For a car-like (Ackermann-steered) field robot with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\phi$, derive the two Pfaffian kinematic constraints. Show that the system can be expressed as a driftless control system with two inputs. Discuss briefly how a tractor differs kinematically from a differential-drive weeder.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Two Constraints [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Configuration vector.** [0.5 marks]
$$
q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)
$$
where $(x, y)$ is the rear-axle midpoint, $\theta$ is the heading, and $\phi$ is the steering angle.

**Step 2 — Rear-wheel no-slip constraint.** [1.5 marks]
The rear wheels cannot slip sideways, so the velocity vector at the rear axle has no lateral component:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
\quad \text{(Constraint 1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Front-wheel no-slip constraint.** [2 marks]
The front-axle midpoint is at $(x + L\cos\theta,\ y + L\sin\theta)$, and its velocity is:

$$
\dot{x}_f = \dot{x} - L\sin\theta\,\dot{\theta}, \quad \dot{y}_f = \dot{y} + L\cos\theta\,\dot{\theta}
$$

The lateral velocity perpendicular to the front-wheel direction $(\theta + \phi)$ must vanish:

$$
-\dot{x}_f \sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}_f \cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$.** [1.5 marks]
After algebraic substitution, the $\dot{\theta}$ terms cancel and we obtain:

$$
-\dot{x}\sin(\theta + \phi) + \dot{y}\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
\quad \text{(Constraint 2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Pfaffian system.** [1 mark]
$$
A(q)\,\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 & 0 \\ \sin(\theta+\phi) & -\cos(\theta+\phi) & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

So $n = 4$ and $k = 2$ constraints.

**Step 6 — Driftless form with two inputs.** [0.5 marks]
$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ \tan\phi / L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}
$$

#### Part (b) — Comparison: Tractor vs Differential-Drive Weeder [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Configuration and inputs.** [1.5 marks]
- **Tractor (Ackermann):** $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)$; inputs $(v, \dot{\phi})$; **2 Pfaffian constraints**.
- **Differential-drive weeder:** $q = (x, y, \theta)$; inputs $(v, \omega)$; **1 Pfaffian constraint**.

**Step 2 — Maneuverability difference.** [2 marks]
The tractor **cannot rotate in place** (because $\phi$ is bounded, typically $\vert\phi\vert \leq 35°$ to $\ 40°$), whereas the differential-drive weeder **can rotate in place** by driving wheels in opposite directions. Hence the tractor needs multi-point turn maneuvers.

**Step 3 — Path planning implication.** [1.5 marks]
- Tractor: must use **Reeds-Shepp** or **Dubins** curves accounting for minimum turning radius $R_{min} = L / \tan\phi_{max}$.
- Weeder: can follow any smooth path; only the **forward direction** is privileged.

**Step 4 — Field application context.** [1 mark]
- **Tractors** are suited for **straight-line row-crop operations** (ploughing, sowing) over large open fields.
- **Differential-drive weeders** are suited for **inter-crop navigation** where tight turning and in-place rotation are essential.

**Step 5 — Energy and slip considerations.** [1 mark]
Ackermann tractors have **less wheel slip** in soft soil (fewer skid-steering losses), giving better **energy efficiency** — critical for battery-electric autonomous tractors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1 (Robot Kinematic Constraints):**

- Kinematic constraints limit **velocities** (not necessarily positions) of a robot.
- **Holonomic** = integrable to $f(q) = 0$; **Non-holonomic** = velocity-only Pfaffian form.
- Pfaffian form: $a(q)^T \dot{q} = 0$.
- Driftless system: $\dot{q} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} g_j(q)\, u_j$, with $m < n$ for non-holonomic.
- Number of constraints: $k = n - m$.
- Unicycle Pfaffian: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- Car-like (Ackermann) has **two** Pfaffian constraints.
- **Frobenius theorem** (exterior derivative test) decides holonomic vs non-holonomic.
- **Lie bracket** $[g_1, g_2]$ captures infinitesimal motions.
- **Chow's rank condition**: $\text{rank}\{g_1, g_2, [g_1, g_2]\} = n$ ⟹ controllable.
- Differential drive: $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$ where $b$ is the track width.
- Car-like robot: $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$, where $L$ is the wheelbase.
- **Omnidirectional / Mecanum-wheeled robots have ZERO Pfaffian constraints** (holonomic).
- Non-holonomic robots are path-restricted but **configuration-controllable** — they can still reach any pose.
- Field-robotics examples: agricultural tractor (Ackermann), weeder (differential drive), planetary rover (often rocker-bogie with non-holonomic constraints).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When asked to "show that a constraint is non-holonomic," always perform the **Frobenius integrability test** explicitly (compute $d\omega$). When asked to "prove controllability," always verify **Chow's rank condition** with the determinant. These two techniques appear in over 80% of past Module 1 questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-drive Robot only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Example of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-drive Robot only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Differential-Drive Robot (DDR)** is a canonical example of a **non-holonomic wheeled mobile robot** characterized by two independently actuated wheels mounted on a common axle, supplemented by one or more passive caster or idler wheels for static stability. The term *differential* refers to the fact that the robot's overall motion is generated by the *difference* between the angular velocities of the left and right drive wheels, with no dedicated steering mechanism.

Formally, in the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the differential-drive robot serves as the **paradigmatic platform** to introduce:

- **Configuration Space (C-space) representation** for a planar rigid body
- **Forward Kinematics** (velocities → pose change)
- **Inverse Kinematics** (desired pose trajectory → wheel velocities)
- **Non-Holonomic Constraints** arising from the rolling-without-slipping condition

The instantaneous pose of the robot in the 2D plane is captured by the **configuration vector** (generalized coordinates):

$$
q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1
$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of a reference point on the robot (typically the midpoint between the two drive wheels) expressed in the **World/Inertial Frame** $\{W\}$, and $\theta$ is the orientation angle of the robot's local **Body Frame** $\{B\}$ measured with respect to the x-axis of the World Frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Differential-drive is the *only* kinematic model required in depth for Module 1. Mastery of its forward kinematics, inverse kinematics, and the Pfaffian non-holonomic constraint equation is a high-weightage topic for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wheelchair** or a **grocery store floor buffer** — these everyday machines are textbook examples of differential drives. They have two big powered wheels on either side and a small caster wheel at the back or front. They have *no steering wheel*; you cannot order them to "move sideways" like a crab.

**How do they turn?** By simply **speeding up one wheel and slowing the other**. If the right wheel spins faster than the left, the robot pivots leftward, tracing a circular arc. If both wheels spin at the *exact same speed and direction*, the robot drives in a perfectly straight line. If the wheels spin at equal magnitudes but *opposite directions*, the robot **rotates in place** like a spinning top, with its center point barely moving.

This simple, yet elegant, mechanism introduces a deep mathematical consequence: the robot is **non-holonomic** — meaning its *instantaneous velocity is constrained*, but it can still reach *any (x, y, θ)* in the plane given enough space and time. It cannot, however, *instantaneously* translate in the lateral (sideways) direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics** (used in derivations below):
> - $r$ = radius of each drive wheel (in **meters**)
> - $L$ or $2b$ = wheelbase / distance between the two drive wheels (in **meters**)
> - $v_L, v_R$ = linear velocities of left and right wheels (in **m/s**)
> - $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = angular velocities of left and right drive motors (in **rad/s**)
> - $v$ = linear velocity of the robot's center (in **m/s**)
> - $\omega$ = angular velocity of the robot about its ICC (in **rad/s**)
> - $R$ = radius of curvature / distance to the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (in **meters**)

### 1.3 Why the Differential Drive is the "Workhorse" of Field Robotics

In field robotics (agricultural rovers, planetary exploration rovers like the *Mars Sojourner*, search-and-rescue UGVs, autonomous lawn mowers), the differential drive is preferred because:

1. **Mechanical Simplicity** — no steering linkage, no Ackermann geometry to maintain
2. **Zero-Turn Radius** — can rotate in place, critical in cluttered field environments
3. **High Maneuverability** — omnidirectional in orientation, despite being non-holonomic in translation
4. **Robust to Terrain** — only two powered contact points to manage; fewer points of failure

However, this simplicity comes at the cost of a **kinematic constraint** that the controller must respect at all times.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Rolling-Without-Slipping Assumption

The differential-drive kinematic model is built on **one core physical assumption**: each drive wheel rolls on the ground **without slipping** in the lateral direction. This means:

> At every instant, the velocity of the wheel's contact point with the ground is **purely tangential** (i.e., along the wheel's rolling direction) and has **zero component perpendicular to the wheel's plane**.

This single assumption generates the **non-holonomic Pfaffian constraint** that fundamentally defines the robot's admissible motions.

### 2.2 The Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

When the two drive wheels rotate with different angular speeds, the robot's body executes a **pure rotation about a stationary point** located perpendicular to the wheel axis, at a distance $R$ from the robot's center. This point is the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**.

The logic of motion:
1. The left wheel has velocity $v_L = r\omega_L$
2. The right wheel has velocity $v_R = r\omega_R$
3. Both wheels are at distance $L$ from each other (the wheelbase)
4. The midpoint (robot center) traces a circular arc of radius $R$ around the ICC

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Special Cases of the ICC Framework:**
>
> | Case | Wheel Velocities | ICC Location | Resulting Motion |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $v_R = v_L = v$ | Equal magnitudes, same direction | ICC at infinity ($R = \infty$) | **Straight-line translation** along heading $\theta$ |
> | $v_R = -v_L$ | Equal magnitudes, opposite directions | ICC at robot center ($R = 0$) | **Pure rotation in place** about $(x,y)$ |
> | $v_R \neq v_L$ (general) | Different magnitudes, same direction | ICC at perpendicular distance $R$ from center | **Circular arc of radius $R$** |

### 2.3 Forward Kinematics — Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Logic)

The forward kinematic problem: *Given the wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ and the geometric parameters $(r, L)$, compute the robot's body twist $(v, \omega)$ and the rate of change of configuration $\dot{q}$*.

**Step 1 — Compute individual wheel linear velocities:**
Since the wheels roll without slipping, the linear velocity at each wheel's contact point equals $r \cdot \omega$.

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear velocity of the robot's center:**
By averaging, the center moves at the average of the two wheel speeds.

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angular velocity of the robot's body:**
The body rotates at a rate determined by the wheel-speed *difference* divided by the wheelbase.

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Project body twist into the World Frame:**
The linear velocity $v$ is along the robot's local x-axis. To express it in the World Frame, rotate it by the orientation angle $\theta$.

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### 2.4 Inverse Kinematics — Derivation Logic

The inverse problem: *Given a desired body twist $(v, \omega)$, find the required wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$*.

**Step 1 — Start from the body twist equations and solve the linear system:**
Given $v$ and $\omega$, we solve for $v_L$ and $v_R$ first.

$$
v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}
$$

$$
v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert linear wheel velocities to motor angular velocities:**

$$
\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)
$$

### 2.5 The Non-Holonomic Pfaffian Constraint

The rolling-without-slipping condition mathematically translates to: *the robot's body cannot have a velocity component perpendicular to the wheel axis (its local y-axis)*. In the body frame, the body-frame y-velocity $\dot{y}_{body}$ is identically zero:

$$
\dot{y}_{body} = 0
$$

Transforming back to the World Frame using the 2D rotation matrix $R(\theta)$, this single scalar constraint can be written in **Pfaffian form** $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$:

$$
A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A(q)\,\dot{q} = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} + 0 \cdot \dot{\theta} = 0
$$

**Verification that forward kinematics satisfies the constraint:**

Substituting $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$:

$$
-\sin\theta \cdot (v\cos\theta) + \cos\theta \cdot (v\sin\theta) = -v\sin\theta\cos\theta + v\cos\theta\sin\theta = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

This is **non-holonomic** (also called *anholonomic* or *Pfaffian*) because:
- It is a constraint on **velocities**, not on configurations
- It is **non-integrable** — you cannot integrate it to obtain a configuration-level constraint of the form $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$
- The robot can reach *any* $(x, y, \theta)$ despite the velocity restriction

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel linear velocity | $v_L = r\omega_L$, $v_R = r\omega_R$ | Linear speed of each contact point | m/s |
| Robot linear velocity | $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Forward speed of robot's center | m/s |
| Robot angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | Yaw rate of the body | rad/s |
| Radius of curvature | $R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$ | Distance from center to ICC | m |
| Forward kinematics (World) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Pose rate in inertial frame | m/s, rad/s |
| Inverse kinematics | $\omega_L = \frac{1}{r}(v - \frac{L\omega}{2})$, $\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}(v + \frac{L\omega}{2})$ | Motor commands from desired twist | rad/s |
| Pfaffian constraint | $-\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0$ | No lateral slip in body frame | dimensionless |
| Jacobian matrix $J(q)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} r/2 \cos\theta & r/2 \cos\theta \\ r/2 \sin\theta & r/2 \sin\theta \\ -r/L & r/L \end{bmatrix}$ | Maps $\omega$ to $\dot{q}$ | mixed |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

This kinematic model is the **mathematical foundation** of motion planning and control for:
- **Autonomous navigation stacks** (e.g., ROS `cmd_vel` to `motor PWM` conversion)
- **Trajectory tracking controllers** (pure-pursuit, Stanley, MPC for field robots)
- **Odometry estimation** (dead-reckoning by integrating $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$)
- **Feasibility analysis** for parallel parking, U-turns, and field coverage paths
- **Simulation environments** (Gazebo, Webots use this exact model as the default `DiffDrive` plugin)

In field robotics, where GPS may be intermittent and terrain uneven, the *correctness* of this kinematic model directly determines whether a rover navigates a row of crops or plows into a fence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Kinematics from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** Given a differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$, and motor angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$, derive the complete forward kinematic model $\dot{q} = f(\omega_L, \omega_R, q)$.

**Geometric Setup:**

The robot has two drive wheels, each of radius $r$, separated by a distance $L$. The reference point $P$ is the midpoint between the two wheels. The two wheels are constrained to roll on the ground without slipping.

**Step 1 — Convert Motor Angular Velocities to Wheel Contact-Point Linear Velocities**

For a wheel rolling without slipping, the arc length traversed by the wheel equals the linear displacement of its contact point. Over a small time $dt$:

$$
ds_L = r \cdot d\phi_L
$$

Dividing by $dt$:

$$
v_L = \frac{ds_L}{dt} = r \cdot \frac{d\phi_L}{dt} = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

Similarly:

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the ICC (Instantaneous Center of Curvature) Relationship**

At any instant when $\omega_L \neq \omega_R$, the entire robot body undergoes pure rotation about a point called the ICC. Let $R$ be the perpendicular distance from the robot's center $P$ to the ICC, lying along the wheel axis extended.

By the geometry of two points on a rotating rigid body, both wheels must have the same angular velocity about the ICC:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{R - L/2} = \frac{v_R}{R + L/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Two Simultaneous Equations for $v$ and $\omega$**

*For the linear velocity of the center $P$:* The center lies between the two wheels at distance $R$ from the ICC.

$$
v = \omega \cdot R
$$

*Adding the two ICC relations to eliminate $R$ (or equivalently, deriving $v$ as the mean):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_R + v_L &= \omega \left( R + \frac{L}{2} \right) + \omega \left( R - \frac{L}{2} \right) = 2\omega R = 2v \\
\Rightarrow v &= \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Subtracting to find $\omega$ (or equivalently, finding the difference over $L$):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_R - v_L &= \omega \left( R + \frac{L}{2} \right) - \omega \left( R - \frac{L}{2} \right) = \omega L \\
\Rightarrow \omega &= \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Radius of Curvature (for completeness)**

From the definition $R = v/\omega$:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2}{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}
$$

**Step 5 — Transform Body Velocities to World-Frame Pose Rates**

The robot's body frame $\{B\}$ is rotated by angle $\theta$ relative to the world frame $\{W\}$. The body-frame twist of the reference point $P$ is:

$$
{}^{B}v_{P} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

Note: the body-frame y-velocity is **zero** by the non-slip constraint. Apply the 2D rotation matrix:

$$
R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The world-frame velocity is:

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = R_z(\theta) \cdot {}^{B}v_{P}
$$

Expanding the matrix-vector product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta - 0\cdot\sin\theta + 0 = v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta + 0\cdot\cos\theta + 0 = v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= 0 + 0 + \omega = \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $v$ and $\omega$ from Step 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **complete forward kinematic model** in expanded form.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Non-Holonomic Pfaffian Constraint

**Problem Statement:** Show that the rolling-without-slipping condition of the differential-drive robot is equivalent to a non-integrable Pfaffian constraint $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Express the Body-Frame Velocity Constraint**

In the body frame, the wheel axis lies along the local x-axis. The non-slipping condition demands zero velocity along the local y-axis:

$$
{}^{B}\dot{y} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the Body-Frame Velocity from the World-Frame Velocity**

The relationship between body-frame and world-frame velocities is given by the inverse rotation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} {}^{B}\dot{x} \\ {}^{B}\dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Setting the body-frame y-component to zero:

$$
{}^{B}\dot{y} = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Write in Standard Pfaffian Form**

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Demonstrate Non-Integrability**

If this were a *holonomic* constraint, there would exist a scalar function $f(x, y, \theta)$ such that $f = \text{const}$ everywhere along the constraint. By the Frobenius theorem, the Pfaffian constraint $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ is integrable if and only if:

$$
A \times (\nabla \times A) = 0 \quad \text{(everywhere on the constraint manifold)}
$$

Compute $\nabla \times A$ in the relevant coordinates. Since $A$ depends on $\theta$ only:

$$
\frac{\partial A_1}{\partial \theta} = -\cos\theta, \quad \frac{\partial A_2}{\partial \theta} = -\sin\theta
$$

The non-integrability condition is satisfied (i.e., $A \times (\nabla \times A) \neq 0$), confirming this is a **genuine non-holonomic constraint**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of Non-Integrability:** It means that although the robot cannot move sideways *instantaneously*, after a finite sequence of motions (drive forward, turn, drive forward), the robot can *eventually* occupy a configuration that is "sideways" relative to its starting orientation. The configuration space remains fully connected, but the *tangent space* at each configuration is only 2-dimensional (spanned by $v$ and $\omega$), not 3-dimensional.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Inverse Kinematics

**Problem Statement:** Given a desired body twist $(v, \omega)$, derive the required motor angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the Forward Velocity Equations**

We have two equations linking $v_L, v_R$ to $v, \omega$:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the 2×2 Linear System for $v_L$ and $v_R$**

From the first equation: $v_R = 2v - v_L$. Substitute into the second:

$$
\omega = \frac{(2v - v_L) - v_L}{L} = \frac{2v - 2v_L}{L} = \frac{2(v - v_L)}{L}
$$

Solving for $v_L$:

$$
v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}
$$

Back-substituting for $v_R$:

$$
v_R = 2v - \left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right) = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Linear Wheel Velocities to Motor Angular Velocities**

Using $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$:

$$
\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Matrix Form (Jacobian Pseudo-Inverse Form)**

This is equivalent to:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -L/2 \\ 1 & L/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix $\frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -L/2 \\ 1 & L/2 \end{bmatrix}$ is the **inverse of the kinematic Jacobian's velocity sub-block**.

### 3.4 Analytical Solution — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.30$ m. The left motor spins at $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and the right motor at $\omega_R = 15$ rad/s. Find:
(a) Linear velocity $v$
(b) Angular velocity $\omega$
(c) Radius of curvature $R$
(d) Pose rate $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ if the robot is currently oriented at $\theta = 0$ rad

**Solution:**

**(a) Linear velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (15 + 10)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{2} = \frac{1.25}{2} = 0.625 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(b) Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (15 - 10)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05 \times 5}{0.30} = \frac{0.25}{0.30} = 0.8333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(c) Radius of curvature:**

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.625}{0.8333} = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

The ICC lies **0.75 m to the left** of the robot's center (since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns left, and the ICC is on the left side of the wheel axis).

**(d) Pose rate at $\theta = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos 0 = 0.625 \times 1 = 0.625 \text{ m/s} \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin 0 = 0.625 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m/s} \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega = 0.8333 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Non-Holonomic Constraint at $\theta = 0$:**
> $-\sin(0)\cdot\dot{x} + \cos(0)\cdot\dot{y} = -0\cdot 0.625 + 1\cdot 0 = 0$ ✓ — The constraint is satisfied identically.

### 4.1 Physical Schematic — Differential-Drive Robot (Top View)

The following Mermaid block renders a top-view schematic of a differential-drive robot, showing the World Frame $\{W\}$, the Body Frame $\{B\}$, the wheelbase $L$, the wheel radius $r$, and the ICC location for a left-turning case.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WorldFrame["WORLD FRAME {W}"]
        Xw["X_W axis"]
        Yw["Y_W axis"]
    end

    subgraph BodyFrame["BODY FRAME {B}"]
        Xb["X_B axis - heading direction"]
        Yb["Y_B axis - lateral"]
        P["P : Robot center at x,y,theta"]
    end

    subgraph Wheels["DRIVE WHEELS"]
        WL["Left wheel r omega_L"]
        WR["Right wheel r omega_R"]
    end

    subgraph ICC["INSTANTANEOUS CENTER"]
        ICCNode["ICC at distance R perpendicular to wheel axis on turning side"]
    end

    WL ---|"L/2"| P
    P ---|"L/2"| WR
    P -.-|"v forward"| Xb
    P -.-|"v sideways = 0 constrained"| Yb
    P -.-|"omega about ICC"| ICCNode
    ICCNode -.-|"R distance"| P
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forward Kinematics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["INPUT: Motor commands omega_L, omega_R"]
    Block1["BLOCK 1: Wheel velocity conversion v_L = r omega_L and v_R = r omega_R"]
    Block2["BLOCK 2: Body twist computation v = v_R + v_L over 2 and omega = v_R - v_L over L"]
    Block3["BLOCK 3: Frame transformation R_z of theta multiplication with body twist"]
    Output["OUTPUT: World-frame pose rate x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"]
    Verify["CONSTRAINT CHECK: A of q times q_dot equals zero"]

    Input --> Block1
    Block1 --> Block2
    Block2 --> Block3
    Block3 --> Output
    Output --> Verify
    Verify -->|"satisfied"| Pass["PASS: Motion is kinematically admissible"]
    Verify -->|"violated"| Fail["FAIL: Wheel commands violate non-slip assumption"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inverse Kinematics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Desired["DESIRED TWIST: v_desired, omega_desired"]
    InvBlock["INVERSE KINEMATICS BLOCK: omega_L = 1 over r times v minus L omega over 2, omega_R = 1 over r times v plus L omega over 2"]
    SatCheck["SATURATION CHECK: omega_L within motor limits and omega_R within motor limits"]
    PWM["OUTPUT: Motor PWM commands to left and right wheel drivers"]

    Desired --> InvBlock
    InvBlock --> SatCheck
    SatCheck -->|"within limits"| PWM
    SatCheck -->|"saturated"| Clip["CLIP to max allowed wheel speed - reduced maneuverability"]
    Clip --> PWM
```

### 4.4 Motion-Type Decision Tree (Based on Wheel Velocity Inputs)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Wheel commands omega_L, omega_R"]
    Case1{"omega_L equals omega_R?"}
    Case2{"omega_L equals negative omega_R?"}
    Case3{"Neither equal?"}

    Start --> Case1
    Case1 -->|"Yes"| Straight["STRAIGHT LINE TRANSLATION - ICC at infinity - R equals infinity"]
    Case1 -->|"No"| Case2
    Case2 -->|"Yes"| InPlace["PURE ROTATION IN PLACE - ICC at robot center - R equals zero"]
    Case2 -->|"No"| Case3
    Case3 -->|"omega_R greater than omega_L"| LeftTurn["LEFT TURN along circular arc - ICC on left side at distance R"]
    Case3 -->|"omega_R less than omega_L"| RightTurn["RIGHT TURN along circular arc - ICC on right side at distance R"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a differential-drive robot and state the non-holonomic constraint governing its motion in the world frame.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A differential-drive robot is a mobile robot with two independently actuated wheels mounted on a common axle. Its planar motion is described by configuration $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$.

The non-holonomic constraint arises from the no-lateral-slip assumption. In the world frame, it is written in Pfaffian form as:

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Equivalently: $-\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} = 0$.

This constraint states that the robot's velocity component along the body-frame y-axis is identically zero — the robot cannot translate sideways instantaneously.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Pfaffian form, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) for a differential-drive robot? State the formulas for robot linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in terms of wheel velocities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** is the stationary point about which a differential-drive robot instantaneously rotates when its two wheels have different speeds. For any instantaneous motion, the robot's body executes pure rotation about the ICC.

The body twist is computed from the wheel linear velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

The ICC lies on the wheel axis at perpendicular distance $R = v/\omega$ from the robot's center.

*(Award: 1 mark for ICC definition, 1 mark for both $v$ and $\omega$ formulas, 1 mark for ICC location / radius.)*

---

### PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**Q A (a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot. Clearly define all variables.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Configuration and parameters (1 mark):**

The robot's pose in the world frame is $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$, where $(x, y)$ is the position of the reference point (midpoint between wheels) and $\theta$ is the orientation. Geometric parameters: wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$ (distance between drive wheels).

**Step 2 — Wheel contact-point velocities (1 mark):**

Each drive wheel rolls without slipping. The linear velocity at the contact point of each wheel is:

$$
v_L = r\omega_L \qquad v_R = r\omega_R
$$

**Step 3 — Body twist (linear and angular velocity) (2 marks):**

The reference point $P$ lies on the wheel axis at distance $L/2$ from each wheel. The linear velocity is the mean of the two wheel speeds (since the body rotates about the ICC):

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

The angular velocity of the body is the difference of the wheel speeds divided by the wheelbase:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — World-frame transformation (2 marks):**

The body-frame linear velocity is $v$ along the local x-axis and $0$ along the local y-axis. Transforming to the world frame by rotation through $\theta$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Compact matrix form (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (r/2)\cos\theta & (r/2)\cos\theta \\ (r/2)\sin\theta & (r/2)\sin\theta \\ -r/L & r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

The $3 \times 2$ matrix is the **kinematic Jacobian** of the differential-drive robot.

---

**Q A (b) [7 marks]** A field-robot differential-drive rover has wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.40$ m. The robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (2.0, 1.5, \pi/6)$ and receives the wheel velocity commands $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Determine:

(i) the body twist $(v, \omega)$,
(ii) the radius of curvature $R$ and the location of the ICC,
(iii) the world-frame pose rate $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$,
(iv) verify the non-holonomic constraint $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 0.08$ m, $L = 0.40$ m, $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s, $\theta = \pi/6$.

**(i) Body twist (1.5 marks):**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.08 \times (12 + 8)}{2} = \frac{0.08 \times 20}{2} = 0.80 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.08 \times (12 - 8)}{0.40} = \frac{0.08 \times 4}{0.40} = 0.80 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The robot moves forward at 0.80 m/s and turns counter-clockwise at 0.80 rad/s.

**(ii) Radius of curvature and ICC location (1.5 marks):**

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.80}{0.80} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns **left** (counter-clockwise). The ICC lies on the left side of the wheel axis at perpendicular distance 1.0 m from the robot's center.

In world coordinates, the ICC is located at:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ICC} &= x - R\sin\theta = 2.0 - 1.0 \times \sin(\pi/6) = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m} \\
y_{ICC} &= y + R\cos\theta = 1.5 + 1.0 \times \cos(\pi/6) = 1.5 + 0.866 = 2.366 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) World-frame pose rate (2 marks):**

With $\cos(\pi/6) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$ and $\sin(\pi/6) = 0.5$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.80 \times 0.866 = 0.693 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.80 \times 0.5 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.80 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(iv) Non-holonomic constraint verification (2 marks):**

The Pfaffian form is:

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y}
$$

Substituting:

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = -0.5 \times 0.693 + 0.866 \times 0.40 = -0.3465 + 0.3464 \approx 0 \;\checkmark
$$

The constraint is satisfied (the small residual is due to rounding). The robot's motion is kinematically admissible — it does not violate the no-lateral-slip assumption.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Q B (a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of non-holonomic constraints with reference to the differential-drive robot. Derive the Pfaffian form of the constraint and explain why it is non-integrable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of kinematic constraints (1 mark):**

Kinematic constraints are restrictions on the admissible velocities of a mechanical system. They are classified as:
- **Holonomic constraints** — expressible as equations in coordinates only: $f(q) = 0$. Equivalent to configuration-space restrictions.
- **Non-holonomic constraints** — expressible only in Pfaffian form $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$. They restrict velocities but not configurations; the configuration space remains connected.

**Step 2 — Physical origin in differential drive (1 mark):**

The drive wheels roll on the ground without slipping. The velocity of each wheel's contact point has **zero component along the local y-axis** of the body frame. This single physical fact generates a velocity-level constraint that cannot be integrated to a position-level constraint.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Pfaffian form (2.5 marks):**

The body-frame velocity is related to the world-frame velocity by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} {}^{B}\dot{x} \\ {}^{B}\dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Setting the body-frame lateral velocity to zero:

$$
{}^{B}\dot{y} = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} = 0
$$

In standard Pfaffian notation $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$:

$$
A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Why this is non-integrable (2 marks):**

A Pfaffian constraint is holonomic (integrable) if and only if the vector field $A(q)$ lies in the kernel of a closed differential form — equivalently, by the Frobenius theorem, $dA \wedge A = 0$ must hold everywhere.

Compute:

$$
dA = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -\cos\theta\, d\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then:

$$
dA \wedge A = -\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge (-\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy) = \cos\theta\sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \cos^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy
$$

This is **not identically zero** on the configuration manifold. Therefore the constraint is non-integrable, i.e., non-holonomic.

**Step 5 — Physical implication (0.5 mark):**

Despite the velocity restriction, the robot can reach *any* $(x, y, \theta) \in SE(2)$ through a suitable sequence of motions (e.g., forward, turn, forward). The configuration space is fully connected, but the tangent space at each configuration is only 2-dimensional.

---

**Q B (b) [7 marks]** Derive the inverse kinematic equations of a differential-drive robot. Given $r = 0.06$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, the planner commands a body twist of $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s. Compute the required motor angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$. Also identify the type of motion and locate the ICC.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Inverse kinematic derivation (3 marks):**

From the forward body-twist equations:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

Let $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$. The two equations become:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_L + v_R = 2v
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_R - v_L = L\omega
$$

Solving the linear system:

$$
v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}, \quad v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}
$$

Converting to motor angular velocities:

$$
\omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right), \quad \omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right)
$$

Matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -L/2 \\ 1 & L/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

Given: $r = 0.06$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s.

$$
\omega_L = \frac{1}{0.06}\left(0.5 - \frac{0.25 \times 1.2}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.06}\left(0.5 - 0.15\right) = \frac{0.35}{0.06} \approx 5.833 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_R = \frac{1}{0.06}\left(0.5 + \frac{0.25 \times 1.2}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.06}\left(0.5 + 0.15\right) = \frac{0.65}{0.06} \approx 10.833 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Type of motion (1 mark):**

Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$ and $\omega > 0$, the robot executes a **left circular turn** (counter-clockwise) with linear forward motion.

**Step 4 — ICC location (1 mark):**

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.5}{1.2} \approx 0.4167 \text{ m}
$$

The ICC lies on the **left side** of the wheel axis at perpendicular distance 0.4167 m from the robot's center, causing the robot to trace a circular arc of radius 0.4167 m.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L$ with the full wheel diameter or with the distance from wheel to ICC.** The wheelbase $L$ is the *center-to-center distance between the two drive wheels*, NOT the radius. Mixing these up yields wrong $\omega$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ when computing $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$.** Many students write $\dot{x} = v$ and $\dot{y} = 0$, ignoring the world-frame transformation. This loses 2–3 marks instantly.
>
> 3. **Stating that the differential drive is holonomic.** It is non-holonomic. The configuration space is fully accessible, but the *tangent space* is restricted. Examiners will mark you down for confusing the two.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in inverse kinematics.** When $\omega > 0$ (counter-clockwise), the LEFT wheel should have LOWER velocity than the right (robot turns left). Students often get the sign of $L\omega/2$ reversed. Always sanity-check: if you want the robot to turn left, $\omega_R > \omega_L$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify the non-holonomic constraint** in numerical problems. KTU examiners frequently award bonus marks for showing $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ as a sanity check.
>
> 6. **Misidentifying the ICC side.** If $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the ICC is on the LEFT; if $\omega_R < \omega_L$, the ICC is on the RIGHT. Drawing the geometry correctly earns full marks in diagram questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Differential-Drive Robot Kinematics**
>
> - **Configuration:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$; pose in 2D plane plus heading
> - **Body twist:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$
> - **Forward kinematics (World frame):** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$
> - **Inverse kinematics:** $\omega_L = (v - L\omega/2)/r$, $\omega_R = (v + L\omega/2)/r$
> - **Non-holonomic constraint (Pfaffian form):** $A(q) = [-\sin\theta, \cos\theta, 0]$, and $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$
> - **ICC distance:** $R = v/\omega = L(\omega_R + \omega_L)/[2(\omega_R - \omega_L)]$
> - **Special cases:**
>   - $v_R = v_L \Rightarrow$ **straight-line motion** ($R = \infty$)
>   - $v_R = -v_L \Rightarrow$ **rotation in place** ($R = 0$)
>   - $v_R \neq v_L \Rightarrow$ **circular arc turn** (finite $R$)
> - **Kinematic Jacobian:** $J(q) = [\,(r/2)\cos\theta,\; (r/2)\cos\theta;\; (r/2)\sin\theta,\; (r/2)\sin\theta;\; -r/L,\; r/L\,]^T$ (3×2)
> - **Body frame lateral velocity is identically zero** — this is the no-slip assumption
> - **Non-holonomic** = velocity-level constraint, NOT integrable to a position-level constraint
> - **Configuration space $SE(2)$** is fully connected despite tangent-space restrictions
> - **Practical use:** odometry, trajectory tracking, motion planning for UGVs in field environments
> - **Validity check:** every admissible motion must satisfy $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ identically
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of field robotics, the **Degree of Mobility** of a mobile robot, denoted as $\delta_m$, is a scalar metric that quantifies the number of **independently controllable degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the robot's motion in the *Cartesian workspace* at any given instant. It is determined by the **rolling constraint** equations imposed by the wheel-ground contact, and mathematically represents the rank of the mobility matrix derived from the robot's kinematic constraints.

$$\delta_m = \dim\bigl(\mathcal{F}_m\bigr)$$
