where $x$ and $y$ are the planar coordinates and $\theta$ is the heading angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The workspace is **not just the area the robot covers** — it includes the **orientation the robot can achieve at each point**. This combined "position + orientation" reach is the *dexterous workspace*.

## 1.2 Definition — Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent real-valued coordinates (generalized coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanical system or robot in its environment.

For field robotics, DOF classification is twofold:

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **System DOF** | Total independent motions permitted by the mechanism (Kutzbach / Gruebler count) |
| **Task DOF** | DOF required to perform a given mission (e.g., spraying, tilling, surveying) |
| **Mobility** | The number of directly controllable DOF of the robot |
| **Steerability** | The number of independently steerable wheels/actuators |
| **Maneuverability** | Sum of Mobility and Steerability (Campion's framework) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shopping Cart at Kakkanad"

Imagine you are pushing a **shopping cart at the Lulu Mall parking lot, Kochi**. The cart has:
- **Two big rear wheels** (driven, fixed orientation) and **two small front casters** (passive, free to swivel).
- You can move it **forward, backward, and turn** — but you **cannot slide it sideways** without first rotating it.
- The cart therefore has **3 DOF in 2D** (x, y, θ) but only **2 controllable inputs** (left wheel speed $v_L$, right wheel speed $v_R$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gap between "DOF available" and "DOF controllable"** is the heart of mobile robot kinematics. A **non-holonomic** robot like the cart has fewer control inputs than configuration DOF — making path planning fundamentally harder than for **holonomic** (omnidirectional) platforms.

## 1.4 DOF of a Rigid Body — The Foundational Truth

A **single rigid body in 3D Euclidean space** has exactly **6 DOF**: 3 translations ($T_x, T_y, T_z$) and 3 rotations ($R_x, R_y, R_z$ — roll, pitch, yaw).

For a **mobile robot constrained to a ground plane** (the dominant case in field robotics), motion in $z$, roll, and pitch is suppressed by ground contact. The reduced set is:

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{ground robot}} = 3 \quad (x, y, \theta)
$$

This is the **Planar Rigid Body DOF** — the foundation for every mobile robot analysis you will do at KTU.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gruebler–Kutzbach Criterion (Mobility Formula)

The most authoritative tool for counting DOF of any mechanism — including mobile robot suspensions and leg linkages — is the **Gruebler–Kutzbach Criterion**. KTU examiners love this formula.

### 2.1.1 Planar Mechanism (all motion in 2D)

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

### 2.1.2 Spatial Mechanism (full 3D)

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mobility / DOF of the mechanism
- $n$ = number of rigid links (including the ground link)
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF) — revolute (R), prismatic (P)
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF) — cam, gear, point contact, wheel-ground
- $j_3$ … $j_5$ = higher-order spatial joints

## 2.2 Campion's Mobile Robot Classification (KTU Favourite)

For a wheeled ground robot, **Maneuverability** is decomposed as:

$$
M_n = M_s + M_m
$$

where:
- $M_m$ = **Mobility** (DOF controllable by wheel rotation)
- $M_s$ = **Steerability** (DOF controllable by wheel steering)

| Robot Type | $M_m$ | $M_s$ | $M_n$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive** (2 driven + casters) | 2 | 0 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| **Ackermann / Car-like** | 1 | 1 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| **Tricycle / Single-steered** | 1 | 1 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| **Synchronous Drive** (e.g., Synchro Drive) | 2 | 2 | 4 | Omnidirectional |
| **Mecanum / Swedish Wheel (4)** | 3 | 0 | 3 | Omnidirectional |
| **Universal / Ball Wheel** | 3 | 0 | 3 | Holonomic |
| **3-DOF Swedish (3 wheels, 90° offset)** | 2 | 1 | 3 | Omnidirectional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A robot with $M_n = 3$ is **omnidirectional (holonomic in 2D plane)**. $M_n = 2$ is **non-holonomic** and requires path planning via Dubins/Reeds–Shepp curves.

## 2.3 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Constraints

A **kinematic constraint** of the form $f(q, \dot{q}) = 0$ is:

- **Holonomic** if it is integrable to a position constraint $g(q) = 0$ (reduces reachable workspace).
- **Non-holonomic** if it restricts the *velocity* but **not the reachable workspace** (e.g., a car can reach any $(x,y,\theta)$ eventually, but never move sideways instantly).

> [!NOTE]
> For a differential drive: the constraint $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$ is **non-holonomic** — the robot cannot translate perpendicular to its wheel axis. The workspace is **unbounded** in $x, y$ but bounded in instantaneous velocity directions.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Gruebler planar mobility | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Gruebler spatial mobility | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{rigid body 3D}} = 6$ | Independent motions in $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 3T + 3R |
| 4 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{ground robot}} = 3$ | Planar reduced set $(x,y,\theta)$ | Used in KTU derivations |
| 5 | $M_n = M_s + M_m$ | Campion's maneuverability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic constraint (DDR) | Differentially flat: $(x,y)$ |
| 7 | $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ | Linear velocity of DDR | m/s |
| 8 | $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$ | Angular velocity of DDR | rad/s |
| 9 | $R_{turn} = \dfrac{L}{2}\left(\dfrac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}\right)$ | Instantaneous turn radius | m |
| 10 | $A_{workspace} \approx \pi (v_{max} T)^2$ | Reachable area (unconstrained) | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** In the Gruebler formula, **always count the ground (frame) as link #1**. A "4-wheel differential drive" has $n = 4$ (chassis + 3 wheels if 1 caster is passive and counted) — read the problem statement carefully. A wheel rolling without slipping is a **higher-pair joint** ($j_2 = 1$).

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Field Robotics

Field robots (e.g., the **IIT Bombay ACKO** agricultural robot, **Bonn's BoniRob**, **John Deere's See & Spray**) operate in **unstructured terrain** — sloped fields, muddy soil, gravel. The DOF count and wheel configuration directly determine:

- **Slip compensation** strategies (use IMU + wheel odometry fusion).
- **Stability on slopes** (raise $M_s$ to allow crab steering, e.g., in 4-wheel-steered tractors).
- **Energy efficiency** (fewer DOF = simpler control, lower power).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Gruebler's Formula for a 4-Bar Planar Linkage (Foundation)

The Gruebler formula is itself a derivative of constraint counting. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1:** Each rigid body in 2D has, in isolation, **3 DOF** (translation in $x$, translation in $y$, rotation about $z$).

For $n$ links, unrestricted DOF:

$$
\text{Free DOF} = 3n
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract 3 DOF for the **fixed ground link** (we don't count the ground as moving):

$$
\text{Remaining DOF} = 3(n - 1)
$$

**Step 3:** Each **lower-pair joint** (revolute R, prismatic P) removes **2 DOF** by enforcing both point contact and surface tangency constraints. So subtract $2j_1$.

**Step 4:** Each **higher-pair joint** (point/line contact such as cam, gear mesh, wheel-ground) removes only **1 DOF** (single-point contact is less restrictive). So subtract $j_2$.

**Combining (since planar motion is 1 rotational DOF less than 3D):**

$$
\boxed{M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: DOF of a Differential Drive Robot (DDR)

**System Description:** A chassis with two independently driven coaxial wheels (radius $r$, axle length $L$) and one passive caster.

**Step 1:** Identify links — chassis (1) + 2 driven wheels + 1 caster (assumed massless) = 4 moving links. Add ground = $n = 5$.

**Step 2:** Identify joints:
- 2 revolute joints (wheel-to-chassis axles) = $j_1^{(R)} = 2$
- 1 revolute joint (caster swivel) = $j_1^{(R)} = 1$ (with 2 more for steering axis — but for DOF counting it acts as 1 R)
- 1 revolute joint (caster wheel rolling) = $j_1^{(R)} = 1$
- 4 wheel-ground contacts (no slip) = $j_2 = 4$

For planar counting, total $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 4$, $n = 5$.

**Step 3:** Apply Gruebler's planar formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(4) - 4 \\
M &= 3(4) - 8 - 4 \\
M &= 12 - 12 \\
M &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The mechanism has **0 DOF as a closed chain in static configuration** — the robot is *grounded* and cannot lift off. This is correct: a static mobile robot in contact with ground is **kinematically locked** in the vertical and roll/pitch senses.

**Step 4:** To find the **in-plane mobility** (the 3-DOF planar motion we care about), we treat the ground as a 2D plane and count differently. The 2 driven wheels each contribute **1 controllable DOF** (their rotation), giving $M_m = 2$. The caster is passive, so $M_s = 0$. Total maneuverability:

$$
M_n = M_s + M_m = 0 + 2 = 2
$$

**Step 5:** The configuration space of a DDR in 2D is:

$$
\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 = \{(x, y, \theta) \mid x,y \in \mathbb{R},\ \theta \in [0, 2\pi)\}
$$

This is a **3-dimensional manifold**, but the **control input space is 2-dimensional** $(v_L, v_R)$. The system is **under-actuated** and **non-holonomic**.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Forward Kinematics of DDR

**Given:** $v_L$ and $v_R$ (wheel angular velocities $\omega_L, \omega_R$).

**Step 1:** Linear velocity of each wheel (no slip):

$$
v_L = r \omega_L, \quad v_R = r \omega_R
$$

**Step 2:** Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) geometry:

The robot rotates about a point on the axle line. Using the perpendicular bisector of the wheel velocities, the angular velocity $\omega$ of the chassis satisfies:

$$
v_R - v_L = L \omega
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}}
$$

**Step 3:** Linear velocity of the chassis center:

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Instantaneous turn radius:

$$
R_{turn} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
$$

**Step 5:** Forward kinematics in world frame:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the canonical KTU forward-kinematics equation for a differential drive.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Workspace of a DDR Under Bounded Inputs

Assume wheel speeds bounded: $\vert \omega_L \vert, \vert \omega_R \vert \le \omega_{max}$ and mission time $T$.

**Step 1:** Maximum linear velocity:

$$
v_{max} = r \omega_{max} \quad (\text{when } \omega_L = \omega_R = \omega_{max})
$$

**Step 2:** If robot moves straight for time $T$, it travels distance $d = v_{max} T$.

**Step 3:** Worst-case (the robot can drive in any direction by rotating then translating) — the **reachable workspace** is a disc of radius $d$:

$$
A_{reachable} = \pi (r \omega_{max} T)^2
$$

**Step 4:** The **dexterous workspace** (orientations reachable at every point) is smaller — for a DDR, the robot can achieve any $\theta$ at any point in the interior, so:

$$
\mathcal{W}_{dex} = \mathcal{W}_{reach} \setminus \partial \mathcal{W}_{reach}
$$

with $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ achievable everywhere inside.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU

| Property | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car) | Mecanum (Omni) | Synchro Drive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M_m$ | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| $M_s$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| $M_n$ | 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Holonomic? | No | No | Yes | Yes (with care) |
| Min turn radius | 0 (rotate in place) | $\frac{L}{\tan\phi_{max}}$ | 0 | 0 |
| Field use | Indoor + light field | Tractors, cars | Warehouses | Research platforms |
| Field robustness | Medium | High | Low (delicate wheels) | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: DOF Taxonomy in Mobile Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvl1 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lvl2 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lvl3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px

    A["DOF OF MOBILE ROBOT"]:::root
    A --> B["CONFIGURATION DOF"]:::lvl1
    A --> C["CONTROL DOF INPUTS"]:::lvl1

    B --> B1["3 in 2D plane x y theta"]:::lvl2
    B --> B2["6 in 3D space xyz roll pitch yaw"]:::lvl2

    C --> D["MANEUVERABILITY Mn"]:::lvl1
    D --> E["Mobility Mm wheels driven"]:::lvl2
    D --> F["Steerability Ms wheels steered"]:::lvl2

    E --> E1["Differential drive Mm=2"]:::lvl3
    E --> E2["Ackermann Mm=1"]:::lvl3
    E --> E3["Mecanum Mm=3"]:::lvl3

    F --> F1["Car-like Ms=1"]:::lvl3
    F --> F2["Synchro drive Ms=2"]:::lvt3
    F --> F3["No steering Ms=0"]:::lvl3

    F2 -.-> F2C["F2 placeholder"]
    classDef lvt3 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#000000
    class F2C lvt3
```

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — Workspace Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inp fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000000

    I1["Robot Geometry Wheels Links Axles"]:::inp
    I2["Joint Types Revolute Prismatic Fixed"]:::inp
    I3["Constraints Nonholonomic Slip"]:::inp

    P1["Step 1 Count links n"]:::proc
    P2["Step 2 Identify joints j1 j2"]:::proc
    P3["Step 3 Apply Gruebler M equals 3n minus 2j1 minus j2"]:::proc
    P4["Step 4 Apply Campion Mn equals Ms plus Mm"]:::proc
    P5["Step 5 Determine Holonomic flag"]:::proc

    O1["Reach Set in R2 times S1"]:::out
    O2["DOF Count M"]:::out
    O3["Path Planner Type Dubins Reeds Shepp RRT"]:::out

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P5
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Differential Drive Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef wheel fill:#374151,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef body fill:#9ca3af,stroke:#374151,color:#000000
    classDef pt fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000

    CH["CHASSIS with center C at x y theta"]:::body
    WL["WHEEL LEFT radius r omegaL"]:::wheel
    WR["WHEEL RIGHT radius r omegaR"]:::wheel
    WC["CASTER WHEEL passive"]:::wheel
    AX["AXLE LENGTH L between two driven wheels"]:::body
    ICR["INSTANTANEOUS CENTER OF ROTATION on axle line"]:::pt
    VT["LINEAR VELOCITY v at center C"]:::body
    OM["ANGULAR VELOCITY omega about ICR"]:::body

    CH --- WL
    CH --- WR
    CH --- WC
    WL --- AX
    AX --- WR
    AX -.-> ICR
    ICR --- OM
    CH --- VT
```

## 4.4 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef q fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000000
    classDef d fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#000000
    classDef endY fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000
    classDef endN fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000

    Q1["Can robot translate sideways without first rotating?"]:::q
    Q2["Number of control inputs equal to configuration DOF?"]:::q

    A1["YES Holonomic Mn equals 3"]:::d
    A2["NO NonHolonomic Mn less than 3"]:::d

    R1["Path planning simple go to goal directly"]:::endY
    R2["Path planning uses Dubins or Reeds Shepp curves"]:::endN

    Q1 --> A1
    Q1 --> A2
    A1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> R1
    A2 --> R2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define Degrees of Freedom of a mobile robot. Why is a ground mobile robot said to have 3 DOF?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot is the minimum number of independent generalized coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of the robot in its environment. A rigid body in 3D has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). However, a **ground mobile robot** is constrained by its wheels in permanent contact with the ground plane. This contact eliminates vertical translation ($z$) and two rotations (roll about $x$-axis and pitch about $y$-axis). The remaining independent motions are:
1. Translation along $x$-axis
2. Translation along $y$-axis
3. Rotation about $z$-axis (yaw / heading angle $\theta$)

Hence a ground mobile robot is said to have **3 DOF** described by the configuration vector $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$.

> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for ground constraint explanation + 1 mark for enumerating 3 DOF)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Holonomic and Non-Holonomic mobile robots with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Number of controllable DOF = configuration DOF | Controllable DOF < configuration DOF |
| Sideways motion | Possible directly | Not possible (must rotate first) |
| Path planning | Straight-line feasible | Requires Dubins / Reeds–Shepp curves |
| Example | Mecanum-wheeled robot, Omni-drive | Differential drive, Ackermann car |
| Constraint | Integrable (position-level) | Non-integrable (velocity-level) |

**Examples:** A **Mecanum-wheeled robot** is holonomic ($M_n = 3$). A **differential drive** with two driven wheels and a caster is non-holonomic ($M_n = 2$ but configuration DOF = 3).

> *(1.5 marks for table-style comparison + 1.5 marks for examples)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **Gruebler–Kutzbach criterion** for computing the Degrees of Freedom of a planar mechanism. State the formula and define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **differential drive mobile robot** has a chassis, two independently driven rear wheels of radius $r = 0.1$ m separated by axle length $L = 0.5$ m, and one front caster wheel. Using the Gruebler formula:
  - (i) Compute the **system DOF** counting all joints including ground contact.
  - (ii) Compute the **in-plane maneuverability** using Campion's framework.
  - (iii) If $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s, compute the **linear velocity $v$**, **angular velocity $\omega$**, and **instantaneous turn radius $R$**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Gruebler–Kutzbach Criterion (7 marks)**

The **Gruebler–Kutzbach criterion** is a topological formula that counts the net Degrees of Freedom (mobility) of a kinematic mechanism by comparing the **unconstrained DOF of all links** with the **DOF removed by joints**.

**For a planar mechanism** (all motion confined to 2D):

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Definitions of each term:**

- **$M$ — Mobility / Degrees of Freedom:** The number of independent inputs (actuators) needed to fully drive the mechanism. *1 mark*
- **$n$ — Number of links:** Total rigid bodies including the fixed **ground/frame** as link #1. *1 mark*
- **$j_1$ — Number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF joints):** Revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints where surfaces maintain contact; each removes 2 DOF (one translation and one rotation). *2 marks*
- **$j_2$ — Number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF contacts):** Point or line contacts such as cam-follower, gear mesh, wheel rolling without slipping. *2 marks*
- **Working of $3(n-1)$:** Each free link in 2D has 3 DOF. With $n$ links, total is $3n$. Subtracting 3 for the fixed ground (which is also counted as a link) gives $3(n-1)$. *1 mark*

---

#### **Part (b) — DOF Computation and Kinematics of DDR (7 marks)**

**(i) System DOF (3 marks):**

Counting the mechanism:
- Links: chassis (1) + 2 driven wheels + 1 caster + ground = $n = 5$
- Lower-pair joints (revolute R): 2 driven wheel axles + 1 caster swivel + 1 caster wheel rolling = $j_1 = 4$
- Higher-pair joints (wheel-ground contact, no slip): 2 driven + 1 caster = $j_2 = 3$

Applying Gruebler's planar formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(4) - 3 \\
M &= 3(4) - 8 - 3 \\
M &= 12 - 11 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The system has 1 net DOF when including all ground contact joints. In field-robotics terms, this means the mechanism is **statically grounded** — the wheels' no-slip condition locks the robot to the ground, leaving only **forward/backward motion as the single degree of freedom** in the static topological count. *(1 mark for computation, 1 mark for interpretation)*

**(ii) In-Plane Maneuverability (2 marks):**

Using **Campion's framework**:
- **Mobility** (driven wheels): 2 wheels, each independently driven $\Rightarrow M_m = 2$
- **Steerability** (steered wheels): none $\Rightarrow M_s = 0$
- **Maneuverability:** $M_n = M_s + M_m = 0 + 2 = 2$

Since $M_n = 2 < 3$ (configuration DOF in 2D), the differential drive is **non-holonomic**. *(1 mark for $M_n$ + 1 mark for holonomic classification)*

**(iii) Kinematics (2 marks):**

Given: $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.5$ m, $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s.

**Linear velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.1 \cdot (4 + 2)}{2} = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.1 \cdot (4 - 2)}{0.5} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Instantaneous turn radius:**

$$
R_{turn} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

> *Optional cross-check using formula:*
> $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L} = \dfrac{0.5}{2} \cdot \dfrac{6}{2} = 0.25 \cdot 3 = 0.75$ m ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain **Campion's classification of wheeled mobile robots** based on mobility ($M_m$) and steerability ($M_s$). Discuss the maneuverability of a **4-wheeled mecanum robot** and a **car-like (Ackermann) robot**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeled mobile robot has two front wheels that are both driven and steered, and two rear wheels that are driven only (no steering). Determine:
  - (i) The number of driven wheels and steered wheels.
  - (ii) The mobility $M_m$ and steerability $M_s$.
  - (iii) Total maneuverability $M_n$ and classify the robot as holonomic or non-holonomic.
  - (iv) The minimum number of actuators required. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Campion's Classification (7 marks)**

**Campion's framework** characterizes a wheeled ground robot by decomposing its maneuverability into two components:

- **Mobility $M_m$**: Number of wheels whose **rotation is independently controllable** (i.e., driven by separate actuators).
- **Steerability $M_s$**: Number of wheels whose **steering angle is independently controllable**.
- **Total maneuverability**: $M_n = M_m + M_s$ *1 mark for definitions*

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
        WHEELED MOBILE ROBOT
        └── MANEUVERABILITY Mn
            ├── MOBILITY Mm (driven wheels)
            │     ├── Differential Drive → Mm = 2
            │     ├── Ackermann Car     → Mm = 1
            │     ├── 4-Wheel Drive     → Mm = 4
            │     └── Mecanum (4)        → Mm = 3
            └── STEERABILITY Ms (steered wheels)
                  ├── Car-like         → Ms = 1
                  ├── Synchro Drive    → Ms = 2 or 3
                  └── Differential     → Ms = 0
```

*1 mark for block diagram*

**4-Wheeled Mecanum Robot:** *2 marks*
- Each Mecanum wheel has passive rollers at 45°. All 4 wheels are independently driven, none are steered.
- $M_m = 4$ (some sources count it as 3 because of kinematic coupling via wheel orientation), but conservatively for the standard 4-Mecanum configuration: $M_m = 3$, $M_s = 0$, $M_n = 3$.
- $M_n = 3 = $ configuration DOF, so the robot is **holonomic / omnidirectional** in the plane.

**Car-like (Ackermann) Robot:** *2 marks*
- Rear wheels driven (often together, or 2-wheel drive), front wheels steered.
- $M_m = 1$ (one effective input — throttle), $M_s = 1$ (one steering input).
- $M_n = 2 < 3$, so the robot is **non-holonomic**. It cannot move sideways.

---

#### **Part (b) — Configuration of Hybrid Robot (7 marks)**

**Given configuration:** 2 front wheels (driven + steered) + 2 rear wheels (driven, no steering).

**(i) Count of wheels (1 mark):**
- Driven wheels: 4 (all 4 wheels are driven)
- Steered wheels: 2 (front wheels only)

**(ii) Mobility and Steerability (2 marks):**
- $M_m = 2$ (there are 2 independent drive systems — front pair and rear pair, even if within each pair wheels are coupled)
- $M_s = 2$ (two independently steerable front wheels — each can have a different steering angle for Ackermann geometry, but in simplified counting, $M_s = 1$ if we consider the steering system as one link)

**For KTU standard answer:** $M_m = 2$, $M_s = 1$ (the two front wheels are coupled by the steering linkage for Ackermann geometry, hence 1 steering input).

**(iii) Total maneuverability (2 marks):**
- $M_n = M_m + M_s = 2 + 1 = 3$
- Since $M_n = 3 =$ configuration DOF, the robot is **holonomic** in the planar sense (in principle capable of omnidirectional motion if steering and drive are coordinated).

**(iv) Minimum number of actuators (2 marks):**
- 1 actuator for steering (one steering motor controlling both front wheels via tie-rod)
- 2 actuators for drive (one for front axle, one for rear axle, each controlling a pair of wheels)
- **Total = 3 actuators minimum**

> *Note: If independent steering of the 2 front wheels is allowed (no tie-rod), $M_s = 2$ and total actuators = 4.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count the ground link** in the Gruebler formula — KTU examiners specifically check whether you used $3(n-1)$ and not $3n$.
> 2. **Misclassifying wheel-ground contact** — it is a higher-pair joint ($j_2$), not a lower-pair. A wheel rolling without slipping is **kinematically equivalent to a 1-DOF higher pair**.
> 3. **Confusing $M_m$ with DOF** — $M_m$ is the *number of controllable inputs*, not the configuration DOF. Always distinguish.
> 4. **Saying "Ackermann has $M_m = 2$"** — Wrong. The two rear wheels are mechanically coupled via a differential; they act as **one effective drive input**. $M_m = 1$ for a standard car.
> 5. **Sign convention in $\omega$**: a positive $\omega_R > \omega_L$ produces **counter-clockwise** (left turn in standard ENU frame) rotation. State the convention explicitly.
> 6. **Units trap**: Gruebler's $M$ is dimensionless — do not write "radians" or "meters".

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition (DOF)**: Minimum number of independent generalized coordinates specifying the robot's configuration.
- **Ground Robot DOF = 3** in 2D: $(x, y, \theta)$. The full 3D rigid body has **6 DOF**.
- **Gruebler Planar**: $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Gruebler Spatial**: $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Campion's Maneuverability**: $M_n = M_m + M_s$
- **Holonomic robot**: $M_n \ge 3$ in 2D (omnidirectional). Example: **Mecanum, Synchro drive**.
- **Non-holonomic robot**: $M_n < 3$ in 2D. Example: **Differential drive, Ackermann car**.
- **Differential drive forward kinematics**: $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$
- **Turn radius (DDR)**: $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$
- **Reachable workspace** (DDR, time $T$): area $\pi (r \omega_{max} T)^2$
- **Dextrous workspace**: subset of reachable where **all orientations are attainable** at each point.
- **Wheel-ground contact** = **higher-pair joint** ($j_2$); revolute/prismatic = **lower-pair joints** ($j_1$).
- **Always count the ground as link #1** in $n$ for Gruebler counting.
- **Field robotics implication**: more DOF = better maneuverability on rough terrain, but more complex control and higher power consumption. Choose configuration based on mission (agriculture → Ackermann; warehouse → Mecanum; research → Synchro).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Holonomic Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Holonomic Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic Robot:** A robotic system is classified as *holonomic* (also termed *fully actuated* or *completely manoeuvrable*) when the number of **independently controllable Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of its end-effector or chassis in the task space is **equal to** the **total Degrees of Freedom** of the system in that same workspace.

Mathematically, for a mobile robot with configuration vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$n_{controllable} = n_{total}$$

The instantaneous feasible velocity set is the **entire tangent space** $T_q\mathcal{C}$ of the configuration manifold $\mathcal{C}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hovercraft vs The Car:**
> Imagine two vehicles at a crossroads trying to make a U-turn:
> - **Holonomic Robot (Hovercraft):** Can instantly slide **sideways**, spin on the spot, and move diagonally without first aligning itself. Every direction is "allowed" right now.
> - **Non-Holonomic Robot (Car):** Must perform a **three-point turn** to change direction because it cannot move perpendicular to its wheels' axis. Even though it has 2 position DOF, it can only use 2 of 3 velocity DOF at any instant (it cannot strafe).

## 1.3 Kinematic Vocabulary Snapshot

| Term | Standard Definition | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Degrees of Freedom | Independent parameters defining configuration | $n$ or $DOF$ |
| Holonomic Constraint | An *integrable* constraint expressible as $f(q,t)=0$ | — |
| Non-holonomic Constraint | A *non-integrable* differential constraint | Pfaffian form |
| Manoeuvrability | Ratio of controllable DOF to total DOF | $M_r$ |
| Steerability | Number of independently steered wheels | — |
| Configuration Manifold | The space of all valid robot poses | $\mathcal{C}$ |
| Tangent Space | Space of all admissible instantaneous velocities | $T_q\mathcal{C}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise the Verdict:** A robot is holonomic **if and only if** the tangent space of feasible velocities at configuration $q$ spans the *full* velocity space.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard metric for planar mobility** in KTU valuation: DOF count = **3** for ground robots (x, y, θ).
- **DOF of a rigid body in 3D space:** **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Mecanum wheel diameter (typical industrial):** 100–200 mm.
- **Swedish wheel roll-axis angle:** typically **45°** for omnidirectional designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Backbone Concept

The number of DOF of a mechanism is the number of independent parameters required to completely specify its configuration in space.

### 2.1.1 Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion (Planar Mechanisms)

For a planar linkage:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh)

### 2.1.2 Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial / 3D Mechanisms)

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where $j_i$ denotes joints of $i$ DOF.

## 2.2 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Constraints

### 2.2.1 Holonomic (Configurational) Constraints

Constraints that can be written as a finite algebraic relation between coordinates:

$$f_i(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n, t) = 0$$

These constraints **reduce the dimension** of the configuration space.

### 2.2.2 Non-Holonomic (Velocity / Pfaffian) Constraints

Constraints of the form:

$$a_i^T(q)\,\dot{q} = 0$$

These are **non-integrable**; they do not restrict the reachable configuration space, but they **restrict the instantaneous feasible velocities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Field Robotics:** A wheeled robot in a field can technically reach *any* (x, y, θ) eventually (reachability preserved), but the path it must take is constrained (maneuverability limited). This is the signature of non-holonomy.

## 2.3 Canonical Mobile Robot Constraint Examples

### 2.3.1 Unicycle / Differential Drive

The wheel cannot move sideways:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is non-holonomic. The robot has 3 configuration variables but only 2 controllable velocities (forward speed $v$ and angular speed $\omega$).

### 2.3.2 Car-Like (Ackermann) Robot

Two constraints apply:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{(no lateral slip)}$$

$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta = L\tan\phi \cdot \dot{\theta} \quad \text{(steering linkage)}$$

With only 2 inputs (steering angle $\phi$ and forward velocity $v$), a car has 3 DOF but only 2 controllable — **non-holonomic**.

### 2.3.3 Omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish Wheel) Robot

A 4-wheel mecanum-drive robot has **3 DOF controllable** matching its 3 DOF configuration — **holonomic**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Planar mobility (Kutzbach) | DOF count problems |
| $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Spatial mobility | 3D robot DOF |
| $f_i(q,t) = 0$ | Holonomic constraint | Identifying integrable constraints |
| $a_i^T(q)\dot{q} = 0$ | Non-holonomic / Pfaffian constraint | Wheeled robot motion |
| $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No-sideways-slip constraint | Unicycle / car-like |
| $M_r = n_{inputs} / n_{DOF}$ | Manoeuvrability ratio | Classify robot type |
| $M_r = 1$ | Holonomic | Omnidirectional |
| $0 < M_r < 1$ | Non-holonomic | Car, differential drive |
| $M_r = 0$ | Immobile / fixed | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Examiners award **partial marks** for correctly identifying the constraint as Pfaffian but deduct heavily if the student does *not* explicitly check the non-integrability condition. Always write the test for integrability using the Frobenius theorem on a KTU answer sheet.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Holonomy Classification

- **Path Planning:** Holonomic robots permit *any-time, any-direction* trajectory tracking — critical for **warehouse AGVs** and **hospital delivery bots**.
- **Field Robotics Application:** In unstructured terrain (KTU Module 1 emphasis), legged robots and spherical robots are often designed holonomically to compensate for unpredictable soil dynamics.
- **Control Simplification:** Holonomic systems are easier to control with classical feedback linearisation because input–output mapping is square (well-posed).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DOF Count of a 4-Wheeled Mecanum-Drive Omnidirectional Robot

### Statement
Compute the Degrees of Freedom of a 4-wheeled mecanum-drive mobile robot moving on a planar surface using Kutzbach's criterion.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify all links.**

The robot consists of:
- 1 chassis (moving link)
- 4 mecanum wheels (4 moving links)
- 1 ground (fixed reference link)

$$n = 1 + 4 + 1 = 6 \text{ links}$$

**Step 2 — Identify all lower-pair joints ($j_1$).**

Each mecanum wheel is connected to the chassis through a revolute joint (allowing free rotation). The ground is connected to each wheel through a rolling contact (a higher pair).

$$j_1 = 4 \quad \text{(revolute joints, chassis–wheel)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify all higher-pair joints ($j_2$).**

Each wheel rolls on the ground without slipping (a higher pair, 2-DOF contact: rolling + sliding-free in the lateral direction — but for a mecanum wheel, the lateral slip is *permitted* and is essential for strafing).

$$j_2 = 4 \quad \text{(wheel–ground contacts)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Kutzbach's planar formula.**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 3(6 - 1) - 2(4) - 4$$

$$M = 3 \times 5 - 8 - 4$$

$$M = 15 - 12 = 3$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The robot has 3 DOF in the plane, which corresponds exactly to planar motion variables $(x, y, \theta)$. Since this matches the total number of configuration parameters, the robot is **holonomic** in the plane.

$$\boxed{M = 3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Holonomic in 2D plane}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Manoeuvrability Ratio of a Car-Like (Ackermann) Robot

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify DOF and controllable inputs.**

- **Configuration DOF in plane:** $n = 3$ (namely $x, y, \theta$)
- **Controllable inputs:** $m = 2$ (namely steering angle $\phi$ and forward speed $v$)

**Step 2 — Write the kinematic model.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the manoeuvrability ratio.**

$$M_r = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 4 — Classify.**

Since $0 < M_r < 1$, the car-like robot is **non-holonomic**.

**Step 5 — Show the Pfaffian constraint explicitly.**

Eliminating the controls yields the no-side-slip constraint:

$$-\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} + 0 \cdot \dot{\theta} = 0$$

This is non-integrable. Proof: integrating along a closed loop produces a non-zero net rotation (the famous "parallel-parking" impossibility theorem).

$$\boxed{M_r = \frac{2}{3} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Non-holonomic}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frobenius Test for Non-Integrability of the Unicycle Constraint

**Constraint:** $a(q) = [-\sin\theta, \cos\theta, 0]^T$, with $\dot{q} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$.

**Pfaffian form:** $a^T(q)\dot{q} = 0 \Rightarrow -\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Test

**Step 1 — Compute $da \wedge dq$ (exterior derivative).**

$$da = \begin{bmatrix} -\cos\theta\,d\theta, & -\sin\theta\,d\theta, & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Form the 2-form $da \wedge dq$.**

$$da \wedge dq = (-\cos\theta\,d\theta)\wedge(dx) + (-\sin\theta\,d\theta)\wedge(dy) + 0$$

$$= -\cos\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\,d\theta \wedge dy$$

**Step 3 — Test for exactness.**

A 1-form $\omega$ is integrable iff $d\omega \wedge \omega = 0$. Compute:

$$(da \wedge dq) \wedge (-\sin\theta\,dx + \cos\theta\,dy)$$

After expansion (the cross terms survive):

$$= -\cos\theta(-\sin\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dx - \cos\theta(\cos\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy - \sin\theta(-\sin\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dx - \sin\theta(\cos\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dy$$

$$= -\cos^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy - \sin^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dx$$

$$= -\cos^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy + \sin^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy$$

$$= (\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy$$

**Step 4 — Conclude non-integrability.**

Since $\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -\cos(2\theta) \neq 0$ in general, $d\omega \wedge \omega \neq 0$.

$$\boxed{\text{Constraint is non-holonomic.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Holonomic Robot | Non-Holonomic Robot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manoeuvrability $M_r$ | $1$ | $< 1$ |
| Instantaneous velocity freedom | Full tangent space | Subspace of tangent space |
| Path tracking | Any path possible | Smooth non-adiabatic paths only |
| Reachable configurations | All states reachable | All states reachable but constrained paths |
| Examples | Mecanum, Swedish-wheel, spherical, hovercraft, underwater ROV | Car, unicycle, differential drive, bicycle |
| Control complexity | Lower (square system) | Higher (underactuated) |
| Energy efficiency in turns | Higher (no 3-point turns) | Lower (must maneuver) |
| Field robotics use | Greenhouses, warehouses | Outdoor roads, agricultural tractors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Mobile Robots by Manoeuvrability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mobile Robot in 2D Workspace] --> nodeB{Number of Controllable Inputs m vs Configuration DOF n}
    nodeB -->|m = n = 3| nodeC[Holonomic Robot]
    nodeB -->|m = 2 less than n = 3| nodeD[Non-Holonomic Robot]
    nodeB -->|m = 0| nodeE[Fixed / Immobile]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Mecanum Wheel Drive]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Swedish Wheel Drive]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Spherical / Hovercraft]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Omnidirectional AGV]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Differential Drive]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Ackermann Car-Like]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Unicycle Model]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Bicycle Model]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Strafes, rotates on spot, moves diagonally]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Same manoeuvrability as mecanum]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ1[Cannot strafe sideways]
    nodeK --> nodeK1[Requires multi-point turn]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Holonomic Mecanum Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[High Level Planner] -->|Desired x y theta vx vy omega| nodeB[Trajectory Generator]
    nodeB -->|Setpoints| nodeC[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    nodeC -->|4 wheel velocities w1 w2 w3 w4| nodeD[Motor Driver Layer]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Mecanum Wheel 1]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Mecanum Wheel 2]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Mecanum Wheel 3]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Mecanum Wheel 4]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Holonomic Platform Pose]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI -->|Encoder IMU feedback| nodeJ[State Estimator]
    nodeJ -->|Estimated state| nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The architecture above shows a closed-loop omni-directional mobile platform. Notice that because the system is *square* (4 actuators, 3 DOF output), the inverse kinematics block $C$ is well-posed — a defining advantage of holonomic systems.

## 4.3 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Motion — Vector Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Velocity Command v] --> nodeB{System Type}
    nodeB -->|Holonomic| nodeC[Direct Mapping to vx vy omega]
    nodeB -->|Non Holonomic| nodeD[Constraint Projection]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Feasible Subspace]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Cartesian Twist Output]
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

## 4.4 Field Robotics Use-Case Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Field Robotics Application] --> nodeB[Environment Type]
    nodeB -->|Structured Indoor| nodeC[Warehouse Hospital]
    nodeB -->|Semi Structured| nodeD[Greenhouse Vineyard]
    nodeB -->|Unstructured Outdoor| nodeE[Forest Mine Agricultural Field]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Holonomic AGV preferred]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Holonomic for row navigation]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Non holonomic Ackermann for roads]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Holonomic legged for rough terrain]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a holonomic robot. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> A holonomic robot is a mobile robotic system in which the number of independently controllable Degrees of Freedom equals the total Degrees of Freedom in its task workspace. Equivalently, the set of feasible instantaneous velocities spans the entire tangent space of the configuration manifold, so the system can move in any direction without re-orienting first.
>
> *Examples:*
> 1. **Four-wheeled mecanum-drive omnidirectional robot** — capable of strafing, rotating in place, and translating along arbitrary planar directions.
> 2. **Spherical robot or hovercraft** — has full 3-DOF planar manoeuvrability via directed thrust.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark comparison with DOF + 1 mark for two correct examples)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Pfaffian constraint? Why is the no-side-slip condition of a wheeled robot non-holonomic?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Pfaffian constraint** is a linear homogeneous constraint on velocities of the form $a^T(q)\dot{q} = 0$ that cannot be integrated into a positional (holonomic) constraint $f(q,t) = 0$.
>
> The no-side-slip condition $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is non-holonomic because integrating it around a closed path yields a non-zero net orientation change (the parallel-parking argument), and the Frobenius integrability test gives $d\omega \wedge \omega = -\cos(2\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy \neq 0$.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark Pfaffian definition + 1 mark no-side-slip equation + 1 mark non-integrability justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** With the help of Kutzbach's criterion, derive the Degrees of Freedom of a 4-wheeled mecanum-drive mobile robot. Hence classify it as holonomic or non-holonomic. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A car-like robot has configuration $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and inputs $u = [v, \phi]^T$. Write the kinematic equations and identify the Pfaffian constraints. Apply the Frobenius test to prove one of them is non-holonomic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Apply planar Kutzbach formula.**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the mechanism components.**

$$n = 6, \quad j_1 = 4 \text{ (chassis-wheel revolute joints)}, \quad j_2 = 4 \text{ (wheel-ground contacts)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve.**

$$M = 3(5) - 2(4) - 4 = 15 - 12 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Classify.**

Since the planar mobility $M = 3$ matches the 3 configuration DOF $(x, y, \theta)$, the manoeuvrability ratio $M_r = 1$ and the robot is **holonomic** in the plane.

**(7 marks: 2 marks formula statement + 2 marks enumeration + 1 mark arithmetic + 2 marks classification reasoning)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Write the car-like kinematic model.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Pfaffian constraints.**

Rearranging the no-slip condition:

$$-\sin\theta\,\dot{x} + \cos\theta\,\dot{y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1^T(q)\dot{q} = 0$$

The second constraint is the steering relationship:

$$\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - L\tan\phi\,\dot{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Frobenius test on the no-side-slip constraint.**

Let $\omega = -\sin\theta\,dx + \cos\theta\,dy$. Then:

$$d\omega = -\cos\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\,d\theta \wedge dy$$

Compute $d\omega \wedge \omega$:

$$d\omega \wedge \omega = (-\cos\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\,d\theta \wedge dy) \wedge (-\sin\theta\,dx + \cos\theta\,dy)$$

The non-zero term is:

$$= -\cos^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy - \sin^2\theta\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy \quad \text{(after using anti-symmetry)}$$

$$= -(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)\,d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy = -d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy \neq 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

Since $d\omega \wedge \omega \neq 0$, the constraint is **non-holonomic**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks kinematics + 2 marks Pfaffian identification + 2 marks Frobenius computation + 1 mark conclusion)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with two examples of mobile robots for each class. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For a differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$, wheel base $b$, and wheel angular velocities $\omega_L, \omega_R$, derive the forward kinematics relating wheel speeds to body twist $(v, \omega)$. Hence compute the manoeuvrability ratio and classify the robot. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

| Aspect | Holonomic Constraint | Non-Holonomic Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form | $f(q,t) = 0$ | $a^T(q)\dot{q} = 0$ |
| Integrable? | Yes (finite relation) | No (only velocity relation) |
| Effect on configuration space | Reduces dimension | Preserves dimension but restricts tangent space |
| Example mobile robot | Mecanum-wheeled AGV, hovercraft | Car, differential-drive |

**Two examples each:**
- *Holonomic:* (i) 4-mecanum-wheel AGV, (ii) Swedish-wheel omnidirectional bot.
- *Non-holonomic:* (i) Car-like Ackermann, (ii) Differential-drive.

**(7 marks: 3 marks tabular differentiation + 2 marks holonomic examples + 2 marks non-holonomic examples)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Express body twist in terms of wheel velocities.**

The forward velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of the differential-drive body are:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \\
\omega &= \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Derive the kinematic model.**

Let $(x, y, \theta)$ be the body pose. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Manoeuvrability ratio.**

Configuration DOF $n = 3$ in the plane. Controllable inputs $m = 2$ (the two wheel speeds $\omega_L, \omega_R$).

$$M_r = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Classification.**

Since $0 < M_r < 1$, the differential-drive robot is **non-holonomic**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks twist derivation + 2 marks kinematic model + 1 mark manoeuvrability ratio + 2 marks classification with reasoning)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Holonomic Robot Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to enumerate links correctly** in Kutzbach problems — students often omit the ground link, leading to off-by-one errors in DOF. Always include ground as link 1.
> 2. **Confusing DOF with controllable DOF** — DOF is the geometric parameter count; controllable DOF is the *actuator-driven* subset. Examiners mark this distinction harshly.
> 3. **Skipping the Frobenius test** — simply stating "the constraint is non-holonomic" without proof costs at least **2 of 7 marks** in Part B.
> 4. **Mixing up spatial and planar Kutzbach formulas** — using $M = 6(n-1) - \ldots$ on a 2D problem is a guaranteed **−2 mark** penalty.
> 5. **Ignoring units** in derivations — write SI units (m, rad, m/s) at least once for full marks on numerical sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Holonomic** ⇔ controllable DOF = total DOF ⇔ full tangent space access.
- **Non-holonomic** ⇔ Pfaffian velocity constraint that is non-integrable.
- **Manoeuvrability ratio** $M_r = m / n$ is the fastest classification test.
- **Kutzbach planar formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — memorize the coefficient **3**.
- **Kutzbach spatial formula:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ — memorize the descending sequence **5, 4, 3, 2, 1**.
- **No-side-slip equation:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — a hallmark non-holonomic constraint.
- **Holonomic examples:** mecanum, swedish-wheel, hovercraft, spherical, omnidirectional AGV.
- **Non-holonomic examples:** car, unicycle, differential drive, bicycle, tractor-trailer.
- **Frobenius integrability test:** $d\omega \wedge \omega = 0$ is the *iff* condition for holonomy.
- **Configuration manifold** $\mathcal{C}$ has dimension $n - k$ for $k$ independent holonomic constraints.
- **Field-robotics context:** Agricultural and field robots often use **hybrid designs** — holonomic for in-row navigation, non-holonomic for inter-row transport.
- **Energy and control:** Holonomic ⇒ easier control but heavier actuation; non-holonomic ⇒ fewer actuators but harder trajectory tracking.
- **Reachability vs Manoeuvrability:** Non-holonomic systems can still reach *any* configuration, just not *directly* — keep this distinction crystal clear in theory answers.
- **Always state the workspace dimension** (2D vs 3D) before applying Kutzbach — examiners love deducting marks for this oversight.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning** is the computational process of determining a continuous, collision-free trajectory for a robot from an initial configuration to a goal configuration, while optimizing a defined cost criterion (such as path length, time, energy, or risk) within a known or partially known environment.

In the formal language of **robotics and motion planning**, a path planning problem is mathematically defined as a tuple:

$$\Pi = (C_{free}, \; q_{start}, \; q_{goal}, \; \mathcal{U}, \; J)$$

Where:
- $C_{free} \subset \mathcal{C}$ — the **free configuration space** (set of all valid robot poses)
- $q_{start}$ — initial configuration
- $q_{goal}$ — target (goal) configuration
- $\mathcal{U}$ — admissible control / action set
- $J$ — performance / cost functional to minimize

A **solution path** $\sigma: [0,1] \rightarrow C_{free}$ must satisfy $\sigma(0) = q_{start}$ and $\sigma(1) = q_{goal}$, and $\sigma(t) \in C_{free}$ for all $t \in [0,1]$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Tourist Navigating a New City**
> 
> Imagine you arrive in Kochi for the first time with a paper map. You know where your hotel is (goal) and where you are now (start). The map shows buildings (obstacles) and roads (free space). Your job is to draw a route.
> 
> - **Road network (Graph method)**: If you use Google Maps, the city is pre-converted into a graph of intersections (nodes) and roads (edges), and the algorithm searches this graph using **Dijkstra's** or **A\***.
> - **Random exploration (Sampling method)**: If you have no map and just want to wander, you randomly sample directions and grow a tree, marking where you have been — this is essentially the **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)**.
> - **Terrain cost (Field Robotics)**: In a field robot on a farm, "roads" have varying costs — mud is slow, gravel is fast. The robot's path planning becomes a **weighted optimization**, not just a shortest-distance problem.

## 1.3 Configuration Space (C-Space) — The Foundational Concept

The **configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the set of all possible poses / states it can assume. For a mobile robot on a 2D plane, $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (x, y, and orientation $\theta$).

The space is partitioned into:
- $C_{free}$ — configurations that are **collision-free** and satisfy kinematic constraints
- $C_{obs} = \mathcal{C} \setminus C_{free}$ — configurations in **collision or infeasible**

The robot, after inflation by its radius $r$, is treated as a **point mass** in C-space, transforming a complex geometric problem into a clean point-navigation problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why C-Space?** A 6-DOF robot arm in a 3D workspace has a configuration space of up to 6 dimensions. Planning directly in workspace is intractable for such systems — **C-space abstraction is what makes motion planning computationally feasible**.

## 1.4 Path Planning vs. Trajectory Planning

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. Remember:
> - **Path Planning** → *Geometric*: "Where do I go?" (sequence of configurations)
> - **Trajectory Planning** → *Dynamic*: "How do I move there in time?" (velocities, accelerations, time-parameterization)
> 
> Path planning is a **prerequisite** to trajectory planning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Path Planning Approaches

Path planning algorithms in field robotics fall into three principal families:

| Family | Principle | Representative Algorithms | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Graph / Search-based** | Discretize C-space, search graph | Dijkstra, A\*, D\*, D\*-Lite | Known static maps, low-DOF |
| **Sampling-based** | Random sampling to build roadmap/tree | PRM, RRT, RRT\*, Informed-RRT\* | High-DOF, complex C-spaces |
| **Reactive / Potential field** | Continuous artificial forces | Artificial Potential Field (APF) | Dynamic obstacles, real-time |
| **Optimization-based** | Numerical optimization of cost | TrajOpt, CHOMP, MPC | Smooth, kinodynamic paths |

## 2.2 Graph-Search Methods — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm (1956)
Guarantees **optimal path** by uniformly expanding the node with the lowest cumulative cost $g(n)$ from the start. It is **uninformed** — it has no knowledge of where the goal lies, leading to exhaustive exploration.

### 2.2.2 A\* Algorithm (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael — 1968)
A\* enhances Dijkstra by adding a **heuristic estimate** $h(n)$ that guides the search toward the goal:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

Where:
- $g(n)$ = actual (known) cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal (heuristic)
- $f(n)$ = total estimated cost of cheapest solution through $n$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A\*'s Power**: A\* is **complete** (always finds a solution if one exists) and **optimal** (guarantees minimum-cost path) **IF** the heuristic is **admissible** (never overestimates the true cost). It focuses search toward the goal, exploring far fewer nodes than Dijkstra.

### 2.2.3 Heuristic Properties — Admissibility & Consistency

**Admissibility**: A heuristic $h(n)$ is admissible if for every node $n$:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n)$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to goal.

**Consistency (Monotonicity)**: A heuristic is consistent if for every successor $n'$ of $n$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$$

where $c(n, n')$ is the step cost. Consistency $\Rightarrow$ admissibility (but not vice versa).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Heuristics in 2D Grid Path Planning**:
> - **Manhattan distance**: $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{goal} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{goal} \vert$ — admissible for 4-connected grids
> - **Euclidean distance**: $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{goal})^2 + (y_n - y_{goal})^2}$ — admissible for any continuous plane
> - **Chebyshev distance**: $h(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_{goal} \vert, \vert y_n - y_{goal} \vert)$ — admissible for 8-connected grids

### 2.2.4 Dynamic A\* (D\* and D\*-Lite)
Used in **field robotics** where the environment is partially known and updated by sensors. D\*-Lite efficiently **re-plans** when new obstacles are discovered, reusing previous computations — critical for **unknown terrain exploration**.

## 2.3 Sampling-Based Methods

### 2.3.1 Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) — Kavraki et al. (1996)
A two-phase method:
1. **Learning phase**: Sample $N$ random configurations, connect nearby valid ones with edges.
2. **Query phase**: Connect $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ to the roadmap, then perform graph search.

PRM is **multi-query** — the roadmap is reusable for many start-goal pairs.

### 2.3.2 Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) — LaValle (1998)
A single-query, **incremental** algorithm that grows a tree rooted at $q_{start}$ by:

$$q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\Vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \Vert}$$

where $\Delta$ is a fixed step size. RRT is **probabilistically complete** — probability of finding a solution approaches 1 as iterations $\to \infty$, but the path is generally **suboptimal**.

### 2.3.3 RRT\* (Optimal RRT) — Karaman & Frazzoli (2011)
RRT\* adds two operations that make it **asymptotically optimal**:
1. **Rewire**: After adding $q_{new}$, check nearby nodes; if a cheaper path to them through $q_{new}$ exists, rewire.
2. **Near-neighbor radius** shrinks as $\gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$, ensuring convergence.

## 2.4 Field-Robotics Specific Considerations

Unlike indoor robots, **field robots** face:

| Challenge | Path Planning Impact |
|---|---|
| **Uneven terrain** | Cost map: slope, traction, soil type modify edge weights |
| **GPS / IMU noise** | Localization uncertainty must be considered |
| **Large outdoor scale** | Graphs become huge → sampling methods preferred |
| **Dynamic obstacles** (animals, humans) | Re-planning algorithms (D\*-Lite, MPC) needed |
| **Energy budget** | Path must minimize energy, not just length |
| **Kinodynamic constraints** | Non-holonomic vehicles, slip — pure geometric paths may be infeasible |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **high-yield reference** — memorize these for Part A and Part B.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A\* evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Costs in same metric |
| 2 | Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Required for A\* optimality |
| 3 | Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Implies admissibility |
| 4 | Manhattan distance (4-grid) | $h = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | Dimensionless / cells |
| 5 | Euclidean distance | $h = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Continuous plane |
| 6 | Dijkstra expansion | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Special case $h = 0$ |
| 7 | RRT extension | $q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\Vert \cdot \Vert}$ | $\Delta$ = step size (m) |
| 8 | RRT\* neighbor radius | $r_n = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ | $d$ = C-space dimension |
| 9 | C-space inflation | $C_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ | $\mathcal{A}$ = robot body, $\mathcal{O}$ = obstacles |
| 10 | Path cost (continuous) | $J(\sigma) = \int_0^1 c(\sigma(t)) \, \Vert \dot{\sigma}(t) \Vert \, dt$ | $c$ = cost density |
| 11 | Configuration | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ for 2D mobile robot | $x, y$ in m, $\theta$ in rad |
| 12 | Completeness bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{solution found}) = 1$ | RRT/PRM property |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Configuration Space Inflation for a Circular Robot

**Problem**: A circular robot of radius $r$ operates in a 2D workspace containing point obstacles. Derive the C-space obstacle representation.

**Step 1: Define workspace and robot**
Let the robot be a disk of radius $r$ centered at $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. The obstacle region in the workspace is $\mathcal{O}_{ws} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 2: Define C-space obstacle**
The robot in configuration $q = (x, y)$ collides with workspace obstacle $\mathcal{O}_{ws}$ if the robot's body intersects $\mathcal{O}_{ws}$. Mathematically:

$$
\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_{ws} \neq \emptyset
$$

**Step 3: Geometric translation — Minkowski sum**
Since the robot is a disk of radius $r$, the set of center positions that cause collision is the **Minkowski sum** of $\mathcal{O}_{ws}$ with the disk $D(0, r)$:

$$
C_{obs} = \mathcal{O}_{ws} \oplus D(0, r) = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \text{dist}(p, \mathcal{O}_{ws}) \le r \}
$$

This means **every obstacle is uniformly grown by $r$**, and the robot is reduced to a **point**.

**Step 4: Resulting free space**

$$
C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus C_{obs}
$$

Now the path planning problem reduces to navigating a point through $C_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$. The 2D distance check is:

$$
\text{If } \sqrt{(x - x_o)^2 + (y - y_o)^2} \le r + r_o \quad \Rightarrow \text{ collision}
$$

where $(x_o, y_o)$ is the obstacle center and $r_o$ is its radius.

> [!NOTE]
> This reduction is the **single most important conceptual step** in path planning — it transforms a complex body-geometry problem into a simple point-navigation problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — A\* Optimality from Admissible Heuristic

**Claim**: If $h(n)$ is admissible, A\* returns an optimal path.

**Setup**: Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the goal node. Let $C^*$ be the optimal path cost from $q_{start}$ to $\mathcal{G}$. Suppose for contradiction A\* terminates with a non-optimal goal node $\mathcal{G}'$ where $f(\mathcal{G}') = g(\mathcal{G}') > C^*$.

**Step 1: Open list at termination**
At termination, the goal node $\mathcal{G}'$ is removed from the open list. Any unexpanded node $n$ in the open list satisfies $f(n) \geq f(\mathcal{G}') > C^*$.

**Step 2: Apply admissibility to optimal node**
Let $n^*$ be the **first node along the optimal path** that is still in the open list. Since $n^*$ is on the optimal path:

$$
g(n^*) \le C^*
$$

Because $h$ is admissible, $h(n^*) \le h^*(n^*)$, so:

$$
f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) \le g(n^*) + h^*(n^*) = C^*
$$

**Step 3: Contradiction**
But we showed $f(n^*) > C^*$. Contradiction. Therefore A\* must expand the optimal goal before terminating. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RRT Step Calculation (Numerical)

**Scenario**: A 2D mobile robot at $q_{near} = (2, 3)$ samples a random point $q_{rand} = (7, 11)$. The RRT step size is $\Delta = 2$ m. Find $q_{new}$.

**Step 1: Compute the direction vector**

$$
\vec{d} = q_{rand} - q_{near} = (7 - 2, \; 11 - 3) = (5, 8)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the magnitude**

$$
\Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{25 + 64} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.434
$$

**Step 3: Normalize and scale by step size**

$$
\hat{d} = \frac{\vec{d}}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert} = \left( \frac{5}{9.434}, \; \frac{8}{9.434} \right) \approx (0.530, 0.848)
$$

**Step 4: Compute new node**

$$
q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta \cdot \hat{d} = (2, 3) + 2 \cdot (0.530, 0.848)
$$

$$
q_{new} = (2 + 1.060, \; 3 + 1.696) = (3.060, \; 4.696)
$$

**Step 5: Collision check**
The edge from $(2, 3)$ to $(3.060, 4.696)$ must be checked against $C_{obs}$. If it intersects any obstacle region, the step is rejected and a new sample is drawn.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost Calculation for Field Robot Traversal

**Scenario**: A field robot must traverse a 50 m field. Section A (0–20 m) has dry soil with cost coefficient $c_1 = 1.2$, and Section B (20–50 m) has wet soil with $c_2 = 2.5$. The robot's speed in dry soil is $v_1 = 1.0$ m/s, and in wet soil is $v_2 = 0.5$ m/s. Compute total cost and total time.

**Step 1: Time in section A**

$$
t_1 = \frac{20}{v_1} = \frac{20}{1.0} = 20 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Time in section B**

$$
t_2 = \frac{30}{v_2} = \frac{30}{0.5} = 60 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Total traversal time**

$$
T_{total} = t_1 + t_2 = 20 + 60 = 80 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Energy-weighted cost**

$$
J = c_1 \cdot d_1 + c_2 \cdot d_2 = 1.2 \times 20 + 2.5 \times 30 = 24 + 75 = 99 \; \text{cost units}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real field robotics, the cost coefficient is often derived from **energy per unit distance** based on wheel slip, slope, and soil compaction (Bekker's terramechanics model).

## 3.5 A\* Trace Example — Worked Numerical Walkthrough

**Grid** (3×3): $S$ = start at $(0,0)$, $G$ = goal at $(2,2)$, `#` = obstacle at $(1,1)$. Movement: 4-connected, step cost = 1. Heuristic: Manhattan.

**Initial state**: $g(S)=0$, $h(S)=4$, $f(S)=4$.

| Step | Expanded | Open list updates | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S(0,0)$ | $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$ | 1, 1 | 3, 3 | 4, 4 |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | $(2,0)$, $(1,1)$ blocked, $(0,1)$ | 2, 1 | 2, 3 | 4, 4 |
| 3 | $(0,1)$ | $(1,1)$ blocked, $(0,2)$ | 1, 2 | 1, 2 | 2, 4 |
| 4 | $(1,0)$ already closed | — | — | — | — |
| 5 | $(2,0)$ | $(2,1)$, $(0,2)$ | 2, 2 | 1, 2 | 3, 4 |
| 6 | $(1,2)$ via $(0,1) \to (1,1)$ blocked... | $(1,2)$ via $(0,2)$ | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| 7 | $(2,1)$ | $(2,2)$ = goal | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| 8 | $(2,2)$ = G | **TERMINATE** | 4 | 0 | 4 |

**Optimal path**: $S \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (2,1) \to G$, with total cost $g(G) = 4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight**: When asked to "trace A\*", always maintain (a) the open list, (b) the closed list, and (c) the parent pointers. Marks are awarded separately for each.

## 3.6 RRT\* Rewire Computation (Sketch)

After adding $q_{new}$, RRT\* identifies $k$ nearest neighbors within radius $r_n$. For each neighbor $q_{nb}$, it computes the candidate cost:

$$
g_{candidate}(q_{nb}) = g(q_{new}) + \Vert q_{new} - q_{nb} \Vert
$$

If $g_{candidate}(q_{nb}) < g(q_{nb})$, then **rewire** $q_{nb}$'s parent to $q_{new}$. This gradually reduces path cost toward optimality as $n \to \infty$.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Path Planning Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PATH PLANNING]:::root --> B[GRAPH SEARCH BASED]
    A --> C[SAMPLING BASED]
    A --> D[REACTIVE BASED]
    A --> E[OPTIMIZATION BASED]

    B --> B1[Dijkstra]
    B --> B2[A Star]
    B --> B3[D Star Lite]
    B --> B4[BFS DFS]

    C --> C1[PRM]
    C --> C2[RRT]
    C --> C3[RRT Star]
    C --> C4[Informed RRT Star]

    D --> D1[Artificial Potential Field]
    D --> D2[Vector Field Histogram]
    D --> D3[DWA]

    E --> E1[TrajOpt]
    E --> E2[CHOMP]
    E --> E3[MPC]

    classDef root fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 A\* Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PROBLEM INPUT]:::input --> S[INITIALIZE OPEN LIST WITH START NODE]
    S --> C[CLOSED LIST IS EMPTY]
    C --> L1{IS OPEN LIST EMPTY}
    L1 -->|YES| FAIL[NO PATH EXISTS RETURN FAILURE]
    L1 -->|NO| SEL[SELECT NODE N WITH LOWEST F SCORE]
    SEL --> CHK{IS N THE GOAL NODE}
    CHK -->|YES| SUCC[RECONSTRUCT PATH VIA PARENT POINTERS]
    CHK -->|NO| ADD[ADD N TO CLOSED LIST]
    ADD --> EXP[FOR EACH NEIGHBOR M OF N]
    EXP --> COST[COMPUTE G TENTATIVE AND F TENTATIVE]
    COST --> T1{M IN CLOSED AND G TENTATIVE GREATER}
    T1 -->|YES| SKIP[SKIP M]
    T1 -->|NO| T2{M IN OPEN AND G TENTATIVE GREATER}
    T2 -->|YES| UPD[UPDATE G AND F OF M]
    T2 -->|NO| INS[INSERT M INTO OPEN LIST]
    UPD --> LOOP[RETURN TO SELECT NODE STEP]
    INS --> LOOP
    SKIP --> LOOP
    SUCC --> OUT[RETURN OPTIMAL PATH]
    FAIL --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#0d6b3d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 RRT Tree-Growth Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT[INITIALIZE TREE T WITH Q START]:::phase1 --> LOOP{ITERATION LESS THAN N MAX}
    LOOP -->|NO| FAILRRT[RETURN FAILURE]
    LOOP -->|YES| SAMP[SAMPLE Q RAND UNIFORMLY FROM C SPACE]
    SAMP --> NEAR[FIND Q NEAR NEAREST NODE IN T]
    NEAR --> STEER[STEER FROM Q NEAR TOWARD Q RAND BY STEP DELTA]
    STEER --> COLL{CHECK COLLISION Q NEW IN C FREE}
    COLL -->|NO| REJ[REJECT AND RESAMPLE]
    COLL -->|YES| ADDNODE[ADD Q NEW TO T WITH EDGE FROM Q NEAR]
    ADDNODE --> RCHK{IS RRT STAR}
    RCHK -->|NO| GOALCHK
    RCHK -->|YES| NEIGH[FIND NEIGHBORS OF Q NEW WITHIN R N]
    NEIGH --> REW[RECONNECT TO MIN COST NEIGHBOR AND REWIRE]
    REW --> GOALCHK{GOAL REACHED}
    REJ --> LOOP
    GOALCHK -->|NO| LOOP
    GOALCHK -->|YES| RETR[RECONSTRUCT PATH FROM GOAL TO START]
    RETR --> OUTRRT[RETURN SOLUTION PATH]
    FAILRRT --> OUTRRT

    classDef phase1 fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Field-Robotics Path Planning System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[SENSORS GPS IMU LIDAR CAMERA]:::sensor --> PERC[PERCEPTION MODULE]
    MAP[PRIOR MAP TERRAIN MODEL]:::map --> PERC
    PERC --> LOC[LOCALIZATION AND STATE ESTIMATION]
    LOC --> CSPACE[CONFIGURATION SPACE BUILDER]
    CSPACE --> PATH[PATH PLANNER A STAR RRT STAR]
    GOAL[GOAL INPUT]:::goal --> PATH
    PATH --> TRAJ[TRAJECTORY GENERATOR]
    TRAJ --> CTRL[LOW LEVEL CONTROLLER]
    CTRL --> ACT[ACTUATORS MOTORS STEERING]
    ACT --> ROBOT[FIELD ROBOT]
    ROBOT -.->|FEEDBACK| SENS
    TERRAIN[TERRAIN COST MAP]:::terrain --> CSPACE
    OBST[OBSTACLE DETECTION]:::obs --> CSPACE

    classDef sensor fill:#0d4d6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef map fill:#6b4d0d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef goal fill:#6b0d4d,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```
