1. **Prediction (Motion Update):** For every existing hypothesis $k$, propagate the state forward using the robot's motion model $p(\xi_t \mid u_t, \xi_{t-1})$.
2. **Measurement Prediction:** Compute the **expected observation** for each hypothesis branch based on the predicted state and the stored map.
3. **Validation Gating:** Compute the **Mahalanobis distance** between the actual measurement $z_t$ and each predicted measurement $\hat{z}_t^{(k)}$; reject pairings with $D_M > \tau$.
4. **Hypothesis Branching:** For every gated pairing $(k, j)$, create a **new branch** representing "this measurement originated from landmark $j$ in hypothesis $k$."
5. **Bayesian Re-weighting:** Update the probability of each branch using the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)}, j)$.
6. **Hypothesis Management:** Apply **pruning** (discard $p^{(k)} < p_{\min}$), **merging** (combine branches that are statistically indistinguishable), and **capping** (retain only the top $K_{\max}$ branches).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these stages?** Each stage mirrors a *cognitive* decision a human operator would make. Branching corresponds to "considering alternatives"; pruning to "ignoring nonsense"; merging to "two roads that lead to the same place."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $B_t = \{(\xi_t^{(k)}, p^{(k)})\}_{k=1}^{K_t}$ | Mixture belief representation | Compact state description |
| 2 | $\sum_{k=1}^{K_t} p^{(k)} = 1$ | Probability normalization | Ensures valid PDF |
| 3 | $p^{(k)}_t = \eta \cdot \Lambda^{(k)} \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}$ | Hypothesis re-weighting | Bayesian update step |
| 4 | $\Lambda^{(k)} = p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)})$ | Measurement likelihood | Sensor-model evaluation |
| 5 | $D_M = \sqrt{(z-\hat{z})^T S^{-1} (z-\hat{z})}$ | Mahalanobis gating | Outlier rejection |
| 6 | $p_{jk} = \frac{1}{c} p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)}, j) \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}$ | Joint association probability | Per-landmark weight |
| 7 | $H_{total} = \prod_{t=1}^{T} (1 + n_t \cdot \beta)$ | Worst-case hypothesis count | Complexity analysis |
| 8 | $\eta = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K_t} \Lambda^{(k)} p^{(k)}_{t-1} \right)^{-1}$ | Normalizer | PDF enforcement |
| 9 | $\text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_k p^{(k)} \log \frac{p^{(k)}}{q^{(k)}}$ | Hypothesis dissimilarity (Kullback–Leibler) | Branch-merging criterion |
| 10 | $\xi_{MMSE} = \sum_{k=1}^{K_t} p^{(k)} \, \xi^{(k)}$ | Minimum Mean-Square Error estimate | Best single estimate output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **MMSE estimate** in row 10 is what the robot actually *uses* for motion control, even though it maintains many internal hypotheses. Examiners frequently ask: "Given the hypothesis set, compute the MMSE pose."

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiple-hypothesis belief is the **de-facto standard** in:
- **Autonomous agricultural tractors** (row-following when GPS is patchy under tree canopies),
- **Underground mine mapping robots** (perceptual aliasing in dark tunnels),
- **Search-and-rescue UAVs** (locating victims in collapsed buildings),
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers use MHT-style data association),
- **Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)** (facing sonar aliasing).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Posterior of the Mixture Belief

We start from the **Chapman–Kolmogorov** prediction and apply **Bayes' theorem** for a discrete mixture of hypotheses.

### Step 1 — Belief before incorporating measurement $z_t$

The prior mixture belief is:

$$
B_{t\mid t-1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K_{t-1}} p^{(k)}_{t-1} \cdot \delta(\xi_t - \xi_t^{(k)})
$$

Here $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta (idealized) or, in practice, a Gaussian kernel centered on $\xi_t^{(k)}$.

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule conditioned on hypothesis $k$

For a single branch $k$, the posterior after observing $z_t$ is:

$$
p(\xi_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, k) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid \xi_t, k) \cdot p(\xi_t \mid u_t, k) \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}
$$

### Step 3 — Marginalize over hypothesis index

The complete posterior is the mixture:

$$
p(\xi_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K_t} p(z_t \mid \xi_t, k) \cdot p(\xi_t \mid u_t, k) \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the normalizer $\eta$

The normalizer is the total probability of receiving $z_t$:

$$
\eta = \left( \int p(z_t \mid \xi_t) \sum_{k=1}^{K_t} p(\xi_t \mid u_t, k) \, p^{(k)}_{t-1} \, d\xi_t \right)^{-1}
$$

In the discrete-mixture representation, this reduces to a finite sum:

$$
\eta = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K_t} \Lambda^{(k)} \, p^{(k)}_{t-1} \right)^{-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Define the new hypothesis weight

The new weight assigned to branch $k$ is:

$$
\boxed{\, p^{(k)}_{t} = \frac{ \Lambda^{(k)} \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1} }{ \sum_{j=1}^{K_t} \Lambda^{(j)} \cdot p^{(j)}_{t-1} } \,}
$$

This is the **core KTU result** — the closed-form update law for hypothesis probabilities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Joint Data-Association Posterior

When measurement $z_t$ may correspond to any of $n_t$ candidate landmarks, we augment the hypothesis to include the association variable $j$.

### Step 1 — Posterior over (state, association)

$$
p(\xi_t, j \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid \xi_t, j) \cdot p(\xi_t \mid u_t) \cdot p(j)
$$

### Step 2 — Marginalize over $j$ to recover the mixture

$$
p(\xi_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n_t} p(z_t \mid \xi_t, j) \cdot p(\xi_t \mid u_t) \cdot p(j)
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the per-association weight

The probability of the combined hypothesis "(state $k$, association $j$)" becomes:

$$
p_{jk} = \frac{1}{c} \, p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)}, j) \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}
$$

with normalizer:

$$
c = \sum_{j'=1}^{n_t} \sum_{k'=1}^{K_{t-1}} p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k')}, j') \cdot p^{(k')}_{t-1}
$$

### Step 4 — Hypothesis-explosion analysis

If every measurement creates $n_t$ branches and every branch produces $n_{t+1}$ branches at the next step, the worst-case hypothesis count grows as:

$$
\boxed{\, K_{max}(T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} n_t \,}
$$

For $n_t = 4$ landmarks and $T = 20$ scans, this yields $4^{20} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{12}$ branches — **computationally infeasible** without pruning/merging.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Mahalanobis Gating Derivation

### Step 1 — Innovation vector

For hypothesis $k$ paired with landmark $j$:

$$
\nu^{(k,j)}_t = z_t - \hat{z}^{(k,j)}_t
$$

### Step 2 — Innovation covariance

$$
S^{(k,j)}_t = H^{(k,j)} \, P^{(k)}_t \, (H^{(k,j)})^T + R_t
$$

where $H$ is the measurement Jacobian and $R_t$ is sensor noise covariance.

### Step 3 — Squared Mahalanobis distance

$$
\boxed{\, (D_M^{(k,j)})^2 = (\nu^{(k,j)}_t)^T \, (S^{(k,j)}_t)^{-1} \, \nu^{(k,j)}_t \,}
$$

### Step 4 — Gating test

A measurement-landmark pair is **accepted** into hypothesis $k$ only if:

$$
(D_M^{(k,j)})^2 \;\leq\; \tau_g^2
$$

For a $\chi^2$ distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom, the threshold $\tau_g$ is chosen so that the gating probability is $1 - P_{FA}$. For $d = 2$ and $P_{FA} = 0.01$, $\tau_g \approx 5.0$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A field robot observes **two candidate landmarks** in its camera view. The prior hypothesis set is:

$$
B_{t-1} = \{ (\xi^{(1)}, 0.7), \ (\xi^{(2)}, 0.3) \}
$$

The measurement likelihoods computed are:

$$
\Lambda^{(1,1)} = 0.8, \quad \Lambda^{(1,2)} = 0.1, \quad \Lambda^{(2,1)} = 0.2, \quad \Lambda^{(2,2)} = 0.9
$$

Compute the new hypothesis weights after the update.

### Solution

The unnormalized weights (hypothesis $\times$ association pairs):

$$
w_{1,1} = 0.8 \times 0.7 = 0.56
$$
$$
w_{1,2} = 0.1 \times 0.7 = 0.07
$$
$$
w_{2,1} = 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.06
$$
$$
w_{2,2} = 0.9 \times 0.3 = 0.27
$$

The normalizer:

$$
c = 0.56 + 0.07 + 0.06 + 0.27 = 0.96
$$

The normalized hypothesis weights:

$$
p^{(1,1)} = \frac{0.56}{0.96} = 0.5833
$$
$$
p^{(1,2)} = \frac{0.07}{0.96} = 0.0729
$$
$$
p^{(2,1)} = \frac{0.06}{0.96} = 0.0625
$$
$$
p^{(2,2)} = \frac{0.27}{0.96} = 0.2813
$$

**Sum-check:** $0.5833 + 0.0729 + 0.0625 + 0.2813 = 1.0000$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The dominant hypothesis is *"(state 1, landmark 1)"* with 58.33% probability. The robot has **deferred commitment** to a single association but the MMSE pose is dominated by $\xi^{(1)}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The MHB Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Cycle t] --> B[Read Odometry u_t]
    B --> C[Predict Hypothesis States xi_t for all k]
    C --> D[Acquire Sensor Measurement z_t]
    D --> E[Compute Mahalanobis Gate for Each Pair]
    E --> F{Measurement Inside Gate?}
    F -- No --> G[Reject Pair Increment False Alarm Hypothesis]
    F -- Yes --> H[Create New Hypothesis Branch]
    H --> I[Evaluate Measurement Likelihood Lambda]
    I --> J[Bayesian Re-weight All Hypotheses]
    J --> K[Apply Pruning Threshold p_min]
    K --> L[Apply Merging Criterion KL Divergence]
    L --> M[Cap to K_max Branches]
    M --> N[Compute MMSE Estimate for Control]
    N --> O[Output Pose to Motion Controller]
    O --> P[Increment t and Loop]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hypothesis Management Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        IMU[IMU Odometry]
        LIDAR[LiDAR Scan]
        CAM[Camera Features]
    end

    subgraph PredictStage["Prediction Stage"]
        PredF[State Predictor]
        PredB[Hypothesis Propagator]
    end

    subgraph AssocStage["Data Association Stage"]
        Gate[Mahalanobis Gate]
        Branch[Hypothesis Brancher]
    end

    subgraph UpdateStage["Bayesian Update Stage"]
        Likeli[Measurement Likelihood]
        Norm[Normalizer eta]
        Weight[Weight Updater]
    end

    subgraph ManageStage["Hypothesis Management"]
        Prune[Pruner]
        Merge[Merger]
        Cap[Cap to K_max]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage["Output Stage"]
        MMSE[MMSE Pose]
        BestHyp[Best Hypothesis]
        AllHyp[Full Hypothesis Set]
    end

    IMU --> PredF
    LIDAR --> Gate
    CAM --> Gate
    PredF --> PredB
    PredB --> Branch
    Gate --> Branch
    Branch --> Likeli
    Likeli --> Norm
    Norm --> Weight
    Weight --> Prune
    Prune --> Merge
    Merge --> Cap
    Cap --> MMSE
    Cap --> BestHyp
    Cap --> AllHyp
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree Diagram — Hypothesis Tree Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[H0 Initial Prior p 1.0] --> H1A[H1A at t 1 p 0.6]
    R0 --> H1B[H1B at t 1 p 0.4]
    H1A --> H2A1[H2A1 at t 2 p 0.35]
    H1A --> H2A2[H2A2 at t 2 p 0.25]
    H1B --> H2B1[H2B1 at t 2 p 0.20]
    H1B --> H2B2[H2B2 at t 2 p 0.15]
    H2A2 -.Prune.- X1[Discarded p less than p_min]
    H2B2 -.Prune.- X2[Discarded p less than p_min]
```

## 4.4 Diagram Explanation

- The **flowchart (4.1)** captures the **per-cycle algorithmic loop** executed by a field robot. Every block corresponds to a software module typically implemented in C++/ROS nodes.
- The **block diagram (4.2)** separates the system into **five functional stages** (Prediction, Association, Update, Management, Output), which is the standard architecture taught in the KTU 2024 robotics curriculum.
- The **tree diagram (4.3)** illustrates the *exponential* growth of hypothesis branches and the **pruning step** that keeps the system tractable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** In a Mars-rover-class implementation (e.g., NASA JPL's vision-based MHT), $K_{\max} = 50$ is common, and pruning is applied every 5–10 cycles to bound memory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief** in the context of field robotics. Why is it preferred over single-hypothesis estimation in perceptually aliased environments?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multiple-Hypothesis Belief (MHB) is a probabilistic state-estimation technique in which a field robot maintains **more than one concurrent hypothesis** about its state, each weighted by a probability reflecting its plausibility given the sensor history.

It is preferred over single-hypothesis estimation (e.g., plain EKF) in perceptually aliased environments because:
1. **Robustness to data-association ambiguity** — the robot does not commit prematurely to a wrong landmark match (1 mark).
2. **Deferment of decisions** — the robot waits for additional evidence to discriminate among hypotheses (1 mark).
3. **Recovery from wrong initial guesses** — even if the highest-probability hypothesis is wrong, lower-probability alternatives are preserved (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** List and briefly explain the **three hypothesis-management operations** used in a Multiple-Hypothesis Belief filter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pruning** — Hypotheses whose probability falls below a threshold $p_{\min}$ (typically $10^{-3}$) are discarded to bound computation (1 mark).
2. **Merging** — Hypotheses that are statistically indistinguishable (e.g., KL divergence below a threshold) are combined into a single branch (1 mark).
3. **Capping** — Only the top $K_{\max}$ hypotheses (by probability) are retained, ensuring bounded memory (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Module 2, ESE Pattern*

**(a)** Derive the **Bayesian update rule** for hypothesis probabilities in a multiple-hypothesis filter. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field robot in an orchard maintains two pose hypotheses: $H_1$ (probability = 0.65) and $H_2$ (probability = 0.35). It now observes a tree. The likelihood of the measurement under $H_1$ is $0.9$ and under $H_2$ is $0.2$. Compute the **posterior hypothesis probabilities** and the **MMSE pose estimate**, given $\xi^{(1)} = (2, 3)$ m and $\xi^{(2)} = (10, 1)$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Assumption 1 (1 mark):** Hypotheses are mutually exclusive and exhaustive:
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K_t} p^{(k)}_t = 1
$$

**Assumption 2 (1 mark):** The measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of past measurements given the current state $\xi_t^{(k)}$.

**Assumption 3 (1 mark):** The prior $p^{(k)}_{t-1}$ is known from the previous cycle.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For branch $k$, applying Bayes' rule:

$$
p^{(k)}_t = p(\xi_t^{(k)} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)}) \cdot p(\xi_t^{(k)} \mid u_t) \cdot p^{(k)}_{t-1}
$$

Define the measurement likelihood $\Lambda^{(k)} = p(z_t \mid \xi_t^{(k)})$. The unnormalized weight is $\tilde{p}^{(k)} = \Lambda^{(k)} p^{(k)}_{t-1}$. The normalizer is:

$$
\eta = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{K_t} \Lambda^{(j)} p^{(j)}_{t-1} \right)^{-1}
$$

Hence the closed-form update:

$$
\boxed{\, p^{(k)}_t = \frac{\Lambda^{(k)} p^{(k)}_{t-1}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K_t} \Lambda^{(j)} p^{(j)}_{t-1}} \,}
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the unnormalized weights.

$$
\tilde{p}^{(1)} = 0.9 \times 0.65 = 0.585
$$
$$
\tilde{p}^{(2)} = 0.2 \times 0.35 = 0.070
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the normalizer.

$$
c = 0.585 + 0.070 = 0.655
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the posterior weights.

$$
p^{(1)} = \frac{0.585}{0.655} = 0.8931
$$
$$
p^{(2)} = \frac{0.070}{0.655} = 0.1069
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the MMSE pose.

$$
\xi_{MMSE} = 0.8931 \times (2, 3) + 0.1069 \times (10, 1)
$$
$$
= (1.7862, 2.6793) + (1.069, 0.1069)
$$
$$
\boxed{\, \xi_{MMSE} = (2.855, \, 2.786) \text{ m} \,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Module 2, ESE Pattern*

**(a)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, describe the architecture of a **Multiple-Hypothesis Tracker (MHT)** used in field-robot SLAM. Explain each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LiDAR-equipped field robot observes a measurement. Three hypotheses $H_1, H_2, H_3$ have prior weights $0.5, 0.3, 0.2$. The measurement likelihoods are $\Lambda^{(1)} = 0.6$, $\Lambda^{(2)} = 0.9$, $\Lambda^{(3)} = 0.1$. After the update, apply **pruning with $p_{\min} = 0.05$** and **capping with $K_{\max} = 2$**, and report the surviving hypotheses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The MHT architecture (refer to block diagram 4.2 in Section 4) consists of **five stages**:

1. **Prediction Stage (1.5 marks):** Propagates each hypothesis forward in time using the motion model $p(\xi_t \mid u_t, \xi_{t-1})$; this is computed independently for every active branch.
2. **Data Association / Gating Stage (1.5 marks):** For each new measurement $z_t$, the Mahalanobis distance to every predicted landmark is computed; only pairings with $D_M \le \tau_g$ are accepted, the rest increment a "false-alarm" hypothesis.
3. **Branching Stage (1 mark):** For every accepted pairing, a new hypothesis branch is spawned, representing the assumption "this measurement is landmark $j$ in state $k$."
4. **Bayesian Update Stage (1.5 marks):** Each branch's weight is re-computed using the measurement likelihood, and the weights are normalized so they sum to 1.
5. **Hypothesis Management Stage (1.5 marks):** Pruning (discard $p^{(k)} < p_{\min}$), merging (combine statistically similar branches), and capping (retain only the top $K_{\max}$) are applied to keep the system computationally tractable.

**Engineering utility (not separately marked but expected):** The MMSE estimate (or MAP estimate) is output to the motion controller.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the unnormalized weights.

$$
\tilde{p}^{(1)} = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30
$$
$$
\tilde{p}^{(2)} = 0.9 \times 0.3 = 0.27
$$
$$
\tilde{p}^{(3)} = 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the normalizer.

$$
c = 0.30 + 0.27 + 0.02 = 0.59
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the posterior weights.

$$
p^{(1)} = \frac{0.30}{0.59} = 0.5085
$$
$$
p^{(2)} = \frac{0.27}{0.59} = 0.4576
$$
$$
p^{(3)} = \frac{0.02}{0.59} = 0.0339
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply pruning with $p_{\min} = 0.05$.

$$
p^{(3)} = 0.0339 < 0.05 \Rightarrow \text{Discard } H_3
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Renormalize the remaining two.

$$
c' = 0.5085 + 0.4576 = 0.9661
$$
$$
p^{(1)} = \frac{0.5085}{0.9661} = 0.5264
$$
$$
p^{(2)} = \frac{0.4576}{0.9661} = 0.4736
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply capping with $K_{\max} = 2$. Both surviving branches are kept (no further cap is needed).

**Surviving hypotheses (final answer):**

$$
\boxed{\, p^{(1)} = 0.5264, \quad p^{(2)} = 0.4736 \,}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in MHB questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer** — many students compute $\Lambda^{(k)} p^{(k)}_{t-1}$ but forget to divide by the sum. The resulting weights will not sum to 1, and the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not applying pruning/merging/capping** — these are integral parts of MHB. A complete answer must mention all three management operations.
> 3. **Confusing MMSE with MAP** — MMSE = probability-weighted average; MAP = argmax. Examiners distinguish these.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement** — without conditional independence and mutual exclusivity stated, the derivation is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring exponential hypothesis growth** — if asked "what is the limitation?", the answer must include $O(n_t^T)$ explosion and the role of pruning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Multiple-Hypothesis Belief (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2):**

- ☐ **Definition** — A belief represented as a discrete mixture $\{(\xi^{(k)}, p^{(k)})\}_{k=1}^{K_t}$ with $\sum_k p^{(k)} = 1$.
- ☐ **Bayesian Update Law** — $p^{(k)}_t = \dfrac{\Lambda^{(k)} p^{(k)}_{t-1}}{\sum_j \Lambda^{(j)} p^{(j)}_{t-1}}$ — *must memorize the full formula.*
- ☐ **Six Per-Cycle Stages** — Predict, Gate, Branch, Re-weight, Manage, Output.
- ☐ **Three Management Operations** — Pruning ($p_{\min}$), Merging (KL divergence), Capping ($K_{\max}$).
- ☐ **Mahalanobis Gating** — $D_M^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu \le \tau_g^2$ with $\tau_g \approx 5.0$ for $P_{FA} = 0.01$ in 2-D.
- ☐ **MMSE Estimate** — $\xi_{MMSE} = \sum_k p^{(k)} \xi^{(k)}$ — *what the controller actually uses.*
- ☐ **Worst-Case Complexity** — $K(T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} n_t$ — *exponential in time.*
- ☐ **Field-Robotics Applications** — GPS-denied orchards, mines, disaster zones, planetary rovers, AUVs.
- ☐ **Comparison** — MHB > EKF in aliasing; MHB < EKF in computation; **trade-off is real-time feasibility.**
- ☐ **Standard numerical threshold values** — $p_{\min} = 0.001$, $K_{\max} = 5$–$20$, $P_{FA} = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differences between Markov and Kalman Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differences between Markov and Kalman Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position and orientation) relative to a given map of the environment, using onboard sensors and prior motion data. In field robotics, two probabilistic paradigms dominate the literature: **Markov Localization (ML)** and **Kalman Filter Localization (KFL)**, also known as the *Gaussian Localization* framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Localization** is a recursive Bayesian filtering approach in which the robot's belief about its pose is represented as a *complete probability distribution* over *all possible poses* in the environment. It assumes the Markov property: the future state depends only on the present state, not on the sequence of events that preceded it. ML can be implemented in **discrete (grid-based)** or **continuous (topological)** form and is **multi-modal**, meaning it can simultaneously represent the robot's belief being spread across multiple distinct locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kalman Filter Localization** is a special case of Bayesian filtering in which the belief distribution is *constrained to a single, unimodal Gaussian*. The robot's pose is represented by a mean vector $\mu$ and a covariance matrix $\Sigma$. Because the state space is continuous and the distributions remain Gaussian under linear-Gaussian assumptions, KFL is computationally efficient but cannot represent global uncertainty or recover from localization failures without external intervention.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lost Tourist in a New City":**
>
> Imagine a tourist who has just landed in a foreign city with no prior knowledge of where their hotel is located.
>
> - **Markov Localization** behaves like the tourist having a *city map sprinkled with a probability mass at every street corner*. The mass is high at corners that look consistent with the last street sign they saw, and low elsewhere. If they get confused, the cloud can split into *two or more* probability peaks — for example, "I am either near the red museum OR near the red church, because both look similar." The tourist is **globally localized** because uncertainty is spread across the whole city.
>
> - **Kalman Filter Localization** behaves like the tourist having *one single bell-shaped (Gaussian) blob* centered at their best guess, like a GPS confidence ellipse. The blob shrinks as they take more measurements, but it can never split into multiple peaks. If the tourist becomes disoriented, that single Gaussian can drift away from the true hotel, and the tourist may become **lost forever** without external help.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The key quantitative descriptors used throughout this topic are:

- **Belief $Bel(x_t)$** — probability mass/density function over robot pose $x_t$ at time $t$.
- **Mean $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$** — expected pose (dimension $n=2$ for planar $(x,y)$ or $n=3$ for $(x,y,\theta)$).
- **Covariance $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$** — symmetric positive-definite matrix representing localization uncertainty.
- **Process noise $R \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$** — covariance of motion model uncertainty.
- **Measurement noise $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$** — covariance of sensor noise (range, bearing, etc.).
- **Position Accuracy Threshold** — typically **±0.05 m to ±0.30 m** for indoor field robotics, **±0.50 m to ±2.00 m** for outdoor GPS-denied terrain.
- **Update rate** — typically **10 Hz to 50 Hz** for real-time field deployments.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Markov Localization

Markov Localization operates as a *discrete or continuous recursive Bayes filter*. The belief update happens in two distinct phases, mirroring any generic Bayesian filter:

1. **Prediction Phase (Motion Update):** The belief at time $t-1$ is propagated forward through the motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ to produce a *predicted* belief before incorporating new sensor evidence.
2. **Correction Phase (Measurement Update):** The predicted belief is multiplied by the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$, where $m$ is the map, and then normalized to a valid probability distribution.

Mathematically:

$$Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Here, $\eta$ is the normalizing constant ensuring the belief sums (or integrates) to **1**. The Markov property ensures the belief at time $t$ depends *only* on the previous belief $Bel(x_{t-1})$, the current control $u_t$, and the current measurement $z_t$ — not on the full history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the field:** Markov localization is the workhorse of *global localization* and *kidnapped robot* scenarios. Because the belief can be multi-modal, a robot that becomes teleported to an unknown location can re-localize itself by maintaining probability mass across the entire environment until measurements disambiguate the true pose.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Kalman Filter Localization

Kalman Filter Localization is a *closed-form* solution to the recursive Bayes filter under two strict assumptions:

- **Linearity:** Both motion model and measurement model are linear functions of the state.
- **Gaussian Noise:** Process and measurement noise are zero-mean, uncorrelated, and Gaussian.

Under these assumptions, the posterior remains Gaussian at every step, allowing the filter to be represented compactly by $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.

**Prediction Step:**

$$\mu_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \mu_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B_t u_t$$

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \Sigma_{t-1 \mid t-1} A_t^{T} + R_t$$

**Correction Step:**

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} \left( C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t \right)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \left( z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1} \right)$$

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t} = \left( I - K_t C_t \right) \Sigma_{t \mid t-1}$$

Here, $A_t$ is the state transition matrix, $B_t$ is the control input matrix, $C_t$ is the measurement matrix, and $K_t$ is the **Kalman Gain**, which optimally weights the trade-off between prediction uncertainty and measurement uncertainty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the field:** KFL is *computationally cheap* (only matrix operations of dimension $n \times n$, where $n \in \{2,3,4\}$), making it ideal for *local tracking* problems where the robot's initial pose is approximately known. It is the de facto standard in INS/GPS fusion for autonomous ground vehicles and in wheel-odometry/LiDAR fusion for indoor AGVs.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Markov Localization** is the core of the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** family, including particle filters, used in ROS `amcl` packages and deployed on warehouse robots (e.g., Kiva/Amazon Robotics systems).
- **Kalman Filter Localization** underlies **sensor fusion in autonomous cars** (Apollo, Autoware stacks), drone INS stabilization, and EKF-SLAM front-ends.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Aspect** | **Markov Localization** | **Kalman Filter Localization** |
|---|---|---|
| Belief Representation $Bel(x_t)$ | Arbitrary PDF / PMF (multi-modal) | Single Gaussian $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ |
| State Space | Discrete grid or continuous topological | Continuous real-valued $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| Update Equation | $Bel(x_t) = \eta \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) Bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | $\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t(z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1})$ |
| Covariance Update | Implicit (encoded in PDF) | $\Sigma_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t C_t) \Sigma_{t \mid t-1}$ |
| Global Localization | Yes (inherently supported) | No (fails without prior) |
| Kidnapped Robot Recovery | Yes (multi-modal re-localization) | No (unimodal collapse) |
| Computational Cost $O(\cdot)$ | $O(N)$ for $N$ grid cells / particles | $O(n^3)$ per step, $n \le 4$ typically |
| Sensor Model | Arbitrary non-Gaussian | Gaussian noise assumed |
| Motion Model | Arbitrary non-linear | Linear (EKF allows linearization) |
| Sensor Modalities | Range finders, cameras, beacons | Range, bearing, GPS, IMU, odometry |
| Memory Footprint | High ($N$ cells or particles) | Low ($n \times n$ matrix) |
| Recovery from Failure | Automatic via posterior re-distribution | Manual reset required |
| Optimality | Exact Bayes-optimal (in discrete form) | Optimal under linear-Gaussian assumptions |
| Convergence Behavior | Converges to Dirac delta on true pose | Converges to a Gaussian centered on true pose |
| Real-Time Feasibility | Depends on grid resolution; particle filters: yes | Always real-time feasible |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Markov Localization Recursion

The recursive update rule can be derived from the laws of total probability and Bayes' rule.

**Step 1 — Establish the prior via the law of total probability.**

The probability of being in state $x_t$ given all measurements up to time $t$ and all controls up to time $t$ is denoted $Bel(x_t) = p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. By the law of total probability, we can write:

$$p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Here, we exploit the **Markov property** to drop all history beyond $x_{t-1}$ in the motion model.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule to incorporate the new measurement $z_t$.**

$$p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the measurement likelihood using conditional independence.**

Since $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the static map $m$ (the environment does not depend on controls or prior measurements):

$$p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the normalizer $\eta$.**

The denominator does not depend on $x_t$, so we collapse it into a scalar normalizer:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the final Markov localization recursion.**

$$Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The integral inside is the *prediction* $\overline{Bel}(x_t)$, the multiplication by $p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$ is the *correction*, and $\eta$ enforces valid probability. This is the **fundamental recursion** evaluated at every control-measurement cycle in a field robot.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Gain

The Kalman gain is the *optimal* linear weighting that minimizes the *a posteriori* covariance $\Sigma_{t \mid t}$. We define the innovation (measurement residual) as:

$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1}$$

**Step 1 — Express the updated mean using the innovation.**

The general form of the updated mean is a linear combination of the prior mean and the innovation:

$$\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$$

**Step 2 — Express the updated covariance in terms of $K_t$.**

Substituting back into the definition of the covariance of the state estimate:

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t} = E\left[ (x_t - \mu_{t \mid t})(x_t - \mu_{t \mid t})^T \right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expanded form.**

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t} = \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} - K_t C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} - \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} K_t^{T} + K_t (C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t) K_t^{T}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the trace with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero.**

To minimize the trace (a scalar measure of covariance magnitude):

$$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(\Sigma_{t \mid t})}{\partial K_t} = -2 \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + 2 K_t (C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the optimal gain $K_t$.**

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} = K_t (C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t)$$

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} (C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t)^{-1}$$

This is the **Kalman gain** that optimally trades off prediction uncertainty and measurement noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When measurement noise $Q_t$ is small, the term in parentheses becomes dominated by $C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T}$, and $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$, meaning the filter trusts measurements heavily. Conversely, when $Q_t$ is large, $K_t \to 0$, and the filter ignores measurements, relying on the prediction.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Markov Localization** | **Kalman Filter Localization** | **Discriminator** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unimodality Assumption | Not required | Strictly required | Recovery from failure |
| Noise Distribution | Arbitrary | Gaussian | Sensor compatibility |
| Initial Pose Knowledge | Not required (can be uniform) | Required (close to true pose) | Global vs. local |
| Time Complexity per Step | $O(N)$ grid cells or particles | $O(n^3)$ matrix ops, $n \le 4$ | Real-time deployment |
| Space Complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(n^2)$ | Memory footprint |
| Non-Linear Sensor Models | Supported natively (lookup tables) | Requires EKF linearization | Robustness |
| Multi-Hypothesis Tracking | Native (multi-modal posterior) | Not possible | Ambiguous environments |
| Failure Recovery | Self-correcting | Permanent divergence | Reliability |
| Typical Update Rate | 5–20 Hz (particle) | 50–200 Hz (EKF) | Computational limits |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Markov Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief Bel0] --> B[Initialize Belief Grid]
    B --> C[Robot Motion ut]
    C --> D[Apply Motion Model pxt_xt1_ut]
    D --> E[Predicted Belief Bar Bel xt]
    E --> F[Sensor Reading zt]
    F --> G[Compute Measurement Likelihood pzt_xt_m]
    G --> H[Bayes Multiplication Bel Bar Bel times Likelihood]
    H --> I[Normalization by Eta]
    I --> J[Updated Belief Bel xt]
    J --> K{Converged to True Pose}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Pose Estimate Output]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Kalman Filter Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Mean Mu0 and Covariance Sigma0] --> B[Set Prior Belief as Gaussian]
    B --> C[Robot Motion ut]
    C --> D[Predict Mean Mu t given t minus 1]
    C --> E[Predict Covariance Sigma t given t minus 1]
    D --> F[Predicted State Estimate]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Sensor Reading zt]
    G --> H[Compute Innovation Nu t]
    H --> I[Compute Kalman Gain K t]
    I --> J[Update Mean Mu t given t]
    I --> K[Update Covariance Sigma t given t]
    J --> L[Updated Gaussian Belief]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Sigma Trace Below Threshold}
    M -- No --> C
    M -- Yes --> N[Pose Estimate Output]
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Pose Estimation Problem] --> B{Sensor Noise and Pose Ambiguity}
    B -- Multi-modal and Arbitrary --> C[Markov Localization]
    B -- Gaussian and Unimodal --> D[Kalman Filter Localization]
    C --> E[Discretized Grid or Particle Set]
    C --> F[Global Localization Capable]
    C --> G[High Computational Cost]
    D --> H[Mean and Covariance Pair]
    D --> I[Local Tracking Only]
    D --> J[Low Computational Cost]
    E --> K[Bayes Recursion]
    F --> K
    H --> L[Kalman Recursion]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> M[Output Pose]
    L --> M
```

## 4.4 Belief Distribution Evolution Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time t equals 0 - Uniform Belief] --> B[Time t equals 1 - Slight Peaks]
    B --> C[Time t equals 2 - Multi-modal Posterior]
    C --> D[Time t equals N - Single Peak at True Pose]
    A2[Time t equals 0 - Gaussian Blob at Guess] --> B2[Time t equals 1 - Blob Shrinks]
    B2 --> C2[Time t equals 2 - Blob Narrows Further]
    C2 --> D2[Time t equals N - Gaussian at True Pose]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> D2
    D -.-> E[Convergence Achieved - Both Methods]
    D2 -.-> E
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain in brief the fundamental difference between Markov Localization and Kalman Filter Localization with respect to the belief representation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The fundamental difference lies in the **form of the belief distribution**. In **Markov Localization**, the belief $Bel(x_t)$ is represented as an *arbitrary probability distribution* over the state space, which can be discrete (grid cells) or continuous (particles), and can be **multi-modal** — meaning it can have multiple probability peaks simultaneously. In contrast, **Kalman Filter Localization** constrains the belief to a **single, unimodal Gaussian distribution** parameterized by a mean vector $\mu_t$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$. Because of this Gaussian unimodality constraint, KFL cannot represent global uncertainty or ambiguous pose hypotheses, and therefore cannot be used for global localization from scratch. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three advantages of Kalman Filter Localization over Markov Localization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lower computational cost** — KFL requires only $O(n^3)$ matrix operations where $n \le 4$, whereas Markov Localization (especially grid-based) requires $O(N)$ operations where $N$ is the number of grid cells, often $10^4$ to $10^6$. (1 mark)

2. **Compact memory footprint** — KFL stores only $\mu_t$ and $\Sigma_t$ (a vector and a small matrix), whereas ML stores a complete probability table or particle set. (1 mark)

3. **Real-time performance** — KFL can run at 50–200 Hz update rates, making it suitable for high-speed field vehicles, while dense Markov Localization often operates at 5–20 Hz. (1 mark)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on this topic by (a) confusing "Markov Localization" with "Markov Decision Process (MDP)" — they are different concepts; (b) writing that KFL "cannot localize" rather than "cannot globally localize" — it is excellent for *local tracking*; (c) forgetting to mention that **EKF** (Extended Kalman Filter) is the *non-linear extension* often used in field robotics, which relaxes the linearity assumption; (d) writing that Markov Localization is "always accurate" — its accuracy depends critically on grid resolution or particle count.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the recursive update equation for Markov Localization. Discuss how the Markov property is utilized in the derivation. **7 marks**

**(b)** A differential-drive field robot operates in a 1D corridor of length **10 m** discretized into 100 grid cells. Initially, the robot's belief is uniform across all cells. The robot moves **0.3 m** to the right with a motion model that has 80% probability of reaching the intended cell and 10% each of overshooting and undershooting by one cell. A range sensor then returns a measurement with likelihood **0.7** at the true cell and **0.05** at the four neighboring cells (otherwise **0.01**). Compute the posterior belief after one motion-measurement cycle for the cells where the posterior is non-negligible. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

We start with the Bayes filter recursion. Let $Bel(x_t) = p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ denote the robot's belief about its pose $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and all controls $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$. (1 mark)

By the law of total probability, the *predicted* belief before incorporating $z_t$ is:

$$\overline{Bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

The key use of the **Markov property** is that the motion model $p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$ depends *only* on the previous state $x_{t-1}$ and the current control $u_t$, not on the full history of past measurements and controls. This allows the integral above to be a simple convolution-like operation rather than a full history enumeration. (2 marks)

Now applying Bayes' rule to incorporate $z_t$:

$$Bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{Bel}(x_t)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

The denominator is independent of $x_t$, so we replace it with a normalizer $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

Therefore the complete Markov Localization recursion is:

$$Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This recursion embodies the Markov property by ensuring $Bel(x_t)$ is fully determined by $Bel(x_{t-1})$, the current control $u_t$, and the current measurement $z_t$ — without any explicit memory of earlier states. (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Initial belief.*
With a uniform prior over 100 cells of cell width 0.1 m each:

$$Bel_0(x) = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \quad \text{for all } x$$

*Step 2 — Motion prediction.*
Let the robot's intended new cell be $c^*$. The motion model gives:

- $P(x_t = c^* \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) = 0.80$ (intended cell)
- $P(x_t = c^*+1 \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) = 0.10$ (overshoot)
- $P(x_t = c^*-1 \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) = 0.10$ (undershoot)

The predicted belief at cell $j$ is the sum over all predecessor cells $i$:

$$\overline{Bel}(j) = \sum_i P(j \mid i, u_t) \, Bel_0(i)$$

Since $Bel_0$ is uniform, $\overline{Bel}(j) = 0.80 \cdot 0.01 + 0.10 \cdot 0.01 + 0.10 \cdot 0.01 = 0.01$. The motion leaves the belief uniform (a known property of convolution with a symmetric kernel on a uniform distribution). (1 mark)

*Step 3 — Measurement correction.*
Let the true cell be $c^*$. The likelihoods are:

- $p(z_t \vert x_t = c^*, m) = 0.70$
- $p(z_t \vert x_t = c^* \pm 1, m) = 0.05$
- $p(z_t \vert x_t = c^* \pm 2, m) = 0.05$
- $p(z_t \vert \text{other cells}, m) = 0.01$

The unnormalized posterior at cell $j$ is:

$$Bel^*(j) = p(z_t \vert x_t = j, m) \cdot \overline{Bel}(j)$$

Computing the unnormalized values at the cells of interest:

- At $j = c^*$: $Bel^* = 0.70 \times 0.01 = 0.0070$ (1 mark)
- At $j = c^* \pm 1$: $Bel^* = 0.05 \times 0.01 = 0.0005$ (1 mark)
- At $j = c^* \pm 2$: $Bel^* = 0.05 \times 0.01 = 0.0005$ (1 mark)
- At all other cells: $Bel^* = 0.01 \times 0.01 = 0.0001$ (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Normalization.*
Sum of all unnormalized beliefs (approximately, assuming negligible far cells):

$$\sum_j Bel^*(j) \approx 0.0070 + 4(0.0005) + 4(0.0005) + 90(0.0001) = 0.0070 + 0.0020 + 0.0020 + 0.0090 = 0.0200$$

Normalizer: $\eta = 1/0.0200 = 50$. (1 mark)

*Step 5 — Posterior beliefs.*

- $Bel(c^*) = 50 \times 0.0070 = 0.350$
- $Bel(c^* \pm 1) = 50 \times 0.0005 = 0.025$
- $Bel(c^* \pm 2) = 50 \times 0.0005 = 0.025$
- $Bel(\text{other}) = 50 \times 0.0001 = 0.005$

The belief is now sharply peaked at the true cell, demonstrating localization convergence. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** State and derive the Kalman Filter prediction and update equations. Clearly define all matrix variables. **7 marks**

**(b)** A 2D field robot is tracked using a 1D Kalman Filter for its $x$-coordinate. The state is $x = [p, v]^T$ (position and velocity). The current estimate is $\mu = [5.0 \text{ m}, 0.5 \text{ m/s}]^T$ and covariance is $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$. The state transition matrix is $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ with $\Delta t = 1$ s, and process noise covariance $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$. A position-only sensor reports $z = 5.8$ m with measurement matrix $C = [1, 0]$ and measurement noise $Q = [0.25]$. Compute the **predicted** state, **predicted covariance**, **Kalman gain**, and **updated state** after one full filter cycle. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Kalman Filter Equations (7 marks):**

**State:** The robot pose is modeled as a random variable with a Gaussian distribution $x_t \sim N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. (1 mark)

**Prediction Step:** Given the prior $\mu_{t-1 \mid t-1}$ and $\Sigma_{t-1 \mid t-1}$, and a control $u_t$, the prediction propagates the state through the linear motion model $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$, where $\epsilon_t \sim N(0, R_t)$. Taking expectations:

$$\mu_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \mu_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B_t u_t$$

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \Sigma_{t-1 \mid t-1} A_t^{T} + R_t$$

Here, $A_t$ is the **state transition matrix**, $B_t$ is the **control input matrix**, and $R_t$ is the **process noise covariance**. (2 marks)

**Update Step:** A measurement $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ is received with $\delta_t \sim N(0, Q_t)$. The **innovation** is $\nu_t = z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1}$. The **Kalman gain** is derived by minimizing the posterior covariance:

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} (C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t)^{-1}$$

The **updated state** and **updated covariance** are:

$$\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$$

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t C_t) \Sigma_{t \mid t-1}$$

Here, $C_t$ is the **measurement matrix** mapping state to expected measurement, and $Q_t$ is the **measurement noise covariance**. (3 marks)

The derivation of $K_t$ proceeds by substituting $\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$ into the expression for $\Sigma_{t \mid t}$, taking the trace, differentiating with respect to $K_t$, and setting the derivative to zero. The optimal gain is the result. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Predicted state.*
With $\Delta t = 1$ s and $B_t = 0$ (no external control):

$$\mu_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \mu_{t-1 \mid t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 + 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m, m/s}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 2 — Predicted covariance.*

$$A_t \Sigma_{t-1 \mid t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$A_t \Sigma_{t-1 \mid t-1} A_t^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma_{t \mid t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.91 & 0.30 \\ 0.30 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 3 — Innovation.*

$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1} = 5.8 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = 5.8 - 5.5 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 4 — Kalman gain.*

$$C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.91 & 0.30 \\ 0.30 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0.91$$

$$C_t \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} + Q_t = 0.91 + 0.25 = 1.16$$

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t \mid t-1} C_t^{T} (1.16)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.91 \\ 0.30 \end{bmatrix} \times \frac{1}{1.16} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7845 \\ 0.2586 \end{bmatrix}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 5 — Updated state.*

$$\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 5.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.7845 \\ 0.2586 \end{bmatrix} \times 0.3 = \begin{bmatrix} 5.5 + 0.2353 \\ 0.5 + 0.0776 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.7353 \\ 0.5776 \end{bmatrix}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 6 — Interpretation.*
The updated position estimate is **5.7353 m**, pulled from the predicted 5.5 m toward the measurement 5.8 m by approximately 78% of the innovation, as dictated by the Kalman gain. (1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Belief Representation:** Markov Localization uses *arbitrary, possibly multi-modal* PDFs; Kalman Localization uses a *single unimodal Gaussian* $N(\mu, \Sigma)$. *(HIGH PRIORITY)*
- **Global vs. Local:** Only Markov Localization supports **global localization** and **kidnapped robot recovery**. KFL is strictly for **local tracking** given a prior. *(FREQUENTLY EXAMINED)*
- **Computational Cost:** ML is $O(N)$ for $N$ grid cells/particles; KFL is $O(n^3)$ with $n \le 4$. KFL is *always* real-time feasible. *(HIGH-YIELD)*
- **Recursion Core:** ML uses the **Bayes recursion** $Bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) Bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$. KFL uses the **Kalman recursion** $\mu_{t \mid t} = \mu_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - C_t \mu_{t \mid t-1})$ and $\Sigma_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t C_t) \Sigma_{t \mid t-1}$.
- **Markov Property:** Used in ML to drop history dependence; *not* the same as a Markov Decision Process (MDP).
- **Kalman Gain Behavior:** $K_t \to 0$ when measurement noise is high; $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ when measurement noise is low.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF):** The non-linear generalization of KFL using Jacobian linearization, widely used in field robotics when motion/measurement models are non-linear.
- **Particle Filter / MCL:** The Monte Carlo approximation of ML using a sample-based representation, used in ROS `amcl`.
- **Convergence:** ML converges to a *Dirac delta* on the true pose; KFL converges to a *Gaussian* centered on the true pose with shrinking variance.
- **Failure Modes:** KFL can suffer *irrecoverable divergence* if the linear-Gaussian assumptions are violated; ML can suffer *particle depletion* in MCL.
- **Sensors:** KFL is favored for IMU, GPS, wheel odometry; ML is favored for LiDAR scan matching, camera-based place recognition, ambiguous beacon observations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for KTU exam:** *Markov Localization is a multi-modal, computationally heavy but globally relocalizing Bayes filter; Kalman Filter Localization is a unimodal, computationally cheap but locally tracking Gaussian filter — the choice depends on whether the robot knows roughly where it is.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation and Reasoning about Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation and Reasoning about Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Decomposition** is a foundational paradigm in field robotics for representing a continuous physical environment as a finite, discrete set of non-overlapping regions (called *cells* or *chunks*) such that every point in the workspace belongs to exactly one cell, and robot-relevant queries (free-space, occupancy, traversability, coverage) can be answered by inspection of these cells.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, spatial decomposition is the *bridge* between the **continuous world** sensed by the robot's exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, stereo camera, IMU-augmented odometry) and the **discrete computational model** that onboard planners and SLAM modules can manipulate. A decomposition is formally defined as a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \langle W, \mathcal{C}, f, \phi \rangle
$$

where $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2}$ (or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$) is the workspace, $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ is the finite cell complex, $f : W \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ is the assignment function, and $\phi : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \{\text{FREE}, \text{OCCUPIED}, \text{UNKNOWN}\}$ is the labeling function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Course Outcome mapping, spatial decomposition is the **prerequisite enabler** for CO2 (Spatial Reasoning and Path Planning) and CO3 (Coverage and Exploration), both of which are direct higher-level consumers of $\mathcal{D}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are asked to clean a large college auditorium. Instead of wandering aimlessly, you first **draw a grid of equal-sized squares on the floor** and number each square. You then systematically clean square 1, then 2, then 3, and so on. You have just *decomposed* the continuous auditorium into discrete, countable units. Spatial decomposition does the same for a robot:

- The **auditorium** = the robot's workspace $W$.
- The **grid squares** = the cells $\mathcal{C}$.
- The **numbering** = the indexing $f$.
- The **"is this square dirty?"** check = the labeling $\phi$.

A quadtree decomposition is analogous to drawing *bigger* squares in open, uncluttered zones (like the middle of the stage) and *smaller* squares in cluttered zones (under chairs, around pillars), thereby saving memory.

## 1.3 Why Spatial Decomposition Matters in Field Robotics

| Field Robotics Need | Role of Decomposition |
|---|---|
| Coverage Path Planning (lawn mowing, demining) | Guarantees every cell is visited at least once |
| SLAM back-end optimization | Provides local submap partition |
| Multi-robot task allocation | Cells become indivisible tasks |
| Obstacle avoidance | Cell occupancy drives reactive control |
| Energy budgeting | Per-cell traversability cost = energy per meter |

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Cell resolution** $r$: typically **0.05 m – 0.50 m** for outdoor UGVs, **0.01 m – 0.05 m** for indoor AGVs.
- **Update rate**: occupancy grid updates at **5–40 Hz**.
- **Working map size**: agricultural robots routinely maintain **$10^6$ – $10^8$** cells.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Spatial Decomposition Strategies

Spatial decomposition strategies are organized along **two orthogonal axes**: *geometric exactness* and *uniformity of cell size*.

### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — Geometric Exactness

- **Exact (Exact Cellular Decomposition):** The cell boundaries coincide with obstacle boundaries. Coverage is provably complete because the union of free cells exactly equals the free space. Algorithmic examples: **trapezoidal decomposition**, **Boustrophedon decomposition** (a.k.a. *morse* decomposition), **slice decomposition**.
- **Approximate (Approximate Cellular Decomposition):** Obstacle boundaries are *rasterized* into cells. Cells are partially occupied. Simpler to implement, but completeness is only guaranteed up to the cell resolution $r$.

### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — Uniformity of Cell Size

- **Uniform (regular) grid:** Every cell is the same size. Trivial indexing, $O(1)$ lookup, but memory-inefficient in open fields.
- **Non-uniform (adaptive) grid:** Cell size depends on local information density. Examples: **quadtree** (2D), **octree** (3D), **k-d tree**, **Voronoi-based irregular decomposition**.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Decomposition Schemes

### A. Uniform Occupancy Grid

The workspace is divided into a fixed array of axis-aligned square cells of side length $r$. Each cell stores a scalar probability $p(c) \in [0,1]$ that it is occupied. Bayes' filter update rule (used in the seminal Moravec & Elfes 1985 algorithm) is:

$$
p(c \mid z_{1:t}) = 1 - \left(1 + \frac{p(c \mid z_t)\,(1 - p(c))}{p(c)\,(1 - p(c \mid z_t))} \cdot \frac{p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(c \mid z_{1:t-1})} \right)^{-1}
$$

where $z_t$ is the current sensor measurement and the cell is treated as a stationary Bernoulli process.

### B. Quadtree (Adaptive 2D Decomposition)

A root cell recursively subdivides into four equal children **iff** the cell is non-uniform in information content (e.g., it intersects an obstacle boundary or contains high measurement variance). Leaf count $L$ grows as:

$$
L = \mathcal{O}\!\left(K \log_2 \frac{L_{\text{world}}}{r_{\min}} \right)
$$

where $K$ is the number of "interesting" regions and $r_{\min}$ is the maximum depth cell size.

### C. Trapezoidal / Boustrophedon (Exact Cellular)

A vertical sweep line slides from $x = x_{\min}$ to $x = x_{\max}$. At every $x$ where the topology of free intervals changes (an *event point*), a new cell is opened or closed. The result is a partition of free space into convex polygons whose "slicing" property makes back-and-forth ("boustrophedon") coverage paths trivial to compute.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Equations for Spatial Decomposition**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \dfrac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil$ | Number of cells in a uniform 2D grid of extents $L_x, L_y$ and resolution $r$ | Memory sizing |
| 2 | $M_{\text{bytes}} = N \cdot b$ | Total memory with $b$ bytes per cell (typically $b=1$ for log-odds) | Onboard RAM budgeting |
| 3 | $t_{\text{update}} = \mathcal{O}(N_{\text{ray}})$ per cell visited by ray-cast | Computational cost per scan | Real-time schedulability |
| 4 | $\text{Coverage}(\%) = \dfrac{\#\{\text{visited cells}\}}{N} \times 100$ | Coverage completeness metric | Mission assessment |
| 5 | $d_{\text{cell}}(p_1, p_2) = \sqrt{(i_1-i_2)^2 + (j_1-j_2)^2} \cdot r$ | Cell-space Euclidean distance | A* heuristic |
| 6 | $h(n) = \sqrt{(n_x - g_x)^2 + (n_y - g_y)^2}$ | Octile / Euclidean heuristic for A* on a grid | Path planning |
| 7 | $L_{\text{quadtree}} \le 4K \cdot (1 + \log_4(L_{\text{world}} / r_{\min}))$ | Worst-case leaf count of a quadtree over $K$ features | Memory analysis |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{\text{covered}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$, $\eta \le 1$ | Coverage efficiency | Agriculture / demining |
| 9 | $C_{\text{ray}} = n_{\text{ray}} \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log_4 N)$ | Octree ray-cast cost | 3D perception |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table the absolute value / magnitude symbol is written as `\vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact — never use the bare vertical bar `|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision agriculture:** A tractor-pulled UGV with RTK-GPS uses a **0.25 m uniform grid** to plan coverage paths that minimize overlap and skip already-sprayed rows.
- **Search and rescue (USAR):** Quadrotors in collapsed buildings use **octrees** so that the same memory footprint can store a 50 m corridor at 1 m and a doorway at 0.05 m.
- **Autonomous mining:** Subterranean UGVs use **exact cellular decomposition** to guarantee that every tunnel cross-section is scanned for ventilation compliance.
- **Maritime demining:** AUVs use **multi-resolution grids** to perform coarse planning at the harbor scale and fine inspection at the contact scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Memory Footprint of a Uniform Grid

**Problem (typical 2-mark KTU short):** A field robot surveys a rectangular field of dimensions $L_x = 200$ m by $L_y = 150$ m using a uniform occupancy grid of resolution $r = 0.25$ m. Each cell stores a 1-byte log-odds value. Compute the total number of cells and the RAM required in kilobytes.

**Step 1 — Cells along the X axis.**

$$
N_x = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{200}{0.25} \right\rceil = \lceil 800 \rceil = 800
$$

*Conversion logic:* We need enough cells to cover the entire 200 m span; each cell covers 0.25 m, so divide, then round up to guarantee full coverage.

**Step 2 — Cells along the Y axis.**

$$
N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{150}{0.25} \right\rceil = \lceil 600 \rceil = 600
$$

*Conversion logic:* Same logic as Step 1, applied to the perpendicular axis.

**Step 3 — Total cell count.**

$$
N = N_x \cdot N_y = 800 \times 600 = 480\,000 \text{ cells}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Because the grid is Cartesian and non-overlapping, every $(i,j)$ pair is unique; the total is the product.

**Step 4 — Memory footprint.**

$$
M = N \cdot b = 480\,000 \times 1 \text{ byte} = 480\,000 \text{ bytes} = 468.75 \text{ KiB}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 byte per cell × total cells gives raw bytes; divide by 1024 to convert to kibibytes.

**Sanity check:** Doubling the resolution to $r = 0.125$ m quadruples $N$ to $1.92$ million cells ($\approx 1.83$ MiB). The scaling is **quadratic** in $1/r$, which is why adaptive representations (quadtree) are favored for large workspaces.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Coverage Completeness with Overlap

**Problem:** A UGV with footprint $W_r = 1.0$ m drives along boustrophedon rows of spacing $s = 0.9$ m. The total area is $A = 10\,000$ m². Compute the coverage efficiency and the over-cover penalty.

**Step 1 — Effective swath.**

$$
W_{\text{eff}} = \min(W_r, s) = \min(1.0, 0.9) = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Only the spacing determines effective coverage; the footprint may be wider but the swath is limited by the inter-row gap to avoid double coverage.

**Step 2 — Covered area per pass.**

$$
A_{\text{pass}} = L_{\text{row}} \cdot W_{\text{eff}}
$$

For a single row of length $L_{\text{row}} = 100$ m,

$$
A_{\text{pass}} = 100 \times 0.9 = 90 \text{ m}^2
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiplying length by swath width gives the rectangular strip covered.

**Step 3 — Number of rows required.**

$$
n_{\text{rows}} = \left\lceil \frac{A}{A_{\text{pass}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10\,000}{90} \right\rceil = \lceil 111.11 \rceil = 112
$$

*Conversion logic:* Total area divided by per-row area, rounded up.

**Step 4 — Coverage efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{A}{n_{\text{rows}} \cdot A_{\text{pass}}} = \frac{10\,000}{112 \times 90} = \frac{10\,000}{10\,080} \approx 0.992
$$

*Conversion logic:* Efficiency is actual needed area divided by actual swept area.

**Step 5 — Over-cover penalty.**

$$
\Delta A = n_{\text{rows}} \cdot A_{\text{pass}} - A = 10\,080 - 10\,000 = 80 \text{ m}^2
$$

*Conversion logic:* Difference between swept and required area quantifies wasted effort.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Quadtree Worst-Case Leaf Count

**Problem:** A quadtree is built over a workspace containing $K = 50$ "feature" regions (each small enough to fit inside a single leaf of size $r_{\min}$). The world has side $L_{\text{world}} = 1024$ m and $r_{\min} = 1$ m. Bound the leaf count.

**Step 1 — Depth in powers of 4.**

$$
D = \log_4\!\left(\frac{L_{\text{world}}}{r_{\min}}\right) = \log_4(1024) = \log_4(2^{10}) = \frac{10}{2} = 5
$$

*Conversion logic:* $1024 = 2^{10}$; $\log_4(2^{10}) = 10/\log_2 4 = 10/2 = 5$.

**Step 2 — Worst-case leaves per feature region.**

Each feature region may be split into as many as $4^D$ leaves in the deepest layer surrounding it (a 4×4 patch in the deepest level), so:

$$
L_{\max} = 4 \cdot K \cdot 4^{D-1} = 4 \cdot 50 \cdot 4^4 = 200 \times 256 = 51\,200 \text{ leaves}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Conservatively we add a 1-leaf margin on every side; hence $4^{D-1}$ per feature.

**Step 3 — Comparison with uniform grid.**

$$
N_{\text{uniform}} = \left(\frac{1024}{1}\right)^2 = 1\,048\,576
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each side has 1024 cells, so the total is $1024^2$.

**Step 4 — Memory saving factor.**

$$
\rho = \frac{L_{\max}}{N_{\text{uniform}}} = \frac{51\,200}{1\,048\,576} \approx 0.0488 \approx 4.88\%
$$

*Conversion logic:* Quadtree uses less than **5%** of the uniform-grid memory for this scenario, confirming the value of adaptive decomposition.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — When to use which decomposition**

| Criterion | Uniform Grid | Quadtree / Octree | Exact Cellular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Implementation complexity | **Low** | Medium | High |
| Memory efficiency in open space | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| Memory efficiency in cluttered space | **Excellent** | Good | Good |
| Coverage proof of completeness | Approximate | Approximate | **Exact** |
| Path planning simplicity | **High** (A*) | Medium | Medium |
| Best for | Indoor AGVs, small UGVs | Aerial & 3D perception | Structured environments |
| Computational cost per update | $\mathcal{O}(N_{\text{ray}})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N_{\text{events}})$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spatial Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Stream LIDAR Camera Radar] --> B[Preprocessing Layer]
    B --> C[Coordinate Frame Transform World Frame]
    C --> D{Decomposition Engine}
    D --> D1[Uniform Occupancy Grid]
    D --> D2[Adaptive Quadtree Octree]
    D --> D3[Exact Cellular Boustrophedon]
    D1 --> E[Cell Labeling FREE OCCUPIED UNKNOWN]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Spatial Reasoning Module]
    F --> F1[Path Planner A Star D Star]
    F --> F2[Coverage Planner Boustrophedon Spanning Tree]
    F --> F3[Frontier Explorer]
    F1 --> G[Motion Controller]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Field Robot Actuators]
    E --> I[Map Storage SLAM Back End]
```

*Reading the diagram:* The sensor stream is preprocessed, transformed into a world-aligned frame, and fed into one of three decomposition engines. The resulting cell map is labelled, then consumed by three spatial-reasoning consumers (path planner, coverage planner, frontier explorer) before issuing actuator commands. The labelled cell map is also archived in the SLAM back-end for loop closure.

## 4.2 State Machine of an Adaptive Quadtree Decomposition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Root
    Root --> Uniform: cell homogeneous
    Uniform --> Subdivided: cell straddles boundary
    Subdivided --> Child1: NE quadrant
    Subdivided --> Child2: NW quadrant
    Subdivided --> Child3: SE quadrant
    Subdivided --> Child4: SW quadrant
    Child1 --> Uniform: per child
    Child2 --> Uniform: per child
    Child3 --> Uniform: per child
    Child4 --> Uniform: per child
    Child1 --> Subdivided: recursive
    Child2 --> Subdivided: recursive
    Child3 --> Subdivided: recursive
    Child4 --> Subdivided: recursive
    Uniform --> [*]: leaf reached max depth
```

*Reading the diagram:* The decomposition starts at the root and recursively subdivides any non-uniform cell into four children. The recursion terminates when a cell is either homogeneous or has reached the maximum permitted depth (smallest legal cell size).

## 4.3 Coverage Mission Workflow on a Boustrophedon Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Acquire Workspace Polygon] --> S2[Step 2 Identify Critical Points]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Cell Boundaries]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Generate Connectivity Graph]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Plan Back and Forth Path]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Execute Coverage Mission]
    S6 --> S7{All Cells Visited}
    S7 -- No --> S5
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Mission Complete]
```

*Reading the diagram:* Boustrophedon coverage begins with the polygon of the free workspace, derives cell boundaries from critical points, builds a cell adjacency graph, and then repeatedly traces back-and-forth passes until every cell is visited.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *spatial decomposition* in the context of field robotics. Mention any two advantages of exact cellular decomposition over a uniform occupancy grid.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Spatial decomposition is the process of partitioning a continuous robot workspace $W$ into a finite, non-overlapping set of discrete cells $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, \ldots, c_n\}$ such that each cell can be labelled as free, occupied, or unknown, enabling discrete algorithms for path planning, coverage, and exploration.
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** Exact cellular decomposition guarantees **complete coverage** because the union of free cells is exactly equal to the free space, removing the resolution-induced gap problem of occupancy grids.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** It is **memory-efficient in structured environments** because cell count grows with the number of obstacle events rather than the total area.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a uniform occupancy grid and a quadtree decomposition. State one typical application of each in field robotics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Uniform grid (1 mark):** A regular tessellation of identical cells; lookup is $O(1)$, but memory grows as $1/r^2$.
- **Quadtree (1 mark):** A recursive adaptive decomposition where non-uniform cells are subdivided into four children; memory scales as $O(K \log(L/r_{\min}))$.
- **Applications (1 mark):** Uniform grid — indoor AGV mapping; Quadtree — multi-resolution aerial mapping with quadrotors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (14 marks)**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a spatial decomposition pipeline for an autonomous field robot. Highlight the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A UGV must cover a 120 m × 90 m agricultural field using boustrophedon rows. The robot has a footprint of 1.2 m, and rows are spaced at 1.0 m. Compute (i) the number of rows, (ii) the percentage coverage, and (iii) the over-covered area. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The spatial decomposition pipeline has **five major blocks** (refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Sensing block (1 mark):** LIDAR, camera, IMU, GPS. Provides raw measurements.
2. **Pre-processing block (1 mark):** Outlier rejection, deskewing, time synchronization.
3. **Coordinate transform block (1 mark):** Converts sensor-centric points into the world frame using the robot pose.
4. **Decomposition engine (2 marks):** Applies the chosen scheme (uniform grid, quadtree, or exact cellular) and maintains the cell structure.
5. **Spatial-reasoning consumer block (2 marks):** Path planner, coverage planner, frontier explorer; each reads the labelled cell map and outputs actuator commands.

The labelling function $\phi$ is the **central data product** that flows from the decomposition engine to all downstream consumers.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Effective swath.**

$$
W_{\text{eff}} = \min(W_r, s) = \min(1.2, 1.0) = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of rows.**

$$
n_{\text{rows}} = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{1.0} \right\rceil = 120
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage coverage (with 1 mark for the formula and 1 mark for substitution).**

$$
\eta = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y}{n_{\text{rows}} \cdot s \cdot L_y} \times 100 = \frac{120 \times 90}{120 \times 1.0 \times 90} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

> Note: by construction, the rows span the full 120 m, so coverage is 100% along the long axis.

**Step 4 — Over-covered area.**

$$
A_{\text{swept}} = n_{\text{rows}} \cdot s \cdot L_y = 120 \times 1.0 \times 90 = 10\,800 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\Delta A = A_{\text{swept}} - A = 10\,800 - 10\,800 = 0 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Alternative: compute overlap footprint if rows are slightly offset.** For an exam, present both the nominal and the offset scenarios. If the robot's footprint is wider than the row spacing, the over-cover is

$$
\Delta A = n_{\text{rows}} \cdot (W_r - s) \cdot L_y = 120 \times 0.2 \times 90 = 2\,160 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** When $W_r > s$, the robot over-sweeps $2\,160$ m², equivalent to $20\%$ redundancy. Designers must trade off swath overlap against the risk of missing strips due to localization drift.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice] (14 marks)**
> **(a)** Explain the Boustrophedon exact cellular decomposition algorithm. How does it improve coverage completeness over a simple zig-zag path? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare uniform occupancy grids, quadtrees, and exact cellular decompositions along the dimensions of (i) memory, (ii) algorithmic complexity, (iii) coverage guarantee, and (iv) suitability for 3D environments. Present the comparison in a table. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3B

#### Part (a) — Boustrophedon Decomposition (7 marks)

- **Definition (1 mark):** Boustrophedon (literally "ox-turning") decomposition is an exact cellular decomposition in which a vertical sweep line moves across the workspace, opening a new cell whenever an obstacle creates a new free interval and closing a cell whenever a free interval disappears.
- **Algorithm steps (4 marks):**
  1. Discretize the sweep line at every $x$ where the set of free intervals changes — these are the *event points*.
  2. Between consecutive event points, the free space is a vertical strip of constant topology.
  3. Each strip becomes one cell of the decomposition.
  4. Cells are connected via an adjacency graph; the planner then sweeps back and forth across adjacent cells.
- **Improvement over plain zig-zag (2 marks):** Plain zig-zag assumes a rectangular free space, which is invalid in cluttered fields; boustrophedon *adapts* to obstacle geometry, guaranteeing that every reachable cell is visited and that no cell is double-visited more than necessary.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks = 1.75 per row)

| Dimension | Uniform Grid | Quadtree / Octree | Exact Cellular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(1/r^2)$ worst case | $\mathcal{O}(K \log(L/r_{\min}))$ | $\mathcal{O}(N_{\text{events}})$ |
| Algorithmic complexity | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ lookup | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ per cell | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ to build |
| Coverage guarantee | Approximate, depends on $r$ | Approximate, depends on $r_{\min}$ | **Exact (complete)** |
| 3D suitability | $O(1/r^3)$ — explodes | **Excellent via octree** | Limited, complex polygons |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** the cell count. Using $\lfloor L/r \rfloor$ instead of $\lceil L/r \rceil$ gives insufficient coverage — a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing world-frame and sensor-frame coordinates** when describing the architecture. Always explicitly state the transform $\mathbf{T}_s^w$ in the diagram.
> 3. **Confusing "coverage" with "exploration."** Coverage means every free cell is *visited*; exploration means every *unknown* cell is *sensed*. Examiners award separate marks for each.
> 4. **Stating "quadtree uses less memory"** without quantifying it. Always provide the $O(\cdot)$ expression or numerical factor.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in the numerical answer. Each numerical step should end with `m`, `m²`, `cells`, or `bytes`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spatial decomposition = discretization of a continuous workspace into labelled cells.** It is the prerequisite for all higher-level spatial reasoning.
- **Three canonical schemes:** uniform grid (simple, expensive memory), quadtree/octree (adaptive, scalable), exact cellular (provably complete coverage).
- **Resolution $r$ is the single most important design knob.** Memory scales as $1/r^d$ in $d$ dimensions for a uniform grid.
- **Bayes' filter with log-odds** is the standard update rule for occupancy grids; remember to convert to log-odds space for numerically stable incremental updates.
- **Boustrophedon decomposition** uses event points along a sweep line and is the *de facto* algorithm for complete coverage in known polygonal environments.
- **Quadtrees are excellent for multi-resolution outdoor mapping** because memory grows with *information content*, not area.
- **A\* on a grid** uses an admissible heuristic $h(n) = \sqrt{(n_x - g_x)^2 + (n_y - g_y)^2}$; the cell-space distance is $r \cdot \sqrt{(i_1-i_2)^2 + (j_1-j_2)^2}$.
- **Coverage efficiency** $\eta = A_{\text{covered}} / A_{\text{total}}$; overlap penalty is the swept area minus the actual area.
- **Standard KTU deliverables:** block diagram (5 blocks), formula derivation, comparison table, numerical problem with explicit unit cancellation.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** always use $\lceil L/r \rceil$, never $\lfloor L/r \rfloor$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geometric Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Field Robotics**, *Geometric Representations* refer to the mathematically rigorous frameworks used to describe the **pose** (combined position and orientation) of a rigid body, the **coordinate frames** attached to robots/sensors/environments, and the **transformations** that map points and vectors between these frames. The canonical mathematical objects used are elements of the **Special Euclidean Groups**, namely **SE(2)** for planar motion and **SE(3)** for spatial motion, often represented using rotation matrices $\mathbf{R} \in SO(n)$ augmented with translation vectors $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to form homogeneous transformation matrices $\mathbf{T} \in SE(n)$.

The five principal representation schemes covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Rotation / Homogeneous Transformation Matrices** ($3 \times 3$ in 2D, $4 \times 4$ in 3D)
2. **Euler Angles** (three successive rotations, e.g., Z-Y-X / Roll-Pitch-Yaw)
3. **Fixed / RPY Angles** (Rotations about the fixed/world frame axes)
4. **Axis-Angle Representation** (Rodrigues' formula)
5. **Unit Quaternions** $\mathbf{q} = (q_w, q_x, q_y, q_z) \in \mathbb{S}^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The *Geometric Representations* topic is foundational for **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**, **forward/inverse kinematics**, **path planning**, and **sensor fusion (EKF/UKF)** in Module 2 of PCRAT601. Mastery of $SE(3)$ and quaternion algebra is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a robotic arm bolted to a factory floor, holding a spray-paint nozzle. To instruct the robot where to paint, you need to specify **two distinct things**:
- **Where** the nozzle tip is located in 3D space (Position: $x, y, z$)
- **Which direction** the nozzle is pointing (Orientation: *e.g.*, tilted $30^\circ$ forward and rotated $45^\circ$ left)

A single 3D point is a *zero-dimensional pin* in space; a *pose* is a **floating coordinate frame** — imagine taping a tiny three-axis ruler (with X-Y-Z arrows) to the nozzle. Now, no matter how the arm moves, you only need to know **where this ruler sits and how it is rotated** relative to the factory-floor ruler. This floating ruler is exactly what a geometric representation encodes.
