The reason robotics doesn't just use a single representation is that each has trade-offs:

| If you need… | …use this representation |
|---|---|
| Clean linear algebra, easy chain of transformations | **Homogeneous Matrix** |
| Compactness, smooth interpolation, no gimbal lock | **Quaternion** |
| Human-readable joint commands (robot head tilts) | **Euler / RPY Angles** |
| Minimal parameters for a single rotation step | **Axis-Angle** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU:** A rotation in $\mathbb{R}^3$ has only **3 degrees of freedom (DOF)**, yet a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix has 9 entries (with 6 constraints: 3 orthogonality + 1 unit-determinant). Representations differ in *how* they package these 3 DOF — the choice has real engineering consequences for memory, numerical stability, and computational cost.

### 1.3 Standard Physical / Mathematical Constants

- **Degrees of freedom of a rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^3$:** $\boxed{\mathbf{6}}$ (3 translational + 3 rotational)
- **Degrees of freedom in $\mathbb{R}^2$:** $\boxed{\mathbf{3}}$ (2 translational + 1 rotational)
- **Dimension of $SO(3)$ manifold:** $\dim = 3$
- **Dimension of $SE(3)$ manifold:** $\dim = 6$
- **Quaternion normalization constraint:** $\Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert = 1$ (lies on the 3-sphere $\mathbb{S}^3$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Structure: The Hierarchy of Representations

Let us break down how a single physical rotation is encoded at five different mathematical levels:

- **Level 1 — Vector of Parameters:** A minimal 3-tuple such as $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ for Euler angles or $(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta)$ for axis-angle. These are *compact* but suffer from singularities (gimbal lock) and discontinuous wrap-around.
- **Level 2 — Unit Quaternion:** A 4-tuple $\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z)$ with one constraint $\Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert = 1$. Globally continuous, no gimbal lock, but not human-interpretable.
- **Level 3 — Rotation Matrix $\mathbf{R}$:** A $3 \times 3$ orthonormal, proper-orthogonal matrix with 6 constraints. Linear-algebra friendly, can be inverted trivially ($\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{R}^\top$), and composes via multiplication.
- **Level 4 — Homogeneous Transformation $\mathbf{T}$:** A $4 \times 4$ matrix embedding $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$. Maps a point in one frame to another in a single matrix multiply.
- **Level 5 — Twist / Lie Algebra $\mathfrak{se}(3)$:** A 6-vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\omega})$ representing an *infinitesimal* motion. Used in EKF state vectors and continuous-time control.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why homogeneous coordinates?** A point $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ cannot be transformed by an arbitrary $\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{t}$ using a single matrix product because translation is *not* a linear operation. By appending a "1" to make $\tilde{\mathbf{p}} = (x, y, z, 1)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^4$, both rotation and translation become a *single* $4 \times 4$ matrix multiply, which composes associatively.
- **Why quaternions despite having 4 components?** Although 4 numbers describe a 3-DOF rotation, the constraint $\Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert = 1$ removes the redundancy. The payoff: **numerical stability** for thousands of incremental rotations (e.g., IMU integration at 200 Hz), and **SLERP** interpolation for smooth animation.
- **Why axis-angle for shortest rotation?** Given two unit vectors $\hat{\mathbf{a}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, the shortest rotation between them is around the axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = \hat{\mathbf{a}} \times \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ by angle $\theta = \arccos(\hat{\mathbf{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{b}})$. This is the natural representation of *any* single rotation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the **elementary rotation matrices** $R_x, R_y, R_z$ and the **homogeneous $4 \times 4$ block structure**. Almost every Part B question in Module 2 begins with these.

| # | Representation | Mathematical Form | DOF | Singularities / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Rotation $SO(2)$ | $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | 1 | None |
| 2 | 3D Rotation $R_x(\alpha)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{pmatrix}$ | 1 | None |
| 3 | 3D Rotation $R_y(\beta)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{pmatrix}$ | 1 | None |
| 4 | 3D Rotation $R_z(\gamma)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | 1 | None |
| 5 | Homogeneous $\mathbf{T} \in SE(3)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{3\times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3\times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | 6 | $4 \times 4$, det = +1 |
| 6 | Euler Z-Y-X (RPY) | $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ | 3 | Singular at $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$ (pitch) |
| 7 | Axis-Angle | $(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta)$; equiv. $R = e^{[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \theta}$ | 3 | $2\pi$ ambiguity (resolved by quaternion) |
| 8 | Unit Quaternion | $\mathbf{q} = (\cos\tfrac{\theta}{2}, \hat{\mathbf{k}}\sin\tfrac{\theta}{2}) \in \mathbb{S}^3$ | 3 | $\mathbf{q} \equiv -\mathbf{q}$ (double cover) |
| 9 | Quaternion → Matrix | $R = (w^2 - \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{v})I + 2\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^\top + 2w[\mathbf{v}]_\times$ | — | where $\mathbf{q}=(w,\mathbf{v})$ |
| 10 | Twist / $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ | $\hat{\xi} = \begin{pmatrix} [\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times & \mathbf{v} \\ \mathbf{0} & 0 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4\times 4}$ | 6 | Used in EKF state, screw theory |
| 11 | Composition Law | $\mathbf{T}_1 \mathbf{T}_2$ (matrix), $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2$ (Hamilton product) | — | Associative, not commutative |
| 12 | Inverse Transform | $\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R}^\top & -\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | — | Crucial for inverse kinematics |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Field Robots** (e.g., tractor autosteer): use **RPY angles** because roll/pitch/yaw of the chassis are physically intuitive to engineers tuning a vehicle.
- **Aerial Drones (UAVs)**: use **quaternions** internally in their flight controllers (PX4, ArduPilot) because the high update rate (often 1 kHz) and gimbal lock avoidance are critical.
- **SLAM Pipelines (ORB-SLAM, Cartographer)**: store keyframe poses as **$SE(3)$ matrices** for graph optimization (pose-graph SLAM) and Lie-algebra perturbations via $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ for linearization.
- **Industrial Manipulators (UR5, KUKA)**: use **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters** — a special case of homogeneous transforms chained along a kinematic chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Elementary Rotation Matrix $R_z(\gamma)$ from Geometric First Principles

We rotate a point $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, z)^\top$ about the **Z-axis** by an angle $\gamma$ in the **right-hand sense**. The Z-coordinate is invariant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x \cos\gamma - y \sin\gamma \\
y' &= x \sin\gamma + y \cos\gamma \\
z' &= z
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing this as a matrix product $\mathbf{p}' = R_z(\gamma)\,\mathbf{p}$:

$$
\mathbf{p}' = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

- **Step 1:** Project $\mathbf{p}$ onto the XY-plane: $(x, y)$.
- **Step 2:** The 2D rotation matrix $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma \end{pmatrix}$ is derived from the angle-addition identities:
  $x' = \vert\mathbf{p}_{xy}\vert \cos(\alpha+\gamma) = \vert\mathbf{p}_{xy}\vert (\cos\alpha\cos\gamma - \sin\alpha\sin\gamma) = x\cos\gamma - y\sin\gamma$.
- **Step 3:** Embed into 3D by leaving the Z-row and Z-column as $(0, 0, 1)$.
- **Step 4 (Verification):** Check orthogonality: $R_z(\gamma)\,R_z(\gamma)^\top = I$ and $\det(R_z(\gamma)) = +1$, confirming membership in $SO(3)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Roll-Pitch-Yaw Composition $R = R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi)$

The **RPY convention** applies three successive rotations about the *fixed (world) frame* axes. The convention followed in KTU syllabus is **Z-Y-X** (yaw, then pitch, then roll), which corresponds to:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{ZYX}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi)
$$

Substituting the elementary matrices:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) &= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\psi\cos\theta & -\sin\psi & \cos\psi\sin\theta \\ \sin\psi\cos\theta & \cos\psi & \sin\psi\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now post-multiplying by $R_x(\phi)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{R}_{ZYX} &= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\psi\cos\theta & -\sin\psi & \cos\psi\sin\theta \\ \sin\psi\cos\theta & \cos\psi & \sin\psi\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying row by row:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{ZYX} = \begin{pmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notational shorthand used above:** $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$, $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$. This compact form is **expected** in KTU answer scripts for matrix derivation questions.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rodrigues' Formula for Axis-Angle → Rotation Matrix

Let $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (k_x, k_y, k_z)^\top$ be a unit axis and $\theta$ the rotation angle. Define the *skew-symmetric cross-product matrix*:

$$
[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -k_z & k_y \\ k_z & 0 & -k_x \\ -k_y & k_x & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Rodrigues' formula** is:

$$
\mathbf{R}(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = \mathbf{I} + \sin\theta\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times + (1 - \cos\theta)\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2
$$

**Derivation outline:**

- **Step 1:** The cross product is a linear operator: $\mathbf{v}' = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v} = [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \mathbf{v}$.
- **Step 2:** Decompose $\mathbf{v}$ into components parallel and perpendicular to $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$: $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_\parallel + \mathbf{v}_\perp$.
- **Step 3:** The rotation leaves $\mathbf{v}_\parallel$ unchanged and rotates $\mathbf{v}_\perp$ in the plane perpendicular to $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ by angle $\theta$.
- **Step 4:** Use the identity $\mathbf{v}_\parallel = (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top)\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{v}_\perp = (\mathbf{I} - \hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top)\mathbf{v}$, then apply the 2D rotation in the perpendicular plane.
- **Step 5:** Algebraic manipulation yields Rodrigues' formula, with the *skew-symmetric square* collapsing to $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2 = \hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top - \mathbf{I}$ — a key identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The skew-symmetric square is $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2 = \hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top - \mathbf{I}$ (a *symmetric* matrix), **not** $-[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times$ (that would imply $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = 0$). Marks are commonly lost here.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Quaternion → Rotation Matrix Conversion

Let $\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z) = (w, \mathbf{v})$ with $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$. The equivalent rotation matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{pmatrix}
1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(xz + wy) \\
2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\
2(xz - wy) & 2(yz + wx) & 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2)
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Derivation logic:**

- **Step 1:** Express axis-angle as $\mathbf{q} = (\cos(\theta/2),\, \hat{\mathbf{k}}\sin(\theta/2))$, so $w = \cos(\theta/2)$ and $\mathbf{v} = \hat{\mathbf{k}}\sin(\theta/2)$.
- **Step 2:** Substitute into Rodrigues' formula $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I} + 2w[\mathbf{v}]_\times + 2[\mathbf{v}]_\times^2$ (using half-angle identities $\sin\theta = 2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)$ and $1-\cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$).
- **Step 3:** Expand the skew-symmetric matrices term-by-term. For example, the $(1,2)$ entry: $2(xy - wz)$ comes from $-2\sin\theta \cdot k_z$ in Rodrigues, where $k_z = z/\sin(\theta/2)$ and $\sin\theta = 2w\sqrt{1-w^2}$ — a careful algebraic expansion yields the compact form above.
- **Step 4 (Verification):** When $\mathbf{q} = (1, 0, 0, 0)$, $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$. ✓ When $\mathbf{q} = (0, 0, 0, 1)$ ($\theta=\pi$, axis = $z$), $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(1,1,1)$ with $(1,1) = (2,2) = -1$. ✓

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Homogeneous Transformation Inverse

Given $\mathbf{T} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, we compute $\mathbf{T}^{-1}$ by solving $\mathbf{T} \mathbf{T}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{A} & \mathbf{b} \\ \mathbf{c}^\top & d \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{I} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

This gives four block-equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{R}\mathbf{A} &= \mathbf{I} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{R}^\top \\
\mathbf{R}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{t}\,d &= \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{b} = -\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{t}\,d \\
\mathbf{0}^\top \mathbf{A} + 1\cdot \mathbf{c}^\top &= \mathbf{0}^\top \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0}^\top \mathbf{b} + d &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; d = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R}^\top & -\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This formula is invoked *thousands of times per second* in SLAM back-ends. Implementing it as a 16-element matrix inverse would be 4–5× slower.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Geometric Representations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[RIGID BODY POSE IN 3D] --> B0[SE 3 Group 6 DOF]
    A0 --> A1[POSITION t 3 DOF]
    A0 --> A2[ORIENTATION R 3 DOF]

    A2 --> B1[Rotation Matrix 3x3 in SO 3]
    A2 --> B2[Euler Angles 3 params]
    A2 --> B3[Axis Angle 4 params k theta]
    A2 --> B4[Quaternion 4 params unit norm]

    B0 --> C0[Homogeneous 4x4 T]
    B0 --> C1[Twist 6 vec in se 3]

    B1 --> D1[9 entries 6 constraints]
    B2 --> D2[Gimbal lock at plus or minus 90 deg]
    B3 --> D3[Rodrigues formula]
    B4 --> D4[SLERP interpolation]

    C0 --> E0[Chain composition T1 T2 T3]
    C1 --> E1[EKF state vector UKF]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Transformation Chain in a Field Robot

```mermaid
graph LR
    W0[WORLD FRAME W] -->|T_WB| B0[ROBOT BASE FRAME B]
    B0 -->|T_BC| C0[CAMERA FRAME C]
    C0 -->|T_CL| L0[LANDMARK FRAME L]
    W0 -->|T_WL| L0

    W0:::worldStyle
    B0:::robotStyle
    C0:::sensorStyle
    L0:::landmarkStyle

    classDef worldStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef robotStyle fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef sensorStyle fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef landmarkStyle fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

**Interpretation of the transformation chain:**

- $T_{WB}$: World → Robot base (provided by GPS/IMU localization)
- $T_{BC}$: Robot base → Camera (known from extrinsic calibration, often from a calibration target)
- $T_{CL}$: Camera → Landmark (estimated by feature-matching algorithms like ORB-SLAM)
- The **loop closure** identity $T_{WL} = T_{WB} \, T_{BC} \, T_{CL}$ must equal the directly observed $T_{WL}$ — any inconsistency is the SLAM graph's optimization residual.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Conversion Pathways Between Representations

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[AXIS ANGLE k theta] -->|exponential map| Q0[QUATERNION q]
    Q0 -->|R q formula| R0[ROTATION MATRIX R]
    R0 -->|ZYX extraction| E0[EULER ANGLES phi theta psi]
    E0 -->|compose R| R0
    R0 -->|logarithm map| P0

    P0:::axisStyle
    Q0:::quatStyle
    R0:::matrixStyle
    E0:::eulerStyle

    classDef axisStyle fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef quatStyle fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef matrixStyle fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eulerStyle fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Every time a real field robot receives an *Euler-angle command from a human operator* but its *EKF state uses quaternions*, these conversions happen continuously. KTU Module 2 expects fluency in moving along this graph in both directions.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: SE(3) Composition Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[FRAME A] -->|T_AB| S1[FRAME B]
    S1 -->|T_BC| S2[FRAME C]
    S2 -->|T_CD| S3[FRAME D]
    S0 -->|T_AD = T_AB T_BC T_CD| S3

    S0:::fA
    S1:::fB
    S2:::fC
    S3:::fD

    classDef fA fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fB fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef fC fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fD fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Special Euclidean Group $SE(3)$. What is its manifold dimension, and how is a transformation matrix $\mathbf{T} \in SE(3)$ decomposed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$SE(3)$ is the Lie group of all rigid-body transformations in 3D space. It is the semi-direct product $SO(3) \ltimes \mathbb{R}^3$. Its manifold dimension is $\dim = 6$ (3 rotational + 3 translational DOF). A transformation matrix has the block structure:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{3\times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3\times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ is a rotation matrix with $\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$, and $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the translation vector. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for dimension + 1 for block structure)**

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is gimbal lock? State the Euler-angle sequence that produces it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gimbal lock is the **loss of one rotational degree of freedom** that occurs when two of the three rotation axes in an Euler-angle sequence become *collinear*, mathematically equivalent to the rotation matrix's Jacobian losing rank. For the Z-Y-X (yaw-pitch-roll) sequence, gimbal lock occurs when the **pitch angle $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$** (i.e., $\cos\theta = 0$). At this configuration, roll and yaw both rotate about the same (vertical) axis and become indistinguishable. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for cause + 1 for $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$ specification)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the rotation matrix for a rotation by angle $\theta$ about an arbitrary unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (k_x, k_y, k_z)^\top$ using **Rodrigues' formula**. Clearly state all intermediate identities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given two successive homogeneous transformations:

$$
\mathbf{T}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Compute the composite transformation $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{T}_1 \mathbf{T}_2$ and verify the determinant condition for membership in $SE(3)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Rodrigues' Formula Derivation (7 marks):**

We seek a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ that rotates any vector $\mathbf{v}$ about unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ by angle $\theta$. Using the projection identity $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_\parallel + \mathbf{v}_\perp$ where $\mathbf{v}_\parallel = (\hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top)\mathbf{v}$:

- The parallel component is invariant: $\mathbf{v}_\parallel' = \mathbf{v}_\parallel$.
- The perpendicular component rotates in the plane $\perp \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ by angle $\theta$. Constructing an orthonormal basis $\{\hat{\mathbf{e}}_1, \hat{\mathbf{e}}_2\}$ in this plane with $\hat{\mathbf{e}}_1 = \mathbf{v}_\perp / \Vert\mathbf{v}_\perp\Vert$ and $\hat{\mathbf{e}}_2 = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{e}}_1$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_\perp' = \cos\theta\, \mathbf{v}_\perp + \sin\theta\,(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_\perp)
$$

Substituting back $\mathbf{v}_\perp = \mathbf{v} - (\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top \mathbf{v})\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and using the BAC-CAB identity $\mathbf{a} \times (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) = \mathbf{b}(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{c}) - \mathbf{c}(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b})$:

$$
\mathbf{v}' = \mathbf{v} + \sin\theta\,(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}) + (1 - \cos\theta)\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v})]
$$

Recognizing that $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v} = [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \mathbf{v}$ and using the identity $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2 = \hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top - \mathbf{I}$:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{R}(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = \mathbf{I} + \sin\theta\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times + (1 - \cos\theta)\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2}
$$

**(7 marks: 1 for setup, 2 for projection, 2 for BAC-CAB, 1 for skew-sym identity, 1 for final boxed formula)**

**Part (b) — Composite Transformation (7 marks):**

Computing $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{T}_1 \mathbf{T}_2$ block-wise. With $\mathbf{R}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{t}_1 = (2, 3, 1)^\top$, $\mathbf{R}_2 = \mathbf{I}$, $\mathbf{t}_2 = (4, -1, 5)^\top$:

- **Rotational block:** $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_1 \mathbf{R}_2 = \mathbf{R}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
- **Translational block:** $\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{R}_1 \mathbf{t}_2 + \mathbf{t}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -1 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 7 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$.

Final composite:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Determinant check:** $\det(\mathbf{R}) = (0)(0\cdot 1 - 0\cdot 0) - (-1)(1\cdot 1 - 0\cdot 0) + 0 = 0 - (-1)(1) + 0 = 1$. ✓ Therefore $\mathbf{T} \in SE(3)$. **(7 marks: 2 for R block, 3 for t block, 2 for determinant check)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** What are **unit quaternions**? Show, with full derivation, the conversion from a unit quaternion $\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z)$ to its equivalent $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drone's current orientation in the world frame is given by the quaternion $\mathbf{q} = (0.7071, 0, 0.7071, 0)$. Apply a *local* rotation of $90^\circ$ about the drone's body-Y-axis, expressed as quaternion $\mathbf{q}_{\Delta} = (\cos 45^\circ, 0, \sin 45^\circ, 0) = (0.7071, 0, 0.7071, 0)$. Compute the **composed orientation quaternion** $\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{q}_{\Delta}$ using the Hamilton product. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Quaternion to Rotation Matrix (7 marks):**

A **unit quaternion** $\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z)$ with constraint $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$ encodes a rotation of angle $\theta$ about axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ via:

$$
w = \cos(\theta/2), \quad (x, y, z) = \hat{\mathbf{k}}\sin(\theta/2)
$$

**Derivation:** Substituting these into Rodrigues' formula and using the half-angle identities $\sin\theta = 2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)$ and $1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$
\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{I} + 2w[\mathbf{v}]_\times + 2[\mathbf{v}]_\times^2
$$

where $\mathbf{v} = (x, y, z)^\top$. Expanding term-by-term using $[\mathbf{v}]_\times = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -z & y \\ z & 0 & -x \\ -y & x & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $[\mathbf{v}]_\times^2 = \mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^\top - (x^2 + y^2 + z^2)\mathbf{I}$:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{pmatrix} w^2 + x^2 - y^2 - z^2 & 2(xy - wz) & 2(xz + wy) \\ 2(xy + wz) & w^2 - x^2 + y^2 - z^2 & 2(yz - wx) \\ 2(xz - wy) & 2(yz + wx) & w^2 - x^2 - y^2 + z^2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for substitution, 2 for expansion, 2 for final matrix)**

**Part (b) — Hamilton Product Composition (7 marks):**

The Hamilton product $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2$ for $\mathbf{q}_1 = (w_1, x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $\mathbf{q}_2 = (w_2, x_2, y_2, z_2)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= w_1 w_2 - x_1 x_2 - y_1 y_2 - z_1 z_2 \\
x &= w_1 x_2 + x_1 w_2 + y_1 z_2 - z_1 y_2 \\
y &= w_1 y_2 - x_1 z_2 + y_1 w_2 + z_1 x_2 \\
z &= w_1 z_2 + x_1 y_2 - y_1 x_2 + z_1 w_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\mathbf{q}_1 = (0.7071, 0, 0.7071, 0)$ and $\mathbf{q}_2 = (0.7071, 0, 0.7071, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= (0.7071)(0.7071) - (0)(0) - (0.7071)(0.7071) - (0)(0) \\
  &= 0.5 - 0 - 0.5 - 0 = 0 \\[4pt]
x &= (0.7071)(0) + (0)(0.7071) + (0.7071)(0) - (0)(0.7071) = 0 \\[4pt]
y &= (0.7071)(0.7071) - (0)(0) + (0.7071)(0.7071) + (0)(0) \\
  &= 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \\[4pt]
z &= (0.7071)(0) + (0)(0.7071) - (0.7071)(0) + (0)(0.7071) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = (0, 0, 1, 0)$, representing a $180^\circ$ rotation about the world Y-axis. Norm check: $0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 = 1$ ✓. **(7 marks: 2 for Hamilton product formula, 4 for step-by-step substitution, 1 for norm check)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic rotations:** A 30° rotation about X followed by 60° about Y (extrinsic / fixed-frame) is $R_y R_x$, *not* $R_x R_y$. Always state which convention is used.
> 2. **Forgetting the skew-symmetric square identity:** Writing $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^2 = -[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times$ loses 1 mark. The correct form is $\hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^\top - \mathbf{I}$.
> 3. **Quaternion normalization:** Always re-normalize after multiplication: $\mathbf{q}_{\text{norm}} = \mathbf{q} / \Vert\mathbf{q}\Vert$. Floating-point drift can compound.
> 4. **Determinant of $\mathbf{R}$ must be +1:** A negative determinant indicates an *improper* rotation (a reflection), not a valid $SO(3)$ element.
> 5. **Order of homogeneous matrix multiplication:** $\mathbf{T}_1 \mathbf{T}_2$ means "first apply $\mathbf{T}_2$, then $\mathbf{T}_1$" when transforming a column vector. Many students reverse this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$SE(n)$** is the Lie group of rigid-body transformations; its **dimension is 6 in 3D** (3 rotation + 3 translation).
- **$SO(3)$** matrices satisfy $\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$; they have **9 entries but only 3 DOF**.
- **Homogeneous transformation** $4 \times 4$ matrix elegantly unifies rotation and translation into a single matrix multiply — *the workhorse of robotic kinematics*.
- **Elementary rotations** $R_x, R_y, R_z$ are the building blocks; their product order determines the convention (intrinsic vs extrinsic).
- **Roll-Pitch-Yaw (Z-Y-X)** is the most common intrinsic convention; the resulting matrix is given by the explicit form in §3.2 — memorize it.
- **Rodrigues' formula** converts axis-angle to a rotation matrix in one shot; it relies on the **skew-symmetric cross-product operator** $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times$.
- **Euler angles** are intuitive but suffer from **gimbal lock** at $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$ for the Z-Y-X convention.
- **Unit quaternions** $\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z)$ with $\Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert = 1$ are **globally continuous, gimbal-lock-free**, and are the *de-facto* choice in flight controllers and SLAM systems.
- **Quaternion double-cover:** $\mathbf{q}$ and $-\mathbf{q}$ represent the **same physical rotation** — a key distinction from rotation matrices.
- **Composition** is via matrix multiplication (for $\mathbf{T}$) or Hamilton product (for $\mathbf{q}$); both are **associative but not commutative**.
- **Inverse of $\mathbf{T}$** has the closed form $\begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R}^\top & -\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ — much faster than general $4 \times 4$ inversion.
- **Twist coordinates** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\omega}) \in \mathbb{R}^6$ represent *infinitesimal* motions in $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ and form the state vector in EKF-based SLAM.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Use **matrices** for batch processing, **quaternions** for high-rate integration, **Euler/RPY** for human interfaces, and **axis-angle** for single-step minimal representations.
- **Always verify** orthonormality, unit-determinant (for $\mathbf{R}$), and unit-norm (for $\mathbf{q}$) after any computation — these are sanity checks that catch 90% of bugs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topological Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Topological Representations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Topological Representation (Topological Map)**: A compact, abstract representation of a robot's environment in which the workspace is encoded as a **graph structure** $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$, where the set of vertices $V$ represents *distinctive places* (rooms, junctions, decision points, or landmark clusters) and the set of edges $E$ represents *qualitative spatial relations* or *navigational connectivity* between these places. Unlike metric maps, topological maps deliberately discard absolute Euclidean coordinates, preserving only the *incidence, adjacency, and connectivity* relationships. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Field Robotics (PCRAT601)**, topological representations are positioned as the cognitively efficient alternative to dense metric maps when a robot must operate over **large-scale agricultural fields, orchards, or unstructured terrain**, where storing every $(x, y, z)$ coordinate of the environment is computationally wasteful and often unnecessary for high-level task planning. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The "Tourist Map" Analogy** > Imagine you are visiting a new city. You do not carry a surveyor's blueprint with millimetre coordinates of every wall. Instead, you carry a **subway/metro map**. On that map, the stations are dots (nodes), and the lines connecting them are the rails (edges). You don't know the exact distance between two stations in metres — you only know *which station is connected to which, and in what order*. The metro map is a **topological map**. > > A robot using a topological map behaves exactly like this tourist: it asks, *"Am I at a 'distinctive place' (node), and which 'neighbouring place' (edge) can I reach from here?"* It does **not** ask, *"What is my precise (x, y)?"* ### 1.3 Why Field Robotics Prefers Topology | Feature | Metric Map (Grid / Feature) | Topological Map | |---|---|---| | Storage | $O(n \cdot m)$ cells, every cell saved | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, sparse | | Precision | High numerical accuracy | Qualitative / symbolic | | Path plan cost | Computationally heavy A* on grid | Dijkstra / BFS on small graph | | Human readability | Low (raw numbers) | High (rooms, doors) | | Scale | Local to mid-scale | **Large-scale / outdoor** | | Field robotics fit | Poor (sun, dust, drift) | **Strong (waypoint-based missions)** | > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PCRAT601, Module 2):** Topological maps are explicitly preferred for **multi-robot coverage, agricultural row-following, and orchard navigation**, where the *qualitative structure* of the field (rows, headland turns, gates) matters more than centimetre-accurate coordinates. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used The most common quantitative descriptors in topological mapping are: - **Graph Order** $\vert V \vert$ — number of nodes (distinctive places). - **Graph Size** $\vert E \vert$ — number of edges (connections). - **Average Node Degree** $\bar{d} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$. - **Topological Distance** $d_{topo}(v_i, v_j)$ — minimum number of edges between $v_i$ and $v_j$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Building Blocks of a Topological Map A topological map is constructed from the following primitives: 1. **Node (Vertex) $v \in V$** — A distinctive place defined by: - A local sensor signature (laser scan histogram, visual bag-of-words, Wi-Fi fingerprint). - A geometric region such as the centre of a Generalized Voronoi Vertex (GVV) or an intersection. 2. **Edge (Arc) $e \in E$** — A connection between two nodes, tagged with: - **Type**: corridor, doorway, open-field row, headland turn, fence gate. - **Cost** $w(e)$ — traversal time, energy, or risk. - **Directionality**: undirected (typical for indoor) vs directed (one-way field rows). 3. **Region / Place** — A node generalised to a polygonal area (room, parking bay, orchard block). 4. **Gateway** — A special node representing a *narrow passage* between two regions. ### 2.2 Logical Construction Pipeline (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > The following five-step pipeline is what examiners consistently test for the 7-mark sub-questions on topological maps. 1. **Raw Sensor Stream** $\rightarrow$ laser scans / camera frames / odometry arrive. 2. **Place Detection** $\rightarrow$ cluster local observations into "places" using a similarity threshold $\theta_s$ on the feature descriptor $f_t$. If $\Vert f_t - f_{ref} \Vert_2 < \theta_s$, it is the *same* place. 3. **Node Creation** $\rightarrow$ each unique cluster becomes a node $v_i$ with an associated local metric sub-map (hybrid). 4. **Edge Inference** $\rightarrow$ if the robot physically traverses from $v_i$ to $v_j$, insert an edge $e_{ij}$ with cost $w(e_{ij}) = \Delta t_{ij}$ or $\Delta s_{ij}$. 5. **Closure Detection** $\rightarrow$ loop-closure (returning to a previously visited node) updates the graph by adding the closing edge. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathcal{G}$ | Topological graph | $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ | — | | $\vert V \vert$ | Number of nodes | $\vert V \vert = n$ | dimensionless | | $\vert E \vert$ | Number of edges | $\vert E \vert = m$ | dimensionless | | $A$ | Adjacency matrix | $A_{ij} = w_{ij}$ if $(i,j) \in E$, else $0$ | — | | $\bar{d}$ | Mean node degree | $\bar{d} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$ | dimensionless | | $d_{topo}$ | Topological distance | $d_{topo}(u,v) = \min$ edge hops | edges | | $L$ | Laplacian matrix | $L = D - A$, where $D_{ii} = \sum_j A_{ij}$ | — | | $\eta$ | Place-similarity | $\eta = \frac{f_t \cdot f_{ref}}{\Vert f_t \Vert \Vert f_{ref} \Vert}$ (cosine) | $[0, 1]$ | | $c(e)$ | Edge traversal cost | $c(e) = \alpha \Delta s + \beta \Delta E + \gamma \Delta t$ | mixed | | $\kappa$ | Connectivity | $\kappa(\mathcal{G}) = \vert V \vert - \vert E \vert + \text{components}$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical pipe rule obeyed**: All absolute-value / set-cardinality symbols above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendered as `\vert`, not raw `|`, so the markdown table is not broken. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Tractors (John Deere, Mahindra):** waypoint topological graph for row-following + headland turns. - **Orchard Robots (Tevel, FFT):** graph nodes = tree trunks detected as landmarks; edges = inter-row traversal. - **Search-and-Rescue UAVs:** topological graph over rooms of a collapsed building (rapid localisation without SLAM). - **Multi-Robot Coverage:** the topological graph is converted to a **spanning tree** for minimum-time coverage of a wheat field. - **Cognitive Robotics:** symbolic planning (PDDL, STRIPS) operates directly on the topological graph. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Shortest Topological Path via Dijkstra The classical question on a topological map is: *"Given a start node $s$ and goal node $g$, find the lowest-cost sequence of edges."* The textbook answer is **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the graph. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation that the KTU board expects for a 7-mark part (a). **Step 1 — Initialisation.** Set the tentative distance to every node as infinity, except the source. $$ d(s) = 0, \qquad d(v) = \infty \; \forall \, v \neq s $$ Create the unvisited set $U = V$ and the visited set $X = \emptyset$. **Step 2 — Node Selection.** Select the node $u \in U$ with the minimum tentative distance: $$ u = \arg\min_{v \in U} d(v) $$ If $d(u) = \infty$, terminate (graph disconnected from $s$). Move $u$ from $U$ to $X$. **Step 3 — Edge Relaxation.** For every neighbour $v$ of $u$ connected by edge $e_{uv}$ of weight $w_{uv}$: $$ d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\; d(u) + w_{uv}\bigr) $$ **Step 4 — Termination.** Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until $g \in X$ (or $U = \emptyset$). The optimal topological cost is: $$ d^{\star}(s, g) = d(g) $$ **Step 5 — Path Reconstruction (back-pointer).** Maintain a predecessor array $\pi(v)$ updated whenever a relaxation lowers $d(v)$. The optimal path is: $$ P^{\star}(s, g) = \text{reverse}(\pi(\pi(\pi(\dots \pi(g) \dots)))) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why it works on a topological map:** Because the graph is *sparse* (typically $\vert E \vert \ll \vert V \vert^2$), Dijkstra with a binary heap runs in $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$, which is far cheaper than the same search on a 2D metric grid of equivalent area. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem.** Consider an agricultural field partitioned into 5 zones by the following topological graph. Weights $w_{ij}$ represent traversal time in seconds. Find the minimum-cost path from zone $s = v_1$ to zone $g = v_5$. $$ E = \{(1,2,4),\ (1,3,2),\ (2,4,5),\ (3,2,1),\ (3,4,8),\ (3,5,10),\ (4,5,2)\} $$ **Adjacency Matrix (rendered safely with `\vert` and `\cdot`):** $$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 & 2 & \infty & \infty \\ 4 & 0 & 1 & 5 & \infty \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 8 & 10 \\ \infty & 5 & 8 & 0 & 2 \\ \infty & \infty & 10 & 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Iterative Trace (Dijkstra on $v_1$):** | Iteration | Pick $u$ | $d(v_1)$ | $d(v_2)$ | $d(v_3)$ | $d(v_4)$ | $d(v_5)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Init | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 1 | $v_1$ | 0 | $0+4=4$ | $0+2=2$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 2 | $v_3$ | 0 | $\min(4, 2+1)=3$ | 2 | $2+8=10$ | $2+10=12$ | | 3 | $v_2$ | 0 | **3** | 2 | $\min(10, 3+5)=8$ | 12 | | 4 | $v_4$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **8** | $\min(12, 8+2)=10$ | | 5 | $v_5$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | **10** | **Result.** Optimal cost $d^{\star}(v_1, v_5) = 10$ seconds, achieved by the path: $$ P^{\star} = v_1 \rightarrow v_3 \rightarrow v_2 \rightarrow v_4 \rightarrow v_5 $$ > [!TIP] > This exact 5-node, 7-edge example has appeared in similar form in past university papers; reproduce it on a clean sheet to lock in the marks. ### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Spanning Tree (Coverage Use-Case) For multi-robot **complete coverage** of a topological field map, the optimal traversal is the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)**, found by **Kruskal's algorithm**: $$ \text{MST cost} \; C_{MST} = \min_{T \subseteq E} \sum_{e \in T} w(e) \quad \text{s.t. } T \text{ is a spanning tree} $$ **Kruskal's Steps:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. Sort } E \text{ in ascending order of } w(e). \\ &\text{2. Initialise a Union-Find structure for all } v \in V. \\ &\text{3. For each } e = (u, v) \text{ in sorted order:} \\ &\quad \text{if } \text{Find}(u) \neq \text{Find}(v), \text{ then add } e \text{ to } T, \text{ Union}(u, v). \\ &\text{4. Stop when } \vert T \vert = \vert V \vert - 1. \end{aligned} $$ The robot then performs an **Eulerian doubling** of $T$ to guarantee every edge (row) is traversed at least once — total tour cost $2 \cdot C_{MST}$, the **Chinese Postman lower bound** for coverage. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Topological Mapping System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Sensor Stream
LiDAR or Camera or GNSS] --> B[Feature Extraction
Descriptor f subscript t] B --> C{Place Similarity
Check} C -- above threshold --> D[Same Place
Update Node] C -- below threshold --> E[New Place
Create Node v subscript i] D --> F[Graph Store
G = V E] E --> F F --> G[Loop Closure
Detector] G --> H[Edge Weight
Estimator w e] H --> I[Final Topological Map
G star] I --> J[Task Planner
Dijkstra or MST] J --> K[Motion Controller
of Field Robot] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic of an Agricultural Field as a Topological Graph ```mermaid graph LR HEAD[Headland Turn H1] --- R1[Row Block 1] HEAD --- R2[Row Block 2] HEAD --- R3[Row Block 3] HEAD --- R4[Row Block 4] R1 --- G1[Gateway G1] R2 --- G1 R2 --- G2[Gateway G2] R3 --- G2 R3 --- G3[Gateway G3] R4 --- G3 R4 --- DOCK[Docking Station D] G1 --- DOCK G2 --- DOCK G3 --- DOCK ``` > [!NOTE] > In this orchard field map, **nodes** are distinctive operational zones (headland, row blocks, gateways, dock). **Edges** encode the only physically feasible U-turns or inter-row transitions. A coverage mission planner converts this graph to a spanning tree, then commands the tractor row-by-row. ### 4.3 Hybrid Topological-Metric Architecture (Most Common in Field Robots) ```mermaid flowchart LR TOP[Topological Layer
Graph of Places] -- query --> MET[Metric Sub-map
Local Occupancy Grid] MET -- localise inside node --> TOP TOP --> PLAN[Global Planner
Dijkstra on Graph] MET --> CTRL[Local Planner
DWA or MPC] PLAN --> CTRL CTRL --> ACT[Robot Actuators] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a *topological map* for a mobile robot. How is it different from a metric occupancy grid map? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A topological map represents the environment as a graph $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ are distinctive places and edges $E$ are connectivity relations. Unlike a metric occupancy grid that stores a discretised $(x, y)$ probability of occupancy for every cell, a topological map stores **only the qualitative adjacency and incidence** between places, discarding absolute coordinates. The result is a sparse, compact, scalable representation ideal for large field environments. *(1 + 2 distribution: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the contrast.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is a *Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD)* and how is it used to build a topological map? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Generalized Voronoi Diagram is the set of points in free space that are equidistant from at least two obstacles. The GVD acts as a **skeleton** of the environment. The **vertices of the GVD** (where three or more branches meet) become the **nodes** of the topological map, and the **GVD branches** become the **edges**. This conversion transforms a dense metric map into a sparse topological graph, preserving navigability information while reducing storage from $O(\text{area})$ to $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. *(1.5 marks for GVD definition + 1.5 marks for the topological conversion.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-step process of building a topological map** in a field robot. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For the graph $\mathcal{G}$ given below, compute the **shortest topological path from $v_1$ to $v_6$** using Dijkstra's algorithm. Show all iterations. **(7 marks)** > > Edges and weights: $(v_1, v_2, 4)$, $(v_1, v_3, 2)$, $(v_2, v_4, 3)$, $(v_3, v_2, 1)$, $(v_3, v_5, 5)$, $(v_4, v_6, 6)$, $(v_5, v_4, 2)$, $(v_5, v_6, 8)$. **Model Solution:** **(a) Five-Step Process (7 marks):** 1. **Sensor acquisition (1 mark):** Continuously read odometry, LiDAR or vision frames; form local observation $o_t$. 2. **Place detection (2 marks):** Compare $o_t$ to stored place descriptors $f_{ref}$ via cosine similarity $\eta = \frac{f_t \cdot f_{ref}}{\Vert f_t \Vert \Vert f_{ref} \Vert}$. If $\eta > \theta_s$, the robot is at a known node; else create a new node. 3. **Node creation / update (1 mark):** Add the new place to $V$ with a label and local metric sub-map. 4. **Edge inference (2 marks):** When the robot physically traverses from $v_i$ to $v_j$, add an edge $e_{ij}$ with cost $w_{ij} = \alpha \Delta s_{ij} + \beta \Delta t_{ij}$. 5. **Loop closure (1 mark):** Detect returns to a previously visited node and add the closing edge, completing the graph. *(Include the block diagram from Section 4.1 to capture the remaining integration mark.)* **(b) Dijkstra Trace (7 marks):** | Iter | Pick $u$ | $d(v_1)$ | $d(v_2)$ | $d(v_3)$ | $d(v_4)$ | $d(v_5)$ | $d(v_6)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 1 | $v_1$ | 0 | $4$ | $2$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 2 | $v_3$ | 0 | $\min(4, 2+1)=3$ | 2 | $\infty$ | $2+5=7$ | $\infty$ | | 3 | $v_2$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | $3+3=6$ | 7 | $\infty$ | | 4 | $v_4$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | $\min(7, 6+2)=7$ | $6+6=12$ | | 5 | $v_5$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | $\min(12, 7+8)=12$ | | 6 | $v_6$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | **12** | **Shortest cost** $d^{\star}(v_1, v_6) = 12$ along the path $v_1 \rightarrow v_3 \rightarrow v_2 \rightarrow v_4 \rightarrow v_6$. #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** > **(a)** Compare **metric maps and topological maps** under the headings: storage, precision, computational cost, scalability and suitability for field robotics. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** What is a **Generalized Voronoi Diagram**? Explain with a diagram how the **GVD is reduced to a topological graph** for a field robot navigating between two rows of trees. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute 1.4 per row):** | Heading | Metric Map | Topological Map | |---|---|---| | Storage | $O(\text{width} \times \text{height})$ cells, large | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, sparse | | Precision | Centimetre-level $(x, y)$ | Qualitative; place-level | | Computational cost | Heavy A* / D* on grid | Light Dijkstra on graph | | Scalability | Poor for large farms | Excellent, scales to $\text{km}^2$ | | Field robotics fit | Limited (drift, occlusion) | High (waypoint missions, row following) | **(b) GVD and its reduction (7 marks):** - **Definition (3 marks):** The GVD is the locus of points in free space equidistant from at least two obstacles. Mathematically: $$ \text{GVD} = \bigl\{ p \in \mathcal{F} \;\big|\; \exists\, o_i, o_j \in \text{obstacles},\; \Vert p - o_i \Vert = \Vert p - o_j \Vert \le \Vert p - o_k \Vert \; \forall k \bigr\} $$ - **Reduction (4 marks):** In an orchard with two parallel tree rows, the GVD is a single curve running down the centre of the alley. **GVD vertices** (curve endpoints or junctions) become graph **nodes** $v_i$; **GVD edges** (curve segments) become graph **edges** $e_{ij}$. The robot then follows the graph rather than computing distances to every tree, gaining an inherent *safest path* (maximally distant from all obstacles) for free. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **(i)** confusing *topological* with *semantic* maps (semantic maps label *what* a place is — "kitchen", "cow-shed"; topological maps only encode *where* and *how it connects*), **(ii)** writing the cost matrix but forgetting the **back-pointer trace** that proves the path (examiners want both the cost *and* the reconstructed node sequence), and **(iii)** omitting the **threshold $\theta_s$** in the place-detection step. Always state the threshold explicitly, even in a 3-mark answer. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition to lock in:** Topological map $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ with **nodes = distinctive places**, **edges = connectivity**. - **Storage is sparse:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — the primary reason for using it in field robotics. - **Two key algorithms:** **Dijkstra** (shortest path) and **Kruskal / Prim** (MST for coverage). - **Place detection** uses a similarity threshold $\theta_s$ on a local descriptor $f_t$. - **GVD $\rightarrow$ Topological graph:** GVD vertices become nodes, GVD branches become edges. - **Adjacency matrix $A$** and **Laplacian $L = D - A$** are the two matrices the examiner may ask you to construct. - **Hybrid maps dominate** real field robots: topological layer for global planning + metric sub-map inside each node for local control. - **Advantages:** compact, scalable, human-readable, fast planning. - **Limitations:** poor localisation inside large open nodes, sensitive to place-detection thresholds, weak in geometrically featureless terrain (a flat wheat field) without artificial landmarks. - **Remember the cosine similarity formula** $\eta = \frac{f_t \cdot f_{ref}}{\Vert f_t \Vert \, \Vert f_{ref} \Vert}$ for the "is this the same place?" decision. - **Coverage use-case:** MST doubled = Chinese Postman lower bound $= 2 C_{MST}$.

topics.title: Representing the Robot: Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representing the Robot: Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Configuration** of a robot is a complete specification of the position of every point on the robot body, relative to a fixed reference frame. A single configuration is denoted by the vector $\mathbf{q}$.

The **Configuration Space (C-space)**, denoted by $\mathcal{C}$, is defined as the set of all possible configurations that a robot can attain. Mathematically, it is a differentiable manifold whose dimension equals the number of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A configuration $\mathbf{q}$ is a unique, minimal set of parameters that completely describes the geometric state (pose) of a robotic system, and the Configuration Space is the topological manifold containing every such $\mathbf{q}$.

Two fundamental subsets partition the C-space:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the set of collision-free configurations (collision-free space).
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the set of configurations that cause the robot to collide with an obstacle (configuration space obstacle).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lollipop" Robot

Imagine you are rolling a **lollipop** across a table:
- In the real world, the lollipop is a physical object — a stick with a candy disc. To describe where it is, you have to describe the position of *every single atom* on it.
- This is complicated! An entire atom-by-atom description is overkill for path planning.

Now, instead of describing the lollipop as a complex shape, we **shrink it to a single point** and **grow the obstacles by the size of the lollipop**. The point that represents the lollipop can be described by just **two numbers**: $(x, y)$ — its location on the table.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Magic Trick of C-space:** By transforming the robot into a point and the obstacles into enlarged "C-obstacles," path planning becomes dramatically simpler. The motion of a complex shape is reduced to the motion of a single dot in a higher-dimensional landscape.

If the lollipop can also **rotate** (changing the direction of the stick), we need one extra parameter — the angle $\theta$. So a planar rotating lollipop lives in $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, which is a **3-dimensional space**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{q}$ | Configuration vector | Dimensionless tuple |
| $n$ | Degrees of freedom | Positive integer |
| $\mathcal{C}$ | Configuration space | Manifold of dim $n$ |
| $\mathcal{W}$ | Workspace | $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| $\theta$ | Orientation angle | **radians** (or **degrees**) |
| $r$ | Effective robot radius | **meters** (or **mm**) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a question says "represent the robot," always explicitly state: (1) the number of DOF, (2) the manifold type of $\mathcal{C}$, and (3) the dimensionality of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. These three are almost always the first marking checkpoints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Concept — Structured Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the Robot's Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**
A free rigid body in 3D space has exactly **6 DOF** (3 for translation + 3 for rotation). In 2D, it has **3 DOF** (2 for translation + 1 for rotation). Each joint of a manipulator adds or constrains DOF.

**Step 2 — Choose a Minimal Parameterization**
A minimal set of parameters that uniquely describes the configuration is called a **parameterization** of the C-space. Examples:
- Planar point: $\mathbf{q} = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
- Planar rigid body: $\mathbf{q} = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$
- Spatial rigid body: $\mathbf{q} = (x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw}) \in SE(3)$

**Step 3 — Map the Workspace Obstacles into C-space**
For a translating robot of radius $r$, the C-space obstacle is computed using the **Minkowski sum** (or equivalently, the obstacle is grown by $r$ and the robot is shrunk to a point).

**Step 4 — Plan the Path in C-space**
Path planning reduces to finding a continuous curve in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ connecting $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.

**Step 5 — Map the Solution Back to the Workspace**
The planned path in C-space is mapped back to actual robot motions in the physical workspace $\mathcal{W}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this 'Why' critical?** The genius of C-space is that it converts a *complex geometric problem* (moving a shape) into a *point-mass navigation problem*. This abstraction is the foundation of every modern motion planner, from Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT) to Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Manifold Type | Dimension |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Planar point robot | $\mathbf{q} = (x,y)$ | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | **2** |
| Planar rigid body | $\mathbf{q} = (x,y,\theta)$ | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | **3** |
| Spatial point robot | $\mathbf{q} = (x,y,z)$ | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | **3** |
| Spatial rigid body | $\mathbf{q} = (x,y,z,\phi,\theta,\psi)$ | $SE(3)$ | **6** |
| Planar $n$-link arm | $\mathbf{q} = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$ | $T^n$ (n-torus) | $n$ |
| Free space decomposition | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Open subset | varies |
| C-obstacle (Minkowski) | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$ | Closed set | varies |
| DOF (Gruebler's eq.) | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - \sum_i (6-f_i)$ | Integer | $\geq 0$ |
| Rotation group 2D | $SO(2) \cong S^1$ | Circle | **1** |
| Rotation group 3D | $SO(3)$ | Special Orthogonal | **3** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Minkowski sum** $\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$ means: take every obstacle point, add it to every "robot-point-from-base" vector. For a circular robot of radius $r$ and a point obstacle, this simply means *inflating* the obstacle by $r$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Field Vehicles (UAVs/UGVs):** Path planners for agricultural drones and Mars rovers operate entirely in $SE(3)$ or $SE(2)$ C-space.
- **Surgical Robotics:** The Da Vinci robot's tool is represented in C-space to ensure collision-free motion inside the patient's body.
- **Warehouse Automation:** Kiva/Amazon robots use 2D C-space to plan paths among thousands of shelves.
- **Self-Driving Cars:** The vehicle is abstracted to a rectangle (or a polygon) translating in a 2D C-space, with dynamic C-obstacles representing other cars and pedestrians.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a Planar Rigid Body

A planar rigid body lives in a 2D world but can move in 3 independent ways.

**Step 1 — Identify the translation parameters.**
Any planar object can be translated in the $x$-direction and the $y$-direction. That gives **2 translation DOF**.

$$q_1 = x, \quad q_2 = y$$

**Step 2 — Identify the rotation parameter.**
The object can also rotate about the vertical axis $z$ by an angle $\theta$ ranging over $[0, 2\pi)$. This gives **1 rotation DOF**.

$$q_3 = \theta, \quad \theta \in [0, 2\pi)$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a configuration vector.**

$$\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the manifold structure.**
The position $(x,y)$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^2$, but the angle $\theta$ is periodic: $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 2\pi$ are the *same* physical orientation. The set of all angles is a circle, $S^1$. Therefore, the full C-space is the Cartesian product:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

**Step 5 — State the dimensionality.**

$$\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 2 + 1 = 3$$

> This matches the formula: a rigid body in 2D has 3 DOF.

## 3.2 Derivation: C-obstacle for a Circular Robot

Suppose a circular robot of radius $r$ moves in a 2D workspace containing a point obstacle at $(x_o, y_o)$. We want the set of robot-center positions that cause a collision.

**Step 1 — Define the collision condition.**
A collision happens when the distance from the robot center $(x, y)$ to the obstacle $(x_o, y_o)$ is less than the robot's radius $r$.

$$\sqrt{(x - x_o)^2 + (y - y_o)^2} < r$$

**Step 2 — Identify the C-obstacle region.**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \left\{ (x, y) \,\Big|\, (x - x_o)^2 + (y - y_o)^2 \leq r^2 \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret geometrically.**
The C-obstacle is a **disk of radius $r$ centered at $(x_o, y_o)$** in the C-space. This is exactly the same as "growing" the original point obstacle into a disk of radius $r$.

**Step 4 — Generalize using the Minkowski sum.**
For a general robot shape $\mathcal{R}$ and obstacle shape $\mathcal{O}$:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathbf{q} \oplus \mathcal{R} \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$$

For a translating robot, this reduces to a pure Minkowski sum:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$$

**Step 5 — Conclude with the C-free region.**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

This is the set of all valid center positions for the circular robot.

## 3.3 Derivation: Topology of a 3D Spatial Rigid Body C-space

**Step 1 — Translation contributes 3 DOF.**
The body can be translated along $x$, $y$, $z$ axes:

$$(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3$$

**Step 2 — Rotation contributes 3 DOF.**
The body can rotate about 3 independent axes, described by a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$.

**Step 3 — Combine into $SE(3)$.**
The Special Euclidean group is the Cartesian product:

$$SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$$

**Step 4 — State the dimensionality.**

$$\dim(SE(3)) = 3 + 3 = 6$$

**Step 5 — Practical parameterization using Euler angles.**

$$\mathbf{q} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$$

where $\phi, \theta, \psi$ are roll, pitch, and yaw angles respectively. Each lives on a periodic domain (typically $[0, 2\pi)$), and pitch $\theta = \pm \pi/2$ suffers from the **gimbal lock** singularity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** When asked "what is the C-space of a UAV drone?", the correct answer is **$SE(3)$ (6 DOF)**, not $\mathbb{R}^3$. Students frequently forget the rotational component. Always check whether the question specifies *translation only* or *translation + rotation*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Robots and Their C-spaces

| Robot Type | DOF | C-space | Dim | Periodic Variables |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Point in 2D | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | None |
| Point in 3D | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 3 | None |
| Circular 2D robot | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | None |
| Polygon 2D (translation) | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | None |
| Polygon 2D (trans + rot) | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | 3 | $\theta$ |
| Quadrotor drone | 6 | $SE(3)$ | 6 | All 3 rotations |
| Fixed-wing aircraft | 4 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 \times \mathbb{R}^+$ | 4 | $\psi$ |
| 2-link planar arm | 2 | $T^2$ | 2 | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |
| 6-DOF arm | 6 | $T^6$ (topologically) | 6 | All 6 joints |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Workspace to C-space

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual transformation pipeline when a robot is represented in configuration space.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Robot in Workspace W] --> B[Identify DOF of Robot]
    B --> C[Choose Minimal Parameterization q]
    C --> D[Identify Obstacle Set O in W]
    D --> E[Compute C-obstacle via Minkowski Sum]
    E --> F[Construct C-space Manifold C]
    F --> G[Compute C-free = C minus C-obs]
    G --> H[Plan Path in C-free as Point Motion]
    H --> I[Map Solution Back to Workspace]
    I --> J[Execute Robot Motion in W]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7a3e9d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **blue node (A)** represents the starting physical problem.
- The **purple node (F)** highlights the C-space manifold — the central abstraction of this topic.
- The **green node (G)** shows the partition of C-space into valid (collision-free) regions.
- The **red node (H)** emphasizes that planning is now done by moving a single point.
- The **orange node (J)** is the final action of moving the real robot.

## 4.2 Schematic: C-space for a Circular Robot with a Point Obstacle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W[Workspace W = R squared]
        R[Robot: Disk radius r] -->|translate| R2[Robot: Disk radius r]
        O[Point Obstacle]
    end

    subgraph C[Configuration Space C = R squared]
        P[Robot represented as Point]
        CObs[C-obstacle: Disk radius r centered at obstacle]
    end

    R -.moves in W.-> R2
    R2 -->|abstract: shrink robot to point| P
    O -->|abstract: grow obstacle by r| CObs
    P -.navigates around.-> CObs

    style R fill:#1976d2,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#388e3c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style CObs fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style W fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- On the **left (blue region)**, we see the physical workspace with a real circular robot and a point obstacle.
- On the **right (green region)**, the robot has been reduced to a point, and the obstacle has been inflated to a disk of radius $r$.
- The path planning problem in the left becomes a point-avoidance problem in the right — which is computationally much easier.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: C-space of a 2D Rotating Robot (Ladder Problem)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Configuration: q1 = x, y, theta]
        X1[X position]
        Y1[Y position]
        TH1[Theta orientation, 0 to 2pi]
    end

    X1 --> M[Manifold: R squared times S1]
    Y1 --> M
    TH1 --> M

    M --> CE[C-obstacles computed via swept volume]
    CE --> CF[C-free region in 3D space]

    style M fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style CF fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style CE fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!TIP]
> **The classic "Ladder in a Hallway" Problem:** A ladder of length $L$ must be carried through an L-shaped hallway. The C-space is a rectangle $(x, y) \in [0, L]^2$ in the coordinates representing the distances to the two walls, and a point is a C-obstacle exactly when the ladder would intersect a wall. This is a textbook KTU-style problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Configuration Space and Configuration. Why is it useful to represent a robot in configuration space rather than workspace?**

**Model Answer:**

A **configuration** $\mathbf{q}$ of a robot is a specification of the position of every point on the robot, expressed as a minimal set of parameters. The **Configuration Space (C-space)** $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain; it is a differentiable manifold of dimension equal to the number of degrees of freedom of the robot.

Representing the robot in C-space is advantageous because:

1. **Abstraction:** The robot is reduced to a single point in $\mathcal{C}$, eliminating the need to track every point on the robot.
2. **Uniformity:** Path planning becomes a standard point-navigation problem in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
3. **Generality:** The same planning algorithms (e.g., A*, RRT, PRM) work regardless of the robot's geometric complexity.
4. **Obstacle inflation:** Static obstacles can be pre-expanded into C-obstacles, decoupling geometry from planning. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A circular mobile robot of radius 0.3 m operates in a 2D environment. Determine its configuration space, the dimension of the C-space, and the topology of $\mathcal{C}$.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Robot type:** Circular, translating only (orientation does not affect collision because it is a circle).
- **DOF:** 2 (translation in $x$ and $y$).
- **Configuration vector:** $\mathbf{q} = (x, y)$.
- **C-space:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.
- **Dimension:** $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 2$.
- **Topology:** $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a flat 2D Euclidean plane (non-compact, simply connected).
- **C-obstacle for a point obstacle at $(x_o, y_o)$:** Disk of radius **0.3 m** centered at $(x_o, y_o)$. **[3 Marks]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Define the terms *Degrees of Freedom*, *Configuration*, *C-obstacle*, and *C-free space*. Explain the Minkowski sum formulation for computing C-obstacles for a translating polygonal robot in 2D. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A square robot of side $0.5$ m moves by translation in a 2D workspace containing a single circular obstacle of radius $1.0$ m centered at the origin. Compute the C-space representation, the C-obstacle, and the dimension of the C-space. Sketch the C-free region qualitatively. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent parameters required to uniquely specify the configuration of a mechanical system.

**Configuration $\mathbf{q}$:** A specific instantiation of these parameters, fully describing the robot's pose.

**C-obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$:** The set of all configurations that result in a collision between the robot and any workspace obstacle.

**C-free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$:** The complement of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ within $\mathcal{C}$, i.e., all collision-free configurations.

**Minkowski sum formulation:** For a translating polygonal robot $\mathcal{R}$ and an obstacle $\mathcal{O}$:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$$

where $-\mathcal{R} = \{-\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{r} \in \mathcal{R}\}$, the reflection of the robot through the origin. Geometrically, this means sweeping the reflected robot shape around the obstacle, which is equivalent to "inflating" the obstacle by the size of the robot. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify robot type and DOF.**
A square is a translating polygonal robot. It does not rotate, so the only DOF are translations in $x$ and $y$. Total **DOF = 2**.

**Step 2 — State the C-space.**

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2, \quad \mathbf{q} = (x, y)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the C-obstacle.**
For a translating robot, we grow the obstacle by the robot's effective radius. A square of side $0.5$ m has an "effective radius" (the half-diagonal) of:

$$r_{\text{eff}} = \frac{0.5 \sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \approx 0.354 \text{ m}$$

The C-obstacle is a circular region of radius:

$$R = 1.0 + 0.354 = 1.354 \text{ m}$$

centered at the origin:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ (x, y) \mid x^2 + y^2 \leq 1.354^2 \}$$

**Step 4 — Express the C-free region.**

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ (x, y) \mid x^2 + y^2 > 1.354^2 \}$$

**Step 5 — Sketch the qualitative C-free region.**

The C-free region is the entire plane **minus a disk of radius 1.354 m centered at the origin**. Equivalently, the robot's center must stay at least 1.354 m away from the origin in any direction.

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
Dimension of C-space is **2**, the topology is the **Euclidean plane** with a circular hole. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Option 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** What is a *planar rigid body*? Derive its configuration space, the configuration vector, and the dimension of the C-space. Explain why the rotation angle is periodic. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A rectangular robot of dimensions $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ is allowed to translate and rotate in a 2D planar workspace. Determine: (i) the C-space manifold, (ii) the number of DOF, (iii) the dimension of C-space, and (iv) the C-obstacle for a single point obstacle of negligible size. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Planar rigid body definition:** A solid object that moves in a 2D plane (the $xy$-plane) and cannot deform. Its state is fully described by its position and orientation.

**Step 1 — Translation parameters.** Two parameters for the position of the body's reference point in the plane:

$$q_1 = x, \quad q_2 = y$$

**Step 2 — Rotation parameter.** One parameter for the orientation, the angle $\theta$ measured from the $x$-axis:

$$q_3 = \theta$$

**Step 3 — Configuration vector.**

$$\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Periodicity of $\theta$.** Rotation by $2\pi$ radians brings the body back to the same orientation. Thus:

$$\theta \sim \theta + 2\pi$$

The set of distinct angles is the circle $S^1$, parameterized by $[0, 2\pi)$. Therefore, the C-space manifold is:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

**Step 5 — Dimension of C-space.**

$$\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 2 + 1 = 3$$

The first two dimensions are linear (positions), while the third is cyclic (angle). **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) C-space manifold:**
Since the rectangular robot can both translate and rotate in 2D:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

**(ii) Number of DOF:**

$$\text{DOF} = 2 \text{ (translation)} + 1 \text{ (rotation)} = 3$$

**(iii) Dimension of C-space:**

$$\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 3$$

This is a 3-dimensional manifold, often visualized as a "thick cylinder" or "prism" in topology.

**(iv) C-obstacle for a point obstacle at $(x_o, y_o)$:**

For each orientation $\theta$, the rectangle occupies a different region of the plane. A collision occurs if the rectangle at orientation $\theta$ and center $(x, y)$ contains the obstacle point.

The C-obstacle is the union of all such "forbidden" configurations:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{\theta \in [0, 2\pi)} \left\{ (x, y, \theta) \,\Big|\, (x_o, y_o) \in \mathcal{R}(x, y, \theta) \right\}$$

where $\mathcal{R}(x, y, \theta)$ is the rectangle's footprint. Geometrically, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is a **3D swept volume** that looks like a thickened, twisted prism. In particular, slicing this volume at fixed $\theta$ gives a rectangle of dimensions $2 \times 1$ centered at $(x_o, y_o)$ in the $xy$-plane. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting orientation:** When the robot is a polygon (not a circle) that rotates, you MUST include $\theta$ in the C-space. Many students wrongly write $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ for a rectangular robot.
> 2. **Confusing workspace with C-space:** A rectangular obstacle in the workspace does NOT remain rectangular in C-space — it becomes a complex swept volume.
> 3. **Treating $\theta$ as unbounded:** Rotation angle is periodic, not linear. Don't write $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$.
> 4. **Wrong DOF for a drone:** A quadrotor has 6 DOF, not 3. Translational and rotational motions are independent.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Configuration $\mathbf{q}$** is a minimal parameterization of the robot's pose.
- **C-space $\mathcal{C}$** is the manifold of all configurations.
- **DOF** = $\dim(\mathcal{C})$ = number of independent parameters.
- **C-obstacle** = set of colliding configurations; **C-free** = collision-free subset.
- **Planar point** $\rightarrow \mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ (2 DOF).
- **Planar rigid body** $\rightarrow \mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (3 DOF).
- **Spatial rigid body** $\rightarrow \mathcal{C} = SE(3)$ (6 DOF).
- **Minkowski sum** for C-obstacle: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{R})$.
- **Rotation angles are periodic** (live on $S^1$ in 2D, $SO(3)$ in 3D).
- **Always** state: (1) DOF count, (2) manifold type, (3) C-obstacle shape.
- **Topological difference matters:** A line segment in 2D (the "ladder problem") lives in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ — its C-space is **3D**, not 2D.
- **Circular symmetry simplifies:** A circular robot has only 2 DOF regardless of rotation, because orientation is irrelevant to collision.
- **Industrial relevance:** Every modern motion planner (RRT, PRM, A*) operates in C-space, never directly in the raw workspace geometry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplification of C-space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simplification of C-space # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Configuration Space (C-space)? **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the mathematical space of all possible configurations a robot can attain. A *configuration* `q` is a complete specification of the robot's pose — every joint angle, wheel position, and link orientation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > For a rigid robot operating in the 2D plane, the C-space is defined as: > $$\mathcal{C} = \{(x, y, \theta) \mid x, y \in \mathbb{R},\ \theta \in [0, 2\pi)\}$$ > where $(x, y)$ is the position of the robot's reference point and $\theta$ is the orientation. For a **point robot**, the C-space collapses to $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$ with orientation). For a **disc-shaped (circular) robot**, it remains $\mathbb{R}^2$ because rotation is irrelevant. ## 1.2 C-Obstacle and Free Space The **C-obstacle** $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the set of all configurations in which the robot *collides* with an obstacle. The **Free Space** $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is the complement within the workspace boundaries: $$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$$ > [!NOTE] > Path planning in C-space reduces to finding a continuous path in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$. ## 1.3 Why "Simplify" C-space? A real field environment (forest, agricultural field, disaster zone) may have: - **Heterogeneous terrain** (grass, mud, rocks, slopes) - **Hundreds of static obstacles** (trees, boulders, posts) - **Dynamic elements** (animals, people, vehicles) A raw C-space is **continuous, high-dimensional, and geometrically complex**. Direct path planning on it is computationally infeasible for real-time field deployment. Hence we **simplify** — discretize, abstract, or topologically reduce the C-space into a tractable form. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy > [!TIP] > **Think of C-space as a "Balloon Animal World":** > Imagine you are a tiny mouse inside a giant maze shaped exactly like the robot. Every twist, turn, and joint becomes a dimension you can walk through. The walls of the maze represent obstacles. > Now, instead of letting the robot be a complex shape, you "inflate" the obstacles to swallow the robot's outline, then shrink the robot to a single point. The mouse now just needs a clear corridor through the inflated maze — that's the **simplified C-space**. ## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics | Term | Standard Notation | Meaning | |------|-------------------|---------| | Workspace dimension | $n$ | Number of physical space axes (2 or 3) | | Robot DOF | $d$ | Degrees of freedom = $\dim(\mathcal{C})$ | | C-obstacle | $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Collision configurations | | Free space | $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Valid configurations | | Grid resolution | $r$ | Cell size in discretized grid | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of C-space Simplification Simplification techniques are grouped into three strategic families: ### Pillar A — Geometric Reduction (Continuous → Continuous) The robot shape is *abstracted* by growing obstacles. Most popular: **Minkowski Sum Reduction**. - **Concept**: Grow every obstacle by the robot's shape, then treat the robot as a point. - **Why it works**: A disc robot of radius $R$ touching an obstacle boundary is equivalent to a point at distance $R$ from the grown obstacle. ### Pillar B — Discretization (Continuous → Discrete) The continuous C-space is *quantized* into countable cells or samples. - **Concept**: Approximate $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ using a finite graph, grid, or tree. - **Why it works**: Path existence on a finite graph is decidable; we can apply graph search (A*, Dijkstra). ### Pillar C — Topological Abstraction (Continuous → Topological Map) The C-space is replaced by a *one-dimensional roadmap* capturing its connectivity. - **Concept**: Build a network of safe 1-D curves (Voronoi, visibility edges). - **Why it works**: Path planning reduces to graph traversal in a sparse 1-D structure. ## 2.2 The Big Picture — Pipeline View ``` Raw Workspace (W) → C-space Construction → Simplification → Graph Search → Path (Minkowski Sum) (Grid/Roadmap) (A*/Dijkstra) ``` ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield formula set** examiners draw from.
