We will perform **Graph-Search DFS** with a closed list $\mathcal{C}$ and LIFO stack $\mathcal{F}$. The goal is to reach $G$. We will trace **every step**, showing the state of $\mathcal{F}$ (top of stack on the right) and $\mathcal{C}$.

| Step | Action Taken | Stack $\mathcal{F}$ (top → right) | Closed $\mathcal{C}$ | Comment |
|------|--------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 0 | Initialize | $[S]$ | $\{S\}$ | Push start node |
| 1 | Pop $S$, expand; push $A$, then $D$ | $[A, D]$ | $\{S, A, D\}$ | Successors of $S$: A, D |
| 2 | Pop $D$ (LIFO — last-in first-out) | $[A]$ | $\{S, A, D\}$ | Test $D$ — not goal |
| 3 | Expand $D$; push $E$, $F$ | $[A, E, F]$ | $\{S, A, D, E, F\}$ | Successors: E, F |
| 4 | Pop $F$ | $[A, E]$ | $\{S, A, D, E, F\}$ | Test $F$ — not goal |
| 5 | Expand $F$; push $G$ | $[A, E, G]$ | $\{S, A, D, E, F, G\}$ | Successor of $F$ is $G$ |
| 6 | Pop $G$ — **GOAL TEST PASSES** | — | — | **Return SUCCESS** |

**Path Reconstruction (using parent pointers):**
- $G$ was pushed from $F$, so `parent[G] = F`.
- `parent[F] = D`.
- `parent[D] = S`.

Therefore, the solution path is $S \to D \to F \to G$ with depth $d = 3$ and path cost $= 3$.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that DFS **did not explore the $A \to B \to C \to G$ branch** because $G$ was reached first via the $D$ branch. This illustrates DFS's **non-optimality**: in a different edge-weighting, the $A$-branch path could have been shorter, but DFS would still prefer the $D$-branch.

### 3.2 Detailed Algorithmic Specification (Pseudocode — Not Python)

Below is the **graph-search DFS** in a language-neutral pseudocode suitable for a robotics exam. **This is NOT a programming language**; it is an algorithmic specification as required by PCRAT601.

```
Algorithm: Graph-Search DFS for Discrete State Space
---------------------------------------------------------------
Input : Initial state s0, Goal test G(s),
        Successor function f(s), Depth bound L (optional)
Output: SUCCESS with path, or FAILURE
Globals: Stack F, Set C, Map parent

1.  F ← empty stack
2.  C ← empty set
3.  parent[s0] ← NULL
4.  PUSH(F, s0)
5.  ADD(C, s0)
6.  WHILE F is not empty DO
7.      s ← POP(F)                         // LIFO: take top
8.      IF G(s) THEN
9.          RETURN RECONSTRUCT_PATH(parent, s)
10.     ENDIF
11.     FOR each s' in f(s) DO
12.         IF s' ∉ C THEN
13.             parent[s'] ← s
14.             ADD(C, s')
15.             PUSH(F, s')
16.         ENDIF
17.     ENDFOR
18. ENDWHILE
19. RETURN FAILURE
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Stack vs. Queue Confusion:** A common mark-loss error is writing `ENQUEUE` / `DEQUEUE` (BFS) instead of `PUSH` / `POP` (DFS). The order of expansion is **directly** determined by this data-structure choice. Always write "**LIFO stack**" explicitly in your answer.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Time Complexity Bound

We derive $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ formally for completeness.

Let $N(d)$ be the number of nodes generated (not just expanded) in the worst case. The search tree rooted at $s_0$ has the root at depth 0, with up to $b$ children at depth 1, up to $b^{2}$ at depth 2, and so on, until the goal at depth $d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(d) &\;=\; b^{0} + b^{1} + b^{2} + \ldots + b^{d} \\[4pt]
     &\;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} \\[4pt]
     &\;=\; \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{(geometric series identity)} \\[4pt]
     &\;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(b^{d}\right) \quad \text{since } b \geq 2.
\end{aligned}
$$

Each node generation and goal test is $\mathcal{O}(1)$, hence the **time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$**.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Space Complexity Bound

DFS stores:

- The **closed set** $\mathcal{C}$: up to all visited nodes $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ in the worst case.
- The **frontier stack** $\mathcal{F}$: at any point, contains the current path of length at most $d$, plus at most $b-1$ unexpanded siblings per level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{DFS}} &\;=\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{current path}} \;+\; \underbrace{(b-1) \cdot d}_{\text{siblings at each level}} \\[4pt]
               &\;=\; \mathcal{O}(b \cdot d).
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with BFS:

$$
S_{\text{BFS}} \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(b^{d}\right).
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Robotics Implication:** For a 4-connected grid ($b = 4$) covering a $100 \times 100$ field ($d \approx 200$), DFS requires $\approx 4 \cdot 200 = 800$ stack entries, while BFS requires $\approx 4^{200}$ — astronomically more. Hence DFS is **the only feasible choice** for large-scale field exploration.

### 3.5 IDDFS Total Node Count Derivation

Iterative Deepening DFS runs DLDDFS with limits $L = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, d$. Total nodes expanded:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{IDDFS}} &\;=\; (d+1) \cdot b^{0} \;+\; d \cdot b^{1} \;+\; (d-1) \cdot b^{2} \;+\; \ldots \;+\; 1 \cdot b^{d} \\[4pt]
                 &\;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{d} (d - i + 1) \cdot b^{i} \\[4pt]
                 &\;\leq\; (d+1) \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} \\[4pt]
                 &\;\leq\; \frac{(d+1)(b^{d+1} - 1)}{b - 1} \\[4pt]
                 &\;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(b^{d}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

The overhead over plain DFS is only a constant factor — yet IDDFS gains **optimality** and **completeness in finite depth**, making it the **preferred variant** in robotics task planning.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | DFS (Graph) | BFS (Graph) | IDDFS | UCS (for reference) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| Frontier | Stack (LIFO) | Queue (FIFO) | Stack (with depth limit) | Priority Queue (cost) |
| Time | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{1 + \lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| Space | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{1 + \lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| Complete? | Yes (finite, no cycle) | Yes | Yes | Yes (if $\epsilon > 0$) |
| Optimal? | **No** | Yes (unit cost) | Yes (unit cost) | Yes |
| Expansion Order | Deepest first | Shallowest first | Level-by-level | Lowest $g(n)$ first |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DFS Algorithm Control Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input s0, G(s), f(s), L"] --> B["F = empty Stack"]
    B --> C["C = empty Set"]
    C --> D["parent = empty Map"]
    D --> E["Push s0 onto F"]
    E --> F["Add s0 to C"]
    F --> G{"F is empty?"}
    G -- Yes --> Z["Return FAILURE"]
    G -- No --> H["Pop top node s from F"]
    H --> I{"G s is true?"}
    I -- Yes --> Y["Return SUCCESS and path"]
    I -- No --> J["depth s exceeds L?"]
    J -- Yes --> G
    J -- No --> K["For each s' in f s"]
    K --> L{"s' in C?"}
    L -- Yes --> K
    L -- No --> M["parent s' = s"]
    M --> N["Add s' to C"]
    N --> O["Push s' onto F"]
    O --> K
    K -- "all successors processed" --> G
```

### 4.2 State-Space Search Tree Generated by DFS

The following diagram shows the **search tree** that DFS would build for the worked example in Section 3.1. Nodes are visited in the order $S, D, F, G$ before backtracking. Greyed-out branches show the *unused* $A \to B \to C$ subtree.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootS["S root depth 0"]
    nA["A depth 1 unexplored"]
    nD["D depth 1 expanded"]
    nB["B depth 2 unexplored"]
    nE["E depth 2 leaf dead end"]
    nF["F depth 2 expanded"]
    nC["C depth 3 unexplored"]
    nG["G depth 3 GOAL reached"]
    rootS --- nA
    rootS --- nD
    nA --- nB
    nD --- nE
    nD --- nF
    nB --- nC
    nF --- nG
    style rootS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style nA fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#888
    style nB fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#888
    style nC fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#888
    style nE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#888
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DFS in a Field-Robot Planner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Perception Module: LiDAR and Camera"] --> B["State Estimator: Grid Map Builder"]
    B --> C["Discrete State Space G = S, E"]
    C --> D["DFS Planner Core: Stack based"]
    D --> E{"Goal Reached?"}
    E -- "No" --> F["Motor Controller: Drive forward or turn"]
    F --> G["Wheel Odometry Feedback"]
    G --> A
    E -- "Yes" --> H["Task Completed: Return to dock"]
    D --> I["Closed List C: Memory of visited cells"]
    D --> J["Frontier Stack F: Current exploration path"]
```

### 4.4 Field-Robot Maze Exploration Sequence (Grid View)

A typical 4-connected grid maze exploration trace. The robot (R) starts at the top-left, the goal (G) is at the bottom-right. Numbers in cells show the **visit order** under DFS.

```mermaid

    c11["1 R"] --- c12["2"]
    c12 --- c13["3"]
    c13 --- c14["4"]
    c14 --- c15["5 dead"]
    c11 --- c21["6 backtrack"]
    c21 --- c22["7"]
    c22 --- c23["8"]
    c23 --- c24["9"]
    c24 --- c25["10"]
    c21 --- c31["11"]
    c31 --- c32["12"]
    c32 --- c33["13"]
    c33 --- c34["14"]
    c34 --- c35["15 G"]
    style c11 fill:#FFD700
    style c15 fill:#FFB6C1
    style c35 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for PCRAT601. The 3-mark questions test **Remember/Understand** levels (Bloom Levels 1 & 2), while the 14-mark questions target **Apply/Analyze/Evaluate** levels (Bloom Levels 3–5), in line with NEP 2020 outcome-based education guidelines.

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**
*Define Depth-First Search in the context of discrete state-space search. State its frontier data structure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Depth-First Search (DFS) is an uninformed graph-search algorithm that explores a discrete state space by expanding the deepest unexpanded node first, using a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack** as the frontier. It proceeds from the initial state $s_0$, generates successors, and descends into them immediately, backtracking only when a dead-end (empty successor list) is reached. DFS terminates upon finding a goal state, exhausting the frontier (failure), or hitting a depth bound. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for LIFO, 1 for termination condition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**
*State the time and space complexity of graph-search DFS. Is DFS optimal? Justify briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Time complexity: $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ where $b$ is the branching factor and $d$ the depth of the first goal found. Space complexity: $S(n) = \mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$. **DFS is NOT optimal** because it expands the deepest node first and returns the *first* goal encountered, which may lie at a greater depth than another goal in a shallower unexplored branch. *(3 marks: 1 for time, 1 for space, 1 for optimality justification.)*

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation: each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b). Below are two complete alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the DFS algorithm for graph search in a discrete state space. Clearly state the role of the **stack** and the **closed list**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Apply graph-search DFS to the following state-space graph, with $s_0 = S$ and $s_g = G$. Show the contents of the **frontier stack** and the **closed set** at every step. Determine the path found and its depth. (7 marks)

```
Adjacency (successors):
    S -> {A, B}
    A -> {C, D}
    B -> {E}
    C -> {G}
    D -> {F}
    E -> {G}
    F -> {}
```

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

DFS for graph search proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialize** a stack $\mathcal{F}$ (frontier) and a set $\mathcal{C}$ (closed/visited). Push the initial state $s_0$ onto $\mathcal{F}$ and add it to $\mathcal{C}$. The **stack** ensures LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) expansion order, which is the defining property of DFS. The **closed list** prevents re-expansion of already-visited nodes — essential for graphs with cycles, and gives DFS its $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ time bound.
2. **Loop:** While $\mathcal{F}$ is not empty, pop the top state $s$. If $G(s)$, return the path reconstructed via `parent[]` pointers.
3. **Expand:** For each successor $s' \in f(s)$ that is neither in $\mathcal{C}$ nor in $\mathcal{F}$, set `parent[s'] = s`, add $s'$ to $\mathcal{C}$, and **push** $s'$ onto $\mathcal{F}$.
4. **Failure:** If $\mathcal{F}$ becomes empty, return FAILURE.

The **stack** ensures deepest-first behavior, while the **closed list** ensures graph-search semantics (each node expanded at most once). The path is recovered by following `parent[]` pointers backward from the goal to $s_0$. *(Full 7 marks: 1 stack role, 1 closed-list role, 1 algorithm steps, 1 goal test, 1 backtracking, 1 path reconstruction, 1 termination.)*

**(b) Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Stack $\mathcal{F}$ (top → right) | Closed $\mathcal{C}$ | Notes |
|------|--------|-----------------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 0 | Init: push S | $[S]$ | $\{S\}$ | |
| 1 | Pop S, not goal. Expand: push B, then A (LIFO → A on top) | $[A, B]$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ | Successors of S: A, B |
| 2 | Pop A, not goal. Expand: push D, then C | $[B, C, D]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ | Successors of A: C, D |
| 3 | Pop D, not goal. Expand: push F | $[B, C, F]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, F\}$ | Successor of D: F |
| 4 | Pop F, not goal. No successors (F's list empty) | $[B, C]$ | unchanged | Backtrack |
| 5 | Pop C, not goal. Expand: push G | $[B, G]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, F, G\}$ | Successor of C: G |
| 6 | Pop G — **GOAL!** | — | — | **SUCCESS** |

**Path Reconstruction:**
- `parent[G] = C`
- `parent[C] = A`
- `parent[A] = S`

**Path found:** $S \to A \to C \to G$ with depth $d = 3$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to record `parent[]` pointers during expansion. Without them, you cannot reconstruct the path and will lose **2 marks** even if the goal is found. Always write `parent[s'] = s` for *every* push.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern — Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a)** Differentiate between Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) for state-space search. Use a comparison table covering **frontier type, expansion order, completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A field robot must navigate a 4-connected grid of size $4 \times 4$ (16 cells). The robot starts at cell $(1,1)$ and must reach cell $(4,4)$. Obstacles exist at $(2,2)$ and $(3,3)$. Apply DFS to find a path. Show the visit order and the closed set. Comment on the optimality of the path found. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DFS vs. BFS Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Property | DFS (Graph Search) | BFS (Graph Search) |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Frontier Data Structure | **Stack (LIFO)** | **Queue (FIFO)** |
| Expansion Order | Deepest unexpanded node first | Shallowest unexpanded node first |
| Complete? | Yes (finite, acyclic) | Yes |
| Optimal (unit cost)? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ |
| Use of Closed List | Optional (recommended) | Optional (recommended) |
| Best for | Deep, narrow state spaces | Shallow, wide state spaces |

The single most important difference is the **frontier data structure**: LIFO stack vs. FIFO queue. *(Full 7 marks: 1 mark per row + 1 mark for the closing statement.)*

**(b) 4×4 Grid DFS Trace (7 marks):**

Coordinate system: $(row, col)$, $1$-indexed, with moves Right, Down, Left, Up tried in that order (standard DFS successor order). Obstacles at $(2,2)$ and $(3,3)$ are impassable.

Visit order (DFS, trying Right→Down→Left→Up at each cell):

1. Start $(1,1)$.
2. Right to $(1,2)$.
3. Right to $(1,3)$.
4. Right to $(1,4)$ — corner; cannot go further right.
5. Down to $(2,4)$.
6. Down to $(3,4)$.
7. Down to $(4,4)$ — **GOAL!**

**Closed set at termination:** $\{(1,1), (1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,4), (3,4), (4,4)\}$.

**Path:** $(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$, length $= 6$ edges.

**Optimality Comment:** The **shortest** path in this grid is of length **6** (e.g., $(1,1) \to (2,1) \to (3,1) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4)$ is also length 6). So in this specific instance, DFS *happens* to find a shortest path. However, this is coincidental — in general, DFS is **not** optimal. Had the obstacles been rearranged so that the deepest DFS path is longer than the shallowest valid path, DFS would have returned a non-optimal solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Order of Successor Expansion:** The successor order (e.g., Right-Down-Left-Up vs. Down-Right-Up-Left) determines the *specific* path DFS returns. The examiner may specify an order; if not, **state your assumed order explicitly** at the start of your trace. Vague statements like "DFS finds *a* path" without the explicit trace lose **2–3 marks**.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DFS = LIFO stack** traversal of a state-space graph; backtracks on dead-ends.
- [x] **Graph Search DFS** uses a **closed list** $\mathcal{C}$ to avoid revisits.
- [x] **Time complexity** $= \mathcal{O}(b^{d})$; **Space complexity** $= \mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$.
- [x] **Complete** for finite acyclic graphs; **NOT optimal** (returns first goal, not shallowest).
- [x] Best suited for **deep, narrow** state spaces (typical in field robotics).
- [x] **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** combines DFS's space efficiency with BFS's optimality and is often the practical choice.
- [x] **Always record `parent[]` pointers** during expansion — required to reconstruct the solution path.
- [x] **Successor order matters** — it determines the specific path returned; always declare the order.
- [x] For a 4-connected grid, $b = 4$; for an 8-connected (octile) grid, $b = 8$.
- [x] The closed list $\mathcal{C}$ and frontier $\mathcal{F}$ are conceptually distinct: $\mathcal{C}$ contains *expanded or seen* nodes; $\mathcal{F}$ contains *unexpanded* nodes on the current frontier.
- [x] In **field robotics** applications, DFS is the workhorse for: row-coverage path planning, GPS-denied exploration, symbolic task planning, and as a subroutine inside RRT-style sampling planners.
- [x] Memorize the **geometric-series identity** for $N(d) = (b^{d+1}-1)/(b-1)$ — it frequently appears in 14-mark derivations.
- [x] Distinguish **Tree-Search DFS** (no closed list, may revisit) from **Graph-Search DFS** (closed list, each node expanded once) — KTU 2024 expects the graph-search version.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breadth-first Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breadth-first Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is an uninformed, graph-traversal search algorithm that systematically explores a state space by expanding all nodes at the present depth level before proceeding to nodes at the next depth level. Formally, given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and a distinguished start node $s \in V$, BFS explores the vertices in *non-decreasing order of their distance (number of edges) from $s$*. The algorithm uses a **FIFO (First-In, First-Out) queue** to maintain the frontier (the boundary between explored and unexplored states), guaranteeing that the first time a node is dequeued, it is reached via the shortest possible path in an unweighted graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of **PCRAT601 – Field Robotics**, BFS is a foundational search primitive used in **occupancy-grid exploration, frontier-based mapping, and short-range path planning** for mobile robots operating in known 2D environments. It is the conceptual parent of more advanced planners like Dijkstra and A*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripple in a Pond" Intuition

Imagine dropping a single stone into a perfectly calm pond. The ripples spread outward in **concentric, equal-radius circles**. The wave reaches every point at radius $1$ simultaneously, *then* every point at radius $2$, and so on.

- The **stone's impact point** = the *start node* $s$.
- The **first ring of water (radius = 1 edge)** = all nodes at depth $d = 1$.
- The **second ring (radius = 2 edges)** = all nodes at depth $d = 2$.
- The pond's surface = the entire *state space / graph*.

BFS works exactly like this: it never "jumps ahead" to a deep corner of the search space before thoroughly covering the shallow neighborhood. The first time BFS *discovers* a target, that path is provably the **shortest** path in an unweighted graph — because no shorter path could have existed, else the target would already have been reached in an earlier ring.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 Glossary)

| Term | Meaning in BFS Context |
|---|---|
| **Frontier** | The set of nodes discovered but not yet expanded (i.e., currently in the FIFO queue). |
| **Explored / Closed Set** | Nodes that have already been expanded (dequeued and processed). |
| **Visited / Discovered Set** | Nodes that have been enqueued at least once. |
| **Parent Pointer** | A back-pointer stored when a node is first discovered, used to reconstruct the path. |
| **Depth of a Node** | The number of edges on the shortest path from $s$ to that node. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU metric for unweighted shortest path:** BFS guarantees the optimal cost $C^* = d(s, g)$, where $d(s,g)$ denotes the unweighted graph distance (in edges) between the start $s$ and goal $g$. The **branching factor** $b$ and **solution depth** $d$ jointly determine the search effort.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The BFS procedure can be decomposed into the following **deterministic sequence**:

1. **Initialization**
   - Create an empty FIFO queue $\mathcal{Q}$ and an empty `visited` set.
   - Enqueue the start node $s$ into $\mathcal{Q}$ and add $s$ to `visited`.
   - Set $\text{parent}(s) \leftarrow \text{NULL}$ (or $-1$).

2. **Termination Check**
   - If $\mathcal{Q} = \emptyset$, terminate and return **FAILURE** (goal unreachable).
   - If the node dequeued equals the goal $g$, terminate and **reconstruct the path** by backtracking parent pointers from $g$ to $s$.

3. **Expansion Loop (the heart of BFS)**
   - Dequeue the *oldest* node $n$ from $\mathcal{Q}$ (FIFO discipline).
   - For every neighbor $m$ of $n$ in the adjacency list $\text{Adj}(n)$:
     * If $m \notin$ `visited`:
       - Add $m$ to `visited`.
       - Set $\text{parent}(m) \leftarrow n$.
       - Enqueue $m$ into $\mathcal{Q}$.

4. **Return to Step 2.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FIFO and not LIFO?** A LIFO (stack) discipline would produce a Depth-First Search (DFS), which dives deep before being complete. The FIFO queue is what *forces* BFS to exhaust all depth-$d$ nodes before any depth-$(d+1)$ node — this is the source of BFS's **optimality** on unweighted graphs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula / Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time Complexity** | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Each vertex and edge is processed at most once. |
| 2 | **Space Complexity** | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Worst case: every vertex sits in the queue simultaneously. |
| 3 | **Optimality Cost** | $C^* = d(s,g)$ | Shortest path in edge-count. |
| 4 | **Nodes Expanded (worst)** | $1 + b + b^2 + \cdots + b^d = O(b^d)$ | Geometric growth with branching factor $b$, depth $d$. |
| 5 | **Completeness** | Yes, if $b$ is finite | Guaranteed to find $g$ if reachable. |
| 6 | **Queue Size at depth $d$** | $O(b^d)$ | Dominant memory term for wide graphs. |
| 7 | **Goal Test Cost** | $O(1)$ per node (dequeue) | Vs. $O(b^d)$ if tested at enqueue (no early termination). |
| 8 | **Depth of a discovered node** | $d(n) = d(\text{parent}(n)) + 1$ | Inductive invariant maintained throughout the run. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU SCRIBES:** Whenever you write a cardinality like $\vert V \vert$ or an absolute value, **always use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid`** in your answer scripts. Writing `|V|` inside a markdown table cell can break the table parser in many IDEs used by the university evaluation portal.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Field Robotics

- **Time $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$** tells the roboticist how many grid cells the onboard processor must touch to plan a path — crucial for real-time replanning on embedded SoCs (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi).
- **Space $O(\vert V \vert)$** bounds the RAM footprint. A 1 km × 1 km grid at 10 cm resolution is $10^8$ cells — each storing a `visited` flag and a parent pointer, so memory planning is *mission-critical*.
- **Optimality** justifies using BFS in safety-critical missions (e.g., planetary rover reaching a science target with the fewest wheel rotations, conserving energy).
- **BFS's optimality fails the moment edges have weights** — that is the *exact* reason Dijkstra's algorithm and A* are introduced next in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Time Complexity Bound

We prove $T_{\text{BFS}} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ rigorously.

**Step 1 — Account for vertex operations.**

Each vertex $v \in V$ is enqueued **at most once** (the `visited` check is the gatekeeper). Enqueue and dequeue are $O(1)$ operations on a standard queue. Therefore, the *aggregate* cost of all queue operations is at most $2 \cdot \vert V \vert$.

$$T_{\text{queue}} = 2 \cdot \vert V \vert = O(\vert V \vert)$$

**Step 2 — Account for edge operations.**

When a vertex $v$ is dequeued, the algorithm scans its adjacency list $\text{Adj}(v)$. The total number of adjacency-list entries scanned across the entire run equals the total number of edges (for an undirected graph, this counts each edge twice; for a directed graph, it counts each edge once). The constant factor does not affect the asymptotic class.

$$T_{\text{edges}} = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert \;\;\text{(undirected case)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine using the sum rule of Big-O.**

Because the algorithm performs these operations **sequentially** (no parallelism assumed in the single-robot planning case), the total time is the sum:

$$T_{\text{BFS}} = T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{edges}} = 2 \cdot \vert V \vert + 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$$

$$T_{\text{BFS}} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic for Step 3:** The sum rule $O(f(n)) + O(g(n)) = O(f(n) + g(n))$ applies when $f$ and $g$ are *sequential* subroutines. We keep the $+$-form because in adversarial inputs one term may dominate (e.g., a dense graph with $\vert E \vert \gg \vert V \vert$).

## 3.2 Worked Example — BFS on a Field-Robot Grid

**Problem Setup.** Consider a 4-connected occupancy grid for a differential-drive robot. Free cells (`.`) and obstacle cells (`#`) are shown. The robot starts at $S$ and must reach $G$ using BFS, where each move (N, S, E, W) costs 1.

```
+---+---+---+---+---+
| S | . | # | . | G |
+---+---+---+---+---+
| . | # | . | . | . |
+---+---+---+---+---+
| . | . | . | # | . |
+---+---+---+---+---+
| # | . | . | . | . |
+---+---+---+---+---+
```

Let $S = (0,0)$ and $G = (0,4)$. Obstacles at $(0,2)$ and $(1,1)$ and $(2,3)$.

**Iteration-by-iteration expansion:**

| Iteration | Queue (front → back) | Visited Set | Dequeued | Action / Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | `[(0,0)]` | `{(0,0)}` | — | Enqueue start. |
| 1 | `[]` | `{(0,0)}` | `(0,0)` | Expand: enqueue `(1,0)`. |
| 2 | `[(1,0)]` | `{(0,0),(1,0)}` | `(1,0)` | Expand: enqueue `(2,0)`, `(1,1)` is obstacle. |
| 3 | `[(2,0)]` | `+ (2,0)` | `(2,0)` | Expand: enqueue `(3,0)`, `(2,1)`. |
| 4 | `[(3,0),(2,1)]` | `+ (3,0),(2,1)` | `(3,0)` | Expand: enqueue `(3,1)`. |
| 5 | `[(2,1),(3,1)]` | — | `(2,1)` | Expand: enqueue `(2,2)`, `(1,1)` blocked. |
| 6 | `[(3,1),(2,2)]` | `+ (2,2)` | `(3,1)` | Expand: enqueue `(3,2)`. |
| 7 | `[(2,2),(3,2)]` | `+ (3,2)` | `(2,2)` | Expand: enqueue `(1,2)`, `(2,3)` blocked. |
| 8 | `[(3,2),(1,2)]` | `+ (1,2)` | `(3,2)` | Expand: enqueue `(3,3)`. |
| 9 | `[(1,2),(3,3)]` | `+ (3,3)` | `(1,2)` | Expand: enqueue `(0,2)` blocked, `(1,3)`. |
| 10 | `[(3,3),(1,3)]` | `+ (1,3)` | `(3,3)` | Expand: enqueue `(3,4)`. |
| 11 | `[(1,3),(3,4)]` | `+ (3,4)` | `(1,3)` | Expand: enqueue `(1,4)`, `(0,3)`. |
| 12 | `[(3,4),(1,4),(0,3)]` | `+ (1,4),(0,3)` | `(3,4)` | Expand: enqueue `(2,4)`. |
| 13 | `[(1,4),(0,3),(2,4)]` | — | `(1,4)` | Expand: enqueue `(0,4) = G`. **GOAL DISCOVERED.** |
| 14 | `[(0,3),(2,4),(0,4)]` | `+ (0,4)` | `(0,3)` | Continue to confirm shortest. |
| 15 | `[(2,4),(0,4)]` | — | `(2,4)` | (Already discovered.) |
| 16 | `[(0,4)]` | — | `(0,4) = G` | **GOAL REACHED.** Path length = 7. |

**Optimal Path Reconstruction (via parent pointers):**

$$\text{Path: } (0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (2,1) \to (2,2) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (0,4) = G$$

The path length is $\vert P \vert = 7$ edges — confirmed as the shortest in this grid by exhaustion of all depth-$\leq 7$ nodes.

## 3.3 Mathematical Optimality Sketch

**Claim:** *If BFS discovers goal $g$ at depth $d$, then $d(s,g) = d$ (the minimum possible edge-count).*

**Proof outline (by contradiction):**

1. Suppose there exists an alternative path $P'$ from $s$ to $g$ with $\vert P' \vert < d$.
2. Consider the prefix of $P'$ of length $\vert P' \vert - 1$ — it ends at some predecessor $u$ of $g$ with $d(s,u) \le \vert P' \vert - 1 < d$.
3. Since BFS processes nodes in non-decreasing depth order, BFS must dequeue $u$ *before* any node at depth $d$ (including $g$).
4. When $u$ is dequeued, $g \in \text{Adj}(u)$ and is undiscovered (else the path would have been shorter), so $g$ is enqueued at depth $\le \vert P' \vert < d$.
5. This contradicts the assumption that BFS first reaches $g$ at depth $d$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — BFS Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start BFS]) --> nodeB[Initialize: visited empty, queue Q contains start s]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Q is empty?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD([Return FAILURE])
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Dequeue node n from Q]
    nodeE --> nodeF{n equals goal g?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Reconstruct path via parent pointers]
    nodeG --> nodeH([Return SUCCESS and path P])
    nodeF -- No --> nodeI[For each neighbor m of n]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{m in visited?}
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeK[Mark m visited]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Set parent of m to n]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Enqueue m into Q]
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeI -- All neighbors done --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — BFS Tree Expansion on a Sample Graph

The graph below is a typical lecture example. Node `S` is the start, node `G` is the goal. BFS explores outward in **shells** (rings of equal depth). Numeric labels on nodes show the **order of dequeue**, which matches depth-first within each shell.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S)) --- nodeA((A))
    nodeS --- nodeB((B))
    nodeA --- nodeC((C))
    nodeA --- nodeD((D))
    nodeB --- nodeE((E))
    nodeB --- nodeF((F))
    nodeD --- nodeG((G))
    nodeE --- nodeH((H))
```

**BFS expansion shells (visualized as concentric rings):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEPTH0["DEPTH 0"]
        nodeS2((S - 1))
    end
    subgraph DEPTH1["DEPTH 1"]
        nodeA2((A - 2))
        nodeB2((B - 3))
    end
    subgraph DEPTH2["DEPTH 2"]
        nodeC2((C - 4))
        nodeD2((D - 5))
        nodeE2((E - 6))
        nodeF2((F - 7))
    end
    subgraph DEPTH3["DEPTH 3"]
        nodeG2((G - 8 GOAL))
        nodeH2((H - 9))
    end
    nodeS2 --> nodeA2
    nodeS2 --> nodeB2
    nodeA2 --> nodeC2
    nodeA2 --> nodeD2
    nodeB2 --> nodeE2
    nodeB2 --> nodeF2
    nodeD2 --> nodeG2
    nodeE2 --> nodeH2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that BFS dequeues *all* depth-1 nodes (A, B) before *any* depth-2 node (C, D, E, F), and *all* depth-2 nodes before *any* depth-3 node. This concentric-ring pattern is the visual signature of BFS.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — BFS in a Field-Robot Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        blockA[Occupancy Grid Map]
    end
    subgraph PLANNING["PLANNING LAYER"]
        blockB[Goal Pose Input]
        blockC[BFS Planner]
        blockD[Path Output]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        blockE[Trajectory Follower]
        blockF[Motor Drivers]
    end
    blockA --> blockC
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockA
```

**Architecture narrative:** The robot's SLAM/odometry pipeline feeds an **occupancy grid** into the BFS planner. The planner receives a **goal pose** (e.g., from a high-level behavior tree) and runs BFS on the grid to emit a **waypoint path**. The path is fed to a low-level trajectory follower that issues wheel-velocity commands. Feedback from the motors re-observes the environment, closing the loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`
> **Define Breadth-First Search. State its time and space complexity in terms of the number of vertices and edges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BFS is a graph-traversal algorithm that explores all nodes at the current depth before moving to nodes at the next depth, using a FIFO queue.
- **Time complexity:** $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$
- **Space complexity:** $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$
where $\vert V \vert$ is the number of vertices and $\vert E \vert$ is the number of edges in the graph.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for time, 1 mark for space.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
> **Why does BFS use a FIFO queue and not a LIFO stack? Justify in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A FIFO queue enforces *level-order* traversal — it dequeues the oldest enqueued node first, guaranteeing that BFS exhausts all nodes at depth $d$ before expanding any node at depth $d+1$. A LIFO stack (used by DFS) would dive deep first, breaking BFS's optimality guarantee on unweighted shortest-path problems.

*(2 marks for FIFO justification, 1 mark for implication on optimality.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the Breadth-First Search algorithm with the help of a flowchart. Mention the data structure used and justify its choice. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot must navigate a 4-connected grid from $S$ at $(0,0)$ to $G$ at $(2,2)$. Obstacles occupy $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$. Apply BFS, show the queue evolution, and report the shortest path. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

- **Algorithm description (3 marks):** Initialization with start node; loop of dequeue → expand neighbors → enqueue undiscovered neighbors; terminate on goal or empty queue.
- **Data structure (2 marks):** FIFO queue $\mathcal{Q}$ for the frontier; a `visited` set to prevent revisits; a `parent` map for path reconstruction.
- **Flowchart (2 marks):** Use the standard BFS flowchart (see Section 4.1); each box should be clearly labelled with operations and decision diamonds for queue-empty and goal-test.

**Justification of FIFO:** The FIFO property is the only queue discipline that preserves non-decreasing-depth expansion, which is the *sufficient condition* for shortest-path optimality on unweighted graphs.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Grid representation:**

```
+---+---+---+
| S | # | G |
+---+---+---+
| # | . | . |
+---+---+---+
| . | . | . |
+---+---+---+
```

**BFS execution table:**

| Step | Queue (front → back) | Dequeued | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `[S(0,0)]` | — | Init. |
| 1 | `[]` | `S(0,0)` | Expand S. |
| 2 | `[(1,0)? no, blocked]` | — | Only `(0,0)`→blocked directions. Enqueue `(0,0)→(0,0)→` no. So enqueue `(1,0)` blocked skip, `(0,1)` blocked skip. Enqueue nothing new? No, S has neighbors `(1,0)` blocked, `(0,1)` blocked. Queue empty after dequeue, so S is a dead end locally — but BFS must continue: the queue is empty **only after expanding S**, but we still have S in the visited set. **Correction**: S still has neighbors — but in a 4-connected grid, S at (0,0) has only right (1,0) and down (0,1), both blocked. So the queue is empty, BFS returns FAILURE? |

**Correction — grid restated for solvability:**

```
+---+---+---+
| S | . | G |
+---+---+---+
| # | # | . |
+---+---+---+
| . | . | . |
+---+---+---+
```

**BFS execution table (corrected):**

| Step | Queue (front → back) | Dequeued | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `[(0,0)]` | — | Init. |
| 1 | `[]` | `(0,0)` | Expand: enqueue `(0,1)`, `(1,0)` blocked. |
| 2 | `[(0,1)]` | `(0,1)` | Expand: enqueue `(0,2) = G`. **GOAL.** |
| 3 | `[(0,2)]` | `(0,2) = G` | Terminate. |

**Shortest path:** $S(0,0) \to (0,1) \to G(0,2)$, length $= 2$ edges. ✓

---

> **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Compare Breadth-First Search and Depth-First Search under the headings: data structure, completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **any three** real-world applications of BFS in the context of field robotics, with one-line justifications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

| Property | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| **Data structure** | FIFO queue | LIFO stack |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite branching) | Yes for finite graphs; fails on infinite / cyclic without visited-set |
| **Optimality (unweighted)** | Yes — returns shortest path | No — returns *a* path, not necessarily shortest |
| **Time complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Space complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ — queue can hold all frontier | $O(\vert V \vert)$ worst case, $O(b \cdot d)$ typical — only current path in stack |

*(1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall comparison narrative.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

1. **Frontier-Based Exploration (2 marks):** A field robot exploring an unknown environment uses BFS-like wavefront expansion over the known occupancy grid to identify and prioritize nearest unexplored *frontier* cells for the next sensing mission — guarantees the robot always attacks the closest unknown territory, minimizing traversal.

2. **Shortest-Path Planning on a Known Grid (2 marks):** In a pre-mapped warehouse, an AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) uses BFS to compute the minimum-turn or minimum-step path to the next pick-station — optimality is critical for throughput and battery conservation.

3. **Connected-Component Analysis of Free Space (2 marks):** BFS is used to *flood-fill* the largest connected free-space region of an occupancy map, allowing the robot to detect whether a doorway / passage is actually traversable before committing to motion — a safety pre-check.

4. *(Bonus, 1 mark)* **Garbage Collection in Sweeping Robots:** BFS-style coverage ensures every cell of the floor plan is visited at least once, achieving complete area coverage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing BFS with Dijkstra:** BFS assumes *unit edge cost*. If the question has weighted edges, you **must** use Dijkstra's algorithm (or A*). Writing "BFS gives shortest path" in a weighted context will fetch **0 marks** for the optimality claim.
> 2. **Forgetting the `visited` check:** If you skip the visited test, BFS can cycle infinitely on graphs with cycles. Examiners specifically check for the visited-set in pseudocode.
> 3. **Confusing queue size with space complexity:** The maximum queue size is $O(b^d)$, but the *total* auxiliary space is $O(\vert V \vert)$ (visited set + parent map + queue combined).
> 4. **Goal test placement:** Testing the goal at *enqueue* time vs *dequeue* time does not change correctness on unweighted graphs but does change the discovered depth by at most 1. State which you used in the exam.
> 5. **Direction convention mismatch:** When BFS is run on a grid, the resulting shortest path may differ by tie-breaking (N-before-S vs S-before-N). Examiners accept any valid shortest path, but penalize paths longer than the optimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BFS = FIFO-queue, level-order, unweighted shortest path.** This sentence alone answers 60% of KTU questions.
- **Time $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, Space $O(\vert V \vert)$.** Memorize and reproduce without confusion.
- **Completeness:** Yes (finite branching). **Optimality:** Yes, **only** when all edge costs are equal (or treated as 1).
- **BFS is the unweighted special case of Dijkstra's algorithm**, and a special case of A* with heuristic $h(n) = 0$.
- **In field robotics, BFS is the workhorse for grid-based shortest path, frontier exploration, and connected-region labelling.** It is *not* used for high-speed replanning in large continuous spaces — there, A* or RRT* are preferred.
- **Always use `\vert V \vert` (not `|V|`) in markdown tables** to avoid breaking the renderer — this is a formatting rule that has cost students actual presentation marks in online KTU submissions.
- **State-machine:** `Init → Dequeue → Goal? → Expand → Mark visited → Enqueue` — reproduce this loop verbatim whenever pseudocode is asked.
- **Parent-pointer reconstruction** is the standard path-retrieval method; reverse the chain at the end.
- **Tie-breaking on grids** (which neighbor is expanded first) affects the *exact* path but not the *length* — both are valid BFS outputs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dijkstra's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a classical, single-source **shortest-path graph search algorithm** developed by Edsger W. Dijkstra (1956). It computes the minimum cumulative cost from a designated *start node* to every other node in a **weighted graph** having non-negative edge weights. In the KTU 2024 *Field Robotics* syllabus (Module 3 — Path Planning), it is positioned as the foundational deterministic graph-traversal method used to plan a globally optimal route for a field robot traversing a discretized environment (grid map, topological road map, or cost-weighted terrain graph).

Formally, given a graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative weight function $w: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, and a source node $s \in V$, the algorithm finds the distance function $d: V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ such that:

$$
\forall v \in V, \quad d(v) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s \to v}} \sum_{e \in P} w(e)
$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s \to v}$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $v$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dijkstra's algorithm is the *guaranteed-optimal* path planner for field robots when the environment is **fully known**, **static**, and represented as a weighted graph. It serves as the *baseline* against which more advanced planners (A*, D*, RRT*) are benchmarked in Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Flow on a Hilly Map"

Imagine you are standing at the *entrance* of a hilly terrain map and you pour a unit of water on your feet. The water flows outward through valleys and ridges. If every ridge/valley has a known "height" or "cost" to cross, then the water will eventually settle into the lowest-cost path to *every other point* on the map. The point on the map that gets wetted first is the *closest*, the second-wetted is the *second-closest*, and so on. **Dijkstra's algorithm is the discrete, mathematical version of this water-flooding intuition.** The robot = the water droplet; the waypoint graph = the terrain; the edge weights = the cost of traversing each patch of land.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Field Robotics

A field robot (agricultural rover, autonomous tractor, search-and-rescue UGV) must navigate from its current pose to a goal pose. The world is discretized into a **graph of waypoints** (cells of an occupancy grid, intersections of a road network, or nodes of a topological map). Each edge is assigned a cost (distance, energy consumption, slope penalty, or risk). Dijkstra's algorithm then:

- **Guarantees** the minimum-cost route — critical for fuel-limited agricultural missions.
- Operates on **static, known maps** typical of pre-surveyed orchards, mines, or campus environments.
- Provides a **complete** solution (if a path exists, it will be found).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant / Metric:** The algorithm requires **non-negative edge weights**. This is guaranteed in field robotics because traversable distances, time, and energy are inherently non-negative. However, *negative weights* (e.g., reward for recharging downhill) **break** Dijkstra's optimality and require the Bellman–Ford algorithm instead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

Dijkstra's algorithm maintains three sets/state vectors over the graph $G = (V, E, w)$:

1. **$d(v)$** — the current best-known distance from source $s$ to node $v$. Initialized as $d(s) = 0$ and $d(v) = \infty$ for $v \neq s$.
2. **$\pi(v)$** — the *predecessor* of $v$ on the current best path (for path reconstruction). Initialized as $\pi(v) = \text{NIL}$.
3. **$S$** — the *settled* (visited) set of nodes whose final shortest distance has been confirmed.

The procedural logic is broken into the following logical blocks:

- **Initialization Block**
  - Set $d(s) = 0$; $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$.
  - Insert all nodes into a min-priority queue $Q$ keyed by $d(\cdot)$.
  - $S = \emptyset$.

- **Main Iteration Block (Greedy Selection)**
  - At each iteration, extract from $Q$ the node $u$ with the **minimum** $d(u)$.
  - Add $u$ to $S$ (mark as *settled*).
  - Terminate when $Q$ is empty, or when the goal node is settled (early-exit optimization).

- **Relaxation Block (Edge Update)**
  - For every outgoing edge $(u, v) \in E$ leaving $u$:
    - Compute tentative distance: $\text{alt} = d(u) + w(u, v)$.
    - If $\text{alt} < d(v)$, then update $d(v) \leftarrow \text{alt}$ and $\pi(v) \leftarrow u$.
    - Re-insert $v$ into $Q$ with the new key (or decrease-key if supported).

- **Path Reconstruction Block**
  - The shortest path to any $v \in V$ is recovered by backtracking: $v \to \pi(v) \to \pi(\pi(v)) \to \cdots \to s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Greedy Selection Works (The Core 'Why'):**
> Because all edge weights are non-negative, once a node $u$ is extracted from $Q$ (i.e., has the smallest tentative distance among all unsettled nodes), no future path through *unsettled* nodes can ever produce a smaller distance to $u$. Any alternative path would have to traverse additional non-negative edges and therefore accumulate cost $\geq d(u)$. This is the **optimal substructure** and **triangle inequality** property that Dijkstra exploits.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Units (Field Robotics Context) | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|------------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Edge weight (cost) | $w(u,v) \geq 0$ | meters, seconds, Joules, or unitless risk | **Must be non-negative**; otherwise algorithm fails |
| 2 | Tentative distance | $\text{alt} = d(u) + w(u,v)$ | same as $w$ | Used in *relaxation* step |
| 3 | Relaxation rule | $d(v) \leftarrow \min\{d(v),\, d(u) + w(u,v)\}$ | same as $w$ | Applied only if $\text{alt} < d(v)$ |
| 4 | Predecessor update | $\pi(v) \leftarrow u$ | — | Done iff $d(v)$ is updated |
| 5 | Time complexity (array) | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | big-O | Best for dense graphs |
| 6 | Time complexity (binary heap) | $O(\bigl(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert\bigr) \log \vert V \vert)$ | big-O | Best for sparse graphs (typical field maps) |
| 7 | Time complexity (Fibonacci heap) | $O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | big-O | Asymptotically optimal |
| 8 | Space complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | memory | Stores graph + $d$ + $\pi$ + $Q$ |
| 9 | Completeness | Yes (finite graph) | — | Will find a path iff one exists |
| 10 | Optimality | Yes | — | Holds for $w(e) \geq 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the table above, the symbol $\vert V \vert$ denotes the *cardinality* (number of elements) of the vertex set $V$ — it is the absolute-value notation rendered via `\vert` to preserve the markdown table integrity.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Robotics:** Planning fuel-optimal coverage paths across a pre-mapped orchard where inter-row transit costs vary with slope and soil type.
- **Autonomous Mining Trucks:** Computing shortest-time routes on a known haul-road network where edges have measured travel-time weights.
- **Search-and-Rescue UGVs:** Pre-computing evacuation paths on a topological map of a building before deployment.
- **Warehouse / Logistics:** Foundational layer beneath the **A\*** algorithm (which adds a heuristic) used in modern AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) fleet dispatchers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — A Field-Robot Traversal Graph

Consider an autonomous field robot surveying a 5-waypoint farm zone. The weighted graph below represents the inter-waypoint traversal cost (in **minutes**, accounting for distance, slope, and crop-row alignment). The waypoints are labelled $A, B, C, D, E$ and $A$ is the robot's start node (the charging station). Our goal is to compute the shortest-time path from $A$ to every other waypoint, and specifically to determine the optimal route to waypoint $E$ (the survey target).

**Edge List $E$ with weights (in minutes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(A,B) &= 4 \\
w(A,C) &= 2 \\
w(B,C) &= 1 \\
w(B,D) &= 5 \\
w(B,E) &= 6 \\
w(C,D) &= 8 \\
w(C,E) &= 10 \\
w(D,E) &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.1 Initialization (Iteration 0)

$$
d^{(0)} = [d(A)=0,\; d(B)=\infty,\; d(C)=\infty,\; d(D)=\infty,\; d(E)=\infty]
$$

$$
\pi^{(0)} = [\pi(A)=\text{NIL},\; \pi(B)=\text{NIL},\; \pi(C)=\text{NIL},\; \pi(D)=\text{NIL},\; \pi(E)=\text{NIL}]
$$

$$
S = \emptyset, \quad Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
$$

### 3.1.2 Iteration 1 — Settle $A$ (smallest $d$ = 0)

Extract $u = A$. Mark $S \leftarrow S \cup \{A\}$. Relax all edges leaving $A$:

- Edge $(A, B)$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty = d(B)$
  - Update $d(B) \leftarrow 4$, $\pi(B) \leftarrow A$.
- Edge $(A, C)$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 2 = 2 < \infty = d(C)$
  - Update $d(C) \leftarrow 2$, $\pi(C) \leftarrow A$.

$$
d^{(1)} = [0, 4, 2, \infty, \infty], \quad \pi^{(1)} = [\text{NIL}, A, A, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}]
$$

### 3.1.3 Iteration 2 — Settle $C$ (smallest unsettled $d$ = 2)

Extract $u = C$. Mark $S \leftarrow S \cup \{C\}$. Relax edges leaving $C$:

- Edge $(C, B)$: $\text{alt} = 2 + 1 = 3 < 4 = d(B)$
  - Update $d(B) \leftarrow 3$, $\pi(B) \leftarrow C$. (This **replaces** the previous path $A \to B$.)
- Edge $(C, D)$: $\text{alt} = 2 + 8 = 10 < \infty = d(D)$
  - Update $d(D) \leftarrow 10$, $\pi(D) \leftarrow C$.
- Edge $(C, E)$: $\text{alt} = 2 + 10 = 12 < \infty = d(E)$
  - Update $d(E) \leftarrow 12$, $\pi(E) \leftarrow C$.

$$
d^{(2)} = [0, 3, 2, 10, 12], \quad \pi^{(2)} = [\text{NIL}, C, A, C, C]
$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration 3 — Settle $B$ (smallest unsettled $d$ = 3)

Extract $u = B$. Mark $S \leftarrow S \cup \{B\}$. Relax edges leaving $B$:

- Edge $(B, C)$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 1 = 4$, but $d(C) = 2$ is already settled — **no update** (C is in $S$).
- Edge $(B, D)$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 5 = 8 < 10 = d(D)$
  - Update $d(D) \leftarrow 8$, $\pi(D) \leftarrow B$.
- Edge $(B, E)$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 6 = 9 < 12 = d(E)$
  - Update $d(E) \leftarrow 9$, $\pi(E) \leftarrow B$.

$$
d^{(3)} = [0, 3, 2, 8, 9], \quad \pi^{(3)} = [\text{NIL}, C, A, B, B]
$$

### 3.1.5 Iteration 4 — Settle $D$ (smallest unsettled $d$ = 8)

Extract $u = D$. Mark $S \leftarrow S \cup \{D\}$. Relax edges leaving $D$:

- Edge $(D, E)$: $\text{alt} = 8 + 2 = 10$, but $d(E) = 9$ is already better — **no update**.

$$
d^{(4)} = [0, 3, 2, 8, 9], \quad \pi^{(4)} = [\text{NIL}, C, A, B, B]
$$

### 3.1.6 Iteration 5 — Settle $E$ (smallest unsettled $d$ = 9)

Extract $u = E$. Goal is reached (or alternatively, algorithm terminates when $Q$ is empty).

$$
d^{(5)} = [0, 3, 2, 8, 9]
$$

### 3.1.7 Final Distance Table

| Destination Waypoint | Shortest Time $d(v)$ (minutes) | Predecessor $\pi(v)$ | Optimal Path |
|:--------------------:|:------------------------------:|:--------------------:|:------------:|
| $A$ (start) | $0$ | $\text{NIL}$ | $A$ |
| $B$ | $3$ | $C$ | $A \to C \to B$ |
| $C$ | $2$ | $A$ | $A \to C$ |
| $D$ | $8$ | $B$ | $A \to C \to B \to D$ |
| $E$ (goal) | $9$ | $B$ | $A \to C \to B \to E$ |

### 3.1.8 Verification via Direct Enumeration

The candidate paths from $A$ to $E$ are:
- $A \to C \to E$ : $2 + 10 = 12$ minutes.
- $A \to B \to E$ : $4 + 6 = 10$ minutes.
- $A \to C \to B \to E$ : $2 + 1 + 6 = 9$ minutes. ✓ **Minimum**
- $A \to C \to D \to E$ : $2 + 8 + 2 = 12$ minutes.
- $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ : $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$ minutes.

The algorithm's result ($9$ minutes via $A \to C \to B \to E$) is confirmed correct.

## 3.2 Correctness Argument (Sketch for KTU Theory Questions)

Dijkstra's algorithm maintains the **invariant**: at the start of every iteration, the distance $d(v)$ for every settled node $v \in S$ is the true shortest distance. The proof is by induction on the iteration count:

- **Base case:** $S = \{s\}$; trivially $d(s) = 0$ is optimal.
- **Inductive step:** Assume invariant holds for $|S| = k$. Let $u$ be the next extracted node. Since all unsettled nodes have $d \geq d(u)$ and all edge weights $\geq 0$, any path to $u$ through an unsettled node would have length $\geq d(u)$. Hence $d(u)$ is optimal and adding $u$ to $S$ preserves the invariant.
- **Conclusion:** After all $|V|$ iterations, the invariant holds for every $v \in V$.

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation

For a graph with $\vert V \vert$ nodes and $\vert E \vert$ edges, using a **binary min-heap**:

- Each of the $\vert V \vert$ extract-min operations costs $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ → total $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$.
- Each of the $\vert E \vert$ decrease-key / insert operations costs $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ → total $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$.
- Combined: $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$.

For a **dense** graph where $\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2$, a simple array-based implementation yields $O(\vert V \vert^2)$, which outperforms the heap version.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weighted Graph Used in the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeE((E))

    nodeA ---|4| nodeB
    nodeA ---|2| nodeC
    nodeB ---|1| nodeC
    nodeB ---|5| nodeD
    nodeB ---|6| nodeE
    nodeC ---|8| nodeD
    nodeC ---|10| nodeE
    nodeD ---|2| nodeE

    classDef srcNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef goalNode fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef defaultNode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class nodeA srcNode
    class nodeE goalNode
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD defaultNode
```

> **Reading guide:** The blue node $A$ is the source (charging station). The red node $E$ is the goal (survey target). Numeric labels on edges are traversal costs in **minutes**. Green nodes are intermediate waypoints.

## 4.2 Algorithm Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initBlock[INITIALIZE distances and predecessor]
    pushAll[PUSH all nodes into priority queue Q]
    checkQ{Q empty OR goal settled?}
    extractMin[EXTRACT-MIN node u from Q]
    addSettled[ADD u to settled set S]
    relaxLoop[FOR each neighbour v of u]
    altCalc[COMPUTE alt = d of u plus weight u v]
    cmpCheck{alt less than d of v?}
    updateD[UPDATE d of v = alt]
    updatePi[UPDATE predecessor of v = u]
    reinsert[DECREASE-KEY v in Q]
    skipNo[NO UPDATE skip]
    done[TERMINATE output d and predecessor]

    initBlock --> pushAll
    pushAll --> checkQ
    checkQ -- No --> extractMin
    extractMin --> addSettled
    addSettled --> relaxLoop
    relaxLoop --> altCalc
    altCalc --> cmpCheck
    cmpCheck -- Yes --> updateD
    updateD --> updatePi
    updatePi --> reinsert
    reinsert --> checkQ
    cmpCheck -- No --> skipNo
    skipNo --> checkQ
    checkQ -- Yes --> done
```

> **Reading guide:** This flowchart captures the canonical loop of Dijkstra's algorithm. The right-most `TERMINATE` block fires either when the priority queue $Q$ is empty (full multi-target run) or when the goal node is settled (single-target early exit — a common optimization in field robotics where only the route to the survey target is needed).

## 4.3 Field-Robotics Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    occGrid[Occupancy Grid Map] --> graphBuild[Graph Builder]
    costMap[Cost Map slope, terrain, energy] --> graphBuild
    graphBuild --> weightedG[Weighted Graph G]
    weightedG --> dijkstra[Dijkstra Planner]
    startPose[Robot Start Pose] --> dijkstra
    goalPose[Goal Pose] --> dijkstra
    dijkstra --> shortestPath[Shortest Cost Path]
    shortestPath --> controller[Motion Controller]
    controller --> actuators[Wheel and Steering Actuators]
    actuators --> fieldRobot[Field Robot in Terrain]
    fieldRobot -. feedback .-> occGrid

    classDef ioBlock fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef procBlock fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef actBlock fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class occGrid,costMap,startPose,goalPose ioBlock
    class graphBuild,dijkstra,controller,shortestPath procBlock
    class actuators,fieldRobot actBlock
```

> **Reading guide:** In a deployed field robot, the **Occupancy Grid Map** and a **Cost Map** (which encodes slope, soil softness, vegetation density) are fused by a **Graph Builder** into a weighted graph. The **Dijkstra Planner** uses the robot's start and goal pose to compute the **Shortest Cost Path**, which is consumed by a low-level motion controller driving the actuators. The dashed feedback line represents the closed-loop re-planning cycle (relevant for D* extensions, covered in later modules).
