# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** State Dijkstra's algorithm. List the data structures required to implement it efficiently and mention its time complexity when a binary min-heap is used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy, single-source shortest-path algorithm** for a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative edge weights. It maintains a *tentative distance* $d(v)$ to each node, initialized as $d(s) = 0$ and $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. At every step it **extracts the unsettled node with minimum $d$**, marks it *settled*, and **relaxes** all outgoing edges using the rule

$$
d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\, d(u) + w(u,v)\bigr)
$$

Required data structures: (i) an array or hash-map for the distance labels $d(\cdot)$, (ii) an array for the predecessor pointers $\pi(\cdot)$, and (iii) a **binary min-heap (priority queue)** keyed on $d(\cdot)$. Time complexity: $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$. Space complexity: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Why does Dijkstra's algorithm fail when applied to a graph containing edges with negative weights? Justify with a one-line counter-example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dijkstra's algorithm relies on the invariant that **once a node is extracted (settled), its distance is final and cannot be improved later**, which is guaranteed only when all edge weights are non-negative. With negative weights, a path that traverses an *unsettled* node may later reduce the distance of an already-settled node, violating the invariant. *Counter-example:* Consider three nodes $A \to B$ with $w=2$, $A \to C$ with $w=4$, and $C \to B$ with $w=-3$. Dijkstra settles $B$ at distance $2$ from $A$, but the true shortest path $A \to C \to B$ has cost $4 + (-3) = 1 < 2$. Hence the algorithm is **not optimal**, and the **Bellman–Ford algorithm** must be used instead.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Answer any one full question from the Module 3 choice. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **step-by-step execution of Dijkstra's algorithm** on the weighted graph $G$ shown below. The source node is $S$. Tabulate the distance vector $d(\cdot)$ and the predecessor vector $\pi(\cdot)$ after every iteration. **(7 marks)**

**Graph edges (weights in metres):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(S,A) &= 6, \quad w(S,B) = 2, \quad w(A,B) = 3, \\
w(A,D) &= 1, \quad w(B,C) = 4, \quad w(C,D) = 2, \\
w(B,D) &= 5, \quad w(D,T) = 7, \quad w(C,T) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Reconstruct the **shortest path from $S$ to $T$** using the predecessor vector and compute its total cost. State the time and space complexity when a Fibonacci heap is used. Mention **two limitations** of Dijkstra's algorithm in the context of field robotics. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step execution:**

**Iteration 0 — Initialization:**

$$
d^{(0)} = [S:0,\; A:\infty,\; B:\infty,\; C:\infty,\; D:\infty,\; T:\infty], \quad \pi^{(0)} = [\text{NIL for all}]
$$

**Iteration 1 — Settle $S$ ($d = 0$):**

- Edge $(S,A)$: alt $= 0+6=6 < \infty$ → $d(A)=6$, $\pi(A)=S$.
- Edge $(S,B)$: alt $= 0+2=2 < \infty$ → $d(B)=2$, $\pi(B)=S$.

$$
d^{(1)} = [0, 6, 2, \infty, \infty, \infty], \quad \pi^{(1)} = [\text{NIL}, S, S, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}]
$$

**Iteration 2 — Settle $B$ ($d = 2$):**

- Edge $(B,A)$: alt $= 2+3=5 < 6$ → $d(A)=5$, $\pi(A)=B$.
- Edge $(B,C)$: alt $= 2+4=6 < \infty$ → $d(C)=6$, $\pi(C)=B$.
- Edge $(B,D)$: alt $= 2+5=7 < \infty$ → $d(D)=7$, $\pi(D)=B$.

$$
d^{(2)} = [0, 5, 2, 6, 7, \infty], \quad \pi^{(2)} = [\text{NIL}, B, S, B, B, \text{NIL}]
$$

**Iteration 3 — Settle $A$ ($d = 5$):**

- Edge $(A,B)$: alt $= 5+3=8$, but $d(B)=2$ (settled) → no update.
- Edge $(A,D)$: alt $= 5+1=6 < 7$ → $d(D)=6$, $\pi(D)=A$.

$$
d^{(3)} = [0, 5, 2, 6, 6, \infty], \quad \pi^{(3)} = [\text{NIL}, B, S, B, A, \text{NIL}]
$$

**Iteration 4 — Settle $C$ ($d = 6$, tie with $D$ — settle $C$ first alphabetically):**

- Edge $(C,D)$: alt $= 6+2=8$, but $d(D)=6$ → no update.
- Edge $(C,T)$: alt $= 6+3=9 < \infty$ → $d(T)=9$, $\pi(T)=C$.

$$
d^{(4)} = [0, 5, 2, 6, 6, 9], \quad \pi^{(4)} = [\text{NIL}, B, S, B, A, C]
$$

**Iteration 5 — Settle $D$ ($d = 6$):**

- Edge $(D,T)$: alt $= 6+7=13$, but $d(T)=9$ → no update.

$$
d^{(5)} = [0, 5, 2, 6, 6, 9]
$$

**Iteration 6 — Settle $T$ ($d = 9$):** Goal reached.

**Final distance table:**

| Node | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $T$ |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $d(\cdot)$ | $0$ | $5$ | $2$ | $6$ | $6$ | $9$ |
| $\pi(\cdot)$ | NIL | $B$ | $S$ | $B$ | $A$ | $C$ |

**(b) Path reconstruction and analysis:**

Backtrack from $T$ using $\pi$: $T \leftarrow C \leftarrow B \leftarrow S$.

- **Shortest path:** $S \to B \to C \to T$.
- **Total cost:** $w(S,B) + w(B,C) + w(C,T) = 2 + 4 + 3 = 9$ metres.

**Complexity with Fibonacci heap:**

$$
T(V, E) = O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr), \quad S(V, E) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)
$$

The Fibonacci heap reduces the amortized cost of decrease-key to $O(1)$, yielding the asymptotic improvement.

**Two limitations in field robotics:**

1. **Static-environment assumption:** Dijkstra's algorithm assumes a fully known, unchanging map. In real field deployments (moving obstacles, weather-induced terrain changes, dynamic agricultural vehicles), the computed path becomes stale and the algorithm must be re-run from scratch — motivating incremental planners like **D\*-Lite** and **LPA\***.
2. **Computational cost on dense grid maps:** For a high-resolution occupancy grid (e.g., $1000 \times 1000$ cells representing a 1 km² farm at 1 m resolution, $\vert V \vert \approx 10^6$, $\vert E \vert \approx 4 \times 10^6$), plain Dijkstra is too slow for real-time replanning. **A\*** with a heuristic or **anytime planners** are preferred in practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of the priority queue** in Dijkstra's algorithm. Compare its performance when implemented using (i) an unsorted array, (ii) a binary min-heap, and (iii) a Fibonacci heap. Tabulate the extract-min and decrease-key amortized costs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field robot must plan a path across a $4 \times 4$ grid (cells numbered row-wise from $1$ to $16$). Each cell has an associated traversal cost in seconds as listed in the table below. The robot uses **4-connected grid motion** (up, down, left, right) and may not enter a blocked cell. Using Dijkstra's algorithm, compute the **minimum-cost path from cell $1$ (top-left) to cell $16$ (bottom-right)**. The blocked cells are $\{6, 7, 11\}$. Show the final distance labels. **(7 marks)**

| Col →  Row ↓ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1** | 1 | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| **2** | 4 | 8 | 2 | 6 |
| **3** | 3 | **6 (BLOCKED)** | **7 (BLOCKED)** | 1 |
| **4** | 2 | 5 | 4 | 9 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of the priority queue and comparison:**

The **priority queue (PQ)** is the central data structure of Dijkstra's algorithm. It always holds the *unsettled* nodes keyed by their current tentative distance $d(\cdot)$. At each iteration, the algorithm calls **extract-min** on the PQ to identify the node with the smallest tentative distance — this is the *greedy* selection step. Then, when an edge is relaxed and $d(v)$ is reduced, the PQ must be updated via **decrease-key** (or equivalently, an insert + lazy-deletion).

**Comparison of implementations:**

| Implementation | Extract-Min | Decrease-Key | Total Time | Best Use Case |
|:--------------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:----------:|:-------------:|
| Unsorted array | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(1)$ (linear scan to find $v$, then update) | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Dense graphs where $\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2$ |
| Binary min-heap | $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Sparse graphs (typical field maps) |
| Fibonacci heap | $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ amortized | $O(1)$ amortized | $O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Asymptotically optimal; large sparse graphs |

The choice of PQ is the single most important implementation decision for Dijkstra's planner in a field robot — for a $1000 \times 1000$ grid ($\vert V \vert \approx 10^6$), a binary heap typically runs **$10$–$100$ times faster** than a naive array.

**(b) Grid-based shortest path computation:**

Indexing (row-major): cell index $i = 4(r-1) + c$, where $r$ is the row (1–4) and $c$ is the column (1–4).

**Cell coordinates and costs:**

| Cell | Coords $(r,c)$ | Cost (s) | Cell | Coords $(r,c)$ | Cost (s) |
|:----:|:--------------:|:--------:|:----:|:--------------:|:--------:|
| 1 | (1,1) | 1 | 9 | (3,1) | 3 |
| 2 | (1,2) | 3 | 10 | (3,2) | — |
| 3 | (1,3) | 5 | 11 | (3,3) | — |
| 4 | (1,4) | 2 | 12 | (3,4) | 1 |
| 5 | (2,1) | 4 | 13 | (4,1) | 2 |
| 6 | (2,2) | — | 14 | (4,2) | 5 |
| 7 | (2,3) | — | 15 | (4,3) | 4 |
| 8 | (2,4) | 6 | 16 | (4,4) | 9 |

*(Dashes indicate blocked cells, which are excluded from $V$.)*

**Execution of Dijkstra (key iterations only):**

- **Init:** $d(1)=0$, all others $=\infty$.
- **Settle cell 1** ($d=0$): Neighbours 2 (cost 3) and 5 (cost 4). Update $d(2)=3$, $d(5)=4$.
- **Settle cell 2** ($d=3$): Neighbours 1 (settled), 3, 6 (blocked). $d(3) \leftarrow 3+5=8$.
- **Settle cell 5** ($d=4$): Neighbours 1 (settled), 4, 9, 6 (blocked). $d(4) \leftarrow 4+6=10$, $d(9) \leftarrow 4+3=7$.
- **Settle cell 9** ($d=7$): Neighbours 5 (settled), 10 (blocked), 13. $d(13) \leftarrow 7+2=9$.
- **Settle cell 3** ($d=8$): Neighbours 2 (settled), 4, 7 (blocked). $d(4) \leftarrow \min(10, 8+2)=10$ (no change).
- **Settle cell 13** ($d=9$): Neighbours 9 (settled), 14, 16. $d(14) \leftarrow 9+5=14$, $d(16) \leftarrow 9+9=18$.
- **Settle cell 4** ($d=10$): Neighbours 3 (settled), 8, 12. $d(8) \leftarrow 10+6=16$, $d(12) \leftarrow 10+1=11$.
- **Settle cell 12** ($d=11$): Neighbours 4 (settled), 8, 11 (blocked), 16. $d(8) \leftarrow \min(16, 11+1)=12$, $d(16) \leftarrow \min(18, 11+9)=18$ (no change).
- **Settle cell 8** ($d=12$): Neighbours 4 (settled), 7 (blocked), 12 (settled). No updates.
- **Settle cell 14** ($d=14$): Neighbours 13 (settled), 15. $d(15) \leftarrow 14+4=18$.
- **Settle cell 16** ($d=18$, via cell 13). **Goal reached.**

**Final distance labels:**

| Cell | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 8 | 9 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 15 | 16 |
|:----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $d$  | 0 | 3 | 8 | 10 | 4 | 12 | 7 | 11 | 9 | 14 | 18 | **18** |

**Shortest path (reconstructed via $\pi$):** $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 12 \to \cdots$ is not optimal; the optimal path is

$$
1 \to 5 \to 9 \to 13 \to 16
$$

with total cost $1 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 9 = 19$? — *Recheck:* the cost-to-enter semantics must be clarified. Under standard 4-connected Dijkstra (edge cost = cell cost, source excluded), the cost to reach cell 16 via $1 \to 5 \to 9 \to 13 \to 16$ is $0 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 9 = 18$ seconds. The predecessor chain $16 \leftarrow 13 \leftarrow 9 \leftarrow 5 \leftarrow 1$ confirms the optimal route.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye):**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the predecessor** when relaxing an edge — even if the distance does not change, the predecessor must be updated whenever $d(v)$ strictly decreases. Examiners specifically look for the column $\pi(\cdot)$ being correctly tabulated.
> 2. **Confusing *node* cost with *edge* cost** in grid-based questions. State explicitly whether the cost of a cell is added when *entering* it (standard) or when *exiting* it. A wrong convention silently inflates/decreases the answer by one cell cost.
> 3. **Using Dijkstra on graphs with negative weights** — a frequent conceptual trap. Always state the non-negativity assumption.
> 4. **Quoting the wrong complexity** — students often write $O(n \log n)$ without specifying whether $n$ is $\vert V \vert$ or $\vert E \vert$, and whether the PQ is a binary heap or Fibonacci heap. Examiners reward a *complete* statement.
> 5. **Skipping the "why greedy selection is safe"** justification in Part B theory questions. At KTU's B.Tech level, a 1–2 line proof sketch is mandatory for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dijkstra's algorithm** solves the **single-source shortest-path problem** on a **weighted graph with non-negative edges**.
- It is a **greedy** algorithm with three key state variables: $d(v)$ (current best distance), $\pi(v)$ (predecessor), and the settled set $S$.
- The core update rule is the **relaxation step**: $d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\, d(u) + w(u,v)\bigr)$.
- The **priority queue** is the heart of the implementation — binary heap is the practical default; Fibonacci heap is the asymptotic optimum.
- **Time complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array; $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ with binary heap; $O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ with Fibonacci heap.
- **Space complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- **Completeness:** Yes (on finite graphs). **Optimality:** Yes (only when $w(e) \geq 0$).
- **In Field Robotics:** foundational for grid-based and topological-map planning in known, static environments; serves as the baseline for A*, D*, and incremental planners.
- **Path reconstruction:** backtrack from goal using the predecessor vector $\pi$ to the source.
- **Limitation 1:** Cannot handle negative edge weights (use Bellman–Ford).
- **Limitation 2:** Replanning from scratch on every map update is expensive (use incremental planners like D\*-Lite or LPA\*).
- **Limitation 3:** Computationally heavy on high-resolution dense grid maps (consider A* with a heuristic, or anytime / sampling-based planners).
- **Key invariant to remember:** once a node is settled, its distance is final and immutable (this is what makes the algorithm correct and is the property that *fails* under negative weights).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Best-First Search — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best-First Search (BFS)** is an informed graph-traversal strategy that expands, at every iteration, the node that appears to be **closest to the goal** according to a domain-specific **evaluation function** $f(n)$. Unlike uninformed searches (BFS, DFS, UCS), it uses a *heuristic estimate* to bias exploration toward the most promising frontier node.

In **field robotics**, the underlying graph is typically an **occupancy grid** or a **roadmap** constructed from SLAM-mapped environment data. Each grid cell / waypoint becomes a graph node, and edges represent drivable (or flyable) transitions.

### 1.2 Variants of Best-First Search

| Variant | Evaluation Function | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)** | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Uses *only* the heuristic — fast but **not optimal** |
| **A\* Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Combines actual cost + heuristic — **optimal & complete** if $h$ is admissible |
| **Weighted A\*** | $f(n) = g(n) + \epsilon \cdot h(n)$, $\epsilon > 1$ | Trades optimality for speed |
| **A Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ *with inconsistent $h$* | Same formula as A\*, but loses optimality when $h$ is not consistent |

> [!NOTE]
> **A** and **A\*** share the identical evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. The distinction lies in the **property of the heuristic**: A\* requires an **admissible (and ideally consistent)** heuristic to guarantee optimality, whereas **A** is the general form that may or may not be optimal.

---

## 1.3 Components of the Evaluation Function

$$
f(n) = \underbrace{g(n)}_{\text{actual cost so far}} + \underbrace{h(n)}_{\text{estimated cost to goal}}
$$

- $g(n)$ — Known path cost from the start node $S$ to the current node $n$ (always exact, monotonically non-decreasing).
- $h(n)$ — **Heuristic estimate** of the cost from $n$ to the goal $G$ (approximate, problem-specific).
- $f(n)$ — Estimated **total cost** of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> 🧭 **Analogy — Hiking Down a Mountain in Fog**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain and want to reach the valley village. You cannot see the village. Best-First Search is like a hiker who repeatedly asks: *"Which direction feels most downhill toward the village?"* Each step, the hiker picks the slope that **appears to descend the fastest** — this is $h(n)$.
>
> - **Greedy Best-First** is the hiker who only follows the steepest perceived slope. Fast, but may end up in a **dead-end ravine**.
> - **A\*** is the hiker who balances two things: *"How far have I already walked?"* ($g(n)$) **plus** *"How steep does the slope look to the goal?"* ($h(n)$). This hiker almost always reaches the village via the **shortest safe trail**.
> - **A** is the same balanced hiker, but uses a faulty altimeter (inconsistent $h$) — sometimes the trail seems longer than it really is, so the optimality guarantee is lost.

In **field robotics**, the mountain is the **obstacle map**, the hiker is the **mobile robot's planner**, and the village is the **goal pose**.

---

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics for KTU

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | — | Will the algorithm find *a* solution if one exists? |
| **Optimality** | — | Will it find the *least-cost* solution? |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | Exponential in solution depth $d$, branching factor $b$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | All frontier nodes must be stored |
| **Heuristic Quality** | $h^*$ | The **true** optimal cost from $n$ to goal |
| **Effective Branching Factor** | $b^*$ | Measure of search effort — closer to 1 ⇒ more efficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Admissibility:** A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for every node $n$. It **never overestimates** the true remaining cost.
> **Heuristic Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \le c(n, n', 1) + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$ of $n$. Guarantees $f$-values are non-decreasing along any path — required for A\* graph-search optimality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The A\* Algorithm — Operational Logic

A\* maintains two data structures:
- **OPEN list** (priority queue, keyed by $f$-value) — frontier nodes to be expanded.
- **CLOSED list** — already expanded nodes.

### Step-by-Step Operational Loop

1. **Initialize:** Insert the start node $S$ into OPEN with $g(S) = 0$, $f(S) = h(S)$.
2. **Termination Check:** If OPEN is empty → return **FAILURE** (no path exists). If the goal $G$ is popped from OPEN → **reconstruct and return** the optimal path.
3. **Select Best Node:** Pop the node $n$ from OPEN with the **minimum $f(n)$**.
4. **Expand $n$:** For every successor $n'$ of $n$:
   - Compute tentative $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
   - Compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
   - If $n'$ is in CLOSED and $g(n') \ge$ previously recorded $g$-value → **skip**.
   - If $n'$ is in OPEN with higher $g$ → **update** its $g$, $f$ and parent.
   - Else → **insert/update** $n'$ in OPEN.
5. **Move $n$ to CLOSED.** Go to step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the **graph-search variant** of A\*. The **tree-search variant** omits the CLOSED list, allowing revisits — necessary when the heuristic is inconsistent but more expensive.

---

## 2.2 The A Search — When Heuristics Fail

A Search applies the identical $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ rule, but with **no guarantee** on $h$ being admissible or consistent. Two pathological phenomena can occur:

- **Re-opening:** A node already in CLOSED may be reached via a cheaper path — A must re-insert it into OPEN.
- **Non-monotonic $f$-values:** Expanding order may briefly include nodes with $f$ greater than an already-expanded node's $f$.

| Property | A\* (Admissible $h$) | A (Inadmissible/Inconsistent $h$) |
|---|---|---|
| Completeness | ✅ Yes (finite graph) | ⚠️ Yes (with re-opening) |
| Optimality | ✅ Yes (proof via induction) | ❌ Not guaranteed |
| Implementation Cost | Lower (no re-opening) | Higher (re-opening overhead) |
| Use Case | Production-grade path planning | Research / dynamic heuristics |

---

## 2.3 Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS) — The Pure Heuristic

GBFS sets $f(n) = h(n)$ only. It ignores $g(n)$ entirely. It is **fast** but suffers from:

- **Local minima traps** — heuristic plateaus cause endless expansion of equivalent nodes.
- **Non-optimal solutions** — committed to the first goal reached.
- **No completeness guarantee** in infinite state spaces with non-goal-directed heuristics.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before the ESE.** Every KTU question on Module 3 best-first search maps to one of these equations.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via $n$ | A\*, A |
| 2 | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Heuristic only | Greedy BFS |
| 3 | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Admissibility condition | A\* optimality proof |
| 4 | $h(n) \le c(n,n',1) + h(n')$ | Consistency (triangle inequality) | A\* graph-search |
| 5 | $d(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$ | Euclidean heuristic (continuous) | Holonomic robots |
| 6 | $d_M(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | Manhattan heuristic (grid) | 4-connected grid worlds |
| 7 | $d_C(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_G \vert, \vert y_n - y_G \vert)$ | Chebyshev heuristic (8-connected) | Diagonal motion allowed |
| 8 | $h_{\text{octile}}(n) = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | Octile distance heuristic | 8-connected uniform grid |
| 9 | $f_{\min} \le f^* \le f_{\text{any returned solution}}$ | A\* returns optimal if $h$ admissible | Optimality verification |
| 10 | $b^* = $ smallest integer $b$ s.t. $N+1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \dots + (b^*)^d$ | Effective branching factor | Empirical search-quality metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** In the KTU exam, when writing $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value, use either `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` — **never the bare `|` symbol** inside a markdown table, as it will break table rendering in the answer script.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Field Robotics

| Domain | Application of A\*/A |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Agricultural Robots** | Coverage planning across rows of crops, re-routing around obstacles |
| **UGV Path Planning** | Off-road navigation on uneven terrain with cost maps (slope, soil traction) |
| **UAV Mission Planning** | 3-D flight corridors with no-fly zones and energy-aware heuristics |
| **Warehouse AMRs** | Multi-agent A\* (MAA\*) for fleet coordination |
| **Search & Rescue** | Time-critical pathfinding through partially known rubble maps |
| **Autonomous Marine Vehicles** | Ocean-current-aware A\* with dynamic cost functions |

> [!TIP]
> In dynamic environments, A\* is re-run periodically (e.g., every **0.5 s**) as new sensor data updates the cost map. This is called **replanning** or **incremental A\*** (e.g., D\*-Lite, LPA\*).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Admissibility Guarantees A\* Optimality

**Theorem (A\* Optimality):** If $h$ is admissible, then any node $n$ popped from OPEN has $f(n) \le f^*$, where $f^*$ is the cost of the optimal solution.

### Proof by Contradiction

**Step 1 — Setup:** Let $G$ be the goal, and let $n$ be the first node popped from OPEN such that $f(n) > f^*$.

**Step 2 — Identify the optimal path:** An optimal solution path $P^* = \langle S, \dots, n^*, \dots, G \rangle$ exists with $\text{cost}(P^*) = f^*$.

**Step 3 — Choose the predecessor on $P^*$:** Let $n'$ be a predecessor of $G$ on $P^*$ that is still on OPEN (such a node must exist, because the search expands along the frontier and at least one optimal-path node remains unexplored when $n$ is popped — otherwise $G$ would have been popped earlier with $f(G) = f^* \le f(n)$, contradicting $f(n) > f^*$).

**Step 4 — Apply admissibility to $n'$:** Because $h$ is admissible,

$$
h(n') \le h^*(n')
$$

Since $G$ is reachable from $n'$ along $P^*$:

$$
h^*(n') = \text{cost}(n', G) \text{ along } P^*
$$

Therefore:

$$
h(n') \le \text{cost}(n', G) \text{ along } P^*
$$

**Step 5 — Lower bound $f(n')$:**

$$
f(n') = g(n') + h(n') \le g(n') + \text{cost}(n', G) \text{ along } P^*
$$

The right-hand side equals the cost of the subpath of $P^*$ from $S$ to $G$ passing through $n'$:

$$
f(n') \le \text{cost}(S, \dots, n', \dots, G) = f^*
$$

**Step 6 — Contradiction:** Since $n'$ is on OPEN and $f(n') \le f^*$, A\* would have selected $n'$ (or some node with $f \le f^*$) before $n$. This contradicts the assumption that $n$ was the first node with $f(n) > f^*$ to be popped.

**Conclusion:** No such $n$ exists. A\* always returns the optimal solution. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Heuristic Dominance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$ satisfy $h_1(n) \ge h_2(n)$ for all $n$, then $h_1$ **dominates** $h_2$, and any A\* search using $h_1$ will expand **no more nodes** than one using $h_2$.

### Justification

The number of nodes with $f(n) < f^*$ expanded by A\* is determined by how close $h$ is to $h^*$. A larger admissible $h$ gives a larger $g(n) + h(n) = f(n)$, but more importantly, a **tighter lower bound on the true cost through $n$**. This prunes more nodes whose $f$-value already exceeds $f^*$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A\* on a 2-D Grid

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
> A mobile robot navigates a **4-connected grid** with unit step cost in cardinal directions. Start $S = (0, 0)$, Goal $G = (4, 3)$. Cells shaded gray are obstacles. Use **Manhattan heuristic**. Trace the A\* expansion order and the optimal path.

| Node | $g$ | $h = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | $f = g + h$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S = (0,0)$ | 0 | $4 + 3 = 7$ | **7** |
| $A = (1,0)$ | 1 | $3 + 3 = 6$ | **7** |
| $B = (0,1)$ | 1 | $4 + 2 = 6$ | **7** |
| $C = (2,0)$ | 2 | $2 + 3 = 5$ | **7** |
| $D = (1,1)$ | 2 | $3 + 2 = 5$ | **7** |
| $E = (0,2)$ | 2 | $4 + 1 = 5$ | **7** |
| $\dots$ | $\dots$ | $\dots$ | $\dots$ |
| $G = (4,3)$ | $7$ | $0$ | **7** |

**A\* expansion order** (ties broken by smaller $h$, then smaller $g$):

1. Expand $S$ — push $A$, $B$.
2. Expand $A$ ($f=7$) — push $C$, $D$.
3. Expand $B$ ($f=7$) — push $E$, $D$ (update $D$ if cheaper).
4. Expand $C$ ($f=7$) — push successors, etc.
5. When $G$ is popped with $f = 7$, reconstruct path.

**Optimal path:** $S \to A \to C \to \dots \to G$ with **total cost = 7** (Manhattan distance, no detour needed since grid is obstacle-free in this example).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test the expansion table. **Always** show the OPEN list state after each expansion. Marks are lost when students only list the final path.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix — A\*, A, GBFS, UCS

> [!TIP]
> This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question** (often as "Compare the four informed/uninformed search strategies with suitable diagrams and a real-world example").

| Property | Uniform Cost Search | Greedy BFS | A Search | A\* Search |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Evaluation fn | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Heuristic used | None | Admissible (not required) | Arbitrary | Admissible (and ideally consistent) |
| Complete? | ✅ (if $\epsilon > 0$) | ❌ | ✅ (with re-opening) | ✅ |
| Optimal? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (in general) | ✅ |
| Expands nodes with $f \le f^*$? | All | Heuristic-guided | All | **Yes — provably minimal** |
| Time complexity | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Typical use | Brute-force cost optimization | Quick plausible path | Research / dynamic | **Production robotics** |

---

## 3.5 Admissibility vs. Consistency — Quick-Derive Test

> [!NOTE]
> Given the graph fragment below, determine whether heuristic $h$ is admissible and/or consistent.

**Graph:** $A \to B$ (cost 2), $B \to C$ (cost 2), $A \to C$ (cost 5), $C$ is goal.  
**Heuristic:** $h(A) = 3$, $h(B) = 2$, $h(C) = 0$.

### Test 1 — Admissibility

- $h^*(A) = 4$ (cheapest path $A \to B \to C$). Check $h(A) = 3 \le 4$. ✅
- $h^*(B) = 2$. Check $h(B) = 2 \le 2$. ✅
- $h^*(C) = 0$. Check $h(C) = 0 \le 0$. ✅

→ **$h$ is admissible.**

### Test 2 — Consistency

- For edge $A \to B$: $h(A) \le c(A,B) + h(B) = 2 + 2 = 4$. $3 \le 4$. ✅
- For edge $B \to C$: $h(B) \le c(B,C) + h(C) = 2 + 0 = 2$. $2 \le 2$. ✅
- For edge $A \to C$: $h(A) \le c(A,C) + h(C) = 5 + 0 = 5$. $3 \le 5$. ✅

→ **$h$ is consistent.**

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 A\* Algorithm — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **internal control flow** of the A\* algorithm — analogous to a robotic path-planner's data-pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start S, Goal G]) --> initOpen[Initialize OPEN list with S]
    initOpen --> initClosed[Initialize CLOSED list as empty]
    initClosed --> openEmptyCheck{OPEN empty?}
    openEmptyCheck -- Yes --> failReturn([Return FAILURE])
    openEmptyCheck -- No --> popNode[Pop node n with minimum f n]
    popNode --> goalCheck{n is Goal G?}
    goalCheck -- Yes --> successPath[Reconstruct optimal path via parent pointers]
    successPath --> returnPath([Return PATH])
    goalCheck -- No --> moveClosed[Move n to CLOSED]
    moveClosed --> genSucc[Generate successors n prime of n]
    genSucc --> loopSucc{For each successor n prime}
    loopSucc -- Done all --> openEmptyCheck
    loopSucc -- Next successor --> computeG[Compute tentative g n prime equals g n plus c n n prime]
    computeG --> computeF[Compute f n prime equals g n prime plus h n prime]
    computeF --> closedCheck{n prime in CLOSED?}
    closedCheck -- Yes skip --> loopSucc
    closedCheck -- No --> openCheck{n prime in OPEN with lower g?}
    openCheck -- Yes update --> loopSucc
    openCheck -- No --> insertOpen[Insert or update n prime in OPEN]
    insertOpen --> loopSucc
```

---

## 4.2 Heuristic Property Decision Tree

Use this diagram to classify a given heuristic $h$ at a glance — a routine KTU viva question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hStart[Given heuristic h n] --> q1{Is h n greater than or equal to 0 for all n?}
    q1 -- No --> invalidH[Invalid heuristic, reject]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Does h n less than or equal to h star n for all n?}
    q2 -- No --> inadmissible[Inadmissible h, A not optimal]
    q2 -- Yes --> admissible[Admissible h]
    admissible --> q3{Does h n less than or equal to c plus h n prime for all edges?}
    q3 -- No --> inconsistent[Consistent? No, only admissible]
    q3 -- Yes --> consistent[Consistent AND admissible, A star is optimal]
    inconsistent --> aStarOk[A star still optimal for tree search, needs re opening for graph search]
    consistent --> ideal[Ideal case, A star optimal and efficient]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ucsBlock[UCS Block]:::ucs --> ucsEq[f n equals g n]
    gbfBlock[Greedy BFS Block]:::gbf --> gbfEq[f n equals h n]
    aBlock[A Search Block]:::a --> aEq[f n equals g n plus h n]
    aStarBlock[A Star Block]:::astar --> aStarEq[f n equals g n plus h n]
    aEq --> aCond[Inadmissible or inconsistent h]
    aStarEq --> aStarCond[Admissible consistent h]

    classDef ucs fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef gbf fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef a fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef astar fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Robot Path-Planning Pipeline — Where A\* Lives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorIn[Sensor Input LIDAR Camera IMU]:::input --> slam[SLAM Module Map Building]:::process
    slam --> costMap[Cost Map Generator Obstacle Inflation]:::process
    costMap --> astarCore[A Star Planner Best First Search]:::core
    astarCore --> trajSmoother[Trajectory Smoother Spline Optimizer]:::process
    trajSmoother --> controller[Motion Controller PID MPC]:::process
    controller --> actuatorOut[Wheel Motor Commands]:::output

    classDef input fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef output fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The A\* planner (green core block) sits between *perception* (left) and *control* (right). The cost map is its input; the smooth trajectory is its output. In real-world deployments, the planner is **re-queried** continuously as the cost map updates.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Uniform Cost Search and A\* Search. State the conditions under which A\* guarantees an optimal solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | UCS | A\* |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses heuristic | No | Yes |
| Optimal | Yes (for positive costs) | Yes, **if $h$ is admissible** |
| Information used | Path cost only | Path cost + estimated cost to goal |

> **Optimality Condition for A\***: The heuristic $h(n)$ must be **admissible**, i.e., $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for every node $n$. If, in addition, $h$ is **consistent** (monotonic), A\* is also optimally efficient — no other optimal algorithm expands fewer nodes with the same information.

*(1 mark for evaluation function distinction, 1 mark for heuristic usage, 1 mark for admissibility condition.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define heuristic consistency. Show with a small example whether a given heuristic is consistent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A heuristic $h$ is **consistent** (or monotone) if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$:
> 
> $$h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$$
> 
> Equivalently, the estimated cost from $n$ to the goal is no greater than the cost of one step plus the estimated cost from $n'$ to the goal.

**Example (2 marks):** Graph: $A \xrightarrow{3} B \xrightarrow{2} C$ (goal). Heuristic: $h(A) = 4$, $h(B) = 1$, $h(C) = 0$.

- Edge $A \to B$: $h(A) = 4 \le c(A,B) + h(B) = 3 + 1 = 4$. ✅ Holds.
- Edge $B \to C$: $h(B) = 1 \le c(B,C) + h(C) = 2 + 0 = 2$. ✅ Holds.

→ $h$ is **consistent**.

---

This loses 3–4 marks. **Always show the OPEN-list snapshot AFTER each node expansion**, with the popped $f$-value, generated successors, and any updates. A 14-mark A\* question without this loses at least 4 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the A\* search algorithm with a neat flowchart. State the properties required for the heuristic function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply A\* algorithm to find the shortest path from **S = (1, 1)** to **G = (5, 5)** on the 4-connected grid shown below. Use the **Manhattan distance heuristic**. Obstacles are shaded. Show the OPEN and CLOSED lists after each iteration. **(7 marks)**

```
Grid (1,1) at top-left, (5,5) at bottom-right:

.  .  X  .  .
.  X  X  .  .
.  .  .  X  .
X  .  .  .  .
.  .  .  .  G
```

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) A\* Algorithm and Heuristic Properties (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. Place start node $S$ in OPEN with $f(S) = h(S)$, $g(S) = 0$.
2. If OPEN empty → return FAILURE.
3. Pop node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$ from OPEN.
4. If $n$ is goal $G$ → reconstruct path via parent pointers and return SUCCESS.
5. Move $n$ to CLOSED. Generate all successors $n'$.
6. For each $n'$:
   - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$, then $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
   - If $n'$ already in CLOSED with $g(n') \ge$ existing → skip.
   - If $n'$ in OPEN with smaller new $g$ → update $g$, $f$, and parent.
   - Otherwise → insert $n'$ into OPEN.
7. Go to step 2.

**Heuristic Properties (3 marks):**

- **Admissibility:** $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$. Never overestimates. Guarantees A\* optimality.
- **Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \le c(n, n', 1) + h(n')$. Guarantees $f$-values are non-decreasing along any path, so CLOSED-list nodes need never be re-opened.
- **Dominance:** If $h_1(n) \ge h_2(n)$ for all $n$ (both admissible), $h_1$ dominates $h_2$ and expands no more nodes.

**(b) A\* Trace on 4 × 5 Grid (7 marks)**

**Heuristic:** $h(x, y) = \vert x - 5 \vert + \vert y - 5 \vert$.

| Step | Pop $n$ | $f(n)$ | Generated successors | OPEN after step (with $f$) | CLOSED |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S(1,1) | 8 | (1,2), (2,1) | (1,2) f=7, (2,1) f=7 | (1,1) |
| 2 | (1,2) | 7 | (1,3)X skip, (2,2) | (2,1) f=7, (2,2) f=7 | (1,1), (1,2) |
| 3 | (2,1) | 7 | (3,1) | (2,2) f=7, (3,1) f=7 | (1,1), (1,2), (2,1) |
| 4 | (2,2) | 7 | (1,2)X in CLOSED skip, (2,3)X skip, (3,2) | (3,1) f=7, (3,2) f=7 | + (2,2) |
| 5 | (3,1) | 7 | (4,1)X skip, (3,2) update? | (3,2) f=7 | + (3,1) |
| 6 | (3,2) | 7 | (2,2) closed skip, (3,3)X skip, (4,2) | (4,2) f=7 | + (3,2) |
| 7 | (4,2) | 7 | (4,3), (5,2) | (4,3) f=7, (5,2) f=7 | + (4,2) |
| 8 | (4,3) | 7 | (4,4), (5,3) | (5,2) f=7, (4,4) f=7, (5,3) f=7 | + (4,3) |
| 9 | (5,2) | 7 | (5,3) update? Same | (4,4) f=7, (5,3) f=7 | + (5,2) |
| 10 | (4,4) | 7 | (4,5), (5,4) | (5,3) f=7, (4,5) f=7, (5,4) f=7 | + (4,4) |
| 11 | (5,3) | 7 | (5,4) update? same | (4,5) f=7, (5,4) f=7 | + (5,3) |
| 12 | (4,5) | 7 | (5,5)=G | (5,4) f=7, (5,5) f=7 | + (4,5) |
| 13 | (5,4) | 7 | (5,5) update? same | (5,5) f=7 | + (5,4) |
| 14 | (5,5) | 7 | GOAL POPPED | — | + (5,5) |

**Optimal path reconstructed:** $S(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (2,2) \to (3,2) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4) \to (4,5) \to (5,5) = G$.

**Total cost = 8 steps** (Manhattan distance, no detour forced).

*(1 mark algorithm, 1 mark heuristic properties, 5 marks step-by-step trace with OPEN/CLOSED snapshots.)*

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **Greedy Best-First Search**, **A\***, and **A** search algorithms in terms of evaluation function, completeness, optimality, and use of heuristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the graph below with start **S** and goal **G**. Step costs are written on edges. Heuristic values $h$ are given inside each node. Apply the A\* algorithm and show the node expansion order, OPEN/CLOSED lists, and final path with total cost. Is the heuristic admissible? Is it consistent? Justify. **(7 marks)**

```
        S
       / \
      1   4
     /     \
    A       B
   / \     / \
  3   1   2   5
 /     \ /     \
C---1---D---1---E
|       |       |
3       2       3
|       |       |
F       G       H
```

Heuristics: $h(S)=6, h(A)=4, h(B)=4, h(C)=5, h(D)=2, h(E)=4, h(F)=3, h(G)=0, h(H)=2$.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Property | Greedy BFS | A Search | A\* Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses $g(n)$? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Uses $h(n)$? | Yes (only) | Yes | Yes |
| Heuristic constraint | None | None | Admissible (and ideally consistent) |
| Complete? | No (local minima) | Yes (with re-opening) | Yes |
| Optimal? | No | Not guaranteed | Yes |
| Speed | Fastest | Medium | Slower than Greedy, faster than UCS |
| Risk | Local minima traps | Inconsistent-cost solutions | Higher memory than Greedy |
| Field-robotics use | Reactive local planning | Adaptive heuristics | **Production global planner** |

> **Mnemonic (1 mark):** "**Greedy** *guesses*, **A** *reasons*, **A\*** *reasons optimally*."

**(b) A\* Trace (7 marks)**

True shortest path from $S$ to $G$ via $A \to D \to G$:

$$
\text{cost} = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4
$$

Other possible paths:

- $S \to B \to D \to G$: $4 + 2 + 2 = 8$
- $S \to A \to C \to D \to G$: $1 + 3 + 1 + 2 = 7$
- $S \to B \to E \to \dots \to H$: very expensive

**Expansion Table:**

| Step | Pop $n$ | $f(n)$ | OPEN after (with $f$) | CLOSED |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S | $0+6=6$ | A(1+4=5), B(4+4=8) | S |
| 2 | A | **5** | B(8), C(4+5=9), D(2+2=4) | S, A |
| 3 | D | **4** | B(8), C(9), G(4+0=4) | S, A, D |
| 4 | G | **4** | GOAL popped | + G |

**Final path:** $S \to A \to D \to G$ with total cost **4**.

**Admissibility check (2 marks):**

- $h(G) = 0 = h^*(G)$ ✅
- $h(D) = 2$ vs $h^*(D) = 2$ (path $D \to G$) ✅
- $h(A) = 4$ vs $h^*(A) = 4$ (path $A \to D \to G$) ✅
- $h(S) = 6$ vs $h^*(S) = 4$. Here $h(S) = 6 > 4 = h^*(S)$ ❌

→ **$h$ is NOT admissible**, because $h(S) > h^*(S)$.

**Consistency check (1 mark):** Since admissibility fails, consistency also fails. Specifically, edge $S \to A$: $h(S) = 6 \not\le c(S,A) + h(A) = 1 + 4 = 5$. ❌

**Important consequence:** Because $h$ is inadmissible, A\* on this graph may **not** return the optimal path — though in this specific trace it does (since the algorithm did not need to use $h(S)$ after expansion). However, the **guarantee is lost**: a different graph with the same heuristic could produce a suboptimal solution.

*(1 mark evaluation table, 2 marks expansion trace, 2 marks admissibility proof, 1 mark consistency, 1 mark final synthesis.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Must-Recall Points for KTU ESE Module 3**

- [x] **Best-First Search** uses an evaluation function $f(n)$ to pick the next node to expand.
- [x] **Greedy BFS** uses $f(n) = h(n)$ — fast but not optimal or complete in general.
- [x] **A\* and A** both use $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; **A\* requires admissible $h$** for optimality.
- [x] **A** is the same algorithm without admissibility/consistency guarantees on $h$ — may require **node re-opening**.
- [x] **Admissibility** ⇔ $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ (never overestimates).
- [x] **Consistency** ⇔ $h(n) \le c(n,n',1) + h(n')$ (triangle-inequality analogue).
- [x] A\* with a **consistent** heuristic never needs to re-open a CLOSED node → **graph-search optimality**.
- [x] A\* is **optimally efficient**: no other optimal algorithm using the same heuristic expands fewer nodes.
- [x] **Heuristic dominance**: $h_1 \ge h_2$ (both admissible) ⇒ $h_1$ expands $\le$ nodes than $h_2$.
- [x] Common grid heuristics: **Manhattan** (4-connected), **Chebyshev** (8-connected), **Octile** (8-connected with $\sqrt{2}$ diagonals), **Euclidean** (continuous).
- [x] **OPEN list** = priority queue of frontier nodes (min-heap on $f$); **CLOSED list** = expanded nodes.
- [x] In **field robotics**, A\* is the de-facto global planner; combined with local controllers (DWA, MPC) for reactive obstacle avoidance; re-planned on every cost-map update (D\*-Lite, LPA\* for incremental replanning).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook for ESE:**
> *"A\* = GBFS + UCS married together — the heuristic steers (GBFS), the path cost verifies (UCS)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Field Robotics** and continuous-state-space motion planning, a **Potential Field** is a scalar field $U(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}$ defined over the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, in which:

- The **goal configuration** $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ is treated as a global minimum of the field.
- **Obstacles** are treated as regions of high (repulsive) potential.
- The **robot** is modeled as a point mass that moves along the **negative gradient** of the field, $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$, under the action of a fictitious generalized force.

This approach was first proposed by **Oussama Khatib (1986)** and is formally classified as a **reactive / local path planning** method, as opposed to global planners (e.g., Visibility Graphs, Cell Decomposition, RRT). Because the configuration space is continuous (uncountably infinite states), potential fields are particularly well-suited to **real-time trajectory generation** for wheeled and legged field robots navigating in unstructured outdoor environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** "A potential field method treats the goal as an attractor and obstacles as repulsors, generating a continuous vector field whose negative gradient produces a collision-free motion direction for the robot at every point in its configuration space."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Inflated Balloon on a Trampoline

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you place a heavy bowling ball at the **goal location** on a stretched rubber trampoline. The bowling ball creates a *dent* (valley) in the trampoline. Now imagine pushing a **marble (the robot)** anywhere on the trampoline. Gravity will automatically roll the marble into the deepest part of the dent — your goal!
> Now suppose you place a few **upward-pointing cones** (obstacles) on the trampoline, pushing it upward into sharp peaks. The marble, when released, will naturally avoid those peaks while still rolling toward the goal valley.
> - The **trampoline surface height** = potential $U(\mathbf{q})$
> - The **slope of the surface** = negative gradient $-\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$
> - The **marble's velocity** = robot's motion direction
> - The **goal valley** = attractive potential minimum
> - The **obstacle peaks** = repulsive potential maxima

This is exactly how a potential-field-based robot "feels" its world: it experiences an **artificial force** pulling it toward the goal and pushing it away from obstacles, and it simply follows the resultant force vector at every instant.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration space point | $\mathbf{q}$ | Robot's current pose (position + orientation) |
| Attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q})$ | "Pull" toward the goal |
| Repulsive potential | $U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})$ | "Push" away from obstacles |
| Total potential | $U(\mathbf{q})$ | Sum: $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$ |
| Artificial force | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q})$ | Negative gradient: $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$ |
| Distance of influence | $\rho_0$ | Radius beyond which obstacles are ignored |
| Local minimum | $\mathbf{q}_{LM}$ | Stationary point where $\nabla U = 0$ but $\mathbf{q} \neq \mathbf{q}_{goal}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Potential Field Method

The potential field planner operates in a closed-loop, iterative manner. The complete reasoning chain is:

1. **Define the Goal Attractor:** The goal $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ is encoded as a paraboloid well $U_{att}(\mathbf{q})$ whose minimum coincides with the goal coordinates.
2. **Define Obstacle Repulsors:** Each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_k$ within the sensor's perceptual range contributes a repulsive potential $U_{rep}^{(k)}(\mathbf{q})$ that grows without bound as the robot approaches the obstacle's boundary.
3. **Superpose the Field:** The total potential is the scalar sum:
$$U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} U_{rep}^{(k)}(\mathbf{q})$$
4. **Compute the Force:** The generalized force acting on the robot at any configuration is the negative gradient:
$$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \nabla U_{rep}^{(k)}(\mathbf{q})$$
5. **Apply Gradient Descent (Continuous State-Space Search):** The robot updates its configuration by integrating the force over time:
$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{q}(t+\Delta t) = \mathbf{q}(t) - \eta \, \nabla U(\mathbf{q})$$
   where $\eta$ is a positive step-size (gain).
6. **Terminate when the robot reaches the goal tolerance $\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert < \epsilon$.**

## 2.2 Why and How Each Component Works

- **Why attractive?** Because $U_{att}$ must vanish (be minimum) at the goal and grow with distance — this guarantees the goal is the *deepest* point of the field.
- **Why repulsive?** Because $U_{rep}$ must be **bounded** (zero) far from the obstacle to avoid corrupting the global gradient, and **unbounded** (or very large) near the obstacle surface to prevent penetration.
- **Why gradient descent?** It is the *steepest descent* method on a scalar surface — by moving opposite to the gradient, the robot always reduces its potential energy, asymptotically reaching a minimum.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Potential fields are an *exact realization* of the Lagrangian mechanics principle of **least action / stationary potential energy**. The robot is treated as a unit-mass particle in a conservative force field.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **canonical formulas** expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. **Master these first.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conic (quadratic) attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \zeta \, d^2(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | $\zeta > 0$ gain, $d$ = Euclidean distance |
| 2 | Conic attractive force | $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\zeta(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | Force vector |
| 3 | Parabolic (quadratic) attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert^2$ | Same as #1 expanded |
| 4 | Distance function to obstacle | $\rho(\mathbf{q}) = \min_{\mathbf{q}' \in \mathcal{O}} \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}' \vert$ | Shortest distance to obstacle |
| 5 | FIRAS repulsive potential (Khatib) | $U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | $\eta$ > 0, $\rho_0$ = influence radius |
| 6 | FIRAS repulsive force | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \begin{cases} \eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \nabla \rho & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\ \mathbf{0} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Force vector |
| 7 | Total potential | $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} U_{rep,k}$ | Scalar |
| 8 | Total artificial force | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$ | Vector |
| 9 | Robot motion update (gradient descent) | $\mathbf{q}_{t+1} = \mathbf{q}_t + \alpha \, \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}_t)$ | $\alpha$ = step size |
| 10 | Goal-reach termination | $\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert < \epsilon$ | Tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table construction note:** The vertical bar character has been replaced with `\vert` throughout this cheat sheet to comply with the markdown table syntax rule (the `|` symbol would otherwise break column separators).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Field Robotics

- **Autonomous Agricultural Tractors:** Avoid standing trees, fence posts, and livestock using reactive potential fields generated from LiDAR point clouds.
- **UGV De-mining Robots:** Navigate a minefield where mines are repulsive poles; the goal is the safe extraction point.
- **Search and Rescue Drones:** Treat victims (heat sources) as attractors and hazardous zones (fire, structural collapse) as repulsors.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Avoid underwater pipelines and coral reefs in GPS-denied environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Force from the Quadratic Potential

**Given:** The conic attractive potential is defined as

$$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert^2$$

We treat $\mathbf{q} = (x, y)^T \in \mathbb{R}^2$ for clarity; the result generalizes to $\mathbb{R}^n$ trivially.

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm:**

$$U_{att}(x, y) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \left[ (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \cdot 2(x - x_g) = \zeta (x - x_g)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \cdot 2(y - y_g) = \zeta (y - y_g)$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla U_{att} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \zeta (x - x_g) \\ \zeta (y - y_g) \end{bmatrix} = \zeta (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the negative sign to obtain the attractive force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\zeta (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}) = \zeta (\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q})$$

**Physical interpretation:** The attractive force is a **restoring linear spring force** with stiffness $\zeta$, always pointing from the robot toward the goal, with magnitude proportional to the distance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Force (FIRAS Function)

**Given:** The Khatib FIRAS repulsive potential is

$$U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, \quad \rho \le \rho_0$$

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative of $U_{rep}$ with respect to $\rho$:**

$$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d \rho} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \cdot 2 \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \right)$$

$$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d \rho} = -\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2}$$

**Step 2 — Use the chain rule $\nabla U_{rep} = \dfrac{d U_{rep}}{d \rho} \cdot \nabla \rho$:**

$$\nabla U_{rep} = -\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 3 — Apply the negative sign to obtain the repulsive force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 4 — Verify boundary behavior:**

- As $\rho \to \rho_0^-$: the bracket $\left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \to 0$, so $\mathbf{F}_{rep} \to \mathbf{0}$ — smooth handoff.
- As $\rho \to 0^+$ (collision): the bracket grows as $\tfrac{1}{\rho}$, and the $\tfrac{1}{\rho^2}$ factor contributes another blow-up, yielding $\mathbf{F}_{rep} \to \infty$ — a guaranteed safety barrier.

**Physical interpretation:** The repulsive force is a **nonlinear, distance-dependent push** that becomes negligible outside the influence radius and dominates near the obstacle boundary.

---

## 3.3 Complete Worked Numerical Example (2D, Single Obstacle)

**Problem Statement:**
A 2-DOF mobile robot is at configuration $\mathbf{q} = (2, 3)^T$ meters. The goal is at $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = (10, 8)^T$ m. A single circular obstacle is centered at $\mathbf{o} = (3, 4)^T$ m with radius $r = 0.5$ m. Use the following gains: $\zeta = 1.0$, $\eta = 5.0$, $\rho_0 = 2.0$ m. Compute the **total artificial force** at the current configuration.

**Step 1 — Compute the attractive force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\zeta (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}) = -1.0 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 10 \\ 3 - 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Euclidean distance to the obstacle surface:**

$$\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o} \vert = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho(\mathbf{q}) = \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o} \vert - r = 1.414 - 0.5 = 0.914 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Verify influence condition:**

Since $\rho = 0.914 \le \rho_0 = 2.0$, the obstacle is **active**.

**Step 4 — Compute the unit gradient direction $\nabla \rho$:**

$$\nabla \rho = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o}}{\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o} \vert} = \frac{1}{1.414}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the FIRAS bracket:**

$$\left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) = \frac{1}{0.914} - \frac{1}{2.0} = 1.094 - 0.5 = 0.594$$

**Step 6 — Compute the repulsive force magnitude factor:**

$$\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2} = 5.0 \cdot 0.594 \cdot \frac{1}{(0.914)^2} = 5.0 \cdot 0.594 \cdot 1.197 = 3.555$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by $\nabla \rho$ to get $\mathbf{F}_{rep}$:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 3.555 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.514 \\ -2.514 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Total force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -2.514 \\ -2.514 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.486 \\ 2.486 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 9 — Robot's next configuration (using $\alpha = 0.1$):**

$$\mathbf{q}_{t+1} = \mathbf{q}_t + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{F}_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5.486 \\ 2.486 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.549 \\ 3.249 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}$$

The robot has moved 0.549 m in $x$ and 0.249 m in $y$, deviating slightly upward-left from the straight-line goal direction to avoid the obstacle — exactly the expected reactive behavior.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Potential Variants

| Variant | Attractive Type | Repulsive Type | Convergence Guarantee | Local Minima Issue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Khatib (1986)** — Classical | Quadratic $U_{att}=\tfrac{1}{2}\zeta d^2$ | FIRAS reciprocal | Local only | **Yes** |
| **Koditschek-Rimon (1992)** — Navigation Function | NF on sphere world | $U_{rep}=\gamma/\rho^2$ | **Yes (NF)** | **No** (provably absent) |
| **Ge & Cui (2000)** — New APF | Distance-weighted | Modified FIRAS | Improved | Partially |
| **Random Walk Recovery** | — | — | Probabilistic | Resolved by stochasticity |
| **Harmonic Functions (Connolly)** | Laplace equation | Boundary-value | Global (numerical) | **No** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an APF Planner

This Mermaid block diagram captures the entire **sensing → planning → actuation** pipeline of a potential-field-based field robot, which is the closest safe representation we can offer without attempting an impossible physical freehand drawing inside a code block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Pose Sensor Encoder IMU GPS] --> B[Current Configuration q_t]
    C[LiDAR or Stereo Camera] --> D[Obstacle Map Construction]
    B --> E[Workspace State Vector]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Attractive Potential U_att]
    E --> G[Compute Repulsive Potential U_rep for each obstacle]
    F --> H[Superpose Total Potential U_total]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Take Negative Gradient gives Artificial Force F]
    I --> J[Gradient Descent Step Compute q_t_plus_1]
    J --> K{Goal Reached Within Tolerance epsilon}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> L[Stop and Signal Task Completion]
    M[Local Minima Detector] --> N{Stuck at Stationary Point}
    N -- Yes --> O[Activate Random Walk or Virtual Goal Escape]
    O --> B
    N -- No --> B
```

**Architecture Walkthrough:**

- The **Robot Pose Sensor** block provides odometric feedback (configuration $\mathbf{q}_t$).
- The **LiDAR/Stereo Camera** block builds a local obstacle map.
- The two parallel computation blocks compute $U_{att}$ and $U_{rep}$, then superpose them.
- The **Negative Gradient** block implements the Khatib force equation.
- A **Local Minima Detector** monitors for stationary points and triggers an **escape mechanism** (random walk or virtual subgoal), which is the standard practical fix for the famous local-minima problem of APF.

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map of the APF Method Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Potential Field Methods] --> P1[Classical APF Khatib 1986]
    P --> P2[Navigation Functions Koditschek Rimon 1992]
    P --> P3[Harmonic Potential Functions Connolly 1990]
    P --> P4[Extended APF with Virtual Obstacles]
    P1 --> I1[Strengths: Simple Real time Low compute]
    P1 --> I2[Weaknesses: Local minima Oscillations GNRON]
    P2 --> I3[Strengths: Provably free of local minima]
    P2 --> I4[Weaknesses: Requires sphere world topology]
    P3 --> I5[Strengths: Smooth global field]
    P3 --> I6[Weaknesses: Heavy numerical PDE solve]
    P4 --> I7[Strengths: Retains simplicity adds escape]
    P4 --> I8[Weaknesses: Heuristic parameter tuning]
    I2 --> R[Standard Recovery: Random Walk or Virtual Target]
    I6 --> R
    I8 --> R
    R --> P5[Hybrid APF plus Global Planner]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Define the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for robot path planning. State any two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by Khatib (1986), is a reactive path-planning technique in which the goal location is modeled as an attractive potential minimum and obstacles are modeled as repulsive potential peaks in the robot's configuration space. The robot moves along the negative gradient of the resulting scalar potential field.

**Advantages:**
1. Computationally inexpensive — only requires local gradient evaluation; suitable for real-time field robotics.
2. Naturally smooth, continuous trajectories are produced without explicit trajectory optimization.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Susceptible to **local minima** (the robot may freeze before reaching the goal).
2. Suffers from oscillations and the **GNRON** (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby) problem in narrow corridors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between the attractive and repulsive potentials used in the APF method. Write the standard Khatib forms for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Attractive Potential $U_{att}$ | Repulsive Potential $U_{rep}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Pulls the robot toward the goal. | Pushes the robot away from obstacles. |
| **Range** | Acts over the entire workspace (global). | Acts only within the influence radius $\rho_0$ (local). |
| **Form (Khatib)** | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert^2$ | $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$ |
| **Behavior near goal** | $\to 0$ (zero at goal). | Independent of goal; depends only on obstacles. |
| **Resulting force** | $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\zeta(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho^2}\nabla \rho$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Q-A]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the artificial force acting on a robot under the influence of an attractive quadratic potential $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta\vert\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{q}_{goal}\vert^2$ and the Khatib FIRAS repulsive potential. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a 2-D mobile robot at $\mathbf{q}=(2,1)^T$ with goal at $\mathbf{q}_{goal}=(6,5)^T$ and a single circular obstacle of radius $r=0.5$ m centered at $\mathbf{o}=(3,2)^T$, compute the total artificial force. Use $\zeta=1.0$, $\eta=4.0$, $\rho_0=2.0$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- **Attractive part (3 marks):** Differentiate $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta[(x-x_g)^2+(y-y_g)^2]$ with respect to $\mathbf{q}$, yielding $\nabla U_{att}=\zeta(\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{q}_{goal})$. Apply the negative sign to obtain $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\zeta(\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{q}_{goal})$.
- **Repulsive part (3 marks):** Differentiate $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho}-\tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ with respect to $\mathbf{q}$ using the chain rule, yielding $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho}-\tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho^2}\nabla \rho$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$, else $\mathbf{0}$.
- **Total force (1 mark):** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{total}$.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Attractive force (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -1.0 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2-6 \\ 1-5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Distance to obstacle center (1 mark):**

$$\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o} \vert = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho = 1.414 - 0.5 = 0.914 \text{ m} \quad (\le \rho_0 = 2.0 \text{ m, obstacle is active})$$

**Step 3 — Repulsive direction (1 mark):**

$$\nabla \rho = \frac{1}{1.414}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive force magnitude (2 marks):**

$$\eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} = 4.0 \cdot (1.094 - 0.5) \cdot \tfrac{1}{0.836} = 4.0 \cdot 0.594 \cdot 1.197 = 2.844$$

**Step 5 — Total force (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} + 2.844 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 - 2.011 \\ 4 - 2.011 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.989 \\ 1.989 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q-B]**
> **(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the **local minima problem** in the classical APF method. List three standard techniques used to overcome it. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A field robot is at $\mathbf{q}=(0,0)^T$ with goal at $\mathbf{q}_{goal}=(10,0)^T$ m. It must pass between two circular obstacles: $\mathcal{O}_1$ centered at $(4,1)$ with $r=0.6$ m, and $\mathcal{O}_2$ centered at $(4,-1)$ with $r=0.6$ m. Compute the total repulsive force when the robot is at the midpoint of the obstacle pair, $\mathbf{q}=(4,0)^T$. Use $\eta=3.0$, $\rho_0=2.0$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Local Minima Problem (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** A local minimum is a configuration $\mathbf{q}_{LM} \neq \mathbf{q}_{goal}$ where $\nabla U(\mathbf{q}_{LM}) = \mathbf{0}$. At such a point, the artificial force vanishes, and the robot stops prematurely.
- **Two canonical scenarios (3 marks):**
  1. **Aligned obstacles and goal:** The repulsive force from an obstacle directly opposite the goal exactly cancels the attractive force.
  2. **Trap configuration:** A concave obstacle arrangement encloses a region where the net force is zero.
- **Three recovery techniques (2 marks):**
  1. **Random Walk / Stochastic Escape:** Inject random motion when $\vert \mathbf{F} \vert < \delta$.
  2. **Virtual Subgoal Injection:** Create a temporary intermediate goal to "nudge" the robot out of the trap.
  3. **Navigation Functions (Koditschek–Rimon):** Use a mathematically proven globally convergent potential.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Distance to $\mathcal{O}_1$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o}_1 \vert = \sqrt{(4-4)^2 + (0-1)^2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_1 = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ m} \quad (\le \rho_0 = 2.0, \text{ active})$$

**Step 2 — Distance to $\mathcal{O}_2$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{o}_2 \vert = \sqrt{(4-4)^2 + (0+1)^2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_2 = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ m} \quad (\le \rho_0 = 2.0, \text{ active})$$

**Step 3 — FIRAS bracket (1 mark):**

$$\left(\tfrac{1}{0.4} - \tfrac{1}{2.0}\right) = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive force from $\mathcal{O}_1$ (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep,1} = 3.0 \cdot 2.0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{0.16} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = 37.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -37.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Repulsive force from $\mathcal{O}_2$ (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep,2} = 3.0 \cdot 2.0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{0.16} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ +1 \end{bmatrix} = 37.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ +1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 37.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Total repulsive force (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep,total} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -37.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 37.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Critical Insight:** The repulsive forces from the two symmetrically placed obstacles **cancel exactly** at the midpoint. This is a **classic demonstration of the local-minima / trap situation** — the robot would be stuck at $\mathbf{q}=(4,0)$ if this were the only force considered, even though the goal lies clearly ahead on the $x$-axis. This numerically justifies the need for escape mechanisms or Navigation Functions in field robotics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the influence condition:** A frequent error is to compute $U_{rep}$ for $\rho > \rho_0$, yielding non-zero fictitious forces far from obstacles. **Always check $\rho \le \rho_0$ first.** (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Sign error in gradient:** Many students write $\mathbf{F} = +\nabla U$ instead of $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$. The robot must **descend** the potential — it moves opposite to the gradient. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Using obstacle center distance instead of surface distance:** $\rho$ must be the distance to the obstacle **boundary**, not to its center. Subtract the radius $r$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Forgetting to list advantages/disadvantages in 3-mark answers:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for advantages, 1 mark for disadvantages — no partial credit beyond this structure.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** State "N" or "m" for every numerical answer. KTU scripts lose up to 0.5 marks for unit omission in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **In derivation sub-parts, writing the answer without intermediate steps:** The 7-mark sub-parts require **explicit gradient calculations**, not just final formulas. Always show $\partial/\partial x$, $\partial/\partial y$ separately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **APF = Reactive + Continuous-State-Space search** — a gradient-descent method on a scalar field.
- **Two potentials:** Quadratic attractive + FIRAS repulsive.
- **The motion law** is $\mathbf{q}_{t+1} = \mathbf{q}_t + \alpha \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}_t)$ with $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$.
- **Attractive force** is a linear spring force: $\mathbf{F}_{att} = \zeta(\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q})$.
- **Repulsive force** is active **only within $\rho_0$** and tends to infinity as $\rho \to 0$.
- **The influence radius $\rho_0$** is a critical tuning parameter — too small = collisions; too large = local minima in cluttered environments.
- **Local minima are the #1 weakness** of APF; they are addressed by Random Walks, Virtual Subgoals, or Navigation Functions.
- **GNRON** (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby) occurs when the goal is adjacent to an obstacle and the repulsive force dominates near the goal.
- **Conic vs. parabolic** attractive potentials: conic gives constant magnitude far from the goal, parabolic gives distance-proportional force (gentler near goal).
- **Koditschek–Rimon Navigation Functions** are the only APF variant that is **provably free of local minima**, but they require a "sphere world" topology.
- **For KTU exams:** always show the gradient calculation, verify the influence condition, and include units in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Field Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Field Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, grid-based, reactive obstacle-avoidance algorithm developed for mobile robot navigation by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)**. It transforms a multi-dimensional Cartesian **occupancy (certainty) grid** into a one-dimensional **polar histogram (H)** — a 360° angular density profile around the robot. The robot then selects a safe steering direction by locating angular "valleys" in this histogram that lie closest to the target heading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRAT601 Module 3):**
> *VFH is a feedback control law that combines the statistical confidence of obstacle occupancy with a sector-wise polar density reduction, allowing a differentially driven robot to navigate dense, unstructured environments at high update rates (typically 10–20 Hz).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Driver in Fog"

Imagine you are driving a car at night in dense fog. You cannot see far, but you have a *360° proximity sensor array* telling you "how close" an object is in each direction. Your brain instantly converts this into a **mental map of "denser" and "thinner" regions** around the car. You then steer toward the "thinnest" gap that points roughly toward your destination.

- The **fog** = the robot's limited sonar / lidar range.
- The **proximity readings** = certainty grid occupancy values.
- The **mental density map** = the **Polar Histogram** (H).
- The **thinnest gap** = the chosen **Valley** (steering direction).

### 1.3 Core Quantitative Metrics

The following KTU-mandated parameters define a VFH system:

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| $r_l$ | Look-ahead radius (active window) | **2 – 5 m** |
| $a$ | Robot's effective radius | **0.3 – 0.7 m** |
| $W$ | Robot's physical width | **0.5 – 1.0 m** |
| $d_{max}$ | Maximum sensor range | **5.0 m** |
| $N$ | Number of polar bins (sectors) | **72** (i.e., 5°/bin) |
| $\tau$ | Dynamic obstacle-density threshold | **0.1 – 0.6** |
| $k_v$ | Speed–threshold gain | **0.1 – 0.4** |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 scheme**, the *active region* $r_l + a$ is the single most important geometric quantity — it determines which grid cells actually contribute to the histogram, filtering out far-field noise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage VFH Data Reduction Pipeline

VFH is fundamentally a **data reduction** technique. Raw sensor returns from a dense certainty grid (often thousands of cells) are compressed, in three stages, into a single steering command.

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Certainty Grid (C):**
- Each cell $C_{i,j}$ holds an integer **0 – 15** representing confidence that the cell is occupied.
- Updated incrementally from sonar / lidar / stereo vision.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram (H):**
- The grid is reduced to an array of $N$ angular sectors.
- Each bin $H_k$ (where $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$) represents the *obstacle density* in the angular sector centered on angle:

$$\theta_k = \frac{2\pi(k-1)}{N}$$

**Stage 3 — Output (Steering Command):**
- A cost function is minimized over candidate valleys to yield the wheel-velocity vector $(v, \omega)$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Acquire sensor readings** and update the certainty grid $C_{i,j}$.
2. **Compute the active region** — only cells with $d \le (r_l + a)$ participate, where $d$ is the Euclidean distance from the robot to the cell.
3. **Determine the angular span** $\gamma_{i,j}$ of each cell's contribution:

$$\gamma_{i,j} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a}{d_{i,j}}\right) \cdot 2$$

4. **Project each cell onto its angular sector(s)** by computing $\beta_{i,j}$, the angle from the robot's heading axis to the cell's center:

$$\beta_{i,j} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y_j - y_r}{x_i - x_r}\right)$$

5. **Accumulate the polar density** for every sector $k$:

$$H_k = \sum_{i,j} C_{i,j} \cdot \delta\!\left(\beta_{i,j}, k\right)$$

where the gating function $\delta(\cdot,k)$ returns 1 if the cell $C_{i,j}$ projects onto sector $k$ within the angular half-width $\gamma_{i,j}/2$.

6. **Apply the Smoothing Pass** to attenuate noise spikes. A 3-tap moving average is used:

$$\hat{H}_k = \frac{H_{k-1} + 2H_k + H_{k+1}}{4}$$

7. **Threshold the histogram** with a dynamic cutoff:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + k_v \cdot v_{current}$$

Any sector with $H_k > \tau$ is marked **OBSTRUCTED**; the remaining continuous arcs are called **candidate valleys**.

8. **Select the best valley** using the cost function $g$ in §2.3.

9. **Output** the steering angle $\theta_{steer}$ to the low-level motor controller.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in the table below are the **exact symbols expected in the KTU University Exam answer book.** No vertical bars `|` are used inside table cells to prevent markdown breakage.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_k = \sum_{i,j} C_{i,j} \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_{ij}$ | Polar density in sector $k$ | unitless |
| 2 | $a_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & d_{ij} \le r_l + a \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Active-region gate | binary |
| 3 | $b_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \vert \beta_{ij} - \theta_k \vert \le \gamma_{ij}/2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Angular-sector gate | binary |
| 4 | $\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{d_{ij}}\right)$ | Angular spread of cell $(i,j)$ | radians |
| 5 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i-x_r)^2 + (y_j-y_r)^2}$ | Range from robot to cell | metres |
| 6 | $\tau = \tau_0 + k_v v$ | Dynamic density threshold | unitless |
| 7 | $g(k) = \mu_1 \Delta(k) + \mu_2 \vert k - k_t \vert$ | Candidate-valley cost (VFH*) | unitless |
| 8 | $v_{cmd} = v_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta_{steer}}{\Delta_{max}}\right)$ | Forward speed from steer error | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega_{cmd} = K_p \cdot (\theta_{steer} - \theta_{curr})$ | Angular velocity (P-control) | rad/s |
| 10 | $\beta_{ij} = \arctan2\!\left(y_j - y_r, x_i - x_r\right)$ | Bearing to cell (4-quadrant) | radians |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

VFH (and its successor **VFH\***, by Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998) is the de-facto standard for:
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) fleets** in warehouses (KIVA/Amazon Robotics uses a descendant of VFH).
- **Autonomous wheelchairs** in cluttered hospital corridors.
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA Mars rovers use VFH-class algorithms for near-field hazard avoidance).
- **Unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs)** for military reconnaissance in GPS-denied zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is VFH preferred over a pure Artificial Potential Field (APF) for cluttered environments?"* — Because VFH **eliminates local minima traps** by performing a one-shot 1D search over candidate valleys, whereas APF can get stuck in saddle points between two close obstacles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why is the Cell's Angular Spread $\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan(a/d_{ij})$?
