**Setup:** Consider a single occupied certainty cell located at distance $d$ from the robot's center, at an effective perpendicular offset equal to the robot's **safety radius $a$**. Geometrically, the cell "appears" to the robot as an angular wedge whose half-angle is subtended by the arc of radius $a$ at distance $d$.

**Step 1.** Draw a right-triangle with hypotenuse $d$ and opposite side $a$. The half-angle $\phi$ satisfies:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{a}{d}$$

**Step 2.** For the small-angle range typical of VFH ($a \ll d$, i.e., $a/d \le 0.3$), the small-angle approximation $\sin\phi \approx \tan\phi$ is justified:

$$\phi \approx \arctan\!\left(\frac{a}{d}\right)$$

**Step 3.** The **full angular spread** of the cell as observed from the robot is the sum of both half-angles (left and right of the cell's line of sight):

$$\gamma_{ij} = 2\phi = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{a}{d_{ij}}\right)$$

This is **Equation 4** in the formula sheet. The result is intuitive: as $d \to \infty$, $\gamma \to 0$ (a far-away cell subtends no angle), and as $d \to a$, $\gamma \to \pi$ (the cell is "surrounding" the robot — the worst case).

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{a}{d_{ij}}\right)\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Smoothing Kernel and Smoothing Pass

**Setup:** Raw $H_k$ values exhibit sharp peaks when a single range reading dominates a sector. Borenstein's original paper applied a **3-tap triangular kernel** to combat this.

**Step 1.** Define a discrete-time smoothing operator $\mathcal{S}$ acting on $H$:

$$\hat{H}_k = \mathcal{S} \cdot H_k = \frac{1}{4}\!\left(H_{k-1} + 2H_k + H_{k+1}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Verify the kernel is normalized (sum of weights = 1):

$$\frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3.** Boundary handling — at $k=1$ and $k=N$ the kernel wraps around (since the polar histogram is cyclic):

$$\hat{H}_1 = \frac{H_N + 2H_1 + H_2}{4}, \qquad \hat{H}_N = \frac{H_{N-1} + 2H_N + H_1}{4}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — The VFH\* Cost Function

**Setup:** With $M$ candidate valleys identified after thresholding, VFH\* picks the *best* one using a weighted linear combination of (a) proximity to target heading, and (b) proximity to the previously selected heading for **motion continuity**.

**Step 1.** Let $k$ index a candidate valley's angular sector, $k_t$ be the target sector, and $k_{prev}$ the previously selected sector.

**Step 2.** Define the two candidate metrics:

$$\Delta_1(k) = \min_{m \in M} \vert k - k_t \vert_m, \qquad \Delta_2(k) = \vert k - k_{prev} \vert$$

where $\vert\cdot\vert_m$ denotes the **modular angular distance** (i.e., wrap-around handled on the unit circle).

**Step 3.** The total cost for sector $k$ is:

$$g(k) = \mu_1 \cdot \Delta_1(k) + \mu_2 \cdot \Delta_2(k) + \mu_3 \cdot \frac{1}{d_k + \epsilon}$$

where $d_k$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle in valley $k$ and $\epsilon$ is a small regularization constant to avoid division by zero.

**Step 4.** The chosen sector:

$$\boxed{\;k^* = \arg\min_{k} g(k)\;}$$

The steering angle is then:

$$\theta_{steer} = \frac{2\pi\,(k^* - 1)}{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **VFH\*** improvement (Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998) is essential for KTU Module 3 — make sure to cite both the *original VFH (1991)* and the *enhanced VFH\** paper in your answer.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

A KTU 2024 style 7-mark analytical sub-question is reproduced below.

> **Problem:** A mobile robot has $a = 0.5$ m, $r_l = 2$ m, and an occupied cell at $(x,y) = (1.5, 1.0)$ relative to the robot at the origin. The certainty value $C_{ij} = 8$. Compute (a) the range $d_{ij}$, (b) the angular spread $\gamma_{ij}$, and (c) the number of 5°-sectors this cell contributes to.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Range from robot (0,0) to cell (1.5, 1.0):

$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{1.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{3.25} \approx 1.803 \text{ m}$$

**Part (b):** Angular spread:

$$\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1.803}\right) = 2\arctan(0.2773) = 2(0.2704) = 0.5408 \text{ rad}$$

**Part (c):** Number of 5° bins = 0.5408 rad ÷ (5° in rad):

$$N_{bins} = \frac{0.5408}{5 \cdot \pi/180} = \frac{0.5408}{0.08727} \approx 6.20$$

The cell contributes to **6 contiguous 5° sectors**, starting at $\beta_{ij} = \arctan2(1.0, 1.5) = 33.69°$.

$$\beta_{ij} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.0}{1.5}\right) = 33.69°$$

**Sectors covered: 32°, 33°, 34°, 35°, 36°, 37° (approximately).**

> [!NOTE]
> This exact 3-part structure is a **classic KTU 2024 Part B sub-question** pattern. Practising it in advance saves 4–5 minutes in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VFH Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Array Sonar Lidar Stereo] --> B[Cartesian Certainty Grid Cij]
    B --> C[Active Region Filter aij]
    C --> D[Angular Projection Bij]
    D --> E[Polar Histogram Accumulation Hk]
    E --> F[Smoothing 3-tap Kernel]
    F --> G[Dynamic Threshold tau v]
    G --> H[Valley Detection]
    H --> I[Cost Function Evaluation g k]
    I --> J[Steering Angle theta steer]
    J --> K[Velocity Controller v omega]
    K --> A
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2c4f7c,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#3b5f8e,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#5b3f7c,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#7c2f3a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 VFH Algorithmic Flow (State-Machine View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Start Init Grid] --> S1[Read Sensors]
    S1 --> S2[Update Certainty Grid Cij]
    S2 --> S3[Compute dij gamma ij]
    S3 --> S4[Project to Polar Bins Hk]
    S4 --> S5[Smooth Hk]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Threshold tau]
    S6 --> S7{Valley Found}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Min Cost Function g k]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Stop or Rotate]
    S8 --> S10[Output theta steer v omega]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S1
    style S7 fill:#7c5a2f,color:#ffffff
    style S10 fill:#2f7c4a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Polar Histogram Visual Schematic (Textual Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Polar_360[Polar Histogram Around Robot]
        direction LR
        S0["0 to 30 deg\nDensity LOW\nFREE"] --> S1["30 to 60 deg\nDensity HIGH\nOBSTACLE"]
        S1 --> S2["60 to 180 deg\nDensity LOW\nVALLEY"]
        S2 --> S3["180 to 270 deg\nDensity MID\nFREE"]
        S3 --> S4["270 to 360 deg\nDensity LOW\nFREE"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#7c2f3a,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#2f7c4a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 KTU 2024 Examiner's Sketch Interpretation (Active Region)

> [!NOTE]
> *In the KTU answer book, draw the following components on a single A4 page in the following order:* **(1)** robot as a circle of radius $a$ at the origin; **(2)** dashed circle of radius $r_l + a$ defining the active region; **(3)** three or four occupied cells inside the active region shaded solid; **(4)** dotted radial lines showing each cell's angular spread $\gamma_{ij}$; **(5)** a small bar-chart sector showing $H_k$ values around the perimeter.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
*Define the term "Vector Field Histogram" as used in mobile robot obstacle avoidance. Mention the role of the polar histogram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time obstacle-avoidance algorithm that compresses a 2D Cartesian certainty grid into a 1D **polar histogram** representing the angular density of obstacles around the robot. **(1.5 marks)**
The polar histogram partitions the 360° field of view around the robot into $N$ angular sectors, each storing an obstacle-density value $H_k$. The robot then identifies "valleys" (low-density sectors) and steers into the one closest to the target heading. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
*What is the significance of the "active region" in the VFH algorithm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **active region** is the annular ring of radius $r_l + a$ centred on the robot. **(1 mark)**
Only certainty-grid cells lying within this ring are projected onto the polar histogram. **(1 mark)**
This filters out far-field noise, reduces computational load, and ensures the robot reacts only to obstacles that are *currently* relevant for its motion-decision horizon. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure of the Vector Field Histogram algorithm for obstacle avoidance in a mobile robot. Explain the role of the certainty grid and the polar histogram in your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the angular spread $\gamma_{ij}$ of a certainty grid cell at range $d_{ij}$ from the robot. Show all geometric steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The VFH algorithm operates in three stages:

**Step 1 — Certainty Grid Update (1.5 marks):**
The certainty grid $C_{i,j}$ stores integer confidence values (0–15) for each cell, updated on each sensor reading. A value of 0 means "known free" and 15 means "known occupied."

**Step 2 — Active Region Filter (1.5 marks):**
Only cells with $d_{ij} \le r_l + a$ are considered for histogram projection, where $r_l$ is the look-ahead radius and $a$ is the safety radius.

**Step 3 — Polar Histogram Construction (2 marks):**
Each qualifying cell $C_{i,j}$ contributes its certainty value to every polar bin $k$ whose centre angle $\theta_k$ lies within $\gamma_{ij}/2$ of the cell's bearing $\beta_{ij}$, using the gating functions $a_{ij}$ and $b_{ij}$.

**Step 4 — Smoothing and Thresholding (1 mark):**
A 3-tap smoothing pass is applied. A dynamic threshold $\tau = \tau_0 + k_v v$ is then used to mark "obstructed" bins. Continuous "free" arcs are identified as candidate valleys.

**Step 5 — Valley Selection and Output (1 mark):**
The valley closest to the target heading is chosen, and the corresponding steering angle $\theta_{steer}$ is sent to the low-level controller.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Refer to the derivation in §3.1 above. Steps:

1. Draw the right-triangle with hypotenuse $d_{ij}$ and opposite side $a$. (1 mark)
2. Apply $\sin\phi = a/d_{ij}$. (1 mark)
3. Use small-angle approximation $\sin\phi \approx \tan\phi$. (1 mark)
4. Solve for $\phi = \arctan(a/d_{ij})$. (2 marks)
5. Double for full angular spread: $\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan(a/d_{ij})$. (2 marks)

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the VFH algorithm. Discuss the role of the smoothing pass and the dynamic threshold. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot has safety radius $a = 0.4$ m and an active-region radius $r_l = 1.8$ m. An obstacle cell lies at $(2.0, 0.5)$ relative to the robot with certainty value $C_{ij} = 10$. Compute the range, the angular spread, and the number of 5° polar bins this cell influences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- Diagram (3 marks): show the three-stage data flow: Grid → Polar → Steering.
- Smoothing (2 marks): 3-tap kernel $\hat{H}_k = (H_{k-1} + 2H_k + H_{k+1})/4$ to attenuate noise.
- Dynamic threshold (2 marks): $\tau = \tau_0 + k_v v$ — higher speed ⇒ stricter threshold.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Range (2 marks):**

$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{2.0^2 + 0.5^2} = \sqrt{4.25} \approx 2.062 \text{ m}$$

**Angular spread (3 marks):**

$$\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.4}{2.062}\right) = 2\arctan(0.1940) = 2(0.1920) = 0.3840 \text{ rad}$$

**Number of 5° bins (2 marks):**

$$N_{bins} = \frac{0.3840}{0.08727} \approx 4.40 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4 \text{ bins}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on VFH questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety radius `a` in the active region** — many students write $r_l$ alone. Always add $a$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Mixing up `arctan` vs `arctan2`** — for bearing computation, **always** use the 4-quadrant `arctan2(y, x)` to avoid sign errors in the 3rd/4th quadrants. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the smoothing step** — the original 1991 VFH includes smoothing; omitting it costs full marks on algorithm-trace questions. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Citing VFH without naming VFH\*** — KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists VFH\* as an advanced variant. Always mention it.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer — always write `m`, `rad`, `rad/s` next to every numerical value.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **VFH** is a **real-time, reactive, grid-based** obstacle-avoidance algorithm by **Borenstein & Koren (1991)**.
- The algorithm is a **3-stage data-compression pipeline**: Cartesian Grid → Polar Histogram → Steering Angle.
- The **active region** is $r_l + a$ — only cells inside this ring contribute to $H_k$.
- Each cell's **angular spread** is $\gamma_{ij} = 2\arctan(a/d_{ij})$.
- The **polar density** in bin $k$ is $H_k = \sum C_{ij} a_{ij} b_{ij}$.
- **Smoothing** uses the 3-tap kernel $\hat{H}_k = (H_{k-1} + 2H_k + H_{k+1})/4$.
- The **dynamic threshold** is $\tau = \tau_0 + k_v v$ — speed-dependent.
- The robot steers into the **valley** (continuous low-density arc) closest to the target.
- **VFH\*** (Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998) adds a cost function $g(k)$ to handle the *motion-continuity* constraint and considers target-distance, not just target-direction.
- **VFH vs APF:** VFH avoids local minima; APF can get stuck between two close obstacles.
- Standard **polar bin count** in KTU questions is $N = 72$ (5° per bin).
- Use **`arctan2(y, x)`** — never `arctan(y/x)` — for bearing computation.
- The **steering command** consists of linear velocity $v_{cmd}$ and angular velocity $\omega_{cmd} = K_p(\theta_{steer} - \theta_{curr})$.
- Remember: VFH is **kinematic-level guidance**, *not* a global planner — it is paired with an A\* or D\* planner in a layered architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bug 1, Bug 2 and Tangent Bug Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Bug 1, Bug 2 and Tangent Bug Algorithms ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Bug Algorithms** are a family of *reactive, sensor-based, local path planning algorithms* designed for a mobile robot operating in a *fully or partially unknown planar workspace* containing stationary (static) polygonal or curved obstacles. The robot begins at a known start configuration $q_{start}$, knows the goal configuration $q_{goal}$, and must terminate at $q_{goal}$ using only on-board sensing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"A Bug Algorithm is a complete, reactive navigation strategy in which a point or disc-shaped robot alternates between two primitive behaviours — **motion-to-goal** and **boundary-following** — using only local sensing, to reach a specified goal in an unknown 2-D obstacle field."* The three foundational algorithms prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are: | # | Algorithm | Sensor Model | Key Distinguishing Rule | |---|-----------|--------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Bug 1** | Contact / Tactile | Always circumnavigates the entire obstacle | | 2 | **Bug 2** | Contact / Tactile | Leaves obstacle at the first opportunity on the m-line | | 3 | **Tangent Bug** | Range (e.g., LIDAR/Sonar) | Uses local tangent graph for smoother, optimal-ish paths | ### 1.2 The Two Behavioural Primitives All three algorithms are built on **two atomic motion primitives** (Verwer, 1990 — formalised by Lumelsky & Stepanov): $$ \text{Primitive}_1: \; \text{MoveTowardGoal} \quad\quad \text{Primitive}_2: \; \text{FollowBoundary} $$ - **Motion-to-Goal (Go-to-Goal):** The robot's heading vector points directly from its current position $q$ to the goal $q_{goal}$, i.e. along the **m-line** (the straight line connecting start to goal). - **Boundary-Following (Wall-Following):** The robot tracks the perimeter of the obstacle at a fixed stand-off distance, keeping the obstacle surface on its left (or right) hand. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Students (Plain English):** > Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in a forest containing circular lakes. You know exactly *where* your campsite is (the goal), but you cannot see. You have a long stick. > > - **Bug 1** = You walk straight toward the camp. If your stick touches a lake, you walk **all the way around** the entire lake to find the point on the shore that is *physically closest* to the camp, then resume straight-line walking. Safe, but you walk twice the perimeter every time. > - **Bug 2** = Same start, but you walk around the lake *only* until you cross the original straight line again **and you are now closer to the camp than when you first hit the lake**. Much faster. > - **Tangent Bug** = Now you wear a headlamp (range sensor). You can see edges of the lake. You continuously pick the direction that locally best trades off "go to camp" vs "go around the lake smoothly" — like skirting a puddle on a wet road. ### 1.4 Standard Notations Used Throughout - $q_{start}$ — Start configuration (start point) - $q_{goal}$ — Goal configuration (target point) - $q_{hit}$ — **Hit Point**: the first point of contact with an obstacle - $q_{leave}$ — **Leave Point**: the point on the boundary where the robot resumes motion-to-goal - $L_i$ — Perimeter length of the $i$-th obstacle - $d$ — Straight-line distance from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ (i.e. $d = \vert q_{start} - q_{goal} \vert$) - $n$ — Total number of obstacles in the workspace - $d_{\min}$ — Minimum distance from any obstacle boundary to $q_{goal}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operating Assumptions (Common to All Three) For the algorithms to be **complete** (i.e. guaranteed to find the goal if a path exists), the following KTU-standard assumptions must hold: 1. The workspace $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^2$ is planar. 2. Obstacles are **static** (immobile) and **bounded** (finite extent). 3. The robot is modelled as a **point** (or, equivalently, obstacles are grown by the robot's radius $r$ via Minkowski sum). 4. The robot is **holonomic** — it can move in any direction without turning constraints. 5. Sensors are idealised (perfect contact, or perfect range measurement with bounded error $\varepsilon$). 6. The goal is **reachable** in a topological sense (path exists in free space $\mathcal{W}_{free}$). ### 2.2 Bug 1 Algorithm — Theoretical Decomposition **Pseudocode Logic (Bulleted Flow):** - **State 1 — Go-to-Goal Mode:** - While no obstacle is detected along the m-line, translate the robot directly toward $q_{goal}$. - If the goal is reached, **terminate with success**. - **State 2 — Boundary-Following Mode (Circumnavigate):** - When an obstacle is contacted, mark the contact point as $q_{hit}$. - Record the Euclidean distance $d_{hit} = \vert q_{hit} - q_{goal} \vert$ from the hit point to the goal. - Begin a **full circumnavigation** of the obstacle (left-hand rule) — keep walking along the boundary. - During the circumnavigation, **continuously record the point that is closest to the goal**, call it $q_{min}$, with $d_{min} = \vert q_{min} - q_{goal} \vert$. - When the robot returns to $q_{hit}$ (full loop completed), terminate the circumnavigation at $q_{min}$. - **Loop:** Switch back to State 1, repeating the cycle for each newly encountered obstacle. > [!NOTE] > **Why circumnavigate the whole obstacle?** > Because Bug 1 has **no memory of geometry** — it cannot know whether the closest point to the goal is "ahead" of the current position along the boundary or "behind" it. A full loop is the brute-force way to guarantee identification of the optimal leave point on the boundary. **Worst-Case Path Bound for Bug 1:** $$ \boxed{\;L_{Bug1} \;\leq\; n \cdot d \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i\;} $$ ### 2.3 Bug 2 Algorithm — Theoretical Decomposition **Pseudocode Logic (Bulleted Flow):** - **State 1 — Go-to-Goal Mode:** - Move toward $q_{goal}$ along the m-line. If obstacle hit, mark $q_{hit}$ and switch to State 2. - **State 2 — Boundary-Following Mode (with m-line exit test):** - Walk along the obstacle boundary using a fixed handedness (e.g., always keep the obstacle on the left). - Maintain a continuous **exit test**: at every infinitesimal step $q$ on the boundary, check two conditions: 1. Is $q$ on the **m-line** (i.e. collinear with $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$)? 2. Is $\vert q - q_{goal} \vert \;<\; \vert q_{hit} - q_{goal} \vert$? - If **both** conditions are satisfied, designate $q$ as $q_{leave}$ and resume motion-to-goal. - If the robot returns to $q_{hit}$ without finding a valid $q_{leave}$, the **goal is unreachable** — terminate with failure. - **Loop:** Repeat indefinitely, or until goal reached / failure declared. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bug 2's clever optimisation:** It exploits the geometry of the m-line itself as a "memory device" — by demanding that the leave point lies on the m-line *and* is closer to the goal, it ensures the algorithm makes **monotonic progress** toward $q_{goal}$ along the straight line. **Worst-Case Path Bound for Bug 2:** $$ \boxed{\;L_{Bug2} \;\leq\; n \cdot d \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i\;} $$ *(The bound is the same as Bug 1, but in practice Bug 2 takes shorter paths because it does not always circumnavigate fully.)* ### 2.4 Tangent Bug Algorithm — Theoretical Decomposition The **Tangent Bug** is the only one of the three that uses **range sensing** (e.g., a 360° LIDAR or sonar ring). This unlocks substantially better performance. **Core Data Structure — The Local Tangent Graph (LTG):** At every instant, the robot's range scan produces a set of *discontinuities* in range readings — these correspond to obstacle edges (corners). The robot constructs a **local tangent graph**: a set of edges connecting the robot to the visible obstacle corners, with two tangents emanating from the robot to each obstacle. - For each obstacle $O_k$ visible to the range sensor, two tangents $T_{k,1}$ and $T_{k,2}$ are computed. - The set of all such tangent directions forms a **discrete set of candidate headings**. **Two Sub-Behaviour Modes:** - **Motion-to-Goal Mode:** - If no obstacle is within the sensor range $R$, move directly toward $q_{goal}$. - If obstacles are detected, evaluate each candidate heading $\theta$ on the boundary using the **heuristic $h(q, \theta)$**: $$ h(q, \theta) \;=\; d(q, q_{goal}) \;-\; d\big(q_{follow}(\theta),\, q_{goal}\big) $$ where $d(q, q_{goal})$ is the current distance to goal, and $d(q_{follow}, q_{goal})$ is the predicted distance to goal if the robot were to follow that boundary direction next. Choose the heading that **maximises** $h$ (i.e. the steepest descent toward the goal). - **Critical distinction from Bug 1/2:** if the heuristic is *maximised by* the "go to goal" direction, the robot **never** engages boundary-following. - **Boundary-Following Mode:** - Engaged when **every** candidate tangent direction would *increase* the distance to the goal. - The robot follows the obstacle contour at the **minimum range distance** (i.e. grazes the obstacle as closely as the sensor/kinematics allow). - While following, it continually re-evaluates the heuristic to detect when a "go to goal" direction becomes viable again. - A **local minimum** is declared if, even after a full rotation of candidate directions, no progress can be made. **Tangent Bug Path Bound (KTU High-Yield Result):** $$ \boxed{\;L_{TangentBug} \;\leq\; d \;+\; \varepsilon \cdot n\;} $$ where $\varepsilon$ is the maximum range-sensing error, and $n$ is the number of obstacles. This is **asymptotically optimal** as $\varepsilon \to 0$. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** Memorise the rightmost column — these are the only three formulas typically asked in Part A and Part B derivations. | # | Algorithm | Sensor Type | Termination Condition | Worst-Case Path Length | Competitive Ratio | Completeness | |---|-----------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------|--------------| | 1 | **Bug 1** | Contact | Reach $q_{goal}$ | $L \leq n d + \sum L_i$ | Path-length-optimal (per-obstacle) | Complete | | 2 | **Bug 2** | Contact | Reach $q_{goal}$ or return to $q_{hit}$ | $L \leq n d + \sum L_i$ | Better than Bug 1 in practice | Complete | | 3 | **Tangent Bug** | Range (LIDAR/Sonar) | Reach $q_{goal}$ or local minimum | $L \leq d + \varepsilon n$ | Approaches **1.0** (optimal) | Complete | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Many students confuse the path bound $L \leq n d + \sum L_i$ with the *competitive ratio*. They are **different**. The path bound is an absolute worst-case length; the competitive ratio compares the robot's path to the optimal offline path. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bug 1 & 2:** Foundational benchmarks used in academia to validate new reactive planners; rarely deployed as-is in industry due to the zigzag behaviour. - **Tangent Bug:** Closely related to deployed algorithms in domestic **robot vacuum cleaners** (Roomba's wall-following), **warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots), and **agricultural field robots** that navigate between crop rows. The range-sensing model maps directly to 2D LIDARs costing under $300 today. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Bug 1 on a Workspace with 2 Obstacles **Setup:** - Start $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ - Goal $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$ - Obstacle 1: circle centred at $(3, 0)$ with radius $1.5$ - Obstacle 2: circle centred at $(7, 0)$ with radius $1.0$ **Step 1: Initial straight-line distance to goal.** $$ d = \sqrt{(10-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 10 $$ **Step 2: Robot moves along positive x-axis. At $q_{hit,1} = (1.5, 0)$, it strikes Obstacle 1.** $$ d_{hit,1} = \sqrt{(10-1.5)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 8.5 $$ **Step 3: Robot begins full circumnavigation of Obstacle 1 (perimeter $L_1 = 2\pi \cdot 1.5 = 3\pi$).** **Step 4: During circumnavigation, the closest point to goal on Obstacle 1's boundary is on the +x side, at $q_{min,1} = (4.5, 0)$ (the rightmost point of the circle).** $$ d_{min,1} = \sqrt{(10-4.5)^2 + 0^2} = 5.5 $$ **Step 5: Robot returns to $q_{hit,1} = (1.5, 0)$ and leaves Obstacle 1 at $q_{leave,1} = q_{min,1} = (4.5, 0)$.** **Step 6: Resume motion-to-goal. At $q_{hit,2} = (6.0, 0)$, robot strikes Obstacle 2.** $$ d_{hit,2} = \sqrt{(10-6.0)^2 + 0^2} = 4.0 $$ **Step 7: Circumnavigate Obstacle 2 (perimeter $L_2 = 2\pi \cdot 1.0 = 2\pi$). Closest boundary point to goal is $q_{min,2} = (8.0, 0)$.** $$ d_{min,2} = \sqrt{(10-8.0)^2 + 0^2} = 2.0 $$ **Step 8: Return to $q_{hit,2} = (6.0, 0)$, then leave at $q_{leave,2} = (8.0, 0)$.** **Step 9: Final motion to goal: distance $= 10 - 8.0 = 2.0$. Goal reached. Termination.** **Total Path Length (Bug 1):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{Bug1} &= \underbrace{1.5}_{\text{Start to }q_{hit,1}} \;+\; \underbrace{2\pi(1.5)}_{L_1,\;\text{full circumnav}} \;+\; \underbrace{(4.5-1.5)}_{\text{Bug 1 returns to }q_{hit},\text{ leaves at }q_{min}} \;+\; \underbrace{(6.0-4.5)}_{q_{leave,1} \to q_{hit,2}} \\ &\quad +\; \underbrace{2\pi(1.0)}_{L_2,\;\text{full circumnav}} \;+\; \underbrace{(8.0-6.0)}_{\text{Back to }q_{hit,2}} \;+\; \underbrace{2.0}_{q_{leave,2}\to\text{Goal}} \\[4pt] &= 1.5 + 3\pi + 3.0 + 1.5 + 2\pi + 2.0 + 2.0 \\ &= 10.0 + 5\pi \;\approx\; 25.71 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Bug 2 on the Same Workspace **Step 1 — Step 3:** Identical to Bug 1 up to and including starting the boundary-follow of Obstacle 1. **Step 4 (Bug 2 differs!):** Robot follows Obstacle 1's boundary (left-hand rule). It traverses from $q_{hit,1} = (1.5, 0)$ counter-clockwise. **Step 5:** It re-encounters the m-line (the x-axis) at the *back* side of the obstacle at $q = (4.5, 0)$. Is $\vert q - q_{goal} \vert = 5.5 < 8.5 = \vert q_{hit,1} - q_{goal} \vert$? **YES** — so the leave test is satisfied. Robot leaves at $q_{leave,1} = (4.5, 0)$ **without completing the full loop**. **Step 6 — Step 9:** Identical to Bug 1 for Obstacle 2. **Total Path Length (Bug 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{Bug2} &= 1.5 + \frac{3\pi}{2} + (6.0 - 4.5) + \frac{2\pi}{2} + (8.0 - 6.0) + 2.0 \\ &= 1.5 + 4.712 + 1.5 + 3.142 + 2.0 + 2.0 \\ &\approx 14.85 \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** $$ \frac{L_{Bug2}}{L_{Bug1}} \;\approx\; \frac{14.85}{25.71} \;\approx\; 0.578 $$ Bug 2 saves $\sim 42\%$ of the path length in this scenario. This is the **classic KTU-style numerical example**. ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Bug 1 vs Bug 2 vs Tangent Bug) | Performance / Behavioural Feature | **Bug 1** | **Bug 2** | **Tangent Bug** | |------------------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------| | Sensor requirement | Contact (touch) | Contact (touch) | Range (LIDAR) | | Number of perimeter circuits per obstacle | **Always exactly 1 (full)** | 0.5 on average (half-loop typically) | 0.5 on average, sometimes **0** | | Uses obstacle geometry beyond hit-point? | No | Only m-line crossing | **Yes** — full local map | | Oscillation between obstacles? | Possible | Possible but mitigated | **Minimised** | | Local-minima handling | Re-evaluates per hit-point | Re-evaluates per hit-point | **Detects local minima explicitly** | | Worst-case path bound | $nd + \sum L_i$ | $nd + \sum L_i$ | $d + \varepsilon n$ | | Computational cost (per step) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(k \log k)$ for $k$ range points | | Industrial deployment readiness | Low | Low | **High** (basis of many AMRs) | ### 3.4 Derivation of Tangent Bug's Heuristic $h(q, \theta)$ **Goal:** Prove that the chosen heading $\theta^*$ maximises progress toward $q_{goal}$. Let $q$ be the current robot position and $\theta$ a candidate boundary-following heading. After an infinitesimal step of length $ds$ in direction $\theta$, the robot reaches: $$ q' \;=\; q \;+\; ds \cdot (\cos\theta,\, \sin\theta) $$ The new distance to goal is: $$ \begin{aligned} d(q', q_{goal}) &= \sqrt{(q_x + ds\cos\theta - q_{g,x})^2 + (q_y + ds\sin\theta - q_{g,y})^2} \end{aligned} $$ The **heuristic** measures predicted reduction in distance to goal: $$ h(q, \theta) \;=\; d(q, q_{goal}) \;-\; d(q', q_{goal}) $$ Taylor-expanding $d(q', q_{goal})$ to first order in $ds$: $$ d(q', q_{goal}) \;\approx\; d(q, q_{goal}) \;+\; \nabla_q d \cdot (ds\cos\theta,\, ds\sin\theta) $$ where the gradient is: $$ \nabla_q d \;=\; \frac{(q - q_{goal})}{d(q, q_{goal})} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} h(q, \theta) &= -\nabla_q d \cdot (ds\cos\theta,\, ds\sin\theta) \\ &= -ds \cdot \frac{(q - q_{goal}) \cdot (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)}{d(q, q_{goal})} \\ &= \frac{ds}{d(q, q_{goal})} \big[ (q_{g,x} - q_x)\cos\theta + (q_{g,y} - q_y)\sin\theta \big] \end{aligned} $$ Maximising $h(q, \theta)$ is equivalent to maximising the **dot product** with the goal-direction unit vector $\hat{u}_{goal} = (q_{goal} - q) / \vert q_{goal} - q \vert$: $$ h(q, \theta) \;\propto\; \hat{u}_{goal} \cdot (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) \;=\; \cos(\theta - \theta_{goal}) $$ Therefore $\theta^* = \theta_{goal}$ **maximises** the heuristic, confirming that the "go to goal" direction is always chosen when no obstacle blocks it — exactly the desired Tangent Bug behaviour. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bug Algorithm Family This diagram shows how the three algorithms differ in their internal sensing and decision blocks. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef sensor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000 classDef action fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000 A[Robot Initialisation
Receive q_start and q_goal]:::action B{Obstacle Detected
in Sensor Range?}:::decision C1[Contact Sensor
Touch Trigger]:::sensor C2[Range Sensor
LIDAR or Sonar Ring]:::sensor D1[Mode Switch Block
Activate Follow Boundary]:::action D2[Local Tangent Graph
LTG Builder]:::action E1{Leave Condition
Satisfied?}:::decision E2{Heading Maximises
Heuristic h?}:::decision F1[Full Circumnavigation
of Obstacle]:::action F2[Boundary Follow at
Minimum Range]:::action G1[Return to q_hit
Leave at q_min]:::action G2[Heuristic Selection
of Best Tangent]:::action H1[Resume Motion-to-Goal
Along m-line]:::action H2[Predict d to q_goal
Choose Steepest Descent]:::action I{Reached
q_goal?}:::decision J[Terminate
Success]:::output K[Terminate
Failure: Local Min]:::output A --> B B -- No --> H1 B -- Yes --> C1 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D2 D1 --> F1 D1 --> E1 E1 -- No --> F1 E1 -- Yes --> G1 G1 --> H1 F1 -- Bug1: Loop Complete --> G1 D2 --> E2 E2 -- Yes --> H2 E2 -- No --> F2 H2 --> H1 F2 -- Local Min Detected --> K F2 -- Progress Made --> D2 H1 --> I I -- Yes --> J I -- No --> B ``` ### 4.2 Finite State Machine for Bug 1 and Bug 2 ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> GoToGoal GoToGoal --> BoundaryFollow : Hit Obstacle
Record q_hit BoundaryFollow --> GoToGoal : Bug1: Full Loop Done
Leave at q_min

Bug2: m-line Cross
Closer to Goal BoundaryFollow --> [*] : Bug2: Return to q_hit
Goal Unreachable GoToGoal --> [*] : Reached q_goal ``` ### 4.3 Tangent Bug State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> MotionToGoal MotionToGoal --> BoundaryFollow : All Tangents
Increase Distance MotionToGoal --> MotionToGoal : Free Heading
Maximises h BoundaryFollow --> MotionToGoal : Free Heading
Reappears BoundaryFollow --> LocalMinimum : Full Tangent Scan
No Progress LocalMinimum --> [*] : Terminate Failure MotionToGoal --> [*] : Reached q_goal ``` ### 4.4 Geometric Schematic of the M-Line and Critical Points ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef geom fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000000 classDef label fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef robot fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000000 P1[/q_start = 0 comma 0/]:::label P2[/q_goal = 10 comma 0/]:::label P3[/q_hit_1 = 1.5 comma 0/]:::label P4[/q_min_1 = 4.5 comma 0/]:::label P5[/q_hit_2 = 6.0 comma 0/]:::label P6[/q_leave_2 = 8.0 comma 0/]:::label O1[Obstacle 1
Circle r=1.5]:::geom O2[Obstacle 2
Circle r=1.0]:::geom R1[Robot Path
Trajectory]:::robot P1 --- P2 P3 --- P4 P5 --- P6 O1 --- P3 O1 --- P4 O2 --- P5 O2 --- P6 ``` > [!NOTE] > The schematic preserves the KTU 2024 textbook convention of labeling hit points, leave points, and the m-line endpoints distinctly. The m-line is the straight line connecting $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$, which in this example coincides with the x-axis. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State and explain the two primitive behaviours used in any Bug algorithm. List the sensors required for Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug respectively.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The two primitive behaviours are: 1. **Motion-to-Goal (Go-to-Goal):** The robot aligns its heading along the straight line from its current position $q$ to the goal $q_{goal}$ and translates until either the goal is reached or an obstacle is detected. (1.5 marks) 2. **Boundary-Following (Wall-Following):** The robot moves tangentially along the perimeter of the obstacle, maintaining a fixed stand-off distance, with the obstacle surface on a consistent side (left-hand or right-hand rule). (1 mark) **Sensor requirements:** (0.5 mark) - Bug 1 → **Contact / Tactile sensor** - Bug 2 → **Contact / Tactile sensor** - Tangent Bug → **Range sensor** (LIDAR, sonar ring, or depth camera) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define the terms "hit point" and "leave point" as used in Bug algorithms. What is the role of the m-line in Bug 2?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - A **Hit Point ($q_{hit}$)** is the position on the obstacle boundary where the robot first makes contact while attempting to move toward the goal. (1 mark) - A **Leave Point ($q_{leave}$)** is the position on the obstacle boundary where the robot terminates boundary-following and resumes motion-to-goal. (1 mark) - The **m-line** is the straight line segment connecting $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$. In Bug 2, the m-line acts as a **geometric memory device** — the robot leaves the obstacle boundary only when it re-crosses the m-line at a point strictly closer to $q_{goal}$ than the original $q_{hit}$. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **(a)** Describe the **Bug 1 algorithm** in detail. Mention its two modes of operation, the leave-point selection rule, and the worst-case path length bound. (7 marks) **(b)** A robot starts at $q_{start} = (0,0)$ and must reach $q_{goal} = (15, 0)$. Two circular obstacles are present: $O_1$ centred at $(5, 0)$ with radius $2$, and $O_2$ centred at $(11, 0)$ with radius $1.5$. Trace the Bug 1 trajectory, identify $q_{hit,1}, q_{leave,1}, q_{hit,2}, q_{leave,2}$, and compute the total path length. (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Bug 1 Algorithm Description [7 Marks] 1. **Algorithm overview:** Bug 1 is a complete, sensor-based path planner that uses contact sensing to navigate in an unknown 2-D workspace with static obstacles. It alternates between two modes. *(1 mark)* 2. **Mode 1 — Motion-to-Goal:** The robot moves in a straight line from its current position toward $q_{goal}$. If the goal is reached, terminate. If an obstacle is encountered, mark the contact point as $q_{hit}$ and switch to Mode 2. *(1.5 marks)* 3. **Mode 2 — Boundary-Following with Full Circumnavigation:** The robot follows the obstacle boundary in a fixed direction (say, keeping the obstacle on the left). During the entire circumnavigation, the algorithm maintains a running record of the point on the boundary that is closest to $q_{goal}$. Upon returning to $q_{hit}$ (completing the full loop), the robot leaves the obstacle at this closest point, called $q_{leave}$. *(2 marks)* 4. **Leave-point selection rule:** $q_{leave} = \arg\min_{q \in \partial O_i} \vert q - q_{goal} \vert$, evaluated over the entire boundary $\partial O_i$ of obstacle $O_i$. *(1 mark)* 5. **Worst-case path length bound:** If $d$ is the straight-line distance from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ and $L_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle: $$ L_{Bug1} \;\leq\; n \cdot d \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i $$ *(1.5 marks)* --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Trace [7 Marks] **Given:** - $q_{start} = (0,0)$, $q_{goal} = (15, 0)$ - $O_1$: centre $(5,0)$, radius $r_1 = 2$, perimeter $L_1 = 4\pi$ - $O_2$: centre $(11, 0)$, radius $r_2 = 1.5$, perimeter $L_2 = 3\pi$ - $d = 15$ **Step 1:** Robot moves along +x-axis. At $x = 5 - 2 = 3$, it contacts $O_1$. $$ q_{hit,1} = (3, 0) $$ Distance to goal: $d_{hit,1} = 12$. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 2:** Circumnavigate $O_1$. Closest boundary point to goal is the rightmost point of the circle: $$ q_{leave,1} = q_{min,1} = (7, 0) $$ Distance to goal: $d_{min,1} = 8$. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 3:** Bug 1 returns to $q_{hit,1}$ to confirm completion, then resumes at $q_{leave,1}$. Path so far: $3 + 4\pi + (7-3) = 7 + 4\pi \approx 19.57$. *(1 mark)* **Step 4:** Move along +x-axis from $(7,0)$. At $x = 11 - 1.5 = 9.5$, it contacts $O_2$. $$ q_{hit,2} = (9.5, 0) $$ Distance to goal: $d_{hit,2} = 5.5$. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 5:** Circumnavigate $O_2$. Closest boundary point to goal is the rightmost point: $$ q_{leave,2} = (12.5, 0) $$ Distance to goal: $d_{min,2} = 2.5$. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 6:** Return to $q_{hit,2}$, then leave at $q_{leave,2}$. Path added: $9.5 - 7 = 2.5$ (travel), then $3\pi$ (full circumnav), then return to $q_{hit}$ not needed after final leave — actually, the robot returns to $q_{hit,2}$ first: $12.5 - 9.5 = 3.0$ to return, then $9.5 - 7 = 2.5$ back along m-line minus re-traversal. Let us recompute carefully. *(1.5 marks)* **Final path computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{Bug1} &= \underbrace{3}_{q_{start}\to q_{hit,1}} + \underbrace{4\pi}_{L_1} + \underbrace{4}_{q_{hit,1}\to q_{leave,1}} + \underbrace{2.5}_{q_{leave,1}\to q_{hit,2}} + \underbrace{3\pi}_{L_2} + \underbrace{3}_{q_{hit,2}\to q_{leave,2}} + \underbrace{2.5}_{q_{leave,2}\to q_{goal}} \\ &= 15.0 + 7\pi \\ &\approx 15.0 + 21.99 \\ &\approx 36.99 \end{aligned} $$ **Summary Table:** | Point | Coordinates | Role | |-------|-------------|------| | $q_{start}$ | $(0, 0)$ | Start | | $q_{hit,1}$ | $(3, 0)$ | First contact with $O_1$ | | $q_{leave,1}$ | $(7, 0)$ | Closest point on $O_1$ to goal | | $q_{hit,2}$ | $(9.5, 0)$ | First contact with $O_2$ | | $q_{leave,2}$ | $(12.5, 0)$ | Closest point on $O_2$ to goal | | $q_{goal}$ | $(15, 0)$ | Final destination | **Total path length: $L_{Bug1} = 15 + 7\pi \approx 36.99$ units.** *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]* **(a)** Describe the **Tangent Bug algorithm** with the help of a flow diagram. Explain the concept of the Local Tangent Graph and the heuristic $h(q, \theta)$ used for heading selection. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare and contrast **Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug** algorithms in terms of sensor requirements, path length bounds, and computational complexity. Which algorithm is most suitable for a real-world autonomous mobile robot with a 2-D LIDAR and why? (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Tangent Bug Description [7 Marks] 1. **Tangent Bug overview:** Tangent Bug is a range-sensor-based reactive planner. Unlike Bug 1 and Bug 2 (which use contact sensing), Tangent Bug uses a 360° range sensor to construct a local map of visible obstacles in real time. *(1 mark)* 2. **Local Tangent Graph (LTG):** At each step, the range scan identifies obstacle edges (range discontinuities). For each visible obstacle $O_k$, the robot computes two tangent directions $T_{k,1}$ and $T_{k,2}$ that graze the obstacle. The union of all such tangents forms the **Local Tangent Graph** — a set of candidate boundary-following directions. *(2 marks)* 3. **Two modes of operation:** (i) **Motion-to-Goal** — move in the direction that minimises the predicted distance to $q_{goal}$, evaluating every candidate heading in the LTG. (ii) **Boundary-Following** — engaged when all candidate boundary directions would *increase* distance to the goal; the robot moves at the minimum range (closest to obstacle). *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Heuristic function:** For each candidate heading $\theta$ on the obstacle boundary, the algorithm computes: $$ h(q, \theta) \;=\; d(q, q_{goal}) \;-\; d(q_{follow}, q_{goal}) $$ This measures the predicted reduction in distance to the goal. The heading $\theta^*$ that **maximises** $h$ is selected. If "go to goal" maximises $h$, the robot never engages boundary-following. *(1.5 marks)* 5. **Termination:** The algorithm terminates successfully when $q_{goal}$ is reached, or with failure if a local minimum is detected (full tangent scan yields no progress). *(1 mark)* --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Comparative Analysis [7 Marks] | Criterion | **Bug 1** | **Bug 2** | **Tangent Bug** | |-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------| | Sensor | Contact | Contact | Range (LIDAR) | | Cost (USD) | < $5 | < $5 | $100 – $500 | | Worst-case path | $nd + \sum L_i$ | $nd + \sum L_i$ | $d + \varepsilon n$ | | Path smoothness | Poor (zigzag) | Moderate | Good (smooth) | | Per-step compute | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(k \log k)$ for $k$ scan points | | Local-minimum handling | Re-attempt at each obstacle | Re-attempt at each obstacle | Explicit detection | | Practical deployment | Academic only | Academic only | **Industrial (AMRs, AGVs)** | **Best choice for a real-world AMR with 2-D LIDAR: Tangent Bug.** *(2 marks for justification)* **Justification:** - A 2-D LIDAR provides exactly the range data Tangent Bug needs. - The path length bound $L \leq d + \varepsilon n$ approaches the optimal straight-line path $d$ as the sensing error $\varepsilon$ becomes small. - The LTG-based heuristic yields smooth, jerk-free trajectories suitable for wheeled robots with kinematic constraints. - Local-minimum detection prevents infinite loops in dead-end corridors. - The slightly higher per-step cost $O(k \log k)$ is negligible for modern embedded processors handling $k = 360$ to $k = 2000$ scan points at 10–40 Hz. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Forgetting to mark $q_{hit}$ before starting circumnavigation** — losing 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing "closest point on boundary" with "closest point to goal in workspace"** — Bug 1's leave point is on the *boundary*, not in free space. > 3. **Stating the path bound as "the path length" instead of the "upper bound on path length"** — examiners are strict about the inequality. > 4. **For Bug 2:** failing to state **both** conditions of the leave test (on m-line *and* closer to goal) — partial credit only. > 5. **For Tangent Bug:** writing "Tangent Bug uses Bug 2's rule" — it does not; it uses a continuous heuristic. > 6. **Numerical errors in perimeter calculation** — use $L = 2\pi r$ for circles, not $\pi r^2$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important Things to Remember (One-Glance Summary):** - ✅ **Bug 1** = full circumnavigation + closest-point leave rule. Sensor: contact. Bound: $L \leq n d + \sum L_i$. - ✅ **Bug 2** = half-loop on average + m-line re-crossing + monotonic-progress test. Sensor: contact. Bound: same as Bug 1, shorter in practice. - ✅ **Tangent Bug** = range-sensor based + Local Tangent Graph + heuristic $h(q,\theta) = d(q, q_{goal}) - d(q_{follow}, q_{goal})$ + smooth trajectories. Bound: $L \leq d + \varepsilon n$ (asymptotically optimal). - ✅ All three are **complete** (will find the goal if a path exists), but only Tangent Bug has a **competitive ratio approaching 1**. - ✅ The two atomic behaviours are **Motion-to-Goal** and **Boundary-Following** — the entire family is built on these. - ✅ Key points: $q_{hit}$ (first contact), $q_{leave}$ (boundary exit), $q_{min}$ (Bug 1's leave point), and the **m-line** (Bug 2's memory device). - ✅ Bug 2's leave test requires **two simultaneous conditions**: (1) on m-line, (2) closer to goal than $q_{hit}$. - ✅ Tangent Bug **never** engages boundary-following if the "go-to-goal" heading maximises the heuristic. - ✅ Real-world deployment: Tangent Bug is the only one of the three used as a direct basis for industrial AMRs, robotic vacuum cleaners, and agricultural field robots. - ✅ For KTU 2024 exams: memorise the three path-length bounds and the per-step computational complexities. - ✅ KTU exam favourite: **trace a Bug 1 or Bug 2 trajectory on a 1-D arrangement of circular obstacles** — practice this until automatic.

topics.title: Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Probabilistic Path Planning (RPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Path Planning (RPP)**, also known as the **Randomized Path Planner (RPP)**, is a hybrid motion planning algorithm developed by **Barraquand and Latombe (1990)** that combines the deterministic **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method with **probabilistic (random) walks** to overcome the classic **local minima problem** encountered in pure potential field approaches.

Formally, RPP is a **single-query, sampling-based local planner** that operates in the **configuration space (C-space)** of a robot. It computes a collision-free path from an initial configuration $q_{init}$ to a goal configuration $q_{goal}$ by alternating between two complementary strategies:

1. **Deterministic Gradient Descent** on a navigation function (artificial potential field).
2. **Stochastic Escape (Random Walks)** when the descent becomes trapped in a local minimum.

The algorithm is *probabilistic completeness*: the probability that it finds a solution, if one exists, approaches **1** as the allowed execution time approaches **infinity**, provided the configuration space is sufficiently well-behaved (i.e., satisfies the **visibility** and **path clearance** conditions).

## 1.2 The Underlying Problem: Local Minima in Potential Fields

Classical **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** methods, introduced by **Khatib (1986)**, model the robot as a point particle under the influence of a virtual potential field:

- The **goal** generates an **attractive potential** pulling the robot.
- The **obstacles** generate **repulsive potentials** pushing the robot away.

The robot's motion follows the **negative gradient** of this combined potential. While elegant and computationally efficient, APF suffers from a fatal flaw: the potential function may contain **local minima** that are not the goal, causing the robot to become permanently stuck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Local Minima Problem:** Points in C-space where $\nabla U(q) = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$. These are equilibrium configurations where attractive and repulsive forces cancel, halting the robot.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Hiker in the Foggy Mountains

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hiker in Foggy Mountains"**

Imagine a **blind hiker** trying to descend a mountain to a village in the valley below. The hiker can only feel the **slope of the ground beneath their feet** (this is the **gradient** of the potential field).

- **Without RPP (Pure APF):** The hiker walks downhill, but accidentally steps into a small **pond** (a local minimum). Because the water is level in all directions, the hiker mistakenly believes they have reached the valley and stops. They are stuck forever.

- **With RPP:** When the hiker senses that the ground is flat (zero gradient / trapped in local minimum), they take a **series of random, wandering steps in random directions** for a fixed duration. This random exploration may carry them out of the pond. Once free, they resume their **goal-directed downhill walk**.

The hiker's **downhill walk** is the gradient descent phase. The **random wandering** is the probabilistic escape phase. The hiker repeats this cycle until they reach the actual village (the goal).

This hybrid strategy is the essence of the **RPP algorithm** — it marries the *directness* of potential field methods with the *escape capability* of randomized exploration.

## 1.4 Historical Context and Significance

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Original Authors** | **Jerome Barraquand** and **Jean-Claude Latombe** |
| **Year Proposed** | **1990** |
| **Institution** | **Stanford University**, Robotics Laboratory |
| **Published In** | *Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)* |
| **Significance** | First practical algorithm to address APF's local minima using randomness; precursor to modern **PRM** and **RRT** sampling-based planners |

## 1.5 Key Parameters in RPP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard RPP Parameters (as per original 1990 paper):**
> - $\zeta$ — **Attractive gain coefficient** (units: $\text{N/m}$ or dimensionless)
> - $\eta$ — **Repulsive gain coefficient**
> - $\rho_0$ — **Distance of influence** of obstacles
> - $\rho(q)$ — **Minimum distance** from configuration $q$ to the obstacle region
> - $L_{max}$ — **Maximum random walk length** (number of steps)
> - $\delta$ — **Step size** for gradient descent and random walk
> - $N_{max}$ — **Maximum number of iterations**

These parameters are tuned to balance **exploitation** (goal-directed motion) versus **exploration** (random escape).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RPP Algorithm — Conceptual Breakdown

The RPP algorithm operates as a **two-phase iterative loop**. Each cycle consists of a *descent phase* followed by an *escape phase* (if needed).

### Phase 1: Gradient Descent (Deterministic)
- The robot computes the **negative gradient** of the total potential function.
- It moves in the direction of steepest descent by a step size $\delta$.
- If $\nabla U(q) \neq 0$, the robot successfully makes progress toward the goal.
- The descent continues until one of three termination conditions:
  1. **Goal reached:** $||q - q_{goal}|| < \epsilon_{goal}$
  2. **Local minimum detected:** $||\nabla U(q)|| \approx 0$ (within threshold $\epsilon_{min}$)
  3. **Maximum descent steps exceeded:** $k > k_{max}$

### Phase 2: Random Walk (Stochastic Escape)
- Triggered **only** when a local minimum is detected.
- The robot performs a **Brownian-motion-like** random walk: at each step, a random direction is sampled uniformly from the unit sphere in C-space.
- The walk continues for a fixed number of steps $L_{max}$ or until the robot escapes the local minimum basin.
- An "escape" is confirmed when the potential $U(q)$ at the new configuration is *strictly less* than the local minimum value $U_{min}$ — a sign that the robot has climbed over the energy barrier.

### Outer Loop
- After random walk termination, the algorithm **resumes gradient descent** from the new configuration.
- This cycle repeats until the goal is reached or a global iteration limit $N_{max}$ is hit (failure).

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation: Artificial Potential Fields

### 2.2.1 Attractive Potential
The attractive potential pulls the robot toward the goal. The most common form is a **quadratic** well:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \| q - q_{goal} \|^2
$$

- **Quadratic form** is used for far-field goals (provides smooth, increasingly strong attraction with distance).
- A **conic** form $U_{att}(q) = \zeta \cdot \|q - q_{goal}\|$ is sometimes used near the goal to avoid vanishing gradients, but the quadratic form is standard in RPP.

The corresponding **attractive force**:

$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \, (q - q_{goal})
$$

This force has **magnitude** $\zeta \cdot \|q - q_{goal}\|$ and **direction** pointing from $q$ toward $q_{goal}$.

### 2.2.2 Repulsive Potential
The repulsive potential creates a "barrier" around each obstacle. For a configuration $q$ and the nearest obstacle at distance $\rho(q)$:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \, \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

- $\rho_0$ is the **range of influence** — beyond this distance, obstacles have no effect.
- The $1/\rho$ singularity ensures the potential blows up as the robot approaches an obstacle.
- The $(1/\rho_0)$ term ensures the potential and its gradient vanish continuously at $\rho = \rho_0$.

The corresponding **repulsive force**:

$$
F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \, \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \cdot \nabla \rho(q) & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.3 Total Potential and Force

$$
U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)
$$

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q)
$$

### 2.2.4 Distance Function $\rho(q)$
In C-space, $\rho(q)$ is the minimum distance from the robot's placement at $q$ to the obstacle region $C_{obs}$. Formally:

$$
\rho(q) = \min_{q' \in C_{obs}} \| q - q' \|
$$

For a point robot in $\mathbb{R}^2$, this reduces to the Euclidean distance to the nearest obstacle boundary. For higher-dimensional C-spaces (e.g., manipulators), it must account for the robot's geometry (swept volume).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \cdot \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2$ | Attractive potential | $\zeta$ has units of $\text{N/m}$ |
| $F_{att}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$ | Attractive force | Vector in C-space |
| $U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \eta (1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$ for $\rho \leq \rho_0$ | Repulsive potential | $\eta$ is repulsive gain |
| $F_{rep}(q) = \eta (1/\rho - 1/\rho_0) \cdot (1/\rho^2) \cdot \nabla\rho$ | Repulsive force | Active only within $\rho_0$ |
| $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ | Total potential | Scalar field over C-space |
| $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Total virtual force | Negative gradient |
| $\rho_0$ | Range of obstacle influence | Euclidean distance |
| $\delta$ | Step size for gradient descent & random walk | Length unit |
| $L_{max}$ | Max number of random walk steps | Integer count |
| $N_{max}$ | Global iteration limit | Integer count |
| $\epsilon_{goal}$ | Goal tolerance | Distance threshold |
| $\epsilon_{min}$ | Local-minimum detection threshold | Gradient norm threshold |
| $\nabla \rho(q)$ | Gradient of distance function | Unit vector away from obstacle |
| $P(\text{success}) \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$ | Probabilistic completeness | Asymptotic guarantee |
| $U_{min}$ | Local minimum potential value | Scalar energy level |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The local minimum is "escaped" during a random walk when $U(q_{new}) < U_{min}$, meaning the robot has moved to a configuration with strictly lower potential — i.e., it has successfully crossed the energy barrier of the local minimum basin.

## 2.4 Two Variants of RPP

### 2.4.1 RPP-1 (Original Version)
- Uses a **fixed-length** random walk (constant $L_{max}$).
- Simpler, but may waste time in deep local minima or fail to escape shallow ones efficiently.

### 2.4.2 RPP-2 (Adaptive Version)
- Adapts the random walk length based on the **depth** of the local minimum.
- For deeper minima (higher $U_{min}$), longer random walks are executed.
- More efficient in cluttered environments.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Applications of RPP in Modern Field Robotics:**
> - **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Navigation in warehouses, agricultural fields, and urban environments with dynamic obstacles.
> - **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs):** Path planning in cluttered underwater terrains (e.g., pipeline inspection).
> - **Aerial Drones (UAVs):** Real-time reactive path planning in unknown or partially known environments.
> - **Mobile Manipulators:** Planning in high-dimensional C-spaces (arm + base).
> - **Planetary Rovers (NASA's Mars rovers):** RPP-inspired planners were used in **NASA's Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit & Opportunity)** for hazard avoidance.
> - **Surgical Robotics:** Navigating flexible tools through anatomical constraints.

## 2.6 Strengths and Weaknesses

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|
| Simple, intuitive implementation | May fail in **narrow passages** of C-space |
| Very fast in simple environments | **No optimality** guarantee on path length or quality |
| Easily handles dynamic environments (re-planning is fast) | Random walk can cause **oscillatory** or **jittery** motion |
| Probabilistically complete | Performance highly sensitive to parameter tuning ($\zeta$, $\eta$, $\rho_0$, $L_{max}$) |
| Low memory footprint (single-query) | Cannot leverage prior computation (unlike PRM) |
| Naturally extends to high-dimensional C-spaces | Random walk can sometimes escape into a *worse* local minimum |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Potential Gradient

We start with the attractive potential as a function of the configuration $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \| q - q_{goal} \|^2
$$

**Step 1:** Express the squared norm explicitly as a dot product.

$$
\| q - q_{goal} \|^2 = (q - q_{goal})^T (q - q_{goal}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (q_i - q_{goal,i})^2
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to each component $q_i$ of the configuration vector.

$$
\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2} \, \zeta \cdot 2(q_i - q_{goal,i}) = \zeta (q_i - q_{goal,i})
$$

**Step 3:** Assemble the partial derivatives into the gradient vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

$$
\nabla U_{att}(q) = \zeta \, (q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the attractive force as the negative gradient.

$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \, (q - q_{goal})
$$

**Interpretation:** The attractive force is a **linear restoring force** (Hookean spring) pulling the configuration $q$ toward the goal $q_{goal}$. Its magnitude grows linearly with distance from the goal, ensuring that distant configurations experience stronger attraction.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Potential Gradient

The repulsive potential (for $\rho(q) \leq \rho_0$) is given by:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \eta \, \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Let us define an intermediate scalar function to simplify notation.

$$
f(q) = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}
$$

So $U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \eta f(q)^2$.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule for the gradient.

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \, f(q) \cdot \nabla f(q)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\nabla f(q)$.

$$
\nabla f(q) = \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \cdot \nabla \rho(q)
$$

Note: $\rho_0$ is a constant, so its gradient is zero.

**Step 4:** Substitute back.

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \right) \cdot \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the repulsive force as the negative gradient.

$$
F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \cdot \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Step 6:** Determine $\nabla \rho(q)$. The distance function $\rho(q) = \|q - q_{obs}\|$ (for the nearest obstacle point $q_{obs}$) has a unit gradient:

$$
\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\| q - q_{obs} \|}
$$

This is a **unit vector** pointing from the obstacle toward the configuration $q$. Therefore, the repulsive force $F_{rep}$ points in this same direction (away from the obstacle), which is physically consistent.

**Interpretation:** The repulsive force has two contributing factors:
1. **$(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)$** — a non-negative scalar that grows as the robot approaches the obstacle (vanishes at $\rho = \rho_0$).
2. **$1/\rho^2$** — a singularity term that explodes as $\rho \to 0$ (contact), ensuring the robot never penetrates the obstacle.
3. **$\nabla \rho(q)$** — a unit vector directing the force radially outward from the obstacle.

## 3.3 Worked Example: 2D Point Robot with Circular Obstacle

Consider a 2D point robot at $q = (x, y)$ with goal $q_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)$ and a single circular obstacle of radius $r$ centered at $q_{obs} = (x_o, y_o)$.

**Step 1:** Compute the Euclidean distance to the obstacle.

$$
\rho(q) = \sqrt{ (x - x_o)^2 + (y - y_o)^2 } - r
$$

The subtraction of $r$ accounts for the obstacle's physical extent (configuration space obstacle for a point robot).

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient of $\rho$.

$$
\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_o \\ y - y_o \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Write the total potential assuming $\rho \leq \rho_0$.

$$
U(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \left[ (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2 \right] + \frac{1}{2} \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the total force.

$$
F(x, y) = -\nabla U(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} -\zeta (x - x_g) - \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{\rho} \\ -\zeta (y - y_g) - \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{y - y_o}{\rho} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Gradient descent update rule. At iteration $k$:

$$
q_{k+1} = q_k + \delta \cdot \frac{F(q_k)}{\| F(q_k) \| + \epsilon}
$$

where $\delta$ is the step size and $\epsilon$ is a small constant to prevent division by zero.

**Numerical Illustration (Sample Calculation):**

Let:
- $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$, $q_{obs} = (5, 0)$, obstacle radius $r = 1$
- $\zeta = 1.0$, $\eta = 100.0$, $\rho_0 = 3.0$, $\delta = 0.5$
- Current configuration: $q = (3, 0)$ (i.e., between the obstacle and the goal)

Compute $\rho$:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + 0^2} - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1.0
$$

Since $\rho = 1.0 \leq \rho_0 = 3.0$, the repulsive potential is active.

Compute the force components:

- Attractive: $F_{att,x} = -1.0 \cdot (3 - 10) = 7.0$, $F_{att,y} = 0$
- Repulsive scalar: $\left( \frac{1}{1.0} - \frac{1}{3.0} \right) = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667$
- Repulsive direction: $\frac{x - x_o}{\rho} = \frac{3 - 5}{1} = -2$, but normalized by $\rho$: the unit vector is $\frac{(-2, 0)}{2} = (-1, 0)$ (pointing from obstacle toward robot, i.e., leftward — which is *away* from the goal!).

Wait — the repulsive force must point away from the obstacle, i.e., to the **left** of the obstacle (since the robot is to the left). So:

- Repulsive force x-component: $F_{rep,x} = 100 \cdot 0.667 \cdot \frac{1}{1.0^2} \cdot (-1) = -66.7$
- $F_{rep,y} = 0$

Total force: $F_x = 7.0 - 66.7 = -59.7$, $F_y = 0$.

The robot is being **pushed to the left** (away from the obstacle) with a force much stronger than the attractive pull. This is correct — the robot is too close to the obstacle. It will move leftward, around the obstacle, and then resume motion toward the goal once $\rho > \rho_0$.

This numerical example demonstrates how the potential field repels the robot from obstacles while attracting it toward the goal — the foundation upon which RPP builds.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: RPP vs. Other Planners

| Property | RPP | APF (Pure) | PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap) | RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Sampling-based local planner | Deterministic | Sampling-based multi-query | Sampling-based single-query |
| **Local Minima Handling** | Random walks escape them | Fails (stuck) | Uses graph search after sampling | Voronoi bias explores space |
| **Probabilistic Completeness** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Optimality** | No | No | No (asymptotic variants exist) | No (asymptotic variants exist) |
| **Re-planning in Dynamic Env.** | Excellent (very fast) | Excellent | Poor (must rebuild graph) | Moderate |
| **Memory** | Very low | Very low | High (stores roadmap) | Moderate (stores tree) |
| **Best Use Case** | Real-time, dynamic field robotics | Simple, uncluttered spaces | Static, complex multi-query | Static, high-dim single-query |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level RPP Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START RPP Algorithm]) --> initB[Initialize q = q_init, k = 0]
    initB --> iterLoop{Iteration k less than N_max?}
    iterLoop -- No --> failA([FAILURE: Max iterations exceeded])
    iterLoop -- Yes --> gradDesc[Gradient Descent Phase]
    gradDesc --> gradStep[Move q along negative gradient by step delta]
    gradStep --> goalCheck{Goal reached? norm q minus q_goal less than epsilon_goal}
    goalCheck -- Yes --> successA([SUCCESS: Path found])
    goalCheck -- No --> minCheck{Local minimum detected? norm grad U less than epsilon_min}
    minCheck -- No --> iterInc[Increment k and continue]
    iterInc --> iterLoop
    minCheck -- Yes --> randWalk[Random Walk Phase begins]
    randWalk --> randStep[Sample random direction from unit sphere]
    randStep --> moveRand[Move q by delta in random direction]
    moveRand --> colCheck{Collision free?}
    colCheck -- No --> randStep
    colCheck -- Yes --> escCheck{Escaped minimum? U q less than U_min}
    escCheck -- No --> walkCount{Random walk steps less than L_max?}
    walkCount -- Yes --> randStep
    walkCount -- No --> resDescent[Resume gradient descent from current q]
    escCheck -- Yes --> resDescent
    resDescent --> iterInc
```

## 4.2 RPP Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock[INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        in1[Start Configuration q_init]
        in2[Goal Configuration q_goal]
        in3[Environment Map C_free and C_obs]
    end

    subgraph sensorBlock[PERCEPTION SUBSYSTEM]
        s1[Distance Function rho q Calculator]
        s2[Gradient of rho q Estimator]
    end

    subgraph plannerBlock[RPP PLANNER CORE]
        p1[Total Potential U q = U_att plus U_rep]
        p2[Gradient Descent Module]
        p3[Local Minimum Detector]
        p4[Random Walk Module with L_max]
        p5[Escape Verifier: U q less than U_min]
    end

    subgraph controlBlock[MOTION CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
        c1[Velocity Command Generator]
        c2[Collision Checker]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        o1[Computed Path: q_init to q_goal]
    end

    in1 --> p1
    in2 --> p1
    in3 --> s1
    s1 --> p1
    s2 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 -- Trapped --> p4
    p3 -- Free --> c1
    p4 --> p5
    p5 -- Escaped --> p2
    p5 -- Not Escaped --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 -- Free --> o1
    c2 -- Collision --> p4
```

## 4.3 C-Space Topological View of RPP Escape Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cspace[C SPACE LANDSCAPE]
        qInitNode[Node A: q_init at high potential on slope]
        slopeDescent[Slope of U_att pulling toward goal]
        localMinBasin[Node B: Local Minimum Basin with U_min]
        energyBarrier[Node C: Energy Barrier Saddle Point]
        goalBasin[Node D: Global Minimum at q_goal]
    end

    qInitNode --> slopeDescent
    slopeDescent -- Descent reaches --> localMinBasin
    localMinBasin -- Pure APF Stuck Here --> stuckA([Stuck Forever in APF])
    localMinBasin -- RPP triggers random walk --> energyBarrier
    energyBarrier -- After crossing --> goalBasin
    energyBarrier -- Insufficient walk length --> localMinBasin
    goalBasin --> reachGoal([Success in RPP])
```

## 4.4 RPP-1 vs. RPP-2 Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rpp1Block[RPP-1 Fixed Walk Length]
        r1a[Detect Local Min] --> r1b[Execute L_max random steps constant]
        r1b --> r1c{Exited basin?}
        r1c -- No --> r1d[Failure or revisit]
        r1c -- Yes --> r1e[Resume descent]
    end

    subgraph rpp2Block[RPP-2 Adaptive Walk Length]
        r2a[Detect Local Min] --> r2b[Measure U_min depth]
        r2b --> r2c[Compute L adaptive = f U_min]
        r2c --> r2d[Execute L adaptive random steps]
        r2d --> r2e{Exited basin?}
        r2e -- No --> r2b
        r2e -- Yes --> r2f[Resume descent]
    end
```
