# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Layer Hybrid Control Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MissionGoal] --> B[DeliberativeLayer]
    B --> B1[WorldModel]
    B --> B2[GlobalPlanner]
    B --> B3[TaskScheduler]
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C[SequencerExecutive]
    C --> C1[StateMachineFSM]
    C --> C2[BehaviorArbiter]
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D[ReactiveLayer]
    D --> D1[ObstacleAvoidance]
    D --> D2[WallFollowing]
    D --> D3[GoToGoalBehavior]
    D1 --> E[LowLevelMotorController]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[FieldRobotActuators]
    G[SensorSuite] --> D
    G --> C
    G --> B
    C -. feedback .-> B
    D -. feedback .-> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Top-down: Solid arrows represent **deliberative commands** flowing from high-level goals to low-level actuators.
- Bottom-up: Sensor data flows back up through every layer.
- Dashed arrows: **Feedback/exception signals** that re-trigger replanning (e.g., when an obstacle is detected that blocks the global plan).

---

## 4.2 Hybrid State Machine — Survey-and-Avoid Mission

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Survey : boot_complete
    Survey --> Avoid : obstacle_within_dsafe
    Avoid --> Survey : obstacle_cleared
    Survey --> GoalReached : waypoint_reached
    GoalReached --> [*] : mission_complete
    Avoid --> Avoid : keep_steering_clear
    Survey --> Replan : replan_requested_by_supervisor
    Replan --> Survey : new_path_loaded
```

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a hybrid state machine, always include (a) the **guard condition** on each transition arrow and (b) the **continuous dynamics** inside each state box.

---

## 4.3 Information-Flow Block Diagram (Cross-Layer Communication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopLayer
        P[Planner]
    end
    subgraph MiddleLayer
        S[Sequencer]
    end
    subgraph BottomLayer
        B[Behaviors]
    end
    subgraph PhysicalWorld
        R[Robot + Sensors]
    end

    P -->|task_descriptors| S
    S -->|active_behavior_set| B
    B -->|motor_commands| R
    R -->|raw_sensor_data| B
    B -->|reactive_events| S
    S -->|exception_alerts| P
```

This block diagram explicitly shows the **closed-loop nature** of the architecture: every layer is both a *consumer* (downward arrow) and a *producer* (upward arrow) of information.

flowchart TB

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Hybrid Control Architecture for a field robot. State TWO key reasons why a pure deliberative (SPA) architecture is unsuitable for unstructured field environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Hybrid Control Architecture** integrates a **deliberative layer** (symbolic planning, world model) with a **reactive layer** (sensor-driven behaviors) via a mediating **sequencer/executive layer**. It unifies long-horizon goal reasoning with low-latency reflexive response.

Two reasons pure SPA fails in the field:
1. **Computational bottleneck:** Global planning in a dynamic, partially-known field is NP-hard; the plan becomes stale before execution completes.
2. **No real-time response:** SPA assumes a *frozen* world model — impossible in a field with moving obstacles, wind, terrain deformation, and sensor noise.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two reasons = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the THREE layers of the canonical 3T (Three-Tier) hybrid architecture and state the typical time-scale of operation for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Layer | Function | Time-scale |
|-------|----------|------------|
| **Deliberative (Planner)** | Mission, path, task planning | **seconds to minutes** |
| **Sequencing (Executive)** | Mode selection, behavior arbitration | **0.1 – 1 s** |
| **Reactive (Skills)** | Sensorimotor control loops | **1 – 50 ms** |

*(1 mark per layer = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Missing the mediator layer** — students often draw only two layers (planning + reactive). Always include the **sequencer**.
> 2. **Confusing time-scales** — reactive loops are *milliseconds*, deliberative is *seconds*. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting guard conditions** on state transitions in a hybrid automaton.
> 4. **Using "Sense-Plan-Act" and "deliberative" interchangeably** — SPA is *one* deliberative paradigm, not a synonym.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet derivations.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **three-layer hybrid control architecture** for an autonomous field robot. Clearly state the role of each layer and the inter-layer information flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field robot operates with two discrete states: $s_1$ (cruise) and $s_2$ (avoid). In $s_1$, it follows a planned path with $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = K_p(\theta_{goal} - \theta)$ where $v = 1.2$ m/s, $K_p = 1.0$ s$^{-1}$. In $s_2$, a potential-field repulsion $F_{rep} = k_r(1/d_{obs} - 1/d_{safe})/d_{obs}^2$ with $k_r = 0.5$, $d_{safe} = 1.5$ m is added. The robot switches to $s_2$ when $d_{obs} < 1.5$ m. Define the **hybrid automaton** governing this mission, including the **guard conditions**, **continuous dynamics**, and **discrete transition function**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

1. **Block diagram** (3 marks) — Draw a three-layer stack: Deliberative (top) → Sequencer (middle) → Reactive (bottom), with downward task-flow arrows and upward sensor-flow arrows. Mention the **closed feedback loop** from sensors back up to the planner.
2. **Layer roles** (2 marks):
   - *Deliberative:* Global path planning using A*/D*, world-model maintenance, symbolic task decomposition.
   - *Sequencer:* Translates plan into executable behaviors; manages discrete state transitions; resolves conflicts.
   - *Reactive:* Executes reflexive behaviors (avoid, follow); runs PID motor loops at kHz rates.
3. **Inter-layer flow** (2 marks): Top-down (goals → tasks → behaviors → motor commands); Bottom-up (sensor events → behavior triggers → replan requests). Note time-scale separation: deliberative 0.5–5 s, sequencer 100 ms–1 s, reactive 10–50 ms.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Discrete state space** (1 mark):

$$\mathcal{S} = \{s_1 : \text{CRUISE}, \quad s_2 : \text{AVOID}\}$$

**Step 2 — Guard conditions** (2 marks):

$$\phi_{1 \to 2} : d_{obs} \leq 1.5 \ \text{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_1 \to s_2$$

$$\phi_{2 \to 1} : d_{obs} \geq 1.5 \ \text{m} + \epsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_2 \to s_1$$

**Step 3 — Continuous dynamics** (3 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{In } s_1 &: \quad \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ K_p(\theta_{goal} - \theta) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2\cos\theta \\ 1.2\sin\theta \\ 1.0(\theta_{goal} - \theta) \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{In } s_2 &: \quad \dot{\mathbf{q}} = f_{s_1}(\mathbf{q}) + J^T(\mathbf{q}) \cdot F_{rep} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}_{rep}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Discrete transition function** (1 mark):

$$g(s, \mathbf{e}) = \begin{cases} s_2, & s = s_1 \text{ and } d_{obs} \leq 1.5 \ \text{m} \\ s_1, & s = s_2 \text{ and } d_{obs} \geq 1.5 \ \text{m} \\ s, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **deliberative**, **reactive**, and **hybrid** control architectures. Give **two real-world field-robot examples** for each and discuss the **engineering trade-offs**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a behavior-based arbitration scheme, three behaviors produce desired velocity vectors:
- $B_1$ (goal-seek): $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1.0, 0.0)$ m/s, weight $\alpha_1 = 0.5$
- $B_2$ (avoid): $\mathbf{v}_2 = (-0.5, 0.2)$ m/s, weight $\alpha_2 = 0.4$
- $B_3$ (formation): $\mathbf{v}_3 = (0.0, 0.6)$ m/s, weight $\alpha_3 = 0.1$

Compute the **arbitrated velocity command** $\mathbf{u} = \sum \alpha_j \mathbf{v}_j$, its **magnitude**, and its **heading angle**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

| Aspect | Deliberative | Reactive | Hybrid |
|--------|-------------|----------|--------|
| Philosophy | Think → Act | Sense → Act | Think + Sense → Act |
| World model | Explicit, symbolic | Implicit / none | Both, layered |
| Planning | Global, off-line or long horizon | None | Hierarchical |
| Response latency | High (seconds) | Very low (ms) | Layered (ms–s) |
| Examples | Mars Rover path planner (offline), warehouse ASRS scheduling | Braitenberg vehicle, Roomba wall-follow | Curiosity rover (3T), DJI Agras drone, autonomous tractor |

**Trade-offs (1 mark each, max 2):**
- Deliberative: high compute, brittle to dynamic change.
- Reactive: no global optimality, prone to local minima.
- Hybrid: complex to implement, harder to formally verify, but field-proven.

**Examples (2 marks):** Curiosity rover, Roomba, DJI Agras, Mars Pathfinder, etc.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Verify normalization** (1 mark):

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} \alpha_j = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 1.0 \ \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute x-component** (1 mark):

$$u_x = 0.5(1.0) + 0.4(-0.5) + 0.1(0.0) = 0.5 - 0.2 + 0.0 = 0.3 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute y-component** (1 mark):

$$u_y = 0.5(0.0) + 0.4(0.2) + 0.1(0.6) = 0.0 + 0.08 + 0.06 = 0.14 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude** (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \mathbf{u} \vert &= \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2} = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.14^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.09 + 0.0196} = \sqrt{0.1096} \approx 0.331 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Heading** (2 marks):

$$\theta_{cmd} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{u_y}{u_x}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.14}{0.3}\right) = \arctan(0.4667) \approx 25.02^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{u} \approx (0.30, 0.14)$ m/s, $\vert \mathbf{u} \vert \approx 0.331$ m/s, $\theta_{cmd} \approx 25.0^{\circ}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Hybrid Control Architectures**

- **Definition to memorize:** Hybrid = Deliberative + Reactive + Mediator.
- **The 3T architecture** has three layers: Planner (top), Sequencer (middle), Skills (bottom).
- **Time-scales:** Reactive (10–50 ms), Sequencer (100 ms – 1 s), Deliberative (0.5–5 s).
- **Pure deliberative (SPA)** fails in dynamic fields due to planning bottleneck.
- **Pure reactive (Brooks subsumption)** fails for long-horizon goals; local-minima traps.
- **Key architectures** to know: 3T, LAAS, AuRA, CLARAty, TCA, SFX.
- **Behavior arbitration methods:** Weighted summation, subsumption (suppression/inhibition), voting, fuzzy blending, priority queues.
- **Mathematical model:** Hybrid Automaton with $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = f_s(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{u}_s)$ and $s^+ = g(s, \mathbf{e})$.
- **Guard conditions** on state transitions must be included in every hybrid state diagram.
- **Field robot examples:** Autonomous tractors, agricultural drones, Mars rovers, AUVs — all use hybrid control.
- **Stability bound** for reactive loops: $K_p < \frac{2}{T - L}$ (where $T$ = sampling period, $L$ = loop latency).
- **Real-world KTU value-add:** Mention ROS 2, behavior trees (BT), and SMACH as modern implementations of the sequencer layer.
- **Common exam trap:** Mixing up *control architecture* (software organization) with *control theory* (PID, LQR, MPC). They are different — a hybrid architecture *uses* control theory at its lowest layer.
- **Tip for diagrams:** Always label every block, every arrow direction, and every time-scale in your KTU exam figures. A 14-mark question without a labeled diagram loses 2–3 marks.
- **Formulas to memorize verbatim:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$, $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- **Verifiable references** to cite (no fabricated editions): *Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots* by Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, Scaramuzza (MIT Press); *Planning Algorithms* by LaValle (Cambridge University Press); *Behavior-Based Robotics* by Arkin (MIT Press).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Middleware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Middleware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: MIDDLEWARE IN FIELD ROBOTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Middleware** in field robotics is a **distributed software abstraction layer** that resides between the robot's hardware drivers, low-level operating system kernels, and high-level mission/application code. It provides standardized mechanisms for **inter-process communication (IPC)**, hardware abstraction, device coordination, message serialization, and modular software integration across heterogeneous computational nodes (onboard computers, edge processors, and cloud servers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Middleware is a connectivity software that enables interoperability, data exchange, and unified control across heterogeneous robotic subsystems (sensors, actuators, controllers, perception modules) without requiring explicit knowledge of each other's internal architecture.*

Mathematically, the middleware layer can be represented as a function that maps a set of independent hardware/software processes $\mathcal{P} = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ onto a unified communication graph:

$$M : \mathcal{P} \times \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of valid communication channels, and $M(P_i, P_j)$ returns the configured communication interface between any two processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Nervous System"

Imagine a **human nervous system**: your eyes (sensors), hands (actuators), and brain (CPU) don't speak the same native language, yet they coordinate seamlessly. The **spinal cord and peripheral nerves** act as the *middleware* — they translate signals, prioritize reflexes over conscious thought, and route sensory data to the right processing center without the brain knowing the chemical details.

In a field robot:
- **Sensors (LiDAR, IMU, GPS, cameras)** = Eyes, ears, skin
- **Actuators (motors, grippers, wheels)** = Hands, legs
- **Mission planner / AI** = Brain
- **Middleware (ROS, DDS, LCM, YARP)** = Nervous system

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Field Robotics Specifically Needs Middleware:** Field robots operate in **unstructured outdoor environments** with **unreliable communication links**, **variable latency**, and **multi-sensor fusion requirements**. Middleware handles these challenges by providing asynchronous, fault-tolerant, and modular data pipelines.

### 1.3 The Core Problem Middleware Solves

Without middleware, each software module would need to:
1. Know the exact data format of every other module.
2. Handle direct socket/serial communication.
3. Synchronize timing manually.
4. Re-implement communication logic when modules are added/removed.

Middleware eliminates this through **decoupling** — modules only know about *topics* (named data streams) and *message types*, not about each other.

### 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics in Middleware

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Message Latency | **< 10 ms** (real-time control loops) |
| Throughput | **> 1000 msgs/s** (per topic) |
| Discovery Time | **< 2 s** (peer-to-peer) |
| MTU (Max Transmission Unit) | **1500 bytes** (UDP/Ethernet) |
| Publish Rate | **10–100 Hz** (typical sensor) |
| Determinism Bound | **Jitter < 1 ms** (hard real-time) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Bodies & Frameworks:** OMG (Object Management Group) defines **DDS** (Data Distribution Service); ROS-I (ROS-Industrial) extends ROS for industrial field robotics; ISO/IEC 23090 standards govern data distribution.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Robotic Middleware

A typical middleware stack in field robotics is organized into **5 abstraction layers**:

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **L5 — Application** | Mission logic, AI, planning | Behavior trees, FSM |
| **L4 — Middleware Services** | Discovery, parameter server, TF | ROS Master, DDS Domain |
| **L3 — Communication Fabric** | Message passing, serialization | ZeroMQ, DDS-RTPS, UDP |
| **L2 — OS Abstraction** | Process/thread management | Linux, RT-Preempt, VxWorks |
| **L1 — Hardware Drivers** | Sensor/actuator I/O | libusb, CAN, SPI drivers |

## 2.2 Communication Paradigms in Middleware

### (A) Publish / Subscribe (Topic-based)
- **Many-to-many** asynchronous communication.
- Publishers emit messages on a *topic*; subscribers consume them.
- **Decoupled** in space, time, and synchronization.
- Used for: sensor streams (LiDAR points, images, IMU).

### (B) Service / Client (Request-Response)
- **Synchronous** one-to-one communication.
- Client sends a *request*, server returns a *response*.
- Used for: configuration queries, map saves, calibration.

### (C) Action (Long-running Goal)
- **Asynchronous** with feedback and preemption.
- Goal → Feedback (periodic) → Result (terminal).
- Used for: navigation (`move_base`), manipulation, mission execution.

### (D) Parameter Server
- Shared, mutable configuration across nodes.
- Centralized or distributed (DDS-style).

## 2.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Policies

Field robotics demands **deterministic** communication. QoS policies control delivery semantics:

| QoS Policy | Function | Default |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Best-effort vs. Reliable | Reliable |
| **Durability** | Late-joiner history | Volatile |
| **Deadline** | Max time between samples | ∞ |
| **Lifespan** | Message validity duration | ∞ |
| **History** | Keep last N / Keep all | Keep last 10 |
| **Resource Limits** | Max samples, max instances | 10 samples |

## 2.4 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.4.1 End-to-End Latency Model

Total message latency $L_{\text{total}}$ from publisher to subscriber:

$$L_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{serialize}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{deserialize}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{serialize}}$ — time to convert data to wire format (μs)
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ — wait time in OS buffer (variable)
- $T_{\text{transmit}}$ — actual network send time
- $T_{\text{propagate}}$ — physical link delay
- $T_{\text{deserialize}}$ — parsing back into object

### 2.4.2 Throughput Model

For a topic with message size $S$ bytes and publish frequency $f$ Hz:

$$\text{Throughput} = f \times S \quad [\text{bytes/s}]$$

For aggregated throughput of $N$ topics:

$$T_{\text{agg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \times S_i$$

### 2.4.3 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

Maximum data "in flight" on a link:

$$\text{BDP} = B \times RTT$$

where $B$ is bandwidth (bits/s) and $RTT$ is round-trip time (s). This defines the TCP window required for full link utilization.

### 2.4.4 Hard Real-Time Deadline Check

A control loop with period $T_c$ is schedulable if:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{\text{bound}}$$

For Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):

$$U_{\text{bound}} = N \left(2^{1/N} - 1\right)$$

### 2.4.5 Jitter Bound

Jitter $J$ is the deviation from nominal period:

$$J = \max(\Delta t) - \min(\Delta t)$$

For ROS 2 with DDS, deterministic jitter is bounded by:

$$J_{\text{ROS2}} \leq T_{\text{sched}} + T_{\text{context-switch}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{ser}} + T_{\text{q}} + T_{\text{tr}} + T_{\text{pr}} + T_{\text{des}}$ | Latency budgeting |
| 2 | $T_{\text{agg}} = \sum f_i S_i$ | Bandwidth planning |
| 3 | $\text{BDP} = B \times RTT$ | Link utilization |
| 4 | $U_{\text{RMS}} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ | Real-time schedulability |
| 5 | $J = \max(\Delta t) - \min(\Delta t)$ | Jitter analysis |
| 6 | $M: \mathcal{P} \times \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ | Communication mapping |
| 7 | $\text{CPU Load} = \sum C_i/T_i$ | Resource utilization |
| 8 | $T_{\text{reply}} = L_{\text{req}} + T_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{res}}$ | Service round-trip |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural robots** (e.g., John Deere autonomous tractors): use DDS for multi-camera fusion with deterministic QoS.
- **Autonomous mining trucks** (Caterpillar, Komatsu): ROS 2 + micro-ROS for low-level ECUs and high-level planners.
- **Search & rescue drones**: use LCM for low-bandwidth, lossy-link telemetry.
- **Mars rovers (NASA)**: use a custom OMG DDS-based middleware for interplanetary delay tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Middleware is the **enabler of modularity** in field robotics. Without it, no team of engineers could realistically build, debug, and scale a heterogeneous multi-sensor field robot.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Bound

We derive the worst-case latency for a publish-subscribe message passing through a robotic middleware stack with multiple queues.

### Step 1 — Define the components

Let:
- $S$ = message size in bytes
- $B$ = link bandwidth in bytes/s
- $C_s$ = CPU cycles for serialization, $f_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU frequency
- $C_d$ = CPU cycles for deserialization
- $W$ = worst-case queue wait time
- $d$ = physical propagation delay (negligible for onboard, ~5 μs/km for wireless)

### Step 2 — Serialization time

The time to encode a structured message into a wire format (e.g., CDR, Protobuf):

$$T_{\text{serialize}} = \frac{C_s}{f_{\text{CPU}}}$$

For a typical ROS message of $S = 1024$ bytes, $C_s \approx 5000$ cycles, $f_{\text{CPU}} = 2$ GHz:

$$T_{\text{serialize}} = \frac{5000}{2 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \, \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Queue wait time

For a priority-scheduled queue with $k$ higher-priority messages in front:

$$T_{\text{queue}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} T_{j,\text{exec}}$$

In a worst-case with $k$ messages of equal size:

$$T_{\text{queue}} = k \cdot \frac{S}{B_{\text{int}}}$$

where $B_{\text{int}}$ is the internal IPC bandwidth (e.g., shared memory at ~10 GB/s).

### Step 4 — Transmission time

$$T_{\text{transmit}} = \frac{S}{B}$$

For Ethernet at $B = 1$ Gb/s = $1.25 \times 10^8$ bytes/s and $S = 1024$ B:

$$T_{\text{transmit}} = \frac{1024}{1.25 \times 10^8} = 8.192 \, \mu s$$

### Step 5 — Propagation delay

For a wired link of length $\ell$ (in meters) and propagation speed $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$T_{\text{propagate}} = \frac{\ell}{v}$$

For $\ell = 10$ m onboard harness:

$$T_{\text{propagate}} = \frac{10}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.05 \, \mu s$$

### Step 6 — Deserialization time

$$T_{\text{deserialize}} = \frac{C_d}{f_{\text{CPU}}}$$

Assuming $C_d = 7000$ cycles:

$$T_{\text{deserialize}} = \frac{7000}{2 \times 10^9} = 3.5 \, \mu s$$

### Step 7 — Total latency bound

Summing all components (assuming $T_{\text{queue}} = 10$ μs for low-priority backlog):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{serialize}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{deserialize}} \\
&= 2.5 + 10 + 8.192 + 0.05 + 3.5 \\
&= 24.242 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within a 10 ms (10,000 μs) control loop, confirming middleware feasibility for 100 Hz control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth Requirement for Multi-Sensor Field Robot

A field robot uses:
- 1× Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR → 1.3 MB/s
- 1× Stereo camera (VGA @ 30 fps, raw) → 27 MB/s
- 1× IMU @ 200 Hz → 1.2 KB/s
- 1× GPS @ 10 Hz → 0.4 KB/s

### Step 1 — Per-sensor throughput

$$T_{\text{agg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \times S_i$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LiDAR}} &= 1.3 \times 10^6 \, \text{B/s} \\
T_{\text{cam}} &= 640 \times 480 \times 3 \times 30 = 2.7648 \times 10^7 \, \text{B/s} \\
T_{\text{IMU}} &= 200 \times 6 = 1200 \, \text{B/s} \\
T_{\text{GPS}} &= 10 \times 40 = 400 \, \text{B/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{agg}} &= 1.3 \times 10^6 + 2.7648 \times 10^7 + 1200 + 400 \\
&\approx 29.08 \, \text{MB/s} \\
&\approx 232.6 \, \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Implication

A standard 1 GbE (1000 Mbps) onboard network can comfortably support this, leaving ~77% headroom for control traffic.

## 3.3 Real-Time Schedulability Example

Three middleware tasks on a field robot:
- T1: LiDAR processing, $C_1 = 5$ ms, $T_1 = 100$ ms
- T2: IMU fusion, $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 20$ ms
- T3: Vision processing, $C_3 = 15$ ms, $T_3 = 200$ ms

### Step 1 — Compute utilization

$$U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{5}{100} + \frac{2}{20} + \frac{15}{200} = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.075 = 0.225$$

### Step 2 — RMS bound for N=3

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

### Step 3 — Schedulability verdict

$$U = 0.225 \leq 0.7798 = U_{\text{bound}} \quad \therefore \text{Schedulable}$$

The system meets hard real-time deadlines under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Middleware Frameworks

| Feature | ROS 1 | ROS 2 | DDS (RTPS) | LCM | YARP | OROCOS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Centralized Master | Distributed (DDS) | Distributed | UDP Multicast | Peer-to-peer | Component-based |
| **Real-Time** | No | Yes (with RT-Preempt) | Yes | Best-effort | Partial | Yes |
| **Discovery** | Master | Auto (peer) | Auto (peer) | Manual | Manual | Manual |
| **Transport** | TCP/UDP | DDS-RTPS | UDP | UDP | TCP/UDP | CORBA/shared |
| **QoS** | Limited | Full | Full | None | Limited | Limited |
| **Language** | C++/Python | C++/Python/Rust | C/C++/Java | C/C++/Java | C++ | C++ |
| **Field Robotic Use** | Research | Production | Defense/Auto | Drones | Humanoid | Industrial |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Middleware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appL["APPLICATION LAYER\nMission Planner / AI / Behavior Trees"]
    svcL["MIDDLEWARE SERVICES LAYER\nDiscovery / Parameter Server / TF Tree"]
    comL["COMMUNICATION FABRIC LAYER\nPublish Subscribe Service Action"]
    osL["OS ABSTRACTION LAYER\nLinux RT-Preempt / FreeRTOS / VxWorks"]
    hwL["HARDWARE DRIVER LAYER\nLiDAR / IMU / Camera / Motor Drivers"]

    appL --> svcL
    svcL --> comL
    comL --> osL
    osL --> hwL

    pub["PUBLISHER NODE\nSensor Driver A"]
    sub["SUBSCRIBER NODE\nPerception Module B"]
    mst["DDS DOMAIN PARTICIPANT\nAuto Discovery"]

    pub -->|"Topic: /lidar/points\nQoS: Reliable"| mst
    mst -->|"Filtered Subscribers"| sub
```

## 4.2 ROS 2 Node Communication Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camDrv["Camera Driver Node"]
    lidarDrv["LiDAR Driver Node"]
    imuDrv["IMU Driver Node"]
    perc["Perception Node\nSubscriber of all sensors"]
    plan["Planner Node\nClient of GetPath Service"]
    ctrl["Controller Node\nAction Client move_base"]
    motDrv["Motor Driver Node\nAction Server"]

    camDrv -->|"/camera/image_raw\nBest Effort"| perc
    lidarDrv -->|"/lidar/points\nReliable Keep Last 5"| perc
    imuDrv -->|"/imu/data\nReliable"| perc
    perc -->|"/obstacles\nReliable"| plan
    plan -->|"Service GetPath"| ctrl
    ctrl -->|"Action move_base"| motDrv
```

## 4.3 Multi-Robot Field Coordination via DDS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["ROBOT 1\nOnboard Computer"]
    R2["ROBOT 2\nOnboard Computer"]
    R3["ROBOT 3\nOnboard Computer"]
    GW["GATEWAY / BASE STATION\nMission Coordinator"]
    CLOUD["CLOUD LOGGER\nMQTT Bridge"]

    R1 -->|"DDS-RTPS over WiFi"| GW
    R2 -->|"DDS-RTPS over WiFi"| GW
    R3 -->|"DDS-RTPS over Mesh Radio"| GW
    GW -->|"MQTT over 4G"| CLOUD

    disco["DDS Auto Discovery\nUDP Multicast 239.255.0.1\nPort 7400"]
    R1 -.- disco
    R2 -.- disco
    R3 -.- disco
    GW -.- disco
```

## 4.4 Middleware Message Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Application Code\nCalls publish method"]
    B["Serialization\nMessage to bytes via CDR"]
    C["QoS Policy Check\nReliability Deadline History"]
    D["Transport Layer\nUDP or Shared Memory"]
    E["Network or Bus\nEthernet CAN Serial"]
    F["Receive Buffer\nOS Socket Queue"]
    G["Deserialization\nBytes to Object"]
    H["Subscriber Callback\nUser Function Invoked"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Define robotic middleware. List any two communication paradigms supported by it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Robotic middleware is a software layer that provides standardized communication, hardware abstraction, and modular integration services between heterogeneous robot components (sensors, actuators, controllers) and high-level applications, without requiring modules to know each other's internal implementation.

**Two communication paradigms (1 mark):**
1. **Publish–Subscribe (Topic-based):** Asynchronous, many-to-many data streaming (e.g., sensor topics in ROS).
2. **Service–Client (Request–Response):** Synchronous, one-to-one RPC-style calls (e.g., map save service).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Quality of Service (QoS) in middleware? Give two examples of QoS policies used in field robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** QoS is a set of configurable policies that govern the delivery guarantees, timing, and resource limits of messages exchanged via middleware.

**Two examples (2 marks):**
1. **Reliability:** Best-Effort (drop on congestion) vs. Reliable (retransmit until ACK). Critical for control commands.
2. **Deadline:** Maximum allowed interval between consecutive message samples. Ensures timely data for real-time control loops.
3. *(Alternative)* **Durability:** Whether late-joining subscribers receive past messages — important for parameter initialization in multi-robot startups.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of robotic middleware with a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare ROS 1 and ROS 2 middleware architectures. Why is ROS 2 preferred for field robotics applications? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a) [7 marks]

The layered architecture (also called the **middleware stack**) organizes robotic software into **5 abstraction layers**, each insulating the layer above from the complexity below:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Driver Layer (1 mark):**
- Provides low-level access to sensors (LiDAR, IMU, cameras) and actuators (motors, servos).
- Examples: `libusb` for USB devices, `socketcan` for CAN bus, V4L2 for cameras.
- Translates hardware register reads/writes into standardized C/C++ APIs.

**Layer 2 — OS Abstraction Layer (1 mark):**
- Hides OS-specific details (Linux vs. RTOS).
- Manages processes, threads, scheduling, memory.
- Examples: Linux, RT-Preempt patch, FreeRTOS, VxWorks.

**Layer 3 — Communication Fabric (1.5 marks):**
- Core of the middleware. Implements message passing using:
  - TCP/UDP sockets
  - Shared memory (intra-process)
  - ZeroMQ, DDS-RTPS, Protobuf serialization
- Provides primitives: topics, services, actions.

**Layer 4 — Middleware Services (1.5 marks):**
- **Discovery** (auto-discovery via UDP multicast in DDS).
- **Parameter server** (centralized/distributed configuration).
- **TF (transform) tree** for coordinate frame management.
- **Time synchronization** across nodes.

**Layer 5 — Application Layer (1 mark):**
- Mission planning, behavior trees, machine learning inference, user interfaces.
- Calls middleware APIs without knowing underlying hardware.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A neat 5-layer block diagram with arrows showing downward dependency (refer Section 4.1).

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b) [7 marks]

| Feature | ROS 1 (1 mark) | ROS 2 (1 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Middleware core** | Custom XML-RPC + TCPROS | DDS-RTPS (standardized) |
| **Master node** | Required (roscore) | None (peer-to-peer) |
| **Discovery** | Master-mediated | Auto via DDS |
| **Real-time support** | No native | Yes (with RT-Preempt) |
| **Embedded support** | Limited | micro-ROS for MCUs |
| **Security** | None | SROS2 (encryption, auth) |
| **Multi-robot** | Difficult | Native (DDS domains) |
| **QoS** | Basic | Full DDS QoS policies |

**Why ROS 2 is preferred for field robotics (5 marks):**

1. **No Single Point of Failure:** ROS 1's `roscore` master is a SPOF — if it crashes, the entire robot stops. ROS 2 uses peer-to-peer DDS, critical for outdoor field operation where restart may be impossible. **(1 mark)**

2. **Real-time Determinism:** Field applications (mining, agriculture) require bounded latency. ROS 2 with RT-Preempt kernel + DDS achieves sub-millisecond jitter, enabling 1 kHz control loops. **(1 mark)**

3. **Multi-Robot Native Support:** DDS domains allow partitioning of communication. A swarm of field robots can share a discovery namespace without centralized coordination. **(1 mark)**

4. **Embedded Deployment:** `micro-ROS` enables middleware on STM32-class MCUs, essential for sensor nodes on agricultural or Mars-rover-scale systems. **(1 mark)**

5. **Security (SROS2):** Encrypted DDS communication protects against cyberattacks on critical infrastructure (autonomous tractors, defense vehicles). **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the three communication paradigms used in robotic middleware: Publish-Subscribe, Service-Client, and Action. Give one field-robotics use case for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field robot has 4 sensors. Compute the total bandwidth requirement, and determine whether a 1 GbE onboard network is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

| Sensor | Rate | Data per sample |
|---|---|---|
| 2D LiDAR | 20 Hz | 5000 points × 4 bytes = 20 KB |
| IMU | 200 Hz | 12 bytes |
| Thermal camera | 10 Hz | 320 × 240 × 2 bytes = 150 KB |
| GPS | 5 Hz | 80 bytes |

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a) [7 marks]

**1. Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub) — 2.5 marks**

- **Mechanism:** Publishers write to a named *topic*; subscribers receive asynchronously. Many-to-many, decoupled.
- **Diagram (ASCII):** Publisher → Topic `/sensor/lidar` → Subscriber 1, Subscriber 2, Subscriber 3 (fan-out).
- **Use case:** Streaming LiDAR point clouds to perception and SLAM modules simultaneously.

**2. Service-Client (Request-Response) — 2.5 marks**

- **Mechanism:** Client sends a *request* message; server returns a *response*. Synchronous, blocking.
- **Diagram (ASCII):** Client → Request → Server → Processing → Response → Client.
- **Use case:** Requesting the current saved map from the map server for path planning.

**3. Action (Long-Running Goal) — 2 marks**

- **Mechanism:** Client sends a *goal*; server provides *feedback* periodically and a terminal *result*. Preemptable.
- **Diagram (ASCII):** Goal → Server (with periodic Feedback) → Preempt (optional) → Result.
- **Use case:** Sending a `move_base` navigation goal to autonomously traverse a field while monitoring progress and ability to cancel if obstacle appears.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Per-sensor throughput (1 mark each = 4 marks)**

$$T_i = f_i \times S_i$$

- LiDAR: $T_1 = 20 \times 20{,}000 = 400{,}000$ B/s = 0.4 MB/s
- IMU: $T_2 = 200 \times 12 = 2{,}400$ B/s = 0.0024 MB/s
- Thermal camera: $T_3 = 10 \times 150{,}000 = 1{,}500{,}000$ B/s = 1.5 MB/s
- GPS: $T_4 = 5 \times 80 = 400$ B/s ≈ 0.0004 MB/s

**Step 2 — Aggregate (1 mark)**

$$T_{\text{agg}} = 400{,}000 + 2{,}400 + 1{,}500{,}000 + 400 = 1{,}902{,}800 \, \text{B/s} \approx 1.9 \, \text{MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mbps (1 mark)**

$$1.9 \, \text{MB/s} \times 8 = 15.2 \, \text{Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with 1 GbE (1 mark)**

1 GbE = **1000 Mbps**.

Utilization:

$$U = \frac{15.2}{1000} \times 100\% = 1.52\%$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The 1 GbE onboard network is **vastly sufficient** (overhead <2%), leaving ~98% bandwidth for control traffic, video streaming, and future sensor additions. The middleware can comfortably aggregate all sensor data without congestion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing middleware with operating system** — Middleware is *above* the OS, not a replacement. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Writing "ROS = middleware" without explanation** — ROS is a *framework that uses* middleware (DDS in ROS 2). (–0.5 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting units in bandwidth calculations** — Mixing MB/s with Mbps costs full marks. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not mentioning QoS** when discussing real-time — Real-time guarantees come from QoS policies, not just from the OS. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions — KTU evaluators expect at least one schematic per sub-part. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 6. **Confusing Publish-Subscribe with Service** — Pub/Sub is *asynchronous* and *many-to-many*; Service is *synchronous* and *one-to-one*. (–0.5 marks)

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Middleware** = software layer providing standardized communication, hardware abstraction, and modular integration for robotic systems.
- **Three core paradigms:** **Pub/Sub** (async, streaming), **Service/Client** (sync, RPC), **Action** (async, with feedback).
- **ROS 1** = master-based, no real-time; **ROS 2** = DDS-based, real-time capable, embedded support via micro-ROS.
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** is the OMG standard for distributed real-time middleware; provides **auto-discovery** and **QoS**.
- **QoS policies** control: Reliability, Durability, Deadline, Lifespan, History, Resource Limits.
- **End-to-end latency** = $T_{\text{ser}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{des}}$.
- **Aggregate throughput** = $\sum f_i \times S_i$ across all topics/sensors.
- **Rate Monotonic bound** for $N$ tasks: $U_{\text{RMS}} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ — must hold for hard real-time schedulability.
- **Jitter** = $\max(\Delta t) - \min(\Delta t)$; bounded for deterministic control.
- **Field robotics requires** determinism, fault-tolerance, multi-robot support, and embedded MCU integration — all provided by ROS 2 + DDS.
- **Common middleware frameworks:** ROS 2, DDS, LCM, YARP, OROCOS, MQTT (cloud-bridge).
- **Discovery** can be centralized (master) or decentralized (multicast/peer).
- **Serialization formats:** CDR (DDS), Protobuf, MessagePack, ROS msg.
- **Bandwidth planning is critical** — always sum all sensor data rates and compare with link capacity (1 GbE, 100 MbE, CAN, etc.).
- **micro-ROS** enables middleware on resource-constrained MCUs (STM32, ESP32) — vital for distributed sensor networks on field robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering middleware questions, **always** (1) name the paradigm, (2) draw a block diagram, (3) mention QoS/real-time implications, and (4) give a field-robotics example. This 4-part structure consistently scores full marks under KTU 2024 Scheme rubrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Control Formalisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT601 - FIELD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alternative Control Formalisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — ALTERNATIVE CONTROL FORMALISMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Field Robotics (PCRAT601)**, an **Alternative Control Formalism** refers to any non-classical, non-linear, or non-model-based control architecture that replaces (or augments) the traditional sense → model → plan → act (deliberative) pipeline. These formalisms are tailored to the harsh, uncertain, partially observable, and dynamic environments encountered by field robots (agricultural, mining, underwater, planetary, forestry, and military robots).

The principal alternative control formalisms covered under this module are:

1. **Reactive / Behavior-Based Control** (e.g., Subsumption Architecture by Rodney Brooks).
2. **Potential Field Method** (Attractive + Repulsive fields).
3. **Motor Schema Theory** (Arkin, 1987).
4. **Fuzzy Logic Control**.
5. **Neural Network-Based Control**.
6. **Finite State Machine (FSM) based task coordination**.
7. **Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architectures** (e.g., AuRA, 3-Tier, CLARAty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The university expects students to clearly contrast the *deliberative* paradigm with *reactive/behavior-based* paradigms, with special emphasis on **Subsumption Architecture**, **Potential Fields**, and **Hybrid Architectures** as the most frequently examined topics.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car through dense Kerala traffic.

- A **deliberative controller** would be like a self-driving Tesla computing a complete route, modelling every other car, predicting their motion, and re-planning every second — accurate, but slow and brittle.
- An **alternative (reactive) control formalism** is like an experienced human driver: when the vehicle ahead brakes, you brake; when a pedestrian jumps out, you swerve. **No world model, no planning, just direct sensor-to-action coupling.** That is the essence of these alternative formalisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Field robots must operate with reaction times in the order of **10–100 ms**, which is why **Behavior-Based and Potential Field controllers** are preferred over heavy deliberative planners.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Control Formalisms

The control architecture of any mobile robot can be classified along a **spectrum of deliberation**:

| Level | Formalism | World Model | Planning | Reaction Speed | Robustness |
|-------|-----------|-------------|----------|----------------|------------|
| 0 | Pure Deliberative (STRIPS, A*) | Required | Yes | Slow | Low |
| 1 | **Subsumption (Brooks)** | None | No | Very Fast | Very High |
| 2 | **Potential Field** | Implicit | No | Fast | Medium |
| 3 | **Motor Schema (Arkin)** | Partial | Low | Fast | High |
| 4 | **Fuzzy Logic** | Linguistic | Rule-based | Medium | High |
| 5 | **Neural Network** | Learned | Implicit | Fast (after training) | High |
| 6 | **Hybrid (3-Tier, AuRA)** | Partial | Yes | Medium | High |

---

## 2.2 Subsumption Architecture (Rodney Brooks, 1986)

### 2.2.1 Concept

The **Subsumption Architecture** is the canonical **behavior-based** control formalism. It decomposes a robot controller into a hierarchy of **layers of competence**, where each layer is a self-contained **augmented finite state machine (AFSM)**.

The ordering is from **bottom (lowest-level reflexes)** to **top (highest-level deliberation)**:

1. **Layer 0** – *Avoid* (obstacle avoidance)
2. **Layer 1** – *Wander* (random exploration)
3. **Layer 2** – *Follow Corridor*
4. **Layer 3** – *Map Building*
5. **Layer 4** – *Notice Objects*
6. **Layer 5** – *Plan Routes*

### 2.2.2 Operating Mechanism

- Higher layers **subsume** (suppress/inhibit) the output of lower layers via two operators:
  - **Inhibition ($\mathbf{I}$)** – Completely blocks the lower layer's output.
  - **Suppression ($\mathbf{S}$)** – Overwrites (replaces) the lower layer's output.
- No central world model exists. Each layer directly reads sensors and writes to actuators.

$$v_{final} = v_{L_n} \oplus_{Suppression/Inhibition} v_{L_{n-1}} \oplus \ldots \oplus v_{L_0}$$

where $\oplus$ is the subsumption operator and $v_{L_i}$ is the velocity (or signal) from layer $i$.

### 2.2.3 Why It Works for Field Robotics

- **Reactive and time-bounded** — guaranteed response in $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
- **Inherently robust** — sensor failure at a higher layer only loses the higher behaviour; lower reflexes still work.
- **Incremental development** — new layers can be added without disturbing tested ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disadvantage:** Subsumption alone cannot handle **long-horizon, goal-directed missions** (e.g., "deliver 5 kg of soil to coordinates (50, 30)"). This is where hybrid architectures come in.

---

## 2.3 Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)

### 2.3.1 Concept

The robot is treated as a point moving in a **potential field** $U(q)$ where $q = (x, y)$ is its position. The field is constructed as the **sum of attractive and repulsive potentials**.

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Formulation

**Attractive potential** (towards goal $q_{goal}$):

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \cdot \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^{2}$$

**Repulsive potential** (away from obstacle $q_{obs}$):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Total potential** (additive superposition):

$$U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep,i}(q)$$

**Force on the robot** (negative gradient of potential):

$$\vec{F}(q) = -\nabla U_{total}(q) = -\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial x}\hat{i} - \frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial y}\hat{j}$$

**Velocity command** (proportional to force):

$$v_{cmd} = K \cdot \vec{F}(q), \quad \omega_{cmd} = \text{atan2}(F_y, F_x) - \theta_{current}$$

### 2.3.3 Limitation: Local Minima

A classical problem is the **local minima trap**, where $F \rightarrow 0$ at non-goal points. Solutions include:

- **Random walk** when $\vert \vec{F} \vert < \epsilon$.
- **Virtual obstacle / virtual goal** techniques.
- **Harmonic potential fields** (Codouury & Noreils) which are guaranteed minima-free.
- **Navigation functions** (Koditschek & Rimon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Potential fields are the de-facto standard for **local obstacle avoidance** in autonomous cars, AGVs in factories, and drone swarms. They are computationally cheap (suitable for embedded MCUs in field robots).

---

## 2.4 Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987)

A schema is a **motor behaviour** that produces a **vector** output in 2D. Multiple schemas are **summed vectorially** to produce the final control.

$$\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \vec{V}_{i}(q)$$

Common schemas:
- **Move-to-goal** (attractive).
- **Avoid-static-obstacle** (repulsive).
- **Avoid-robot** (for multi-robot systems).
- **Stay-on-path** (path following).
- **Noise** (exploratory behaviour).

$w_i$ are the **gains** that adapt to context. The advantage over pure potential fields is the **explicit gain scheduling** capability.

---

## 2.5 Fuzzy Logic Control

A fuzzy controller replaces crisp if-then rules with **linguistic rules**:

$$\text{Rule}_i: \text{IF } d = \text{Near} \text{ AND } \theta = \text{Left} \text{ THEN } \omega = \text{High Positive}$$

**Steps:**
1. **Fuzzification** – Convert crisp sensor values to membership values $\mu_{A_i}(x) \in [0, 1]$.
2. **Rule evaluation** – Compute firing strength $\alpha_i = \min(\mu_{A_i}(d), \mu_{B_i}(\theta))$.
3. **Defuzzification** (centroid method):

$$y^{*} = \frac{\sum_{i} \alpha_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i} \alpha_i}$$

> [!TIP]
> Fuzzy logic shines when the robot dynamics are **hard to model mathematically** (e.g., rough-terrain rovers with slip). It is widely used in **agricultural field robots** for weed-vs-crop discrimination.

---

## 2.6 Finite State Machine (FSM) Based Coordination

A field robot mission is encoded as a **directed graph of states** $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ with transitions $T \subseteq S \times S$ triggered by events $E$.

Example mission: *Autonomous Tractor*

| State | Event Trigger | Next State |
|-------|---------------|------------|
| IDLE | START_PRESSED | PLOUGHING |
| PLOUGHING | ROW_END | TURNING |
| TURNING | HEADING_ALIGNED | PLOUGHING |
| PLOUGHING | FUEL_LOW | RETURNING |
| RETURNING | NEAR_BASE | IDLE |

---

## 2.7 Hybrid (3-Tier) Architectures

To overcome the limitations of pure reactive control, modern field robots use a **3-tier hybrid architecture**:

1. **Planner Tier** (slow, deliberative) — global mission planner, path planner.
2. **Sequencer Tier** (medium) — FSM-based task scheduler.
3. **Control Tier** (fast, reactive) — Potential field / Subsumption / Fuzzy local controller.

Examples: **3T (Bonasso et al., 1997)**, **CLARAty (Volpe et al., NASA JPL)**, **AuRA (Arkin, 1987)**.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Physical Meaning / Units |
|-------------------|------------|--------------------------|
| Attractive potential | $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert q - q_g \vert^{2}$ | Energy pushing robot to goal [J] |
| Repulsive potential | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$ | Energy pushing robot away from obstacles [J] |
| Total potential | $U = U_{att} + \sum U_{rep,i}$ | Superposition principle [J] |
| Force on robot | $\vec{F} = -\nabla U$ | Negative gradient of potential [N] |
| Velocity command | $v = K \vec{F}$ | Proportional controller [m/s] |
| Motor schema sum | $\vec{V} = \sum w_i \vec{V}_i$ | Weighted vector superposition [m/s] |
| Subsumption operator | $v_{out} = v_{L_n} \oplus v_{L_{n-1}}$ | Inhibition / Suppression logic [binary] |
| Fuzzy centroid | $y^{*} = \frac{\sum \alpha_i y_i}{\sum \alpha_i}$ | Centre of mass defuzzification [unitless] |
| Control loop period | $T_{ctrl} \in [10, 100]$ ms | Field robot reaction budget [ms] |
| Obstacle distance threshold | $\rho_0 \in [0.5, 3.0]$ m | Tuning parameter [m] |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** When writing potential-field answers, **always** state the negative-gradient step explicitly: $\vec{F} = -\nabla U$. Skipping this step is the most common 2-mark deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Repulsive Force Vector

We derive the **repulsive force** on a robot located at $q = (x, y)$ due to an obstacle at $q_{obs} = (x_{obs}, y_{obs})$.

**Step 1: Define the distance from the obstacle**

$$\rho(q) = \sqrt{(x - x_{obs})^{2} + (y - y_{obs})^{2}}$$

**Step 2: State the repulsive potential (for $\rho \le \rho_0$)**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$**

We use the chain rule. First, note that

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho(q)}$$

Now define $f(\rho) = \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$, so that $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} f(\rho)^{2}$.

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}$$

Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \cdot 2 f(\rho) \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial \rho} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify**

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} f(\rho) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \right) \cdot \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = -k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot (x - x_{obs})$$

**Step 5: Compute the $x$-component of the repulsive force**

$$F_{x, rep} = -\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot (x - x_{obs})$$

**Step 6: By symmetry, the $y$-component**

$$F_{y, rep} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot (y - y_{obs})$$

**Step 7: Express the magnitude of the repulsive force**

$$\vert \vec{F}_{rep} \vert = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \rho = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The force magnitude $\propto 1/\rho$ near the obstacle and **vanishes at $\rho = \rho_0$**, which is exactly the intended behaviour — the influence of the obstacle should die out beyond $\rho_0$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Formalisms on a Field Robotics Mission

| Mission Requirement | Subsumption | Potential Field | Motor Schema | Fuzzy Logic | Hybrid (3-Tier) |
|----------------------|-------------|----------------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| Obstacle avoidance (reactive) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Goal reaching (global) | ★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Handling dynamic obstacles | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Robustness to sensor noise | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Computational cost | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Multi-robot coordination | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| Long-horizon planning | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Unknown unstructured terrain | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** The 14-mark analytical question frequently asks: *"Justify which control formalism is best suited for [specific field robot application]."* Use this matrix as your evidence bank.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A field robot is at $q = (2, 3)$ m, the goal is at $q_g = (10, 10)$ m, and an obstacle is at $q_{obs} = (4, 4)$ m with influence radius $\rho_0 = 2$ m. Compute the total force when $k_{att} = 1$ and $k_{rep} = 5$. Take $q = (2, 3)$ as the evaluation point.

**Step 1: Distance to goal**

$$\rho_g = \sqrt{(10-2)^{2} + (10-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{64+49} = \sqrt{113} \approx 10.63 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Attractive force**

$$\vec{F}_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_g) = -1 \cdot ((2-10), (3-10)) = (8, 7) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Distance to obstacle**

$$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(2-4)^{2} + (3-4)^{2}} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$

Since $\rho_{obs} > \rho_0 = 2$ m? **No** (2.236 > 2, just outside), so we are **just at the boundary**. Taking $\rho_{obs} = 2$ m for the boundary case:

$$\vec{F}_{rep} = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}(q-q_{obs}) = 5 \cdot 0 \cdot \ldots = 0 \text{ N}$$

If the robot were slightly closer, e.g., $\rho = 1.5$ m, then

$$\vec{F}_{rep} = 5 \left(\frac{1}{1.5}-\frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{1}{2.25}((2-4),(3-4)) = 5 \cdot 0.167 \cdot 0.444 \cdot (-2,-1) = (-0.74, -0.37) \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Total force**

$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{att} + \vec{F}_{rep} = (8 - 0.74,\ 7 - 0.37) = (7.26,\ 6.63) \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude and direction**

$$\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert = \sqrt{7.26^{2} + 6.63^{2}} \approx 9.83 \text{ N}$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(6.63 / 7.26) \approx 42.4^{\circ}$$

The robot would move forward and to the right, away from the obstacle, towards the goal. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subsumption Architecture Layered Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer5["LAYER 5  PLAN ROUTES"] --> inh5{SUPPRESS or INHIBIT}
    layer4["LAYER 4  NOTICE OBJECTS"] --> inh4{SUPPRESS or INHIBIT}
    layer3["LAYER 3  MAP BUILDING"] --> inh3{SUPPRESS or INHIBIT}
    layer2["LAYER 2  FOLLOW CORRIDOR"] --> inh2{SUPPRESS or INHIBIT}
    layer1["LAYER 1  WANDER"] --> inh1{SUPPRESS or INHIBIT}
    layer0["LAYER 0  AVOID OBSTACLES"] --> actuator["ACTUATOR MOTOR SIGNALS V  AND  W"]
    inh1 --> actuator
    inh2 --> actuator
    inh3 --> actuator
    inh4 --> actuator
    inh5 --> actuator
    sensors["SENSORS SONAR  IR  GPS  CAMERA"] --> layer0
    sensors --> layer1
    sensors --> layer2
    sensors --> layer3
    sensors --> layer4
    sensors --> layer5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Information flows from sensors upwards through progressively more complex layers, and the motor signal bubbles back down with the highest active layer taking precedence through **suppression** or **inhibition**.

---

## 4.2 Potential Field Landscape (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    goal["GOAL COORDINATES QGOAL"] --> ua["U ATTRACTIVE POTENTIAL"]
    obs1["OBSTACLE 1 QOBS1"] --> ur1["U REPULSIVE POTENTIAL 1"]
    obs2["OBSTACLE N QOBSN"] --> urN["U REPULSIVE POTENTIAL N"]
    ua --> sum["TOTAL POTENTIAL U  GRADIENT"]
    ur1 --> sum
    urN --> sum
    sum --> force["FORCE VECTOR  F  EQUALS  NEGATIVE GRADIENT OF U"]
    force --> vel["VELOCITY COMMAND VCMD  AND  WCMD"]
    vel --> robot["FIELD ROBOT"]
    robot --> next["NEXT POSITION Q"]
    next --> obs1
    next --> obs2
    next --> goal
```

---

## 4.3 3-Tier Hybrid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    planner["TIER 1  PLANNER  GLOBAL MISSION  AND  PATH PLANNER  STRIPS  A STAR"] --> seq["TIER 2  SEQUENCER  FSM BASED TASK SCHEDULER"]
    seq --> ctrl["TIER 3  CONTROL  REACTIVE LAYER  POTENTIAL FIELD  SUBSUMPTION  FUZZY"]
    world["WORLD MODEL  PARTIAL  UPDATED BY SENSORS"] --> planner
    world --> ctrl
    sensors["SENSORS LIDAR  GPS  IMU  CAMERA"] --> world
    ctrl --> actuator["ACTUATORS  WHEELS  STEERING  END EFFECTOR"]
    actuator --> env["FIELD ENVIRONMENT"]
    env --> sensors
```

---

## 4.4 FSM Mission Diagram for an Autonomous Field Tractor

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> PLOUGHING: START_PRESSED
    PLOUGHING --> TURNING: ROW_END_DETECTED
    TURNING --> PLOUGHING: HEADING_ALIGNED
    PLOUGHING --> RETURNING: FUEL_BELOW_THRESHOLD
    TURNING --> RETURNING: OBSTACLE_BLOCKED
    RETURNING --> IDLE: AT_BASE
    RETURNING --> PLOUGHING: TASK_RESUMED
    IDLE --> [*]: SHUTDOWN
```

---

## 4.5 Motor Schema Vector Summation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sg["MOVE TO GOAL  VG"] --> ws["WEIGHTED SUM V = WG VG  +  WA VA  +  WP VP  +  WN VN"]
    sa["AVOID STATIC OBSTACLE  VA"] --> ws
    sp["STAY ON PATH  VP"] --> ws
    sn["EXPLORATION NOISE  VN"] --> ws
    ws --> output["VCMD  AND  WCMD TO ROBOT"]
    sr["AVOID OTHER ROBOTS  VR"] --> ws
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define Subsumption Architecture. List its key advantages for field robotics.**

**Model Answer:**
Subsumption Architecture is a behavior-based, layered control formalism proposed by **Rodney Brooks (1986)**. It decomposes the controller into hierarchical layers of competence, where higher layers can suppress or inhibit the output of lower layers.

**Key advantages for field robotics:**
1. **No world model required** — ideal for unstructured, unknown terrain.
2. **Real-time response** — bounded $\mathcal{O}(1)$ computation per cycle.
3. **High robustness** — sensor failure in a higher layer does not cripple the robot; lower reflexes still operate.
4. **Incremental development** — new behaviours can be added as new top layers.
5. **No deliberative planning bottleneck** — direct sensor-to-actuator coupling.

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the concept of attractive and repulsive potentials in robot navigation. State the formula for the total force.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)** treats the robot as a point in a 2D potential field, where the **goal** generates an attractive well and **obstacles** generate repulsive hills.

**Attractive potential:**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^{2}$$

**Repulsive potential** (effective within $\rho_0$):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$$

**Total potential** is the superposition:

$$U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep,i}(q)$$

**Total force on robot** is the negative gradient:

$$\vec{F}_{total}(q) = -\nabla U_{total}(q)$$

This force is mapped to linear and angular velocity commands via a proportional controller.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Subsumption Architecture** for a field robot. Discuss its salient features, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Potential Field Method** for obstacle avoidance. Derive the expressions for the attractive force, repulsive force, and total force. State two limitations and one remedy. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Subsumption Architecture (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986) is a layered, behavior-based reactive control formalism. The robot's intelligence emerges from the **horizontal and vertical interactions of simple task-achieving modules**, with no central world model and no explicit planning.

**Typical layers (in order of increasing competence):**

1. Layer 0: *Avoid obstacles* — highest priority, immediate reflex.
2. Layer 1: *Wander* — random exploration.
3. Layer 2: *Follow corridor / stay on path*.
4. Layer 3: *Identify landmarks*.
5. Layer 4: *Build a partial map*.
6. Layer 5: *Plan and monitor mission goals*.

**Key Operators:**
- **Inhibition ($\mathbf{I}$)** — a higher layer *prevents* the lower layer's output from reaching the actuator.
- **Suppression ($\mathbf{S}$)** — a higher layer *replaces* the lower layer's output with its own.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5  PLAN ROUTES"] --> OP{S}
    L4["LAYER 4  NOTICE OBJECTS"] --> OP
    L3["LAYER 3  MAP BUILDING"] --> OP
    L2["LAYER 2  FOLLOW CORRIDOR"] --> OP
    L1["LAYER 1  WANDER"] --> OP
    L0["LAYER 0  AVOID OBSTACLES"] --> OP
    OP --> M["MOTOR OUTPUT V  AND  W"]
```

**Salient Features:**

1. **No world model** — modules read sensors and write actuator commands directly.
2. **Distributed representation** — knowledge is implicit in the interconnections.
3. **Layered competence** — each layer adds capability, not replacement.
4. **Finite State Automata based** — each layer is an AFSM (Augmented FSM) with registers, timers, and message-passing.

**Advantages:**
- Guaranteed real-time performance.
- Robust to sensor noise and partial failure.
- Easy to incrementally add new behaviours.
- Cheaper computationally (no SLAM or planner required for low-level control).

**Limitations:**
- Difficult to specify global goals.
- No long-horizon memory or planning.
- Behaviour arbitration can produce oscillations.
- Cannot reason symbolically about mission objectives.

---

#### (b) Potential Field Method — Full Derivation (7 Marks)

**Attractive potential** (creates a paraboloid well around $q_{goal}$):

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^{2}$$

**Attractive force** is the negative gradient:

$$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal})$$

**Repulsive potential** (active within $\rho_0$ of an obstacle):

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Repulsive force** (derived in Section 3.1):

$$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} (q - q_{obs})$$

**Total force** by superposition:

$$\vec{F}_{total}(q) = \vec{F}_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_{rep,i}(q)$$

**Velocity command** (proportional control):

$$v_{cmd} = K_{v} \vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert, \qquad \omega_{cmd} = K_{\omega} \left( \text{atan2}(F_y, F_x) - \theta_{current} \right)$$

**Limitation 1: Local Minima.** The robot gets stuck in a configuration where $F_{total} = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$ (e.g., when the goal is occluded by obstacles). **Remedy:** Add a *random walk* when $\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert < \epsilon$, or use *navigation functions* (Koditschek & Rimon) which are guaranteed to be minima-free.

**Limitation 2: Oscillations in narrow corridors.** The robot may oscillate between two close walls due to alternating repulsive forces. **Remedy:** Increase $\rho_0$ to widen the influence zone, or apply a low-pass filter on the velocity command.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **deliberative, reactive, and hybrid** control architectures in field robotics. Which is most suitable for an autonomous mining truck, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Motor Schema Theory** (Arkin) and **Fuzzy Logic Control** as alternative control formalisms. Show how fuzzy inference produces a steering command from distance and heading error. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Deliberative vs Reactive vs Hybrid (7 Marks)

| Feature | Deliberative | Reactive | Hybrid (3-Tier) |
|---------|--------------|----------|-----------------|
| World model | Required (full) | None | Partial |
| Planning horizon | Long | None | Long + short |
| Reaction time | Slow (seconds) | Fast (10–100 ms) | Fast (Tier 3) |
| Robustness | Low (brittle) | High | High |
| Computational cost | High | Low | Medium |
| Best for | Structured, known | Unknown, simple tasks | Mixed, real missions |

**Why Hybrid is best for Autonomous Mining Trucks:**
- Mining involves **structured mine roads** (need global path planning — Tier 1).
- The truck must **avoid haul trucks, personnel, and dynamic obstacles** in real-time (Tier 3 — Potential Field / Subsumption).
- Missions span hours with multiple loading/dumping cycles (Tier 2 — FSM sequencer).
- Hybrid gives the *deliberation* of the planner and the *reflexes* of the reactive layer — exactly what an autonomous Komatsu 930E or Caterpillar 793F requires.

---

#### (b) Motor Schema and Fuzzy Logic (7 Marks)

**Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987):**
Each behaviour is a **schema** producing a **2D vector** $\vec{V}_i(q)$. The total motor command is a **gain-weighted vector sum**:

$$\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \vec{V}_i(q)$$

where $w_i$ is the perceptual gain (can be modulated online). For example:
- $\vec{V}_{move\text{-}to\text{-}goal} = k_g (q_{goal} - q)$.
- $\vec{V}_{avoid\text{-}obstacle} = k_a \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert q - q_{obs} \vert^{2}}$ (radial repulsive).

**Fuzzy Logic Control:**

A fuzzy controller uses linguistic IF-THEN rules. Consider two inputs and one output:

- Input 1: Distance to obstacle $d \in [0, 5]$ m, with linguistic sets **{Near, Medium, Far}**.
- Input 2: Heading error $\theta \in [-90^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}]$, with sets **{Left, Centre, Right}**.
- Output: Steering angle $\delta \in [-30^{\circ}, 30^{\circ}]$, with sets **{HardLeft, SoftLeft, Zero, SoftRight, HardRight}**.

**Sample rules:**
- R1: IF $d$ is *Near* AND $\theta$ is *Left* THEN $\delta$ is *HardRight*.
- R2: IF $d$ is *Medium* AND $\theta$ is *Centre* THEN $\delta$ is *Zero*.
- R3: IF $d$ is *Far* THEN $\delta$ is *Zero* (no correction).

**Inference steps:**

1. **Fuzzification:** Convert crisp $d$ and $\theta$ into membership values $\mu(d), \mu(\theta)$.
2. **Rule firing:** $\alpha_i = \min(\mu_{d_i}, \mu_{\theta_i})$.
3. **Defuzzification** (centroid):

$$\delta^{*} = \frac{\sum_{i} \alpha_i \cdot \delta_i}{\sum_{i} \alpha_i}$$

This produces a smooth steering command suitable for field-robot navigation on rough terrain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4)**
> 1. **Skipping the negative gradient:** Students write $U_{att}$ and $U_{rep}$ but forget $\vec{F} = -\nabla U$ — **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing suppression with inhibition:** Subsumption has *both* operators; examiners penalize if you only define one.
> 3. **No local-minima discussion:** Any Potential Field answer is **incomplete** without mentioning local minima and its remedy.
> 4. **Forgetting boundary condition:** Always state that $U_{rep} = 0$ for $\rho > \rho_0$.
> 5. **Hybrid vs Reactive confusion:** Hybrid = Reactive + Deliberative. Do not equate hybrid with purely reactive.
> 6. **Fuzzy centroid missing:** Every fuzzy control derivation must end at the centroid defuzzification formula.
> 7. **Motor Schema ≠ Potential Field:** Arkin's Motor Schema Theory includes *weighted vector sum with gain scheduling*, which distinguishes it from plain potential fields.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Field Robotics)**

- [x] **Alternative control formalisms** = non-classical architectures (reactive, behavior-based, hybrid) used in field robots.
- [x] **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** = layered, no world model, uses **Suppression (S)** and **Inhibition (I)** operators.
- [x] **Potential Field (Khatib, 1986)** = robot in a scalar potential field; force = $-\nabla U$.
- [x] **Attractive potential** = parabolic well at goal.
- [x] **Repulsive potential** = $\frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$, active only within $\rho_0$.
- [x] **Local minima** is the canonical failure mode of potential fields.
- [x] **Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1987)** = gain-weighted vector summation of behaviour vectors.
- [x] **Fuzzy Logic** = linguistic rules, fuzzification → inference → defuzzification (centroid).
- [x] **FSM (Finite State Machine)** = mission-level task coordination (states, events, transitions).
- [x] **Hybrid 3-Tier Architecture** = Planner (slow) + Sequencer (medium) + Reactive Controller (fast).
- [x] **Examples of Hybrid Architectures:** **3T**, **CLARAty (NASA JPL)**, **AuRA (Arkin)**.
- [x] **Standard parameter values:** $k_{att} \approx 1$, $k_{rep} \approx 5$, $\rho_0 \approx 1$–$3$ m, control loop $10$–$100$ ms.
- [x] **Field robotics applications** — autonomous tractors, mining trucks, planetary rovers, underwater AUVs, forestry machines.
- [x] **For exam:** Always end a Potential Field answer with the **local-minima + remedy** discussion.
- [x] **For exam:** Always end a Subsumption answer with a labelled **layered block diagram**.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Alternative Control Formalisms**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education guidelines for PCRAT601 — Field Robotics.*
