# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Transmission Equation

The Friis equation relates received power to transmitted power in an untethered RF link. It is the single most-tested formula in KTU board questions for this module.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Isotropic Radiator

A lossless isotropic antenna radiates power uniformly over a sphere of radius $d$. The power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}}
$$

**Reasoning:** The total radiated power $P_t$ is distributed over the surface area of a sphere, $A = 4\pi d^{2}$.

### Step 2 — Capture by the Receiving Antenna

A receive antenna with effective aperture $A_e$ intercepts the power:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e
$$

**Reasoning:** Aperture is the "collecting area" of an antenna for incident plane waves.

### Step 3 — Express Aperture in Terms of Gain

The relationship between the effective aperture of an antenna and its gain $G$ for a wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi}
$$

**Reasoning:** Higher-gain antennas have larger effective apertures; the relationship follows from antenna reciprocity.

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting Step 1 and Step 3 into Step 2:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi}
$$

Reordering:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Reasoning:** We added $G_t$ symmetrically because both transmit and receive antennas are real (not isotropic) — each amplifies the flux relative to isotropic.

### Step 5 — Convert to dB Form (the Board-Exam Form)

Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides and substituting $\lambda = c / f$:

$$
P_r (\text{dBm}) = P_t (\text{dBm}) + G_t (\text{dBi}) + G_r (\text{dBi}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}(f) - 32.44
$$

where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

**Reasoning:** This is the link-budget form examiners expect. The constant 32.44 = $-10 \log_{10}[(4\pi/c)^{2}]$ where $c$ in m/s gives a numerical value that yields FSPL in dB when $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Channel Capacity (Maximum Data Rate)

A field robot's communication link can carry information only up to a maximum rate set by the available bandwidth and the signal-to-noise ratio.

### Step 1 — Define the Channel

A noisy channel has bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and additive white Gaussian noise with power $N$. If the signal power is $S$, the SNR is $S / N$.

### Step 2 — Apply Shannon's Theorem

$$
C = B \, \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** Shannon proved that reliable (error-free) communication is possible at any data rate $R \le C$, and impossible for $R > C$, provided appropriate coding is used. The $\log_{2}$ arises from the binary decision framework of information theory.

### Step 3 — Special Case — High SNR (S/N ≫ 1)

For strong signals:

$$
C \approx B \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** Doubling $S$ (or halving $N$) adds approximately $B$ extra bits per second to capacity.

### Step 4 — Special Case — Low SNR (S/N ≪ 1)

For weak signals:

$$
C \approx \frac{B \cdot S}{N \ln 2} \quad \text{(in bps)}
$$

**Reasoning:** Capacity becomes linear in SNR — every doubling of SNR doubles capacity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GEO Satellite Round-Trip Latency

For a field robot controlled from a ground station via a Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellite.

### Step 1 — Define Orbital Altitude

A GEO satellite orbits at altitude $h = 35{,}786$ km above the equator.

### Step 2 — Compute One-Way Light-Time

The signal travels at the speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s through vacuum/free space. One-way time:

$$
t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{h}{c} = \frac{35{,}786 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.1193 \text{ s} = 119.3 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Round-Trip (Uplink + Downlink)

For a control loop, the signal must travel up to the satellite and back down:

$$
L_{\text{RTT}} = \frac{2 h}{c} \approx 238.6 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 4 — Add Protocol/Processing Overhead

In practice, encoding, decoding, and queueing add **20–80 ms** extra, so total user-perceived latency is:

$$
L_{\text{total}} \approx 240 \text{ ms} \text{ to } 320 \text{ ms}
$$

**Reasoning:** This is the classic KTU insight — a robot being tele-operated through a GEO satellite experiences > 0.5 s of latency, which is *too slow* for fine manipulation. LEO constellations (h ≈ 550 km) reduce this to ≈ **20–40 ms**, making them the future of field-robot tele-operation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acoustic Time-of-Flight (Underwater Ranging)

For an AUV emitting a ping to a beacon:

### Step 1 — Time of Flight

The acoustic pulse travels at $c_{\text{water}} \approx 1500$ m/s. If the measured one-way travel time is $\Delta t$:

$$
d = c_{\text{water}} \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Variant

If the beacon reflects the pulse (like sonar):

$$
d = \frac{c_{\text{water}} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{RTT}}}{2}
$$

**Reasoning:** Halving accounts for the down-and-back path. This is the basis of every side-scan sonar, multibeam echo sounder, and underwater acoustic modem on an AUV.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — How to Pick the Right Untethered Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is the **single most important analytical tool** for KTU 14-mark questions asking "Compare and contrast types of untethered communication for a given field-robot scenario."

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | BLE | ZigBee | LoRa | Cellular 4G/5G | SATCOM (LEO) | Acoustic | Optical/FSO |
|-----------|-------|-----|--------|------|----------------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| Range | 30–200 m | 10–100 m | 10–100 m | 2–15 km | 1–10 km cell | Global | 1–20 km | 100 m–5 km |
| Data Rate | High (Gbps) | Low (Mbps) | Low (250 kbps) | Very low (kbps) | High (Gbps) | Med (Mbps) | Very low (kbps) | Very high (Gbps) |
| Latency | Low (ms) | Very low (ms) | Low (ms) | Med (s) | Low (1–50 ms) | Low (20–40 ms) | High (s) | Very low (ms) |
| Power | High | Very low | Low | Very low | Medium | High | High | Low |
| LoS needed | No | No | No | No | No | No | No (water) | **Yes** |
| Cost | Low | Very low | Low | Low | Subscription | High | High | Medium |
| Field-robot example | Indoor rescue | Wearable link | Greenhouse mesh | Farm telemetry | Mining tele-op | Oceanic AUV | Subsea AUV | Drone-to-drone |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Friis Link Budget

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-style calculation (frequently asked as a 7-mark part question):**

A ground robot transmits at $P_t = 20$ dBm on a $f = 2.4$ GHz carrier. Antennas have $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi. Compute the received power at $d = 500$ m, assuming free-space propagation.

### Step 1 — Compute FSPL

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) + 32.44
$$

$$
\text{FSPL} = -6.02 + 67.60 + 32.44 = 94.02 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Link Budget

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} = 20 + 3 + 3 - 94.02 = -68.02 \text{ dBm}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Linear Watts

$$
P_r (\text{W}) = 10^{\left(\frac{-68.02 - 30}{10}\right)} = 10^{-9.802} \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} = 158 \text{ pW}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is below the noise floor of a typical receiver, so the link would *fail* in practice. To make it work, the robot would need a higher-gain directional antenna, a more sensitive receiver, or to move closer (since FSPL grows with $20 \log d$, halving distance gains 6 dB).

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Untethered Communication

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[UNTETHERED COMMUNICATION] --> B1[RF COMMUNICATION]

    B2 --> D1[INFRARED IRDA]

```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Untethered Field-Robot Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    OPR[OPERATOR GCS] --> ENC1[COMMAND ENCODER]
    ENC1 --> MOD1[MODULATOR]
    MOD1 --> TXA1[TRANSMIT ANTENNA]
    TXA1 -. RF LIGHT SOUND .-> RXA1[RECEIVE ANTENNA]
    RXA1 --> MOD2[DEMODULATOR]
    MOD2 --> DEC1[COMMAND DECODER]
    DEC1 --> RBT[FIELD ROBOT CONTROLLER]
    RBT --> SENS[SENSORS AND PAYLOADS]
    SENS --> ENC2[TELEMETRY ENCODER]
    ENC2 --> MOD3[MODULATOR UPSTREAM]
    MOD3 --> TXA2[UPLINK TRANSMITTER]
    TXA2 -. RF LIGHT SOUND .-> RXA2[DOWNLINK RECEIVER]
    RXA2 --> DEC2[TELEMETRY DECODER]
    DEC2 --> OPR
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Untethered Link

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[START] --> Q2{IS THE ROBOT UNDERWATER}
    Q2 -- YES --> A1[USE ACOUSTIC MODEM]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{IS THE ROBOT AERIAL AND HIGH BANDWIDTH}
    Q3 -- YES --> A2[USE FSO OR MMWAVE]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{DISTANCE LESS THAN 200 M}
    Q4 -- YES --> A3[USE WI-FI OR BLE]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{IS THERE CELLULAR COVERAGE}
    Q5 -- YES --> A4[USE 4G OR 5G]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6{NEED GLOBAL COVERAGE}
    Q6 -- YES --> A5[USE SATCOM LEO OR GEO]
    Q6 -- NO --> A6[USE LORA OR ZIGBEE MESH]
```

## 4.4 Layered Protocol Stack for a Field-Robot Untethered Link

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5[APPLICATION LAYER TELEMETRY AND VIDEO]
    L4[TRANSPORT LAYER UDP OR TCP]
    L3[NETWORK LAYER IPV6 FOR IOT OR ROS2 DDS]
    L2[DATA LINK LAYER MAC PROTOCOL]
    L1[PHYSICAL LAYER RF OPTIC OR ACOUSTIC MODEM]
    L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the architecture:** Every untethered link in field robotics is implemented as a stack. The **physical layer** is where the "untethered" carrier (RF, optical, acoustic) lives. Higher layers (e.g., ROS 2 / DDS, MAVLink) carry the actual robot data. The choice of physical layer dictates the constraints of every higher layer.

## 4.5 Comparison Flow — RF vs. Optical vs. Acoustic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[CHOOSE CARRIER MEDIUM] --> S1{OPERATING MEDIUM IS AIR}
    S1 -- YES --> S2{WEATHER FOG OR DUST PRESENT}
    S2 -- YES --> S3[PREFER RF OVER OPTICAL]
    S2 -- NO --> S4[HIGH BANDWIDTH NEEDED]
    S4 -- YES --> S5[OPTICAL FSO PREFERRED]
    S4 -- NO --> S6[RF WI-FI OR LORA]
    S1 -- NO WATER --> S7[ACOUSTIC IS THE ONLY VIABLE MEDIUM]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following question bank mirrors the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference. Part A carries 3 marks each; Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two full questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *untethered communication* as applied to field robotics. List **any three** advantages it offers over tethered (cable-based) communication.

**Model Answer:**

> **Untethered communication** refers to wireless data exchange between a field robot and its operator/control infrastructure using electromagnetic, acoustic, or optical carriers propagated through free space, water, or air, **without** any physical cable connecting the robot to the base.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Mobility:** The robot can traverse unstructured, obstacle-rich terrain without a trailing cable that could snag or break.
2. **Operational Range:** Tether length physically limits distance; wireless links extend this from metres to thousands of kilometres (via SATCOM).
3. **Operator Safety:** The human operator can remain at a safe standoff distance in hazardous environments (EOD, nuclear, mining).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** and **LoRa** as untethered communication technologies for a field robot, with respect to *data rate*, *range*, and *power consumption*.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11) | LoRa |
|-----------|----------------|------|
| Data Rate | Up to **Gbps** (high) | **0.3–50 kbps** (very low) |
| Range | **30–200 m** (outdoor) | **2–15 km** (rural) |
| Power Consumption | **High** (hundreds of mW) | **Very low** (tens of mW) |
| Use case in field robotics | Real-time HD video from a UGV | Periodic telemetry from agricultural robots |

**Conclusion:** Wi-Fi is preferred when high bandwidth and short range are needed (tele-op), while LoRa is preferred when long range and low power are needed (large-scale telemetry).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of an untethered communication link between a ground operator station and a field robot. Clearly mark the *uplink* (command) and *downlink* (telemetry) directions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Friis free-space transmission equation** and compute the received power at a distance of **2 km** for a transmitter radiating **1 W** at **900 MHz** with isotropic antennas on both ends. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

A complete untethered link consists of two **half-duplex or full-duplex** paths:

**Uplink (Operator → Robot, Command path):**
1. **Operator input** at the Ground Control Station (GCS) is encoded.
2. The encoded command is **modulated** (FSK, PSK, QAM, or LoRa chirp, depending on the protocol).
3. The modulated signal is fed to a **transmit antenna** and radiated as an electromagnetic wave.
4. The robot's **receive antenna** captures the wave; the signal is **demodulated** and **decoded** back to a command packet.
5. The robot's **onboard controller** (e.g., a ROS 2 node) executes the command.

**Downlink (Robot → Operator, Telemetry path):**
1. Sensors (LiDAR, IMU, camera) produce **telemetry** data.
2. The data is **encoded**, **modulated**, and transmitted by the robot's antenna.
3. The operator station's **receive antenna** captures the signal; it is **demodulated** and **decoded**.
4. The telemetry is displayed on the operator's HMI/GCS.

**Functional Architecture Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[OPERATOR GCS] --> E1[ENCODER CMD]
    E1 --> M1[MODULATOR]
    M1 --> T1[TX ANTENNA]
    T1 -. WIRELESS CARRIER .-> R1[RX ANTENNA]
    R1 --> DM1[DEMODULATOR]
    DM1 --> D1[DECODER CMD]
    D1 --> RC[ROBOT CONTROLLER]
    RC --> SENSE[ONBOARD SENSORS]
    SENSE --> E2[ENCODER TLM]
    E2 --> M2[MODULATOR]
    M2 --> T2[TX ANTENNA]
    T2 -. WIRELESS CARRIER .-> R2[RX ANTENNA]
    R2 --> DM2[DEMODULATOR]
    DM2 --> D2[DECODER TLM]
    D2 --> HMI[OPERATOR HMI]
```

The wireless carrier can be RF, optical, or acoustic depending on the field-robot application.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — State the Friis Equation:**

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Parameters:**

- $P_t = 1$ W
- $f = 900$ MHz $= 900 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic)
- $d = 2$ km $= 2000$ m

**Step 3 — Compute Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{900 \times 10^{6}} = 0.333 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Path-Loss Term:**

$$
\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d} = \frac{0.333}{4 \pi \times 2000} = \frac{0.333}{25132.7} = 1.325 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 5 — Square It:**

$$
\left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2} = (1.325 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 1.756 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Received Power:**

$$
P_r = 1 \times 1 \times 1 \times 1.756 \times 10^{-10} = 1.756 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to dBm:**

$$
P_r (\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.756 \times 10^{-10}}{10^{-3}}\right) = -67.5 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Interpretation:** A received power of $-67.5$ dBm is well above the typical noise floor of a LoRa receiver ($\approx -148$ dBm), confirming that 1 W of isotropic transmit power is *vastly more* than necessary for a 2 km LoRa link — illustrating why LoRa can be used with mW-level transmitters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **RF, Optical, and Acoustic** untethered communication technologies for field robots, using a structured table covering *carrier frequency/wavelength*, *typical range*, *data rate*, *propagation medium*, and *key limitation*. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A field robot operates in a **seawater environment** at a depth of 100 m. Explain **why RF communication is unsuitable**, and describe the **underwater acoustic communication** alternative, including a derivation of the one-way latency for a 5 km horizontal link. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

| Parameter | RF | Optical (FSO) | Acoustic |
|-----------|----|---------------|----------|
| Carrier | Electromagnetic wave **3 kHz – 300 GHz** | Light wave (**λ** ≈ 1550 nm) | Sound wave (**f** ≈ 10 kHz – 1 MHz) |
| Propagation medium | Air, vacuum, but **severely attenuated in water** | Air, vacuum (very narrow beam) | Water, air (slower than RF by 5 orders of magnitude) |
| Typical range | m to **thousands of km** (with SATCOM) | 100 m to **several km** (line-of-sight) | 1 km to **20+ km** (subsea) |
| Data rate | Up to **Gbps** (Wi-Fi, 5G) | Up to **10 Gbps** (lab), hundreds of Mbps (practical) | **bps to kbps** |
| Key limitation | Spectrum congestion, security, RF interference in mines | Weather (fog, dust), requires precise pointing | Low bandwidth, high latency, multipath |

**Engineering takeaway:** No single technology fits all field-robotics scenarios — engineers must trade off **range, bandwidth, latency, and environment**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Why RF is unsuitable underwater:**

1. **Severe attenuation:** Seawater has electrical conductivity of ≈ 4 S/m. Electromagnetic waves suffer attenuation proportional to $\sqrt{f}$ due to ionic conduction.
2. **Quantitative proof:** Attenuation in seawater is given empirically by:

$$
\alpha \approx 0.017 \sqrt{f_{\text{MHz}}} \quad \text{dB/m for } f < 1000 \text{ MHz}
$$

For $f = 2.4$ GHz (Wi-Fi), $\alpha \approx 14$ dB/m, meaning a **1 cm link loses 14 dB**, and a 1 m link loses 1400 dB — completely unusable. Even at 100 kHz, attenuation is ≈ 0.5 dB/m, limiting practical range to a few metres.

3. **Conclusion:** Because RF attenuates within centimetres in seawater, an underwater robot has no choice but to use acoustic or optical (blue-green) communication. Optical is range-limited by turbidity; acoustic is therefore the **standard** for AUV/ROV command and telemetry.

**Underwater acoustic communication:**

- **Frequency band:** 10 kHz – 1 MHz (lower frequencies for longer range).
- **Modem example:** WHOI Micro-Modem, Evologics S2C.
- **Mechanism:** Acoustic transducers (piezoelectric) convert electrical signals to pressure waves; hydrophones receive them.

**Derivation of one-way latency for a 5 km link:**

**Step 1 — Sound speed in seawater:**

$$
c_{\text{water}} \approx 1500 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — One-way travel time:**

$$
t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{d}{c_{\text{water}}} = \frac{5000}{1500} = 3.33 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Round-trip time (typical for request-acknowledge protocols):**

$$
t_{\text{RTT}} = \frac{2 d}{c_{\text{water}}} = 6.67 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

- This **3.3 s one-way latency** is roughly **30× larger** than a GEO satellite's one-way latency (≈ 120 ms) and about **100× larger** than a 5G link (≈ 10 ms).
- Such latency is **incompatible with real-time tele-operation**; hence AUVs are typically *autonomous* between acoustic way-point updates.
- Bandwidth is limited to **kbps**, so video streaming is impossible — only low-rate telemetry and commands are exchanged.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams for this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing units in Friis equation:** Students frequently plug $d$ in metres into a formula expecting km. Result: the constant **32.44** only works for km/MHz. **Always declare units first.**
> 2. **Confusing "range" definitions:** Wi-Fi range "30 m" is *outdoor* with line-of-sight; indoor range is half of that. Examiners dock marks for vague statements like "Wi-Fi works over 30 m" without conditions.
> 3. **Forgetting the speed-of-light vs. sound-of-water distinction:** Acoustic latency is 5 orders of magnitude higher than RF latency. Mark loss is common when students assume "wireless = same speed."
> 4. **Saying "Bluetooth and Wi-Fi are the same":** They share the 2.4 GHz ISM band, but the protocols, range, data rate, and use cases are entirely different. Examiners will deduct at least one mark for this conflation.
> 5. **Not specifying the carrier band:** If a question says "explain LoRa," you must state it operates in the **sub-GHz ISM band** to score full marks.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Untethered Communication in Field Robotics**

- **Definition:** Untethered communication is wireless (RF / optical / acoustic / magnetic) data exchange between a field robot and a remote operator/peer, with **no physical cable**.
- **Why untethered?** Mobility, range, operator safety, terrain impassability for cables.
- **Five families:** (1) **RF** (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, Cellular, SATCOM, UWB, SDR), (2) **Optical** (IR, VLC/Li-Fi, FSO/Laser), (3) **Acoustic** (underwater modems, ultrasonic), (4) **Magnetic Induction** (near-field, through-earth), (5) **Hybrid/Emerging** (cognitive radio, mmWave, molecular).
- **Key metrics to memorise:** Range, data rate, latency, power, frequency, BER, LoS requirement.
- **Friis Equation (the most-tested formula):**
  $$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$
- **dB form of FSPL:** $20 \log_{10} d + 20 \log_{10} f + 32.44$ (with $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz).
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- **Underwater:** RF attenuates within cm → use **acoustic** ($c \approx 1500$ m/s). Latency for 5 km ≈ 3.3 s one-way.
- **GEO satellite:** $h = 35{,}786$ km → one-way latency ≈ 119 ms; round-trip ≈ 240 ms.
- **LEO satellite:** $h \approx 550$ km → one-way latency ≈ 4–20 ms — preferred for tele-operation.
- **Wi-Fi:** 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz, 30–200 m, high data rate, high power.
- **BLE:** 2.4 GHz, 10–100 m, very low power, low data rate, peripherals.
- **ZigBee:** 2.4 GHz, 10–100 m, **mesh**, sensor networks.
- **LoRa:** sub-GHz, 2–15 km, very low data rate, very low power, telemetry.
- **Cellular 4G/5G:** licensed bands, km-scale, high data rate, 5G latency ≈ 1 ms (URLLC).
- **SATCOM:** LEO / GEO, global, Mbps, high latency for GEO.
- **UWB:** 3.1–10.6 GHz, centimetre-level localisation, used in swarm robotics.
- **Optical/FSO:** high bandwidth, line-of-sight, weather-sensitive.
- **Trade-off matrix:** Wi-Fi for short-range high-bandwidth; LoRa for long-range low-bandwidth; SATCOM for global; acoustic for underwater; optical for high-bandwidth line-of-sight drone links.
- **Layered stack:** Application (telemetry) → Transport (UDP/TCP) → Network (IPv6 / ROS 2 DDS) → Data Link (MAC) → Physical (RF/optic/acoustic modem).
- **Exam strategy:** Always state the carrier band, range, data rate, and a field-robot use case when defining any untethered technology — this guarantees a full 3-mark answer in Part A.
- **Common pitfall:** Never confuse the speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) with the speed of sound in water ($\approx 1500$ m/s) — acoustic links are **200,000× slower** than RF links.
